<a id="t-1e6a4f6979"></a>
## Thread: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com>`

```
hmmm.... maybe i should duck... but I do want to respond to this
thread... I realize some may strongly disagree, but that's the fun of
this group, right? we share different perspectives....  here's mine...

First, I would like to propose that all posts be in text format, not
HTML... just my preference. having to open a browser to read posts is
inconvenient....   (to me)...

Second, the older preference to put periods after each statement
should be discontinued (my opinion). Should I ever have the
opportunity to wear the dark cloak of the inquisition, I would be
seeking the programmers who use periods within paragraphs.... LOLOL   

The period's syntax meaning is basically "Stop execution of sequential
instruction...."  This contradicts the concept of structured
programming and sequential execution. You need to look back at COBOL's
origin to understand that the early developers were trying for a
readable language and mistakenly felt it should read like a book. Pick
up a respected text book of the late '60's (such as McCracken's...)
and you will see what is now considered horrible code... IF statements
that run on as sentences and paragraphs as though written by Dickens.


The concept of 'statement' and 'sentence' is out-of-date... focus on
statements will cause better code to be generated (my opinion.) the
NEXT SENTENCE clause means " stop execution, search for a period, and
pick up execution at statement following period", whereas CONTINUE
means "continue execution at next possible statement"  -- may sound
similar but very different. 

My belief is that every paragraph should have just one period in it.
Why? because almost every serious programming error I've encountered
was due to a period in the wrong place -- and always unnecessary.  the
period no longer serves any useful purpose, now that ansi 85 features
are available. 
whew! uh-oh... do i hear incoming?  (david ducking....<g>)
david
```

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Just as there are logic errors from misplaced periods, I have encountered
just as many from missing END-IF's.  For example:

        IF a = b
             IF  b = c
                   IF d =e
                       PERFORM something
             END-IF
        END-IF

       PERFORM something-else.

*      Woops, para something-else will only be performed if the previous 3
IF's  are true.  I don't think this code fragment is any clearer than:


        IF a = b
             IF  b = c
                   IF d =e
                       PERFORM something.

      PERFORM something-else.

This is obviously a very trivial example, but consider if the IF's went on
for 50 - 100 lines or more, with a cavalier attitude toward indenting.

David wrote:

> hmmm.... maybe i should duck... but I do want to respond to this
> thread... I realize some may strongly disagree, but that's the fun of
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> david
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363c75eb.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Your example shows a case where the period IS useful. A large
number of END-IF is indeed error-prone.
To be really good, you should put the period on a line by itself
.You could also just use the AND operator:

IF a = b
AND b = c
AND d = e
      PERFORM something
END-IF   (or period)

John Trifon wrote in message <363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com>...
>Just as there are logic errors from misplaced periods, I have encountered
>just as many from missing END-IF's.  For example:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363C8952.1D0F6315@ix.netcom.com>`

```
AND would work well if there were only IF's, but not in this example:
    IF a = b
        PERFORM AAA
        IF  b = c
              MOVE x TO y
              IF d = e
                  PERFORM something.

       PERFORM something-else.

I don't put a period on a separate line, can't tell if it's dirt on the
monitor. I've also seen various programmers put the period anywhere from col 12
to 72.


Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> Your example shows a case where the period IS useful. A large
> number of END-IF is indeed error-prone.
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…

```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<363c9bf9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com> <363c75eb.0@news1.ibm.net> <363C8952.1D0F6315@ix.netcom.com>`

John Trifon wrote in message <363C8952.1D0F6315@ix.netcom.com>...
>AND would work well if there were only IF's, but not in this example:
>    IF a = b
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>monitor. I've also seen various programmers put the period anywhere from
col 12
>to 72.
>

In this case you are right, but if you need to do something
before each IF, you will end up eventually to have to do
something AFTER one of more of them and then you
have to use END-IF. If I need deeply nested IF statements
like this I tend to avoid the complicated clause by making
some of the inner IFs into PERFORMED paragraphs.
This has several advantages and one disadvantage.

I fully agree that the construction
IF
    IF
          IF
                 IF
                        IF
                               IF
                               END-IF
                        END-IF
                 END-IF
         END-IF
   END-IF
END-IF

is hard to manage and maintain and is not very readable
(especially if it sprawls over several pages)
\



>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >Just as there are logic errors from misplaced periods, I have
encountered
>> >just as many from missing END-IF's.  For example:

3

>> >This is obviously a very trivial example, but consider if the IF's went
on

IF   ((state-number-one is equal to state-number-two)
        AND
        (something-that-means-something is equal to another-meaningful-name)
        AND
        (a-third-condition is equal to something-that-i-can-read))
                 PERFORM SOMETHING.
I'll bet the compiler would not even notice the difference.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.66545.16964@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<363C4ED9.ABB6E2FD@ix.netcom.com>> John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> writes:
> Just as there are logic errors from misplaced periods, I have
encountered
> just as many from missing END-IF's.  For example:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> IF's  are true.  I don't think this code fragment is any clearer than:

How did you determine that this wasn't an indenting error ?

Actually I am in favour of a compiler option that enfores
all scope terminators to be present, then a warning would
have been issued.

>         IF a = b
>              IF  b = c
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> for 50 - 100 lines or more, with a cavalier attitude toward indenting.

If you are going to use full stops then please don't 'bury'
them on the end of a line where they are hard to see.  They
are far more obvious if on a line by themselves:

           IF ( a = b )
               IF ( b = c )
                   IF ( d = e )
                        PERFORM Something
                        .
            PERFORM Something-else
            .

Having some lines with '.' on the end and some without leads
to problems.  This can be solved by 'only having . at the
end of a paragraph' or by 'having . in its own line', or
both (preferably).

By never having a '.' on the end of a line you remove the
problem of needing to add or remove them when the code
requires to be changed to being under a conditional
or not.

I have also started to adopt parantheses around conditions
(as above).  I find this tends to make them stand out
better, especially the multiline IFs:

           IF ( File-Status = Record-Missing
            AND Inserting-Option = "Y"
              )
               PERFORM .....

Some are bound to hate that too    ;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71hst8$mgc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```

David wrote in message <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...

>The period's syntax meaning is basically "Stop execution of sequential
>instruction...."  This contradicts the concept of structured
>programming and sequential execution. You need to look back at COBOL's


No, I disagree.  The period's syntactical meaning is "this is a delimiter
for an item in a list".  A list of instructions is basic to structure.  By
eliminating
it, you are limiting yourself to one instruction per program label.

>origin to understand that the early developers were trying for a
>readable language and mistakenly felt it should read like a book. Pick
>up a respected text book of the late '60's (such as McCracken's...)
>and you will see what is now considered horrible code... IF statements
>that run on as sentences and paragraphs as though written by Dickens.


Structure does not impose good coding.  It is a tool, nothing more
and nothing less.  Readability comes about because somebody
expends effort making it readable, not because they blindly follow
a programming rule.  A program should be an easily followed set
of instructions for both a computer and a human.  The rest is just
fad/style.

>The concept of 'statement' and 'sentence' is out-of-date... focus on
>statements will cause better code to be generated (my opinion.) the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>similar but very different.


They mean different things, that is true.  At times, though, programmers
want to *do* different things.  When you make a rule saying "you can
never express anything like this", it does not make differences clearer. It
means I have to trick the language into expressing something that someone
else has decided I shouldn't want to do.  It is my job to make the meaning
clear, not to decide what meanings should be allowed.  That is chosen
by the reality of the specification.

>My belief is that every paragraph should have just one period in it.
>Why? because almost every serious programming error I've encountered
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>whew! uh-oh... do i hear incoming?  (david ducking....<g>)


<G>  yep.

I have seen lots of similar errors.  However I have also spent many hours
flitting about trying to find them, mainly because someone broke the code
down far too finely, and there are bits and pieces all over the place that
are almost impossible to read without reverse engineering the program.

As a simple example, I will take code where the writer has been extremely
careful in choosing data names that make the meaning clear but a bit
sloppy on structure, over well structured code with private little mnemonics
for data names that are entirely meaningless to a different brain, any
day of the week.

I am assuming, of course, that all aspects of the code are at least
reasonable.  Totally ignoring *anything* seldom produces a "good"
program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.67744.17603@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71hst8$mgc$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<71hst8$mgc$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> David wrote in message <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> it, you are limiting yourself to one instruction per program label.

Simply not true.  The '.' is not the _only_ "delimiter for an
item in a list".  All the END-~ scope terminators are also
delimiters.  It just happens that '.' delimits all active
lists and a END-~ scope delimits just one each.

In fact the full stops actuall meaning is: "this is a delimiter
for _all_ lists".  It is too limiting on structure.  By using
it you are limiting yourself to .....
```

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71i6jl$i3m$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```


David wrote:
> 
> hmmm.... maybe i should duck... but I do want to respond to this
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> seeking the programmers who use periods within paragraphs.... LOLOL

Don't you think that RED would be a more appropriate color Cardinal???

> 
> The period's syntax meaning is basically "Stop execution of sequential
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> david

Some interesting thoughts David. The problem I see is that the average 
Cobol programmer is not spending the time to learn the new features
that are available in the language. Using a simple Evaluate can confuse
a number of programmers. While this is slowly changing and programmers
are learning the newer features what you are suggesting is...risky.

It is risky because 95% or more of the existing code contains multiple
periods per paragraph. I for one would not want to be the person who
attempts to introduce the concept of one period per paragraph. You drop
one or two of those little land mines into a shop and I think the sparks
will really fly. 

Nice idea and I think it is interesting but I am not brave enough to 
lead this new wave you may have that honor. :-)

PS, I enjoy your books and wish you success with you latest. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.65455.16359@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>> d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com  writes:
> 
> Second, the older preference to put periods after each statement
> should be discontinued (my opinion). Should I ever have the

Actually, while some claim that they 'put a full stop after
every statement', this is usually untrue.  They may 'put
a full stop after every statement that is not part of a
conditional block'.  This is very different.

Of course it is possible that they really do use one after
every statement by only ever putting one statement in each
conditional (IF, AT END, ON SIZE ERROR, ...) and never
using ELSE, but such programs can easily be consigned to
the round file without further ado.

I suspect that if a block of code become conditional then
they remove all the full stops (or most of them), and then
put them back on should the code be moved to outside the
IF statement.  I never understood why they thought it
was necessary or desirable to do this.

It seems to fall into the categorey of 'If you don't know
what you are doing then do it neatly'.  But then I don't
consider the '.' to ne neat either.


BTW, in history a 'period' is from the start to the end,
in music it is from the first note to the last.  In a 
sentence the 'period' is from the first letter or sylabyl
until the last.  These are consistent usages of the 
word.  The '.' is a full stop.

   ;-)
```

```
In article <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
David <d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>hmmm.... maybe i should duck... but I do want to respond to this
>thread... I realize some may strongly disagree, but that's the fun of
>this group, right? we share different perspectives....  here's mine...

[snippage]

>You need to look back at COBOL's
>origin to understand that the early developers were trying for a
>readable language and mistakenly felt it should read like a book.

Hmmmm... maybe I'm missing something.  Wasn't COBOL designed to be
'English-like' for precisely this reason?

>Pick
>up a respected text book of the late '60's (such as McCracken's...)
>and you will see what is now considered horrible code... IF statements
>that run on as sentences and paragraphs as though written by Dickens.

Ummmm... McCracken was *my* textbook; to say that a programmer 'writes
COBOL that reads like a novel' used to be a Very High Compliment... has
something changed while I wasn't looking or are you bemoaning the fact
that COBOL isn't more like, say, Modula-2?

>
>
>The concept of 'statement' and 'sentence' is out-of-date... focus on
>statements will cause better code to be generated (my opinion.)

Ummmm... is there a contradction there?

>the
>NEXT SENTENCE clause means " stop execution, search for a period, and
>pick up execution at statement following period", whereas CONTINUE
>means "continue execution at next possible statement"  -- may sound
>similar but very different. 

... and both are useful constructs, or so I've seen... and both can be
*ab*used, or so I've seen.

>
>My belief is that every paragraph should have just one period in it.
>Why? because almost every serious programming error I've encountered
>was due to a period in the wrong place -- and always unnecessary.  

That's the only kind of 'serious programming error(s)' you've ever
encountered?  Dear me, old boy, *do* try to get out more often.

DD

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363DE9D1.9667B6BA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36379812.1C197F2D@home.com> <36393E21.EFDB9C1E@inficad.com> <363cac6f.31145767@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> My belief is that every paragraph should have just one period in it.
> Why? because almost every serious programming error I've encountered
> was due to a period in the wrong place -- and always unnecessary.  the
> period no longer serves any useful purpose, now that ansi 85 features
> are available.

Only if you use END- for each command.    Periods make it certain that
each command is complete.  I have seen quite a few errors caused by not
having periods (and at least one case where a global edit moved the
period to column 73 - this was before CRTs, the programmer was looking
at printed code and did not see the problem!

The END-IF, END-PERFORM etc serve the same purpose of terminating a
statement unambiguously.   Such termination makes maintenance and
debugging much easier.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298307.65832.13697@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<363DE9D1.9667B6BA@home.com>`

```
In message <<363DE9D1.9667B6BA@home.com>> Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> writes:
> > Why? because almost every serious programming error I've encountered
> > was due to a period in the wrong place -- and always unnecessary.  the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Only if you use END- for each command.    Periods make it certain that

Actually it is only necessary to use END- terminators for the
statements that have conditional phrases.  It is possible
to use END- on almost every 'command' (in Cobol we call them
statements), such as:

          ADD X TO Y
          END-ADD

but it is only required when the conditional phrase is used:
  
          ADD X TO Y
              ON SIZE ERROR
                  block of statements
          END-ADD

I would disuade programmers from using scope terminators
on _every_ statement.  It is unnecessary.

> each command is complete.  I have seen quite a few errors caused by not
> having periods (and at least one case where a global edit moved the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The END-IF, END-PERFORM etc serve the same purpose of terminating a

END-PERFORM does _not_ server the same purpose as a '.'
It is a required part of an in-line PERFORM.  In ANSI85
Cobol you cannot have an in-line PERFORM terminataed by
a full-stop (though this is an MF extention).

> statement unambiguously.   Such termination makes maintenance and
> debugging much easier.

One problem here is that people find 'easier' what it is they
have trained themselves to do.  If they automatically and
unconsciously scan for stops at the end of lines they
will pick up missing/extra ones without thinking about it,
while END-~ terminators will grate on them because their
training can't deal with them.

The reverse is true too.  Programmers trained on END-'s
will simply not see the stops and will find them 
annoying.

The scope terminators give more flexibility, but what is
'easier' depends on what one is used to and what one 
expects.

Programmers that have honed their skills for 30 years on
one style may look old fashioned, but they can probably
see the bugs in programs in that style at a glance, and
would struggle for days with a missing END- scope
terminator, simply because it goes against their
training.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3640BECB.650166D3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<363DE9D1.9667B6BA@home.com> <3298307.65832.13697@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
> Programmers that have honed their skills for 30 years on
> one style may look old fashioned, but they can probably
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> training.

My first COBOL program was in 1969, but I did take a few years off, so I
still have most of a decade to go.  I still put periods after every
statement by default, only leaving a statement unterminated by design.

Unlike lots of people, I scatter semi-colons after statements also. 
They show my intention of not having a period (never commas, they can
look like periods).
```

Never had any where the programmer used semi-colons.

<grin> I must admit that really amused me. :) Leaving off a
scope indicator almost gives me the hives, but that is because I 
come from an non-COBOL background. 

Really though, a hunk of code that looks like:

if this-condition-is-true then
  perform a-do-this
  perform a-do-that-too
  compute this = this + that
else
  perform a-do-that
  perform a-do-this-to
  end-if. 

Is much more comfortable with that scope terminator. :) 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<724aa6$pan$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3640BECB.650166D3@home.com> <3298309.63692.18720@kcbbs.gen.nz> <7200is$j4$1@news.igs.net> <722i6k$8o6@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
About 95% of it is habit, I gotta admit. But keep in mind
that one is used to not having them, then one can re-arrange
the code so that the absence is not missed.  Cobol has always
been my main high level language; I have coded in it or one
of several dozen assemblers for thirty years now. I have gotten
so used to the problem of the "at end" in Cobol, that I just
naturally code with a pre-read and a read at the end of the
loop for example.  Also I would never do an in-line perform,
but would simply give it a paragraph name.

With your example, I would code it as:

if this-condition-is-true
  perform a-do-this
  perform a-do-that-too
  add this to that
else
  perform a-do-that
  perform a-do-this-to.

The compute statement and the end-if, are incongruous to me,
and serve no purpose.  I try to write Cobol as if the reader was
an excellent programmer who had never seen a Cobol program
before.  That can be done better by really careful chunking of
the code down into paragraphs, careful choice of paragraph names
and careful choice of data names than the addition of all
the scope indicators in the world, ant least in my opinion.

A run-on sentence is bad form in any language.  I think that
is what it boils down to for me.  If the scope indicators are being
used to make the code readable, then fine.  If they are being
used to cure the fact that the writer is prone to poor sentence
structure, and needs them to make the meaning clear, then
they should be avoided as a simple matter of discipline, like
that old adage of "never use a pronoun when there is a noun".


paulr wrote in message <722i6k$8o6@lotho.delphi.com>...
><grin> I must admit that really amused me. :) Leaving off a
>scope indicator almost gives me the hives, but that is because I
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>: to say that I never use them, but just not often.  It is also not
laziness.
>
>: >Even those within an IF ?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298311.4503.15320@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<7200is$j4$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<7200is$j4$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> 
> Yep, even if an if, and yes, it is work, but not much.  And no, I would
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to say that I never use them, but just not often.  It is also not laziness.

What would you say is "religious use of the scope delimiters" ?
Perhaps wherever it is possible to use them ? such as on
every verb that caters for them whether a conditional exists
or not ?

          ADD 1        TO This-Counter END-ADD
          CALL "MYSUB"                 END-CALL
          etc

or just where they usefully terminate the scope of the verb
and are necessary to do so ?

How would you characterise using a full-stop wherever it is
possible to do so even if it is not needed for any purpose
such as terminating a scope ?

In general, peoplelike what they have trained themselves
to use.  This tends to become more important than any 
technical merits (this applies to me too).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364716b5.406137945@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<7200is$j4$1@news.igs.net> <3298311.4503.15320@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 8 Nov 98 01:15:03 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:


>
>What would you say is "religious use of the scope delimiters" ?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>          etc

Those would get my vote and I'm probably more religious with scope
delimiters than most. I use END-IF on every IF I use though I don't
use end-call unless there are a large amount of parameters. It's
sometimes useful for debugging to have end-call though as you can set
break points on them.

>
>or just where they usefully terminate the scope of the verb
>and are necessary to do so ?

I guess that's where I most use them. My line in the sand. So much so
that I do it in other languages where most programmers who haven't
used COBOL wouldn't use scope delimiters. eg. using begin...end in
Pascal for one statement if's. Using multiple lines and braces in C
and Java.

>
>How would you characterise using a full-stop wherever it is
>possible to do so even if it is not needed for any purpose
>such as terminating a scope ?

Now that's religious! Spawn of satan obviously. ;-)

>
>In general, peoplelike what they have trained themselves
>to use.  This tends to become more important than any 
>technical merits (this applies to me too).

Sure but that doesn't mean that people shouldn't re-train themselves.
The people that have to support the code in the future will be trained
later than yourself so sticking to a style from the dark ages isn't
going to help them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36435C20.519F05E7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3640BECB.650166D3@home.com> <3298309.63692.18720@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> In message <<3640BECB.650166D3@home.com>> Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> writes:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Even those within an IF ?

The default is period.  I leave it out only where dictated by logic.


> Don't you find it a real pain taking the off and putting them
> back on if you need to make a block subject to an if ?

Don't recall that ever happening.  But with over 20 years experience, it
probably has.  If the block is large, I pull it out and make a paragraph
out of it.  

> 
> No. Don't tell me, you don't put the IF in and indent the
> block but put in:    IF ( NOT condition )
>                          GO TO jumpoverthisblock.

Yuck!  Good thing for your smiley below!

Cutting and pasting is easy.  And creating new paragraphs is easy to
maintain. 
 
>        ;-)
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Never had any where the programmer used semi-colons.

Commas can be mis-read, causing confusion.  Semi-colons add clarity -
they cannot be mistaken for periods, and they indicate a purpose, sort
of like redundant parenthesis in complicated compute statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c94cd.295935@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<3640BECB.650166D3@home.com> <3298309.63692.18720@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

>> [...]I still put periods after every
>> statement by default, only leaving a statement unterminated by design.
>
>Even those within an IF ?
>

I think the previous poster would consider statements within an IF
would be considered "by design."

Obviously, newer techniques can be implemented for new development,
within the confines of whatever in-house coding "standards" one must
follow.  Many programs that were written for COBOL-68 or COBOL-74 were
modified only the minimum necessary for a successful compile under
COBOL-85.  In fact, some were never changed; they still use the older
compilers.

When modifying an existing program, a drastic change in style,
appearance, etc. may lead to confusion for those that wade through the
code in subsequent years.  (All scopes are terminated by periods,
except for what you added/changed.)


>Don't you find it a real pain taking the off and putting them 
>back on if you need to make a block subject to an if ?
>
So is adjusting the indentation, but ...


>No. Don't tell me, you don't put the IF in and indent the 
>block but put in:    IF ( NOT condition )
>                         GO TO jumpoverthisblock.

Don't remind us of such things.  On the other hand, I have seen worse.


[snip]
>
>Certainly I strip out commas from any source that I am given.
[...]

Personally, I am not sure of the advisability of any changes to a
source file outside the scope of the project requirements.


Just my opinion.  YMMV.
```

---

<a id="t-9b7e3c9498"></a>
## Thread: How many bytes is S9(03).... — 55 this month / 72 total

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-9b7e3c9498)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.63833.15461@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71fuk9$d0g$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<71fuk9$d0g$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net>...
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything?  Not even a copy library?

The way that numerics are represented does not affect the
way that the code performs and so the variations will
not prevent (of itself) a program producing the same results
on various compilers.

There may, however, be an issue with file portability, which
is why you may want to specify such features as

      USAGE DISPLAY SIGN LEADING SEPARATE

and then you only have to worry about the character set.
```

Plus all the screen I/O.  Plus any file sharing, though the

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> >
> >How about old fashioned master file update?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Plus all the screen I/O.  Plus any file sharing, though the

How much 'screen I/O' or 'File Sharing' do you think exists
in a <old fashioned> master file update ?

ACCEPT and DISPLAY are standard.

> *first" digit of the file-status is probably fairly consistent,

All file status codes except '9x' should be consistent on
all '85 compilers.  For batch master file updates this is
enough.

> so maybe there we are ok.  The initialization differences
> can be handled by coding for the lowest common
> denominator, but do we need declaratives for error
> handling or not?

Declaratives ?  You don't _need_ declaratives, I never use 
them.  All file statements give a File status that can be
checked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> writes:

>How much 'screen I/O' or 'File Sharing' do you think exists
>in a <old fashioned> master file update ?


How old fashioned?  Pre 1970? Any master file update
that I've written since then has had to cope with both.

>ACCEPT and DISPLAY are standard.


Not a chance.  In fact, screen sections are probably more
compatable than straight up accept and display.  I am talking
about *real* programs here, something that actually has to
be good enough to be used regularly.

>> *first" digit of the file-status is probably fairly consistent,
>
>All file status codes except '9x' should be consistent on
>all '85 compilers.  For batch master file updates this is
>enough.


Not even the format is guaranteed.  The second byte may
be be usage display or usage binary.  Certainly I
would never do a conversion and trust *that* to work.  What's
batch?  You mean run from a card deck?  No screen?  No
keyboard?  No network?  Does such a thing still exist?

>> so maybe there we are ok.  The initialization differences
>> can be handled by coding for the lowest common
>> denominator, but do we need declaratives for error
>> handling or not?



>Declaratives ?  You don't _need_ declaratives, I never use
>them.  All file statements give a File status that can be
>checked.


Sorry but that is not true.  Without a declaratives section, the
Fujitsu Compiler (for example) will not even get you to where
you can look at it. You will be back in the OS with the PC
equivalent of an ABEND.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz> <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net>...
>Richard Plinston wrote in message
  <some snippage>
>
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

  WRONG: for file status codes OTHER THAN 9x, the first and second byte MUST
be "display".   It is true that some compilers store the 2nd byte (of 9x -
ONLY file status codes) as binary - but even then it is the "alphanumeric"
version of the bits that (theoretically - according to the Standard) being
used.

<some snippage>

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>


If you have no DECLARATIVES but do define a file-status code (which you
check in the Procedure Division), then any STANDARD conforming compiler
*must* continue execution.  If the Fujitsu compiler does not provide (at
least) an option to do so - then it is NOT conforming.
```

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> If you have no DECLARATIVES but do define a file-status code (which you
> check in the Procedure Division), then any STANDARD conforming compiler
> *must* continue execution.  If the Fujitsu compiler does not provide (at
> least) an option to do so - then it is NOT conforming.

that can be disabled.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Declaratives, Compiling, Error Reporting and Fujitsu COBOL - was something unrelated.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be07a1$885a4920$dfbc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz> <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net> <71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-yxxhFtDiCuhm@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-yxxhFtDiCuhm@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>...
> On Mon, 2 Nov 1998 20:37:11, "William M. Klein" 
> <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > *must* continue execution.  If the Fujitsu compiler does not provide
(at
> > least) an option to do so - then it is NOT conforming.
> > 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There I was spouting my mouth off at work - where I have no documentation
to back myself up.

However, here at home - I do.

The 74 and 85 standards state that INVALID KEY is REQUIRED if there is no
Appropriate USE statement (Declarative).

The same is true of AT END.

Most compilers ignore this restriction.  Some don't but report errors - ala
Fujitsu.

Others (VMS COBOL) won't even compile if you follow the rules.  

A notation in the draft standard makes it clear that this restriction is
going to be removed in the next standard.
------
73) INVALID KEY phrase. The INVALID KEY phrase does not have to be
specified if there is no applicable USE
statement.
------

At any rate, a compliant compiler WILL require the declarative if no
invalid key is coded, and that is the root of the question.

BTW - to remove the annoying messages with Fujitsu COBOL - do this:

When you run the program and the runtime options window appears, in the
Environment Variables Information field type in @NoMessage=YES. Click Set
and then click Save. Follow the prompts. Then click OK to run the program.
The error message window is now disabled.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declaratives, Compiling, Error Reporting and Fujitsu COBOL - was something unrelated.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be07ae$bc776dc0$f7bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz> <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net> <71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-yxxhFtDiCuhm@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <01be07a1$885a4920$dfbc48a6@default>`

```


Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article 
> Others (VMS COBOL) won't even compile if you follow the rules.  
> 

Read - "if you DON'T follow the rules.".

The existence or non existence of a file status value for a file has no
impact on the necessity of declaratives or invalid key/at end clauses ----
IN Ansi 85 COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71n9sf$qck$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz> <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net> <71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>...
>  WRONG: for file status codes OTHER THAN 9x, the first and second byte
MUST
>be "display".   It is true that some compilers store the 2nd byte (of 9x -
>ONLY file status codes) as binary - but even then it is the "alphanumeric"
>version of the bits that (theoretically - according to the Standard) being
>used.

The key word is "theoretically".  "Actually" is another matter. (BTW, I
wish I could tell my compiler that).

>>>Declaratives ?  You don't _need_ declaratives, I never use
>>>them.  All file statements give a File status that can be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>equivalent of an ABEND.


>If you have no DECLARATIVES but do define a file-status code (which you
>check in the Procedure Division), then any STANDARD conforming compiler
>*must* continue execution.  If the Fujitsu compiler does not provide (at
>least) an option to do so - then it is NOT conforming.


Again, there is a major difference between what is supposed to happen
and what does.  There may even be a compiler directive to make it happen,
but after searching the docs for two or three hours, then asking for two or
three days, you reach a point where you have to bow to the inevitable
and just make the code work.

If it takes me two weeks to find out how to make a compiler work in
a "standard" fashion, and two days to make the code work by adding
extra code, then the trade-off is obvious, at least to me.  I pay my own
salary, and two weeks boils down to about four grand net.

But that brings me back to my original point.  There is no "standard".
There are a whole bunch of compilers that can be made to mimic
each other, sort of, if you are intimately acquainted with the 750
options of each, and are willing to maintain completely separate
I/O for each of them.  Here is as simple an example as you can
get: It will work on about half the Cobol's on the market, and fail
on the other half.

Identification Division.
working-storage section.
01 literal-to-display.
    02 filler            picture x(15) value "Hello World".
procedure division.
    display literal-to-display.
    stop run.

How can we claim to have a standard, when there is absolutely
*NO* standard test?  Or when the standard simply defines
everything that varies from computer to computer as
"user implemented" so that you have the choice of "standard"
code that does not work on any compiler, or "non standard"
that works on with your particular compiler/OS but no others?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

- **Message-ID:** `<3298307.66319.14064@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
In message <<71l6c0$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
> Donald Tees wrote in message <71irgj$aq9$1@news.igs.net>...
> >>Declaratives ?  You don't _need_ declaratives, I never use
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> least) an option to do so - then it is NOT conforming.

Exactly, Fujitsu is conforming.  Checking the file status
can be done just as in every other compiler without any
need for declaratives.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-hIb7HW24iDgh@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.66995.17164@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On Sun, 1 Nov 1998 18:36:35, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) 
wrote:

> Declaratives ?  You don't _need_ declaratives, I never use 
> them.  All file statements give a File status that can be
> checked.
> 

ANSI conforming compilers require you to either use "Invalid Key" or 
"Declaratives" with indexed files.  

MOST compilers ignore this requirement.  The VMS Alpha compiler does 
NOT and won't even COMPILE if you do not do one of the two.

The Fujitsu compiler WILL compile, but displays annoying file status 
errors when an error occurs for which there is no Invalid Key/At End 
check or Declarative coded.  This message can be disabled with a 
runtime option setting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36449998.73537297@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <19981030230503.07522.00001708@ng120.aol.com> <71e4rl$3ss$1@news.igs.net> <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net> <71func$d0t$1@news.igs.net> <363e1a2e.40924545@news.netvision.net.il> <71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sun, 1 Nov 1998 09:01:45 -0500 "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote in message
:><363e1a2e.40924545@news.netvision.net.il>...
:>>On Sat, 31 Oct 1998 16:24:08 -0500 "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
:>wrote:

:>>:>I am starting to feel, with all the variations that I see here,
:>>:>that there *is* no standard Cobol.  Is there such a thing
:>>:>as a large piece of functional code that will actually run
:>>:>on every Cobol compiler on the market without changing
:>>:>anything?  Not even a copy library?

:>>How about old fashioned master file update?

:>>Other than the
:>>               ENVIRONMENT DIVISION
:>>               FILE-CONTROL
:>>                   SELECT statements


:>Plus all the screen I/O.  Plus any file sharing, though the
:>*first" digit of the file-status is probably fairly consistent,
:>so maybe there we are ok.  The initialization differences
:>can be handled by coding for the lowest common
:>denominator, but do we need declaratives for error
:>handling or not?

Grasshopper,

I call you that because you associate the concept of an old fashioned master
file update with concepts such as screen I/O and file status.

The old fashioned master file update consisted off:

      Old-Master  (Tape)
      Transactions  (Card, though may be tape)

and produced:

      New-Master  (Tape)
      Edit-Errors (Print + optionally cards) 
      Various-Other-Reports (Print)

It did not do an update in place, as tape drives do not support inserting
records.

Such a program, other than SELECT statements, should compile/run identically
on all platforms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71ivcj$d3j$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <19981030230503.07522.00001708@ng120.aol.com> <71e4rl$3ss$1@news.igs.net> <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net> <71func$d0t$1@news.igs.net> <363e1a2e.40924545@news.netvision.net.il> <71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <36449998.73537297@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote in message
<36449998.73537297@news.netvision.net.il>...

>Grasshopper,


>I call you that because you associate the concept of an old fashioned
master
>file update with concepts such as screen I/O and file status.


born /47, coded Cobol first in 1967.

>The old fashioned master file update consisted off:
>      Old-Master  (Tape)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>      Various-Other-Reports (Print)


You mean, of course, the "IBM mainframe for my entire career" master
file update.  I have never used a tape drive for anything but backup.  Such
things will *not* work on all platforms.  They will work on a single
platform;
an IBM mainframe.  You have to *have* a tape drive, for example.  I was
using screens in Cobol in the early seventies.  I will grant you though,
maybe there is a standard that will run on most platforms that existed
in 1970.

>It did not do an update in place, as tape drives do not support inserting
>records.
>Such a program, other than SELECT statements, should compile/run
identically
>on all platforms.


As long as it is an IBM mainframe, that is. Or a PC with a nine track
tape drive attached.  Bet there are lots of those around.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363d0950.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <19981030230503.07522.00001708@ng120.aol.com> <71e4rl$3ss$1@news.igs.net> <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net> <71func$d0t$1@news.igs.net> <363e1a2e.40924545@news.netvision.net.il> <71hp6j$kfu$1@news.igs.net> <36449998.73537297@news.netvision.net.il> <71ivcj$d3j$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,

ETK (e.g http://www.etk.com and ETKPAK at same) is about
a million lines of code and runs completely unchanged on all
platforms with a Cobol compiler. I grant you that if ETK needs
I/O that is done by a generated  (non-portable) Cobol program,
but there are lots on programs (e.g. all of ETKPAK) that are
completely portable.

What we have done with ETK is to externalize all I/O to called
modules which are generated from platform dependent templates,
but ALL the other programs are indeed completely portable
and non-trivial.  We have tools in ETK to check that the code
is portable to prevent non-portable code to "sneak" in.


Donald Tees wrote in message <71ivcj$d3j$1@news.igs.net>...
>Binyamin Dissen wrote in message
><36449998.73537297@news.netvision.net.il>...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363eb4d7.33298306@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <19981030230503.07522.00001708@ng120.aol.com> <71e4rl$3ss$1@news.igs.net> <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net> <71func$d0t$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sat, 31 Oct 1998 16:24:08 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363b0811.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
hmmm.... as 'the old cobol man of the mountain', may I come down to
talk a bit?   <g>  

for one, the use of the term 'byte' immediately corrupts the question.
'byte' is not a universal term and, as such, prevents a proper answer.
We're not addressing PIC clause here, but USAGE clause. In USAGE, the
default is 'character display' for the designated computer. The
implementor of the compiler defines what format is display, comp, and
binary -- and the positioning of sign indicators... that's right in
the ansi documentation.  so... there *is* a standard -- but it allows
the implementor for each platform to designate the bit structure.
Actually, that makes sense, since different computers have different
structures.  some computers are not character-oriented, and the term
'byte' doesn't exist universally.   
david
p.s. in many computers, the term 'byte' refers to an 8-bit addressable
memory space. this isn't universal. 

so... if the question is "how many character positions are represented
by PIC S9(03) USAGE DISPLAY?   then the answer is 3 -- but that does
NOT answer how many bits are involved.   the question then, is
incomplete.
```

#### ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZUq%1.353$FY5.2014184@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com>`

```
>I am having trouble in figuring out how many bytes does the following have:
>            01  W001-CNT-LINE-ITEM       Pic  S9(03)    Value +0.


Amazing how many different answers you can get from such a simple question.

There are 2 ways I can think of to find the exact size:
1) Compile the program and look at the listing. Given the proper compiler
options the listing should show you the size of each field in working
storage off to the right of the definition in the listing.

2) Compile the program with the variable defined. Note size of load module.
Comment out the variable definition and recompile. Take difference in load
module sizes and you have your field size.

Neither are for sure answers, but in most cases s9(03) usage display will be
3 bytes, but in the absence of a compiler type you cannot be sure.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Eur%1.143$yi1.337908@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <ZUq%1.353$FY5.2014184@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:
>>I am having trouble in figuring out how many bytes does the following have:
>>            01  W001-CNT-LINE-ITEM       Pic  S9(03)    Value +0.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>module sizes and you have your field size.

FUNCTION LENGTH(W001-CNT-LINE-ITEM)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363f28f9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <ZUq%1.353$FY5.2014184@storm.twcol.com> <363e63e3.0@news1.ibm.net> <363F168F.9D6486D3@home.com>`

```
I meant that no matter what HE does, Cobol's answer is always three.
On all platforms with all versions of all compilers.
Now, the original question has a slight subtlety (probably lost
on the original poster). Sizes of Cobol items are measured in
characters, not in bytes. You *could* construe the question
to be: how much will the size of my program increase if I
add a pic s9(03) item? This is of course not the same as
"what is the size of s9(03) in characters?"
Many posters to this thread seem to think the two questions
are the same.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <363F168F.9D6486D3@home.com>...
>> >There are 2 ways I can think of to find the exact size:
>> ........snippage
>> >Neither are for sure answers, but in most cases s9(03) usage display
will
>> be
>> >3 bytes, but in the absence of a compiler type you cannot be sure.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the data to a full word boundry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Bytes vs Characters (was How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71nis2$dlh@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F%s_1.13246$_e3.1799382@news2.randori.com> <ZUq%1.353$FY5.2014184@storm.twcol.com> <363e63e3.0@news1.ibm.net> <363F168F.9D6486D3@home.com> <363f28f9.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif,
  You are absolutely correct in what you say for the CURRENT COBOL Standard.
However, if you haven't seen it, you might want to look at the draft of the
next Standard - where "bytes" are finally mentioned.

Especially, because of "multi-byte character sets,"  it has been interesting
to see where the Standard has and has not been changed to distinguish
between size in bytes versus size in characters.

This is not a trivial change and I don't think that the committee thinks
that they have got it completely correct yet.


Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363f28f9.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>I meant that no matter what HE does, Cobol's answer is always three.
>On all platforms with all versions of all compilers.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

on some platforms a byte is not 8 bits.
On Bull-GCOS8 it is 9.
I've heard a rumor that on IBM's mainframes they
are going to 9 bits too.

so using "bytes" in a standards document is a dangerous thing.
In many languages other that English, people say "octet" for byte
(again having trouble with 9-bit bytes).

Now with unicode and Java "character" becomes 16 bits.
What a mess...


>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363f28f9.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> >
> >A more interesting question would be: 'how much would my
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just keeping it stirred  ;-}

Not in a record layout one hopes.  Slack bytes certainly apply
in many cases between 01 and 77 levels but should not apply
to elementary items unless the the SYNCRONISED clause does
anything useful. 

Of course, there may be slack bytes at the end to round the
size up to some boundry, but would not 4 be the next step ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz...
>In message <<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
writes:
>> >
>> >A more interesting question would be: 'how much would my
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

On systems (such as IBM/390) where binary items have to be aligned
to the halfword, word, or doubleword (depending on their size) the following
record layout:

01  MY-RECORD.
      02  MY-ALPHA-NUM    PIC X.
      02  MY-BINARY1           PIC S9(4)  COMP.
      02  MY-DISPLAY           PIC S9(03).
      02  MY-BINARY2           PIC S9(4)  COMP.

would be 10 bytes long (one slack byte before MY-BAINRY1 and
another one before MY-BINARY2, forced upon us by MY-DISPLAY
not ending on a halfword boundary).

I don't think the SYNC clause does anything here. The alignment
is necessary and automatic, although it didn't HAVE to be
(all references to e.g. MY-BINARY1 *could* go thru a hidden, aligned
working variable, but for efficiency they don't (there is a *slight*
chance of my being wrong - it has been a while since I worked
on the 390 (actually it was the 360!) architecture).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
>news:3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz...
>>In message <<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
>writes:
>>> >

  <much snippage>
>>
>
>On systems (such as IBM/390) where binary items have to be aligned
>to the halfword, word, or doubleword (depending on their size) the
following
>record layout:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Leif,
  I think you are wrong here.  I think that IBM (MVS or OS/390) at least
will NOT insert slack bytes UNLESS the SYNC clause is added (at the 01 or
elementary levels).  This would lead to a pretty inefficient application,
but I don't think the slack bytes would be inserted.  On the other hand, I
think they might be for the following definition:


 01  MY-RECORD.
       02  Get-off-word-boundary   Pic X.
       02 Table-of-mixed-items occurs 10 times.
          05  MY-BINARY1          PIC S9(4)  COMP.
         05  MY-DISPLAY           PIC S9(03).
         05  MY-BINARY2           PIC S9(4)  COMP.

NOTE: I have not compiled and looked at a DMAP or XREF of either, so I could
be mistaken - but this does reflect my understanding of the MVS compilers
(old and new).

Note2:  It does not matter (as I understand it) whether this 01 were in the
file section or working-storage (or linkage).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com>`

more recent IBM compilers, but I haven't heard of such a change.

to the appropriate boundary only  they are declared with SYNC.

that were NOT on a halfword boundary. The compilers then always
inserted slack bytes.  Out of curosity, would someone please check
(not go by what you think it should be) what the situation is now.
Thanks.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<725nl5$gjs@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net> <71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com> <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com> <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>John Trifon wrote in message <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com>...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <snip>

Your (logic) problem is that you are thinking that the OS will execute COBOL
rather than the compiler generated code.  It is true that there are some
OS/360-OS/370-OS/390 instructions that only work on properly aligned binary
items.  HOWEVER, this does not mean that the code won't work if the COBOL
items are not aligned correctly.  What does happen is that the compiler (in
such cases) has to move the binary items to temporary (invisible to the
programmer) fields where the binary instructions are executed (and then the
results are returned to the original field).  Actually, as pointed out in an
older thread, in many cases, the binary items are converted to
packed-decimal before operations so their alignment isn't even a factor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36479C84.A8C2D1C4@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net> <71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com> <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com> <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net> <725nl5$gjs@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Good guess but wrong. I have appended  the generated code from  the COBOL for
MVS compiler.  It is quite evident that binary items stored on non boundary
addresses can be loaded into registers without moving it to a temporary work
area.  I hope don't think IBM  has a  (logic) problem with binary fields.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01.
           02 a PIC S9(4) BINARY SYNC.
           02   PIC X.
           02 b PIC S9(4) BINARY.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            MOVE b TO a
      *>>>>>>>>>         LH   2,3(0,8)
      *                            CVD  2,152(0,13)
      *                             CVB  2,152(0,13)
      *>>>>>>>>>         STH  2,0(0,8)
            ADD  1 TO b
      *>>>>>>>>>        LH   2,3(0,8)
      *                           CVD  2,152(0,13)
      *                          AP   156(4,13),21(1,10)
      *                          CVB  2,152(0,13)
      *>>>>>>>>>        STH  2,3(0,8)
            ADD  1 TO a
      *>>>>>>>>>        LH   2,0(0,8)
      *                          CVD  2,152(0,13)
      *                          AP   156(4,13),21(1,10)
      *                         CVB  2,152(0,13)
      *>>>>>>>>>     STH  2,0(0,8)

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net>...
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> packed-decimal before operations so their alignment isn't even a factor.
```

You say you don't think IBM has a logic problem with binary fields yet
I'm wondering why the generated code converts the binary value in b to
decimal, then coverts it back to binary, then stores it in a.  Seems
like a waste of time when an MVC instruction would have accomplished
the same thing, i.e.

MVC            0(2,8),3(8)


>            ADD  1 TO b
>      *>>>>>>>>>        LH   2,3(0,8)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>      *>>>>>>>>>        STH  2,3(0,8)

Again the generated code is converting the values to decimal to do the
addition insteading of performing binary arithmetic.  Why not:

               LH            2,3(0,8)
               A              2,21(4,10)  <  assumes Cobol setup a
                                                      constant binary 
                                                      full word = 1

              STH          2,3(0,8)

>            ADD  1 TO a
>      *>>>>>>>>>        LH   2,0(0,8)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>      *>>>>>>>>>     STH  2,0(0,8)

See previous wonderment.

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>> packed-decimal before operations so their alignment isn't even a factor.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

>>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>>> Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>>> >John Trifon wrote in message <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com>...
>>>
>>> Your (logic) problem is that you are thinking that the OS will execute
COBOL
>>> rather than the compiler generated code.  It is true that there are some
>>> OS/360-OS/370-OS/390 instructions that only work on properly aligned
binary
>>> items.  HOWEVER, this does not mean that the code won't work if the
COBOL
>>> items are not aligned correctly.  What does happen is that the compiler
(in
>>> such cases) has to move the binary items to temporary (invisible to the
>>> programmer) fields where the binary instructions are executed (and then
the
>>> results are returned to the original field).  Actually, as pointed out
in an
>>> older thread, in many cases, the binary items are converted to
>>> packed-decimal before operations so their alignment isn't even a factor.


It is clear from the assembler code that no temporary variable is used.
It is also clear that the LH load halfword instruction works even if the
halfword is not halfword aligned LH 2,3(0,8) - the displacement is 3 -
so no slack byte is needed. What must have happened is that the
restriction that halfword operands must be halfword aligned has been
dropped somewhere along the way. As I said, it has been a while
(I worked on a 360 model 50 way back).

It is also clear that there seems to be a lot of misinformation running
around. Even from (otherwise reliable) Bill Klein, viz.. the above
statement. Maybe he meant the HARDWARE moves the binary
items to temporary fields, rather than the COMPILER doing it.
If you say the COMPILER does it, I read that to mean that it issues
assembler instructions to that effect, which clearly is not the case.

The atrocious conversions to packed is something completely different
(which may or may not be controlled by a compiler switch - dunno).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981111175024.14992.00000097@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36487d1b.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

In article <36487d1b.0@news1.ibm.net>, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:

>It is clear from the assembler code that no temporary variable is used.
>It is also clear that the LH load halfword instruction works even if the
>halfword is not halfword aligned LH 2,3(0,8) - the displacement is 3 -
>so no slack byte is needed.

I am pretty sure the old DOS/VS COBOL compilers would do MVCs or equivalent of
unaligned binary fields to temporary fields that were aligned because the old
S360 binary instructions would abend if storage locations were unaligned. (One
would get specification exceptions.) Less ancient processors do not require
alignment except for potential performance benefits.

The only time I am aware of that even that compiler generated slack bytes were
when SYNC were specified.

However, working storage 01's were always double-word aligned.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM S390 BINARY was Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ed1p$42$1@garnet.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<36479C84.A8C2D1C4@ix.netcom.com> <3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net> <71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com> <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com> <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net> <725nl5$gjs@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
It is true that the S370-390 series do not require binary data to be aligned      
but many of the models suffer performance degradation if the S9(4) BINARY      
data is not on a half word boundary and the S9(9) BINARY data is not on a      
full word boundary.     
    
Incidentally, you may get more efficient code when using the TRUNC(OPT)      
compile option if you use S9(8) BINARY instead of S9(9) BINARY.  BINARY to      
BINARY and literal to BINARY operations don't truncate according to picture      
but rather according to word boundary.  BINARY to DECIMAL and DECIMAL to      
BINARY truncate according to picture.  Confusing, ain't it?     
>     
>     
> Good guess but wrong. I have appended  the generated code from  the COBOL      
for    
> MVS compiler.  It is quite evident that binary items stored on non boundary    
> addresses can be loaded into registers without moving it to a temporary      
work    
> area.  I hope don't think IBM  has a  (logic) problem with binary fields.    
>     
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >>I agree. 01 & 77 levels are sync'd on double word boundaries. Others      
are    
> > >sync'd    
> > >>to the appropriate boundary only  they are declared with SYNC.    
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Your (logic) problem is that you are thinking that the OS will execute      
COBOL    
> > rather than the compiler generated code.  It is true that there are some    
> > OS/360-OS/370-OS/390 instructions that only work on properly aligned      
binary    
> > items.  HOWEVER, this does not mean that the code won't work if the COBOL    
> > items are not aligned correctly.  What does happen is that the compiler      
(in    
> > such cases) has to move the binary items to temporary (invisible to the    
> > programmer) fields where the binary instructions are executed (and then      
the    
> > results are returned to the original field).  Actually, as pointed out in      
an    
> > older thread, in many cases, the binary items are converted to    
> > packed-decimal before operations so their alignment isn't even a factor.    
>     
> 

Clark F. Morris, Jr.    
CFM Technical Programming Services    
Bridgetown, Nova Scotia, Canada    
cmorris@fox.nstn.ca    
on assignment at morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM S390 BINARY was Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3658ef63.42494664@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36479C84.A8C2D1C4@ix.netcom.com> <3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net> <71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com> <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com> <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net> <725nl5$gjs@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <72ed1p$42$1@garnet.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Clark Morris    <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote:

>It is true that the S370-390 series do not require binary data to be aligned      
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  
It should be noted that all 360/370 and up systems do all  arithmetic
on COMP data as register operations, not as storage to storage
operations like packed decimal. This was shown in the example that
accompanied this originally. Performance degradation often occurs
because of problems moving data to non-aligned fields. setting slack
bytes, etc. rather than as the result of the arithmetic operation. 
 
>
>Incidentally, you may get more efficient code when using the TRUNC(OPT)      
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not if you understand the underlying concepts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** as999@torfree.net (Adrian Boldan)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F29K5K.2rC.0.bloor@torfree.net>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net> <71vmq5$t40@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <36436D9B.44202AE6@home.com> <3643A855.7D8C0014@ix.netcom.com> <3643abab.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
com.com> <3646ff3b.0@news1.ibm.net>
Organization: Toronto Free-Net
Distribution: 

Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:

: Yes, I can see this, but as I said when I was working on the MVS or VSE
: I AM SURE that there were slack bytes. I have worked with dumps and
: compiler listings and they all showed slack bytes. The manual even had
: a section on how to compute the number of slack bytes.

: So, I reiterate, what *is* it? Not, what could it be.

The 01 level starts always on a full word boundary. So, if the previous 
fields didn't end on end of full word, slack bytes are inserted before 
the new level 01 starts.

In my changes for Y2K, I had to extend a year field, that was defined on 
level 10. It happened that, by doing this, its "mother" 01 level had to 
enter where the next 01 has been. The compiler allocated that next 01 
level 8 bytes further. This created a nice abend. It took me some time to 
realize what had happened.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>

Just a minor clarification:  01 lvls start on double word boundaries. You did
note it when you found the next 01 started 8 bytes further down.  But how did
the field expansion and the subsequent relocation of the next 01 cause an
abend?
