###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298310.69124.23888@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<36433cb2.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> news:3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz...
> >In message <<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the 390 (actually it was the 360!) architecture).

Looking at the standard (actully the MF language manual which
is annotated where it varies from the standard) it tends to
imply that synchronization is done using the SYNC clause or
manually by adding FILLERs.  It is possible that some
compiler option implicitly sets SYNC for COMP items.

Certainly from my mainframe past I recall having to use SYNC
where I wanted to force alignment, and on the M680x0 machines
that I worked on briefly there was no automatic SYNC for
records *in spite of the M680x0 going belly up if it tried
to use non-aligned COMP, so there was an overhead of moving
the bytes to an area where it could be used.

But, regardless, in these cases, adding a 4 byte field cannot
cause anything but an extra 4 bytes to be added to the
record, though a system where 8 byte alignment is required
may.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re:Slack Bytes - Was How many bytes is S9(03)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-M0UpksyNIfxB@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3640b72a.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298309.62942.18255@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On Fri, 6 Nov 1998 17:29:02, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) 
wrote:

>> > The answer could be any number larger than 3 because
> > of 'slack bytes' that some machines need if there are
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Ever seen slack bytes in a record?  I have.  A system I used to work 
on use 2 CICS screens for user input of pending maintenance.  To save 
the records, they concatinated the two symbolic maps  - and wrote them
to a KSDS file.

Here's how they did it.

01  Data-Area.
      03  Key-Info.
            05  Account pic 9(9).
Copy Symbolic1.
Copy Symbolic2.


Then they issue a EXEC CICS WRITE Data-File, From Data-Area, Lenght 
(full lenght of data area  + symbolic 1 + symbolic 2).

Even though Symbolic1 and 2 bot start with 01 levels!!!!

CICS doesn't care - it just starts at that location and goes for that 
many bytes.

Now, the data record was defined to be a specific size - I think 2000 
bytes.

So, imagine the WORK involved for these two processes.

1.  Modify the map for new fields or expanded fields.
2.  Now CONVERT the file that exists so the fields are in the new 
positions.

Sound easy?  Nope.  The number of "slack bytes" betteen the first map 
and the second does not change by the number of bytes inserted when 
field sizes change.  Thankfully most maint was to the second map, 
which made things eaiser.

Guess what happened when the record was too small to hold the data 
when a new field was added to the first map?  

(I've told that nightmare store here before)

All this cause someone was "Cute" .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re:Slack Bytes - Was How many bytes is S

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298310.70193.24493@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-M0UpksyNIfxB@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-M0UpksyNIfxB@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> redsky@ibm.net  writes:
> 
> Ever seen slack bytes in a record?  I have.  A system I used to work 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> bytes.

Apart from not knowing the implications of CICS, the first time
reading this I thought 'FILE SECTION', then I realised it
was writing from WORKING-STORAGE so it made more sense (though
less in the way of programming).

I have worked on machines that had a 4k word 'immediate access'
capability, beyond this the instruction set need to use a 
register to address memory.  The Cobol compiler would put
W-S into this (reserving some for run time libraries) until
4K words was reached and then would put the remainder at the 
end of program.  It was important to put frequently used
data at the start of W-S and arrays at the end (because
they needed register access anyway).  But at some point,
at an 01 or 77 level, there would be a serveree discontinuity
in memory.  No one who used that system ever did these 
sort of stupid tricks with 01 levels.
```

```
Jeff wrote:
> 
> >I am having trouble in figuring out how many bytes does the following have:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3 bytes, but in the absence of a compiler type you cannot be sure.

I disagree with your second method.  Synchronization can make the load
modules vary by more than the size of the field.
```

```
the usage clause will also affect the number of bytes used
```

## Thread: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL — 15 this month / 16 total

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-975d003790)._

#### ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71i55c$cn1$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<71g88p$b8r$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Christopher,

    While C++ is the language that is being emphasized nowadays in schools,
combining both COBOL and C++ build a strong foundation for both sides of the
development issue. I also see JAVA as a formidable presence in the
development arena and I don't mean for Web-Page design either.

    As far as C++ being less forgiving, I believe that to be true. The COBOL
compiler will flag potential error situations (not all) whereas I don't
believe C++ will flag as many. If C++ is anything like ASSEMBLER (which most
of the time doesn't care what you do) you can really dig a deep hole without
even knowing it.

    Having the knowledge and experience using C++ and COBOL will definitely
put food on the table in the years to come. COBOL is NOT going away as many
Pollyanna's would predict. IMHO, get some ASSEMBLER knowledge as well and
you can round out the categories.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

Christopher Lewis wrote in message <71g88p$b8r$1@juliana.sprynet.com>...
>I am taking a intro course to C++ this semester in college.  Because I am
>switching from a technical emphasis to information systems, next semester I
>will have to take intro to COBOL.  I seem to be doing well with C++ and
have
>some concerns if COBOL is less or more forgiving than C++
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364380bd.2259339@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71g88p$b8r$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <71i55c$cn1$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
On Sun, 1 Nov 1998 12:18:49 -0500, "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
wrote:

>    Having the knowledge and experience using C++ and COBOL will definitely
>put food on the table in the years to come. COBOL is NOT going away as many
>Pollyanna's would predict. IMHO, get some ASSEMBLER knowledge as well and
>you can round out the categories.

Here's my opinion:

I would not, under any circumstances, learn assembler, unless you have
a specific company who puts up the money beforehand.

My crystal ball says code will be ported from asm to compiler specific
C, and asm will die. Compiler specific C has alot of company loyalty,
but it has the advantage of porting the code at a later point.

non-standard C does the same as assembler, has a bigger market, and is
even the direction most embedded chips are moving in.

C++ on the other hand, I have never used. It's basically a C patch
job, and having seen some older COBOL code that is still around, I'd
rather spend time maintaining COBOL, then write new applications in
C++.

Java, nope, not gonna even go there. I'd rather invest my time in
salesmanship of middleware and software libraries. VB, nope. sorry. I
did basic for 15 years, and what I have learned is that they have no
intention of standardizing basic. Ever.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71nshj$j1h$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71g88p$b8r$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <71i55c$cn1$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <364380bd.2259339@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote in message <364380bd.2259339@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...

>I would not, under any circumstances, learn assembler, unless you have
>a specific company who puts up the money beforehand.
>
>My crystal ball says code will be ported from asm to compiler specific
>C, and asm will die. Compiler specific C has alot of company loyalty,


I would agree with a caveat.  I think anybody that wants to understand
computers should learn an assembler or two, just to understand the
basic hardware.  In terms of use, though, I cannot see writing much
more assembler.  I think that the real low level stuff will be written in
very basic C.  The language was designed as an assembler
replacement, and that is what it does well.  C++ as a business language
is about as useful as assembler as a business language.  One
could build up huge subroutine libraries and macros there, too, but
it was still never a high level language.  The only real advantage it
has over assembler is portability.(somewhat).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<721uov$eae$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<71g88p$b8r$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <71i55c$cn1$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <364380bd.2259339@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <364380bd.2259339@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
  gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore) wrote:

>
> Here's my opinion:
>
> I would not, under any circumstances, learn assembler, unless you have
> a specific company who puts up the money beforehand.

<Rest Snipped>

I believe that all programmers should know the ASSEMBLER basics. It would
give them a greater appreciation for high-level languages as well as the
people who designed them (Grace Hopper to name one). Afterall, high-level
language design requires a very thorough knowledge of ASSEMBLER. Without
PMAP's (Mainframe speak), some pesky problems would not be as easy to find in
a dump without some ASSEMBLER knowledge. I'm sure you've probably seen a
single COMPUTE statement that generates dozens of PACK's, UNPKS's, ZAP's,
AP's, SRP's, etc. If you bomb on the third ZAP, it all that easier to find
the offending data-area.

Many programmers rely on third-party debugger's to find their abend. I can
tell you (from personal experience) that these debuggers are NOT found in
shops located in developing countries or 3rd-world nations. If you have a
problem, you must dig through the dump until you find the bad boy.

I'm not here advocating the resurrection of ASSEMBLER. I would like to see
more emphasis on COBOL in colleges and universities. I believe a little dose
of ASSEMBLER would serve students well.

Cheers,

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<726o5e$4i4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<721uov$eae$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981107135139.15206.00000594@ng-fa2.aol.com> <e1g12.692$vt.1116451@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <364bfaef.6466759@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message <364bfaef.6466759@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>Judson McClendon wrote:


>Unless the company *pays* for assembly, it is an advantage not to
>learn it.


Now *that* is a silly statement.  There is no advantage to
ignorance, ever.  I would grant that one will seldom use
assembler any more, but I cannot see how anybody can
claim to understand a computer without understanding
assembler ... even if only a pseudo assembler designed
for learning purposes.

As to "the company" paying for it ... I am flabbergasted. Do
you still expect your mommy to wash your socks?

Company paid for courses are a nice perq, but nobody
gets far in this business (or any other for that matter) until
they realize that they are responsible for their career, and
that ultimately everybody works for themselves.  A company
might very well assist you in learning how to work for them
better, but you are crazy if you think they have any interest
whatsoever in helping you work for yourself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switching programming courses C++ to COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364a6d4c.3536401@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<721uov$eae$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981107135139.15206.00000594@ng-fa2.aol.com> <e1g12.692$vt.1116451@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <364bfaef.6466759@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <726o5e$4i4$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sun, "Donald Tees" wrote:

>As to "the company" paying for it ... I am flabbergasted. Do
>you still expect your mommy to wash your socks?

If the company made the sock, and they pay me to wash it, I will.
If they made the sock, and I happen to work for them, and they teach
me to wash it, *their* way, and company B down the street will pay
more, I'd make a judgement call.

The judgement call has a few factors:
a> if company b is more productive in material goods
b> how much effort it will take to establish new business ties

>Company paid for courses are a nice perq, but nobody
>gets far in this business (or any other for that matter) until
>they realize that they are responsible for their career, and

Yes, and if I don't get paid for learning, that is the first
responsibility I recognize.

>that ultimately everybody works for themselves.  A company
>might very well assist you in learning how to work for them

A company who *assists* me to work for them isn't really doing me a
favor. A company who pays more is. A company who offers me money for a
skill which will work only on their machine is not competitive.

I learned that, when I learn a new skill, they get money from all the
new maintenance nightmares they can charge for that new *technology*.

If they get money, and I am doing the work, then I want a piece of the
action.

I will even go so far as learning, on condition that they are legally
responsible for paying the course costs, plus 1 extra days salary to
find the location, all parking fees, and produce the same amount of
code from the course, all while matching my current salary, and
possible bonuses. I'll might even cover some of the course, if I find
that it is currently in the newspaper as a high paying job.

How much would I cover? Well, $8 an hour for a single persons time,
and $1.50/hour in groups of 10 or more.

I figure if a peer tutor costs $8/hour, that they can't teach me more
than a professor can, to get through the course and the examples. And
the professor only basically hands over graded papers to the peer
tutor anyways. Ok, maybe I would pay an extra $3/day for the
hardware/software.
leasing computers is about $100/month or so...

so your basic company course (8 hours), I would not pay more than $70,
out of my own pocket.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

you can quite easily get in a mess. COBOL on the other hand has a solid base to
work from, you can't really go wrong with it! COBOL is an older language but it is
still a very high level one to work with, I think you'll do fine making the change
from C++ to COBOL. Have a good time with it.  :o)
```

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 29 Oct 1998 15:53:52, swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB) wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

What about question 2?  I thought it was 1, but I found this in my very
first COBOL textbook:
"Indexes contain displacement values, whereas subscripts are used for
the storage of occurrence numbers.  Displacement refers to the number of
positions away from the starting position of the table."

So, if you set an index to 1, is it zero?  And if the picture clause is
PIC X(5), if you set index up by 1, will the index contain 5?  

If you put 'THIS-INDEX     USAGE IS INDEX' in Working-Storage instead of
using 'OCCURS 25 TIMES INDEXED BY THIS-INDEX' and you say 'SET
THIS-INDEX TO 1' (in working-storage), can you display the contents of
THIS-INDEX?  (I was gonna try this today but our system was down.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71pl8u$f13$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>`

```

Kitty Carr wrote in message <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>...

>So, if you set an index to 1, is it zero?  And if the picture clause is
>PIC X(5), if you set index up by 1, will the index contain 5?


A data name, usage index, is just a data representation that is best
for subscripting for a specific machine implementation. Probably
16 or 32 bit binary.

A table index could be anything that the compiler designer decides,
from an offset like you mention, to an actual memory address, to
a fairly standard subscript type of value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be07ae$66f3a4e0$f7bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>`

```


Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote in article
<363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> > 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > >              1.  If you move a I to a subscript, what will it
contain?
> > >                                        1
> > > >              2.  If you set an index to 1, what will it contain?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A usage index item set to 1 will have a value of 1.  However, an INDEX to a
table, which is a related but different animal will have a value of ???? -
who cares - the vendor/platform determine the contents, and it is one
(usually) that is most efficient for access.

You can USE a Usage Index item to determine WHAT value is in the index
however.

IE:

01  The-Table.
      03  Table-Element Pic X(5) Occurs 5 times Indexed by Table-Index.
01  Index-Value Usage Index.  (No picture clause).

You can then MOVE this value to a field - ala:

Set Index-Value to Table-Index
Move Index-Value to Pic-9-item
Display Pic-9-item.

some compilers I played with had 'strange' values in there, but most had an
absolute character offset in the table.  IE when the Table-Index was set to
3 and Index-Value was set to Table-Index, the value of Index-Value was 11.

Go figure.

The bottom like is that if you set an index to a value, it may not ACTUALLY
contain that value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MgW%1.12$Tk.29140@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net> <01be07ae$66f3a4e0$f7bc48a6@default>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>01  The-Table.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Display Pic-9-item.

You should not be able to move a USAGE IS INDEX field to anywhere, or
to SET it to anything but to an INDEX NAME or another USAGE IS INDEX
field.  To check it out, I ran the above syntax with Micro Focus and
it flagged this:

               Move Index-Value to Pic-9-item
    *  49-S********************
    **    Illegal use of Index-name or Index Data-item

You get a similar error if you try this:

               Set Pic-9-item to Index-Value
    * 307-S*********************************
    **    Wrong combination of data-types

This makes sense, because the value of an index data item is only
meaningful when associated with a specific table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-lhV4e11kqgDG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net> <01be07ae$66f3a4e0$f7bc48a6@default> <MgW%1.12$Tk.29140@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 4 Nov 1998 10:47:08, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> 
> You should not be able to move a USAGE IS INDEX field to anywhere, or
> to SET it to anything but to an INDEX NAME or another USAGE IS INDEX
> field.  To check it out, I ran the above syntax with Micro Focus and
> it flagged this:


Whoops.  You are right.  Here's some interesting stuff.

Identification Division.
Program-Id.  Test4.
Environment Division.
Configuration Section.
Data Division.
Working-Storage Section.
01 THE-TABLE.
   03  TABLE-ENTRIES PIC X(5) OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY FIELD-4.
01 FIELD-2 USAGE INDEX.
Procedure Division.
test-4-start.
    SET FIELD-4 TO 10
    SET FIELD-2 TO FIELD-4
    DISPLAY FIELD-2
    Stop Run
    .


Under Realia this program displays a 00045.

Under VMS you have to say Display Field-2 with Conversion and it 
displays 10.

It won't compiler under MVS COBOL for this and that.

Under Fujitsu 3.0 - 16 and 32 bit  - it displays 000000045


I can't get "at" the value under MVS - but I can store it in a file!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364141E0.7082B9F5@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <363FE3B4.E4A@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
> 
(snip)
> What about question 2?  I thought it was 1, but I found this in my very
> first COBOL textbook:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> THIS-INDEX?  (I was gonna try this today but our system was down.)

I think this is a good example where understanding S/360+ Assembler
helps. In my mind (and I suspect this is correct) a COBOL index (e.g.,
blah-blah-blah  occurs many times indexed by in-dex) is an Assembler
index register. This means that the value of the index to the first
occurrence is zero. If the table entry is 128 (decimal) bytes long, the
index (register) will contain 128 (decimal) for the 2nd entry, 256 for
the 3rd, 368 for the 4th, etc. 'Don't recall seeing any indexes in a
dump, just at Xpediter breakpoints (where Xpediter doesn't show the raw
contents of the index, but interprets it for us).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363C2A65.DA98CE87@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <36395847.781398E1@att.net> <3639CC72.160D7233@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I move values to subscripts all the time.  I set values to indices.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > differently.
(snip)

This turned into quite a thread. I took Kelly's statement to mean she
wanted to move an index (a I) to a subscript. In the MVS/COBOL II world
(at least) a field with USAGE INDEX is not legal in any move, see the
clips below:

 -------  Defining the index (I) & the subscript (SS-1) --

       01  BINARY-STUFF                BINARY  SYNC.
           05  SS-1                    PIC  S9(9).

       01  STJ-TABLE.
           05  STJ-COUNT               PIC  S9(9)  BINARY  SYNC.
           05  STJ-ENTRY               OCCURS  1 TO 99999 TIMES
                                       DEPENDING ON STJ-COUNT
                                       ASCENDING KEY STJT-SEQNO
                                       INDEXED BY  I.
               10  STJ-STJ-NO.
                   15  FILLER          PIC  X(9).
                   15  STJT-SEQNO      PIC  9(5).
                   15  FILLER          PIC  XX.
               10  STJ-LASER-SEQNO     PIC  9(5).

  -------  Manipulating the index (I) & the subscript (SS-1) --

                SET   I                     TO  +1
                SET   I                     TO  SS-1

                MOVE  I                     TO  SS-1

IGYPS2074-S "I" was defined as a type that was invalid in this context. 
The
            statement was discarded.

                MOVE  SS-1                  TO  I

IGYPS2074-S "I" was defined as a type that was invalid in this context. 
The
            statement was discarded.


I think if we have another go around on this thread, I'll volunteer now
to he "Hitler".

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363ba74c.29830643@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36395847.781398E1@att.net> <3298302.64136.16312@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 30 Oct 98 17:48:56 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:

>In message <<36395847.781398E1@att.net>> William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> writes:
>> > 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I concur. subscripts are just numeric items (COMP for best efficiency
with PIC S9(4) for half-word boundary....)  A subscript will always
contain the numeric value assigned to it. for example, if you 
    MOVE 10 TO SUB-X     the value will be 10.   

HOWEVER..... subscripts and indexes are equivalent in performance
*ONLY* if the value changes for each instruction execution.  Where an
index improves performance is where you have multiple variables
addressed by the setting of index or subscript. In this case,
generated object code will favor the use of indexes. Where you are
executing only one instruction per change of index or subscript, the
execution code will be equivalent. 

Oops... one correction. If using cobol/mvs with OPTIMIZE compile
option, performance will be improved but not as clear-cut on index vs.
subscript.  
david

p.s. i don't understand the original question. how do you move an "I"
to a subscript?  Was the original post referring to the binary value
of an "I"?  doesn't make sense (to me) otherwise...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.64558.15949@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<363ba74c.29830643@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<363ba74c.29830643@nntp.ix.netcom.com>> d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com  writes:
> 
> HOWEVER..... subscripts and indexes are equivalent in performance
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> execution code will be equivalent. 

You are describing a specific implementation's behaviour as
if it were a generalisation.  A Cobol implementation that
treats INDEX exactly like numeric items used as subscripts
may be fully conforming to the standard.

Compiler optimisation could also extract subscript calculations
and perform this once for a block of code using a particular
subscript or index value.

It is likely that an index will be more efficient in many
cases, but this is by no means assured.  It is unlikely
that it would be less efficient, but could be if there
was more manipulation of the index than usage of it.
```

---

<a id="t-28ec55bae2"></a>
## Thread: OT: my excuse? it's my birthday — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: my excuse? it's my birthday

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363D120A.BA039CE1@mindspring.com>`

```
well, this is as far from COBOL as you can get but so was the last
thread on drugs, taxes, et al.
Today, I turned 27.
6 years, 5 months, and 4 days ago, a 20 year old boy with a ponytail and
2 earrings had a dream.
I had just lost a pretty decent albeit highly stressful job.  While
waiting for my last paycheck, I had my life's dream burned into my mind.
I would cash that last check, pack up all I had (a few clothes and my
.44), and head west on my 76 Honda 750 Chopper.
Right before the check arrived, the Chopper (affectionately reffered to
as the "Honda Davidson") suffered an untimely demise when a woman pulled
out in front of me and totalled the bike.
As I sat and watched the sunset tonight, I say a happy-go-lucky kid
riding an old motorcycle westward into that sinking red orb to
destination unknown while I sit here burdened with obligations of a
mature, "productive citizen" such as working 60 hours a week and writing
COBOL code to run a muffler shop on the side.
Not that I'd give up what I have now willingly, but I wonder where he is
now.
If you see that 20 year old ponytailed kid with a dream inspired by
"Easy Rider", tell him to send me a postcard.  I really miss him.
```

```
I have a dream that when I retire, me an' me gal will ride west on a H.D.
into the sunset.
```

```
> 6 years, 5 months, and 4 days ago, a 20 year old boy with a ponytail and

not resist... but dreams change with times)

He is as much a part of you as the child wondering how they hide the

bare breast and the youngster staring proudly at his First Real
Paycheck.

> If you see that 20 year old ponytailed kid with a dream inspired by

different dreams, this is nothing new... and then you become a man and
put away childish things.  *Not* 'lose' them, not 'destroy them'... just
put them away, so that you can take them out and smile at them once in a
while.

DD
```

## Thread: Fujitsu error handling — 3 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-caca43e408)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu error handling

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298304.62732.14896@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71dfjd$jfd$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<71dfjd$jfd$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:

> for 18 years now, 12 hours per day, in 75 installations. Every file access
> is contained in the same copy that goes into a real tight loop displaying my
> phone number whenever an unexplained error status occurs. I can recite

I trust that the 'tight loop' includes an OS despatch in every
cycle (DOS INTx2F,AX=x1680 or Windows PeekMessage or equivalent).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu error handling

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71ifh3$42s$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71dfjd$jfd$1@news.igs.net> <3298304.62732.14896@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298304.62732.14896@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<71dfjd$jfd$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> writes:
>
>I trust that the 'tight loop' includes an OS despatch in every
>cycle (DOS INTx2F,AX=x1680 or Windows PeekMessage or equivalent).


That depends on the error.  And not under DOS, after all that is
a single user system, and a display by itself has a hook.  Under
Windows 95, I have a programmed 500 millisecond delay for sharing
errors, but lock up tighter than a drum if the files are damaged
structurally(and quite deliberately).  I do not want the user doing
*anything* further until the files are rebuilt.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu error handling

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-OzTJOzCeeWxL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<71d09m$a7b$1@news.igs.net> <19981030164831.28289.00001321@ng76.aol.com>`

```
On Fri, 30 Oct 1998 21:48:31, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

> The behavior you describe for MF compilers is identical to what I see with IBM
> mainframe COBOL-85 compilers.  I do not know the standards on this issue, but
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Maybe in a perfect world.

However, in reality the IBM and MicroFocus compilers violate the COBOL
standard in this respect, where Fujitsu does not.

The PRESENT standard requires either coding Invalid Key or 
Declaratives - one of the two is required.

That said - Fujitsu has a simple runtime option to turn of the 
reporting of the offending errors, and it will behave as you describe 
the others behaving.
```

---

<a id="t-db8c47a2fa"></a>
## Thread: Binary Search Compares — 6 msgs

### Binary Search Compares

- **From:** Rob Anderson <rta@2xtreme.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363D2F54.ABA335B7@2xtreme.net>`

```
I have an assignment for COBOL class that ask us to find the number of
compare for different size tables.  Unfortunately, i can't find the
formula for a binary search.  Does anyone know the formula for
calculating the number of campares for  given table size for a binay
search?

RTA
```

#### ↳ Re: Binary Search Compares

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363d38d7.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<363D2F54.ABA335B7@2xtreme.net>`

```
Rob Anderson wrote in message <363D2F54.ABA335B7@2xtreme.net>...
>I have an assignment for COBOL class that ask us to find the number of
>compare for different size tables.  Unfortunately, i can't find the
>formula for a binary search.  Does anyone know the formula for
>calculating the number of campares for  given table size for a binay
>search?


1 for size of 1
2 for size of 3
3 for size of 5
4 for size of 9
5 for size of 17
6 for size of 33
7 for size of 65
8 for size of 129
...
log2(n) for size of 2**n+1
```

```
8<

The Frog.

mostly gets wrong.
```

<a id="t-3bcd60cf3a"></a>
## Thread: Length of string — 10 this month / 24 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-3bcd60cf3a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Length of string

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@getridofspam.worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71gvjn$bie@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<71cndj$22a$1@news3.infoave.net> <v3m_1.1540$Hr5.2461890@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <3639f5cb.0@news1.ibm.net> <lyn_1.1564$Hr5.2508172@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <363a0cfc.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363a0cfc.0@news1.ibm.net>...
<SNIP>
>Anyway, now we are nit-picking. The basic idea of my posting
>(no matter what the silly details) was to use the very efficient
>group tests on 256 chars at a time to cut out 256 times thru the
>loop.

What do you think of this:
The problem can be solved in 21 executed statements maximum,
using a hard-coded binary search, counting each IF / ELSE as 2 statements.

The example is structured using an 8-byte variable length to keep
the size of my post down.  The technique will work equally well
for a 1024-byte variable with some cut-and-paste expansion
of the Procedure Division logic and the Working Storage definition.

No loop, no compute, no goto.

PROCEDURE DIVISION logic:

IF  POS-0005-THRU-0008 > SPACES
 IF  POS-0007-THRU-0008 > SPACES
  IF  POS-0008-THRU-0008 > SPACES
      MOVE +0008 TO STRING-LENGTH
  ELSE
      MOVE +0007 TO STRING LENGTH
 ELSE
  IF  BE-0006-THRU-0006 > SPACES
      MOVE +0006 TO STRING-LENGTH
  ELSE
      MOVE +0005 TO STRING LENGTH
ELSE
 IF  POS-0003-THRU-0004 > SPACES
  IF  POS-0004-THRU-0004 > SPACES
      MOVE +0004 TO STRING-LENGTH
  ELSE
      MOVE +0003 TO STRING LENGTH
 ELSE
  IF  POS-0002-THRU-0002 > SPACES
      MOVE +0002 TO STRING-LENGTH
  ELSE
      MOVE +0001 TO STRING LENGTH.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  STRING-LENGTH                 PIC S9(05) COMP-3.
01  POS-0001-THRU-0008.
    05  POS-0001-THRU-0004.
        10  POS-0001-THRU-0002.
            15  POS-0001-THRU-0001 PIC X.
            15  POS-0002-THRU-0002 PIC X.
        10  POS-0003-THRU-0004.
            15  POS-0003-THRU-0003 PIC X.
            15  POS-0004-THRU-0004 PIC X.
    05  POS-0005-POS-0008.
        10  POS-0005-THRU-0006.
            15  POS-0005-THRU-0005 PIC X.
            15  POS-0006-THRU-0006 PIC X.
        10  POS-0007-THRU-0008.
            15  POS-0007-THRU-0007 PIC X.
            15  POS-0008-THRU-0008 PIC X.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Length of string

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363c749b.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<71cndj$22a$1@news3.infoave.net> <v3m_1.1540$Hr5.2461890@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <3639f5cb.0@news1.ibm.net> <lyn_1.1564$Hr5.2508172@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <363a0cfc.0@news1.ibm.net> <71gvjn$bie@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
This is actually not very efficient, because some characters will be tested
several times (in the previous posts the maximum number was 2).
Even in your little example character 8 is tested 3 times.. For a 1024
character string the maximum rises to 10 times.

The test *should* be NOT EQUAL rather than GREATER to be strictly
accurate (which probanly is not needed, so more nit-picking).

Hugh Candlin wrote in message <71gvjn$bie@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...
>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <363a0cfc.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>several times (in the previous posts the maximum number was 2).
```

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

```
In message <<363a527c.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> 
>            INSPECT S TALLYING WS-STRING-LENGTH
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> of "large spaces" is not quite clear)

It would also fail to give the correct result when the 
string was > 1020 as it would fail to find the terminator
and would respond with 1024.

> So far in this thread we have seen the following *fundamental*
> techniques:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4) exiting loops in the middle using GO TOs

It may also be that a binary chop technique would work
reasonably efficiently.

Because the variable is 1024, start with Sp = 513 and Sl = 512

         PERFORM
             UNTIL Sl < 1

              IF ( Str(Sp:Sl) = SPACES )
                   DIVIDE 2 INTO Sl
                   SUBTRACT Sl FROM Sp
              ELSE
                   DIVIDE 2 INTO Sl
                   ADD Sl        TO Sp
              END-IF
         END-PERFORM
         < final coding not implemented >
```

```
In article <19981030173831.28289.00001332@ng76.aol.com>,
   rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
+On Fri, 30 Oct 1998 14:51:42 -0600
+ "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
+disagreed with a proposed solution, saying
+
+>>
+Try one more time!
+
+You always have to scan from the rear.
+If you scan from the beginning you stop on the first space.
+<<
+
+(The try one more time was in reference to a previous post that got chopped,
+and Leif is kind enough to be looking at my re-attempt to transmit).
+
+Leif,
+
+I respect your comment, however... 
+
+If you review the original post from Patrick L Archibald, there is an implied
+contiguous sequence of CHARACTER data until the first SPACE.  Thats why I 
have
+elaborate notes to the effect "... AND YOUR (the origirnal poster's) DATA IS 
AS
+CLEAN AS YOU (he) HAVE IMPLIED."

Yes, indeed, let's go back to the original. I think if you look carefully
at what you have cited here...

+On Fri, 30 Oct 1998 10:56:19 -0500
+"Patrick L Archibald" Patrick.Archibald@HomeTelco.Com>
+>>           INSPECT S TALLYING WS-STRING-LENGTH
+>                   FOR CHARACTERS
+>                   BEFORE INITIAL '    '.

.. you will find that the literal consists of *four* spaces. Hence the
original poster's comment that the method works unless the data contains
"large spaces."
+>
+<<
+
+I just took that at face value, and elaborated.  The forward scan I suggest
+uses SPACE as the delimitter, just as the original INSPECT.

Except that the original INSPECT uses a delimiter of four spaces, not one.
```

#### ↳ Re: Length of string

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71irj0$lhb@journal.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<71cndj$22a$1@news3.infoave.net>`

```

01  INPUT-AREA    PIC X(1024).
01  TALLY-COUNTER PIC 9(4).
01  DATA-LENGTH   PIC 9(4).

MOVE ZERO TO TALLY-COUNTER.
INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE(INPUT-AREA)
    TALLYING TALLY-COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES.
COMPUTE DATA-LENGTH = LENGTH OF INPUT-AREA - TALLY-COUNTER.
```

```
Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron] wrote in message
<71jakc$ic6@chronicle.concentric.net>...
>In <363cf8d7.0@news1.ibm.net>, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
>>We timed this one several weeks ago in this NG. It is extremely slow, so
>>does not meet the requirement of being *efficient*.
>
>"Extremely slow" on what platform?


This is a good question. We did not test it on all
possible platforms with all possible compilers.

As I recall, the platform was a PC and the compilers were
MicroFocus and Fujitsu.


I'm reproducing one of the posts from then:

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Since we're starting to really get into the optimization thing, let's
>take a look at another tweak.  I took Rick's program and tweaked it
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   0.33 jud-coded


I have converted the TEST3 program to Cobol-85 (the issue, I learned
-thanks Bill and Andreas - was the non-standard 'move function' statements).
I left in the comp-5 in order to compare with your times. I also timed
separately
the reverse function and the inspect statement, as well as cirrected for the
loop overhead. Here are my results for two different compilers on a 266MHz
PC MMX; I used your loop count of 500,000. The first number is for Fujitsu
V3.10 (32-bit). The second is for MicroFocus V3.1 DOS (16-bit).
My program are at the end of this after yours.

loop:           3.19   4.01
times below are corrected for loop:
rick:            58.38  28.03
reverse:     26.41  10.22
inspect:      2.91    3.13
Judson:     34.05  16.31
HandRick:   6.32    3.41
HandJud:    3.62    2.04


I'm puzzled the hand-coded results are about 10 times
slower than yours, while the functions are 2 times as
fast.

It would be interesting to see what Acucobol and RM
give. I suspect that at least with Acucobol, then results would be
reversed, with the hand-coded versions being the slowest.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.  TEST3.
000300* RIGHT JUSTIFY
000400
000500 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000600 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000700 SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000800 OBJECT-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000900
001000 DATA DIVISION.
001100 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001200
001300 01  INPUT-FIELD                 PIC X(30).
001400 01  OUTPUT-FIELD                PIC X(30).
001500
001600 01  COUNTERS.
001700     02  COUNTER                 PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
001800     02  I                       PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
001900     02  J                       PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
002000     02  K                       PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
002100     02  LOOP                    PIC S9(9) COMP-5 VALUE +5000000.
002200
002300 01  TEST-VARIABLES.
002400     02  THE-TIME                PIC 9(8).
002500     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  THE-TIME.
002600         03  THE-HOURS           PIC 99.
002700         03  THE-MINUTES         PIC 99.
002800         03  THE-SECONDS         PIC 99.
002900         03  THE-CENTISECONDS    PIC 99.
003000
003100     02  TIME-CENTISECONDS       PIC 9(8).
003200     02  TIME-BEFORE             PIC 9(8).
003300     02  TIME-AFTER              PIC 9(8).
003400     02  TIME-OVERHEAD           PIC 9(8)   VALUE ZEROES.
003500     02  SECONDS-ELAPSED         PIC 9(3).99.
003600     02  CHAR                    PIC X.
003700
003800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
003900 TEST-3-START.
004000     ACCEPT THE-TIME FROM TIME
004100     DISPLAY "start " NO ADVANCING
004200     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
004300
004400     PERFORM TEST3-START-LOOP LOOP TIMES
004500     DISPLAY "loop  " NO ADVANCING
004600     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
004700     COMPUTE TIME-OVERHEAD = TIME-AFTER - TIME-BEFORE
004800     DISPLAY "times below are corrected for loop"
004900
005000     PERFORM TEST3-START-RICK LOOP TIMES
005100     DISPLAY "rick      " NO ADVANCING
005200     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
005300
005400     PERFORM TEST3-START-REVERSE LOOP TIMES
005500     DISPLAY "reverse   "  NO ADVANCING
005600     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
005700
005800     PERFORM TEST3-START-INSPECT LOOP TIMES
005900     DISPLAY "inspect   " NO ADVANCING
006000     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
006100
006200     PERFORM TEST3-START-JUDSON LOOP TIMES
006300     DISPLAY "judson    " NO ADVANCING
006400     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
006500
006600     PERFORM TEST3-START-HAND-RICK LOOP TIMES
006700     DISPLAY "hand rick " NO ADVANCING
006800     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
006900
007000     PERFORM TEST3-START-HAND-JUD  LOOP TIMES
007100     DISPLAY "hand jud  " NO ADVANCING
007200     PERFORM DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED
007300
007400     ACCEPT CHAR
007500     STOP RUN
007600     .
007700
007800 DISPLAY-TIME-ELAPSED.
007900     PERFORM GET-CENTISECONDS
008000     MOVE TIME-CENTISECONDS TO TIME-BEFORE
008100
008200     ACCEPT THE-TIME FROM TIME
008300     PERFORM GET-CENTISECONDS
008400     COMPUTE TIME-AFTER = TIME-CENTISECONDS - TIME-OVERHEAD
008500
008600     COMPUTE SECONDS-ELAPSED = (TIME-AFTER - TIME-BEFORE) / 100
008700     DISPLAY SECONDS-ELAPSED
008800     .
008900
009000 GET-CENTISECONDS.
009100     COMPUTE TIME-CENTISECONDS = (THE-HOURS    * 3600
009200                               +  THE-MINUTES  * 60
009300                               +  THE-SECONDS) * 100
009400                               +  THE-CENTISECONDS
009500     .
009600
009700 TEST3-START-LOOP.
009800     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
009900     .
010000
010100 TEST3-START-RICK.
010200     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
010300     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
010400         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (INPUT-FIELD) TO INPUT-FIELD
010500         MOVE 1 TO COUNTER
010600         INSPECT INPUT-FIELD TALLYING COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES
010700         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (INPUT-FIELD (COUNTER:))
010800           TO INPUT-FIELD (COUNTER:)
010900     END-IF
011000     .
011100
011200 TEST3-START-REVERSE.
011300     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
011400     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
011500         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (INPUT-FIELD) TO INPUT-FIELD
011600     END-IF
011700     .
011800
011900 TEST3-START-INSPECT.
012000     MOVE "                  ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
012100     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
012200         MOVE 1 TO COUNTER
012300         INSPECT INPUT-FIELD TALLYING COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES
012400     END-IF
012500     .
012600
012700 TEST3-START-JUDSON.
012800     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
012900     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
013000         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE(INPUT-FIELD) TO OUTPUT-FIELD
013100         MOVE 1 TO COUNTER
013200         INSPECT OUTPUT-FIELD TALLYING COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES
013300         STRING INPUT-FIELD DELIMITED BY SIZE
013400           INTO OUTPUT-FIELD WITH POINTER COUNTER
013500         MOVE OUTPUT-FIELD TO INPUT-FIELD
013600     END-IF
013700     .
013800
013900 TEST3-START-HAND-RICK.
014000     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
014100     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
014200         COMPUTE I, J = FUNCTION LENGTH (INPUT-FIELD)
014300         PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD (I:1) NOT = SPACE
014400             SUBTRACT 1 FROM I
014500         END-PERFORM
014600         PERFORM UNTIL I = 0
014700             MOVE INPUT-FIELD (I:1) TO INPUT-FIELD (J:1)
014800             SUBTRACT 1 FROM I
014900             SUBTRACT 1 FROM J
015000         END-PERFORM
015100         IF J > 0
015200             MOVE SPACES TO INPUT-FIELD (1:J)
015300         END-IF
015400     END-IF
015500     .
015600
015700 TEST3-START-HAND-JUD.
015800     MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD
015900     IF INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES
016000         COMPUTE I, J = FUNCTION LENGTH (INPUT-FIELD)
016100         PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD (I:1) NOT = SPACE
016200             SUBTRACT 1 FROM I
016300         END-PERFORM
016400         SUBTRACT I FROM J
016500         ADD J, 1 GIVING K
016600         MOVE INPUT-FIELD (1:I) TO OUTPUT-FIELD (K:I)
016700         IF J > 0
016800             MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD (1:J)
016900         END-IF
017000         MOVE OUTPUT-FIELD TO INPUT-FIELD
017100     END-IF
017200     .

>
>Note that with the overhead of 500,000 perform loops, the time to
…[121 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Length of string

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363fb5fa.33588987@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71cndj$22a$1@news3.infoave.net>`

```
On Fri, 30 Oct 1998 10:56:19 -0500, "Patrick L Archibald"
<Patrick.Archibald@HomeTelco.Com> wrote:

>Hi
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
hmmm.... ok, i've read several constructive and innovative posts...
all well and good.  i'm wondering though, if we would help you better
if you explained WHY you need to do this. For example, if we knew the
business need, it may be that a well-constructed UNSTRING statement
may eliminate the need to count the size of the characters in the
array. If the elements that are filling the array have a consistent
pattern of data elements, then there may be a better solution than
counting. I mention this because we often see questions that assume a
particular technique without explaining the issue.  
david
```

## Thread: Cleveland Opportunities — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Cleveland Opportunities

- **From:** "Roskos Family" <roskos@stratos.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gX8%1.7$r4.526172@news.axxsys.net>`

```
My name is David Roskos, and I am manager of consulting for modis, Cleveland
(formerly Datacorp Business Systems, Inc.).  Like everyone else, we are in
need of experienced mainframe programmers.  Unlike everyone else, we are
looking for folks who want full-time employment (salaried positions), with
opportunities to move into other areas of programming (client-server,
client-main, ERP, and more).  If you are interested, send your resume to
roskos@datacorp.com.  You can also call me at our Cleveland headquarters at
1-800-327-3274.

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cleveland Opportunities

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363D93D8.57DC@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<gX8%1.7$r4.526172@news.axxsys.net>`

```
Roskos Family wrote:
> 
> My name is David Roskos, and I am manager of consulting for modis, Cleveland
> (formerly Datacorp Business Systems, Inc.).

When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of
rates, associated with the position(s) offered; to do otherwise leads
many to conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a
blind ad to determine rates.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** Philippe Le Marchand <mkt@raincode.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363DCF0B.730617F7@raincode.com>`
- **References:** `<rvq1znue0zc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de>`

```
Dear Burkhard,

Looking at your news correspondence here, let me introduce you the tool you're
after: RainCode.

RainCode is a complianceware meta-tool for Cobol.
It first generates a parsing tree, on which you can make any compliance check
using its easy script language.
As RainCode can manipulate several parsing trees at a time, all you would have
to do is to write a script in RainCode that goes through one tree, and then
verifies that the other is equivalent...

Let me suggest you, as Leif Svalgaard did, to visit our website
http://www.raincode.com, to see that RainCode meets your needs, and much more.

Then let me know your reaction about it.

Best regards.


Burkhard Perkens-Golomb wrote:

> We're using generators to produce cobol source code and are now in a
> process of switching from an old to a new generator.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Burkhard
```

<a id="t-3e5393d887"></a>
## Thread: Post-Y2K Employment Prospects — 6 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-3e5393d887)._

#### ↳ Re: Post-Y2K Employment Prospects

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363C80F8.D4793770@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<71fpj1$n1v$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
As much as it pains me to agree with faculty members, most of the consultants
that I know say "yes", there is life after Y2K.

Most firms do not have a data processing budget large enough to cover anything
other than Y2K and legal requirements.  In addition to deferred maintenance
requests, normal corporate activities, e.g. acquisitions, spin-offs,
out-sourcing, in-sourcing, etc., and the failure of two-tier client/server
technology are going to create a huge demand for COBOL programmers.

Also, a significant number of COBOL programmers/contractors will be retiring
after the Y2K feeding frenzy.

David J. Crook wrote:

> As a new COBOL student, I'm interested in professionals' opinion of post-Y2K
> employment prospects. My profs at college say that the job market won't dip
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> ICQ# 7251032
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Post-Y2K Employment Prospects

- **From:** Amy Boyd <amyboyd@dentech.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363C87E4.BA9FA681@dentech.net>`
- **References:** `<71fpj1$n1v$1@news1.epix.net> <363C80F8.D4793770@sprynet.com>`

```
This is something that has been bugging me as a student as well.  I have been
frustrated at my university for forcing me to take 3 semesters of COBOL before I
graduate.  I want to have a good job, but I also want to keep that job.  Greg. I
am glad you pointed out that many programmers will be retiring after the Y2K stuff
is over with, but what else can I learn to make me a marketable employee after
this Y2K thing?

Greg Ferro wrote:

> As much as it pains me to agree with faculty members, most of the consultants
> that I know say "yes", there is life after Y2K.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> > ICQ# 7251032
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Post-Y2K Employment Prospects

- **From:** "NiteStar" <SpamNitestar@nightmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71i5co$9m0$1@supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<71fpj1$n1v$1@news1.epix.net> <363C80F8.D4793770@sprynet.com> <363C87E4.BA9FA681@dentech.net>`

```
To have a future in Cobol, Make sure that you learn
CICS and a database, mostly DB2.  With these 3 things
you should be able to work at 85% of the cobol shops.
I have also found a number of cobol shops still using the
program language PL1 in conjunction with Cobol.

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: Post-Y2K Employment Prospects

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

```
> What are professionals opinions on post Y2K employment potentials?

I think they are very good.  But I hedged my bet by paying a fortune and
a couple of years of time to get my masters in CIS oriented to
object-oriented methodologies and languages.  I expect I will never use
those professionally, but am unwilling to take chances.  In 2 weeks,
I'll be done and soon I will spend time getting a server for my home
LAN, and maybe an Object oriented COBOL for home.

I probably won't use any of this - but then it might be just what
differentiates me from some other COBOL programmer looking for a job
someday.
```

<a id="t-3d39278271"></a>
## Thread: COBOL 74 — 1 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-3d39278271)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 74

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981101132046.26673.00001772@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<71fspi$2g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

In article <71fspi$2g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, kcarrusa@my-dejanews.com writes:

> check-sym not equal '2214' and '2218' and '2220'

That is something I had to double-check for our conversion efforts.

LANGLVL(1) interpretation:

  (check-sum not = '2214') and (check-sum = '2218') and (check-sum = '2220')

LANGLVL(2) and subsequent interpretations:

  (check-sum not = '2214') and (check-sum not = '2218') and (check-sum not =
'2220')

That is not where we got tripped up in our conversion efforts. One of our
programmers was converting a real old DOS/VS COBOL program to COBOL/VSE and did
not realize that these two write statements are not equivalent:

  05 NEXT-FORM  PIC X VALUE '2'.
  WRITE ... AFTER POSITIONING NEXT-FORM

and

  WRITE ... AFTER POSITIONING 2.

It took me a while to convince the programmer that the two produced radically
different paper motions (as if the physical printing not lining up wasn't proof
enough, sigh).

After all our in-house COBOL programs were rewritten in COBOL/VSE, it turns out
that it was only that one program that skipped to a channel other than channel
1, so that turned out to be the only program that needed a SPECIAL-NAMES to be
able to identify to the WRITE/AFTER ADVANCING that it was suppose to skip to a
channel other than 1. (Channel 1 is easy: WRITE ... AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.)

Mark A. Young
```

### Cobol Unleashed book

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363D0492.9A4@netbox.com>`

```
I received the following announcement today and thought I would pass it
along.  I am not associated with this company, but I have bought many
books from them without any problems.  

Here are the November Specials at
README .DOC Discount Computer Books,
   http://www.readmedotdoc.com

COBOL Unleashed w/CD
By Wessler, Jon, et al.

Most books simply do not cover all the features you need to
know about COBOL. However, this book, written for the
programming professional who uses COBOL, provides quick,
accurate information on all areas of the language. This book
is both an IBM COBOL book & a PC COBOL book because it
emphasizes the language, which is virtually identical in
both environments, & the same on UNIX, Unisys, CDC, VAX, &
HP-3000. COBOL Unleashed presents real-world solutions to
the key programming problems facing COBOL programmers today.
Key topics include: Year 2000 problems & solutions; legacy
code migration, maintenance & reengineering;
interoperability & compatibility of legacy systems;
client/server COBOL; COBOL database programming; & more.

©1998, Publisher: SAMS, 1021 Pages. 
Retail $49.99 ISBN 0672312549 
README .DOC's Special $32.49
```

```
that uses SlickEdit for its IDE (Enterprise edition).  SlickEdit has a very
good search and replace engine, and many other nice features such as
multi-language support, keyword highlighting, and a nice macro language for
customizing the editor.
```

<a id="t-988d3b378f"></a>
## Thread: JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX

- **From:** bobnon@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<71kmfs$utd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hello world,

I have a batch MVS job where a Cobol program calls a REXX program.
The REXX program is issuing return codes of -3 for Host commands.
The REXX program failing with at the ping command and ftp command.
The return code indicates that the system can't find the host command.

What librarie(s) or pds(s) does my jcl need to point to?

bobnon

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX

- **From:** "James W. Richards" <jwrichDELTHIS@wwa.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wjevpujjnpbz.f1tpcz5.pminews@news.wwa.com>`
- **References:** `<71kmfs$utd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Bobon,

I am not certain about this, but I think the Rexx procedure
will need to be compiled.  This can get tricky as to linking
since typically a linkage stub is required for C and C++ compatibility.
I don't know if there is one for COBOL.  

You will need to check with you OS/390 installation people
to see if the capability to compile REXX programs is installed.
If so, they will be able to tell you the libraries and whether or
not they are included in the system IPL parms.

I hope this helps.

On Mon, 02 Nov 1998 16:26:37 GMT, bobnon@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>Hello world,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<71n1o7$43h@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<71kmfs$utd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You probably need to explicitly call out the load 
path for the COBOL program. Also, watch your quotes.
Try something like:

    "CALL 'D001.TEST.LOADLIB(MYCOBPRG)'" 

Yours,
-Paul


bobnon@my-dejanews.com wrote:
: Hello world,

: I have a batch MVS job where a Cobol program calls a REXX program.
: The REXX program is issuing return codes of -3 for Host commands.
: The REXX program failing with at the ping command and ftp command.
: The return code indicates that the system can't find the host command.

: What librarie(s) or pds(s) does my jcl need to point to?

: bobnon

: -----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
: http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX

- **From:** Mickey <mickeyb@nospam.home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<364517C1.DB47C1F0@nospam.home.com>`
- **References:** `<71kmfs$utd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <71n1o7$43h@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Ping and FTP are commands that reside in libraries that are concatenated
to your TSO session, but not part of your SYS1.LINKLIB. See which
loadlibs are part of your session under TSO, and put them eitehr in a
STEPLIB or a JOBLIB. The -3 you are getting is the REXX of an MVS 806
(module not found)

mickey

paulr wrote:
> 
> You probably need to explicitly call out the load
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> : http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL needed for Cobol to call REXX

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981105100011.17259.00004261@ng125.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<71kmfs$utd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
bobnon writes ...

>Hello world,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>What librarie(s) or pds(s) does my jcl need to point to?

When you run a batch job, you normally don't have access to TSO commands, since
the TSO environment is not automatically established in the batch. You can
establish the TSO environment from your COBOL program by calling IKJTSOEV.

To do this, call IKJTSOEV passing five parameters, each of them fullword binary
fields. And you really only care about the second. So, you might include
something like this:

01 TSO-parms.
    05 word-1 pic s9(9) binary value 0.
    05 word-2 pic s9(9) binary value 0.
    05 word-3 pic s9(9) binary value 0.
    05 word-4 pic s9(9) binary value 0.
    05 word-5 pic s9(9) binary value 0.
.
.
.
         call 'ikjtsoev' using word-1,
                                           word-2,
                                           word-3,
                                           word-4,
                                           word-5

Now, assuming the value in word-2 is zero after the call, you have established
the TSO environment (if it is 8, by the way, you have established the TSO
environment but not the REXX environment).

Next, to invoke your REXX exec, do it through a call to IKJEFTSR, the TSO
service facility....

01 IKJ-parms.
    02  IKJ-flags pic x(4) value x'00010001'.
    02 IKJ-command pic x(??) 
                      value '%exec_name'
    02 IKJ-buflen pic s9(9) binary value ??.
    02 IKJ-RC      pic s9(9) binary value 0.
    02 IKJ-Reason-code pic s9(9) binary 
                      value 0.
    02 IKJ-Abend-code pic s9(9) binary
                      value 0.
.
.
.
        call 'ikjeftsr' using IKJ-flags,
                                          IKJ-command,
                                          IKJ-buflen,
                                          IKJ-RC,
                                          IKJ-Reason-code,
                                          IKJ-Abend-code

after this call, check RETURN-CODE; if this is non-zero, check the values in
the last three parameters for more detailed information. There are other
possible values for the IKJ-flags field. 

At run time, include a DD statment named SYSEXEC that points to the library
containing your exec.

All this, and more, is detailed in my course "Advanced TSO REXX Programming",
see the website in my signature.

Hope this helps get you started.

Cheers,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

## Thread: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps? — 15 msgs

### Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

Probably a dumb question, so anyone willing to point my nose to the 
right answer, please accept my advanced thanks. 

I always thought that the compiler pretty much took care of 
data representation translations, but in this case...  <grumble>

With the following:

   ...  record definition ...
      10 transaction-amount                    pic x(13).
      10 dec-transaction-amount redefines transacton-amount
                                               pic 9(11)v99.
   ...
   ...
   ...

   01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is comp.



    
and later in the program....

    move dec-transaction-amount to ws-trans-amount.


The above statement almost invariably causes a data format error
at runtime. Since I thought a numeric edited field could contain 
spaces, and this data is coming right justified with space fill,
I'm really cornfused. 

Appreciate someone pointing out the obvious to me at this point. : )

Thanks
-paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363E322E.EC549A8D@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:

> Probably a dumb question,

Hmmm. Not to ask may be dumbier
8<

>       10 dec-transaction-amount redefines transacton-amount
>                                                pic 9(11)v99.
>    01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is comp.

8<

>    move dec-transaction-amount to ws-trans-amount.
>
> The above statement almost invariably causes a data format error
> at runtime. Since I thought a numeric edited field

I see *no* numeric edited field in your code sample. A picture composed from
the chars SVP(9) alone is numeric, but not edited.

> could contain
> spaces, and this data is coming right justified with space fill,
> I'm really cornfused.

The spaces are betraying you: they are not numeric. If the space fill is
always guarenteed on the left, use
INSPECT REPLACING LEADING SPACE[S] BY ZERO[[E]S]
just before the MOVE

> Appreciate someone pointing out the obvious to me at this point. : )
>
> Thanks

Please

> -paul

Huib
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71snd0$c7j@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com> <363E322E.EC549A8D@IMN.nl>`

```

I tried that one - doesn't even phase it. Blasted thingamajjig...
I wound up doing some - ah - 'old style' data manipulation on this,
based pretty much on some stuff Leif, among others, pointed out. 
In short, I defined the dratted text field, using placeholders

  10 transaction-amount.
     15 b100            pic 9
     15 b10             pic 9
     15 b1              pic 9
     15 comma-1         pic x
     15 m100            pic 9
... etc

The just defined the numeric field the same way...
  10  dec-transaction-amount 9(12)v99.
  10  dec-transaction-fields   redefines dec-translateion-amount.
      15 b100           pic 9.
      15 b10            pic 9.
      15 b1             pic 9
      15 m100           pic 9
      15 m10            pic 9.
... etc

Then just did a move corresponding. Blasted thing worked and
I gave up while I was ahead. Old compilers are soooo much fun... :)

(BTW: I don't have the source code in front of me, so typo's
in the above are just typos, the don't exist in the real source
wich is running fine. I also expanded the fields to handle 100B vice 10B. ) 

-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

```
paulr wrote in message <71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Probably a dumb question, so anyone willing to point my nose to the
>right answer, please accept my advanced thanks.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>   01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is comp.

>and later in the program....
>    move dec-transaction-amount to ws-trans-amount.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'm really cornfused (sic).

The redefined dec-transaction-amount must contain a valid
number before the move to ws-trans-amount. There are no
spaces in it. If you have spaces, inspect transaction-amount
replacing with spaces before the move.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** e=mc^3@netcom.com (Richard Anderson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71la13$i20@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com> <363dfd4d.0@news1.ibm.net> <71l5k5$tdh@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 2 Nov 1998 20:44:53 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

>  Boy, I'm sorry to sound dumb, but the stuff coming in is an 
>edited numeric field. They are space filled on the left. 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>: >                                               pic 9(11)v99.
This guy does NOT describe what you are redefining.
Try:      10 dec-transaction-amount-edit
             redefines transaction-amount    pic zzz,zzz,zzz.99.

>: >   01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is comp.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Then:  move dec-transaction-amount-edit to ws-trans-amount.

The IBM COBOL-for-this-and-that manual PROMISES that this will
de-edit the edited field properly.

However, neither the Lang Ref  nor the Programming Guide makes it
even slightly clear whether the numeric edited picture clause must
match the picture clause that created transaction-amount or if any
old valid picture clause for edited numeric will do the trick.
E.g., they say nothing about sign format, scaling, whatever.  Ah,
the joys of converting alphnumeric strings to numeric! :(

Rick Anderson
Seattle
anderson  ï¿½atï¿½  pobox  ï¿½fullstopï¿½  com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363e632c.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com> <363dfd4d.0@news1.ibm.net> <71l5k5$tdh@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote in message <71l5k5$tdh@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Hi Leif -
>  Boy, I'm sorry to sound dumb, but the stuff coming in is an
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>>  IWZ901S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.

Now that you have shown an example:

>>  TRANSFER AMOUNT .... [    17,269.53]
>>  DECIMAL AMOUNT ..... [    17,269.53]


you can see that the pic 9(11)v99 does not correspond to
the actual data 17,269.53 which has a picture more like
pic zz,zzz,zz9.99.  Now de-editing from an edited format
to a numeric format is not supported by many older compilers.
So a simple move won't work on these. If you have a newer
complier it should work (and you could use one of the functions),
but on an old compiler you may have to resort to the
following code if your data is welformed enough:
(I'm being slightly paranoid about mixing alphanumeric
and numeric values here, hence the redefinitions)


10  transaction-amount                   pic x(13).
10  transaction-amount-chars        redefines transaction-amount.
     15  transaction-amount-char     pic occurs 13 times.
10  transaction-amount-digits        redefines transaction-amount.
     15  transaction-amount-digit    pic occurs 13 times.
....
      move zeroes to ws-trans-amount
      perform get-amount-digit
          varying char-nbr from 1 by 1
               until char-nbr > 13

      divide 100 into ws-trans-amount
      .

get-amount-digit.
     if transaction-amount-char (char-nbr) is numeric
         compute ws-trans-amount = ws-trans-amount * 10
                                      + transaction-amount-digit (char-nbr)
     .








>
>: paulr wrote in message <71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>: >   01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is
comp.
>
>: >and later in the program....
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363E78BA.6D0499B@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com> <363dfd4d.0@news1.ibm.net> <71l5k5$tdh@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

Your picture clause does not agree with your data. the V in the pic means an
implied decimal, but your example is a numeric-edited field with  an explicit
decimal point. Try defining transaction-amount as PIC Z(10).99. Then you can
MOVE  transaction-amount  to ws-trans-amount.  As a side note, it is more
efficient to store/manipulate numbers that contain a V in the picture clause as
COMP-3.  COMP forces the compiler to do a lot of extra work.


paulr wrote:

> Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> : >I'm really cornfused (sic).
```
