#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71l6c2$lra@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>...
>
>Probably a dumb question, so anyone willing to point my nose to the
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>
>The above statement almost invariably causes a data format error
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are correct that a numeric-edited item CAN contain spaces (if it is
correctly defined) but your field is numeric, not numeric edited.

Although there are some other possibilities, what it SOUNDS like you want is

  10 dec-transaction-amount redefines transacton-amount
                                               pic 9(11)v99
                       BLANK WHEN ZERO.

Or you want to change 9(11)v99 to Z(11).ZZ (which is one byte longer and
assumes that the input has a period "." rather than an implied decimal
point.

Note: There are some odd rules concerning when, if, and what it means to
have a BLANK WHEN ZERO clause with a PICTURE with "v" (and even funnier when
you have an "S") - but my guess is that this is what you really want.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363E2BE1.EE5F6E47@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
You CANNOT move a non-numeric into a COMP-3 defined field.  Verify that the
redefined field is NUMERIC before the MOVE.

paulr wrote:

> Probably a dumb question, so anyone willing to point my nose to the
> right answer, please accept my advanced thanks.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields with IBM Comps?

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3640E868.3D0D@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<71kte5$su2@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> With the following:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                pic 9(11)v99.

>    01 ws-trans-amount                         pic 9(11)v99 usage is comp.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Why do you want to define a numeric transaction amount (currency) as a
comp (binary) field?? I would think you mean to use comp-3 (packed).
That may be part of your problem.

Bob
```

<a id="t-70a0174c17"></a>
## Thread: is this a sliding window? — 7 msgs

### is this a sliding window?

- **From:** higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
found this when i was checking for needed y2k remediation:
(code simplified)

if todays-date-cc = 19
  add 1900 to ccyy-field
else
  if birth-year > todays-date-yy
    add 1900 to ccyy-field
  else
    add 2000 to ccyy-field
  end-if
end-if.

i never really understood the sliding window method of remediation. is
this an example of this technique?? i understand this (it's not
intuitive to me, but i can grasp it if i focus; one of the confusing
things about the original code is that the 'birth-year' has century in
its name, so i always have to ascertain that that field does not contain
any century info before it becomes clear.), and in cases where one does
not have persons over 100 years old, it would work for me. but i would
like to be able to tell my supervisor and users *if they are interested*
what technique i've used.

suzie
```

#### ↳ Re: is this a sliding window?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ls%1.162$yi1.366925@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote:
>found this when i was checking for needed y2k remediation:
>(code simplified)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>end-if.

That code is broken.  Right now a birth year of 99 would be
in the 1800's, not 1900's.  It is also not a sliding window,
because the ranges are fixed.

Birth date is one of the dates in which windowing is often
not appropriate, because many people live to be over 100.
If it is for students at a grade school, or patients at a
pediatrician's office, or other such restricted birth ranges,
it would be okay.  Windowing is a technique used to resolve a
two digit year into a four digit year.  For conversion use, a
static window is usually acceptable, because the conversion
will be done within a limited time.  But to be effective for
indefinite use, you must use a sliding window, based on the
current date at the time.  Sliding windows using the current
date can be very useful, and a completely valid method for
conversion and permitting users to continue to enter two digit
years indefinitely, so long as the dates they are entering can
always be assumed to be within a single century defined by the
current date at the time of entry.

The following code will resolve a six digit 'input' date into
a century that is 'windowed' from the current year.  This
'window' is defined by low and high range.  The low and high
range can be any offset from the current year, but since the
range must be exactly 100 years, the difference between high
range and low range (RANGE-HIGH - RANGE-LOW) must = 99.  The
example below uses a common range, current year -79 to current
year + 20.  You could use low and high of 0 and 99, -99 and 0,
-120 and -21, 10 and 109, or any other range, so long as you
always choose a low and high where high - low = 99.

     03  RANGE-HIGH           PIC S9(02) VALUE +20.
     03  RANGE-LOW            PIC S9(02) VALUE -79.

     03  TODAYS-CCYYMMDD      PIC  9(08).
         05  TODAYS-CCYY      PIC  9(04).
         05  TODAYS-CCYY-R    REDEFINES DW-RESOLVE-CCYY.
             07  TODAYS-CC        PIC  9(02).
             07  TODAYS-YY        PIC  9(02).
         05  TODAYS-MM        PIC  9(02).
         05  TODAYS-DD        PIC  9(02).

     03  WINDOW-CCYYMMDD      PIC  9(08).
         05  WINDOW-CCYY      PIC  9(04).
         05  WINDOW-CCYY-R    REDEFINES DW-RESOLVE-CCYY.
             07  WINDOW-CC        PIC  9(02).
             07  WINDOW-YY        PIC  9(02).
         05  WINDOW-MM        PIC  9(02).
         05  WINDOW-DD        PIC  9(02).

     MOVE TODAYS-CC TO WINDOW-CC.
     MOVE INPUT-YY  TO WINDOW-YY.
     MOVE INPUT-MM  TO WINDOW-MM.
     MOVE INPUT-DD  TO WINDOW-DD.
     IF (WINDOW-CCYY > TODAYS-CCYY + RANGE-HIGH)
         SUBTRACT 1 FROM WINDOW-CC
     ELSE
         IF (WINDOW-CCYY < TODAYS-CCYY + RANGE-LOW)
             ADD 1 TO WINDOW-CC.

Note that if you want to window to the century immediately
prior to the exact current date, such as for birth date,
you will need to compare the whole date rather than the
year, but you do not need to test for a lower range.

     MOVE TODAYS-CC TO WINDOW-CC.
     MOVE INPUT-YY  TO WINDOW-YY.
     MOVE INPUT-MM  TO WINDOW-MM.
     MOVE INPUT-DD  TO WINDOW-DD.
     IF (WINDOW-CCYYMMDD > TODAYS-CCYYMMDD)
         SUBTRACT 1 FROM WINDOW-CC.

Analogous rules apply if you want to window to the century
immediately subsequent to the exact current date.

     MOVE TODAYS-CC TO WINDOW-CC.
     MOVE INPUT-YY  TO WINDOW-YY.
     MOVE INPUT-MM  TO WINDOW-MM.
     MOVE INPUT-DD  TO WINDOW-DD.
     IF (WINDOW-CCYYMMDD < TODAYS-CCYYMMDD)
         ADD 1 FROM WINDOW-CC.
```

#### ↳ Re: is this a sliding window?

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363f24ae.3728900@news-server>`
- **References:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
On Mon, 02 Nov 1998 14:47:57 -0600, higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us
enlightened us:

>found this when i was checking for needed y2k remediation:
>(code simplified)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>suzie

I don't believe this is an example of the "sliding window" technique.
That technique, as I recall, requires that you set an arbitrary year
and anything before that year is 2000 and anything equal to or greater
is 1900.  For example, let's say the arbitrary year is 50.  If you
were presented data where the year = 49, then you would add 2000 to
come up with ccyy (2049).  If the data contained 51, then you would
add 1900 to come up ccyy (1951).

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Think. It's good practice for when the computer is down.
 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: is this a sliding window?

- **From:** "AM" <AM69107@glaxowellcome.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be080c$f0f12d80$c58c3398@us0071826>`
- **References:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us> <363f24ae.3728900@news-server>`

```
having a fixed year as a reference point give you a 100 year window, as per
example 
  window year = 50,  then all years less than 50 will be 21st century 
2000-2049
                               then all years greater or equal 50 will be
20th Century 1950-1999.

A sliding window will have the window year "sliding" with the current year.
   window year = current year - 20...   where 20 is an arbitrary number you
determine 
   


SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in article
<363f24ae.3728900@news-server>...
| On Mon, 02 Nov 1998 14:47:57 -0600, higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us
| enlightened us:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
| >

<snipped..>
 
| I don't believe this is an example of the "sliding window" technique.
| That technique, as I recall, requires that you set an arbitrary year
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

You are correct of course.  What I described was a fixed window.  What
the original poster described was not any kind of window!

Cheers,

#### ↳ Re: is this a sliding window?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71nili$s88$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```

higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote in message
<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>...

>i never really understood the sliding window method of remediation. is
>this an example of this technique?? i understand this (it's not


Don't know what the example was, but it was not a "sliding window".
That is fairly straightforward ... to understand it consider a "fixed
window" first.  You can handle 2 digit dates quite simply by assuming
that every date with a year less than 50 is in the 21st century, while
every date with a year greater than 50 is in the 20th century.  That
gives you a "window" of dates --- from January 1, 1951 to December
31, 2050 that the code works for.

To make it a sliding window, you calculate the window based on the
current date, so the software will work in a window starting
(for example) twenty-five years ago, and ending 75 years from "now".

As the date changes, the dates that the software will handle changes,
thus the "sliding" nomenclature.  Obviously, the methodology will not
work with some applications, but for a system that managed (for example)
mortgages, given that no mortgage lasts over 35 years, it can save
having to modify all the data files for a four digit date.
```

#### ↳ Re: is this a sliding window?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-6TeR2qr69ngi@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<363E1A7D.2A010C43@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
On Mon, 2 Nov 1998 20:47:57, higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote:

> 
> i never really understood the sliding window method of remediation. is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No.  Here's some sliding Window code:

It slides with the date so it works perpetually.

Our particular data will have no individuals under 13 in the data.  
This code allows the storage of birthyear in 2 digit format with 
individuals of approx 13-126 being handled without error.

--- some names chaged to disquise the purpose of this code ---

 MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:8) TO J-DATE           
 MOVE J-DATE (1:4) TO BASE-YEAR                       
 SUBTRACT 13 FROM BASE-YEAR                           
 MOVE xxx-RESP-DOB(NDX-RESP)    TO BIR-DATE.          
 MOVE BD-YR TO WORK-YY                                
 MOVE J-DATE (1:2) TO WORK-CC                         
 IF WORK-YEAR > BASE-YEAR                             
    SUBTRACT 100 FROM WORK-YEAR                       
    IF WORK-YEAR > BASE-YEAR                          
       SUBTRACT 100 FROM WORK-YEAR                    
    END-IF
 END-IF                                               
 MOVE WORK-YEAR TO BIRTH-YEAR                         
 MOVE BD-MM TO BIRTH-MONTH                            
 MOVE BD-DD TO BIRTH-DAY                              
 IF BIRTH-DAY = ZEROS OR BIRTH-DAY > 31               
    MOVE 01 TO BIRTH-DAY                                     
 END-IF                                                      
 COMPUTE TEMP-INTEGER = FUNCTION                             
                        INTEGER-OF-DATE (W-BIRTH-DATE)       
 COMPUTE BIRTH-JD = FUNCTION DAY-OF-INTEGER (TEMP-INTEGER)
```

## Thread: Wang VS e-zine — 5 msgs

### Wang VS e-zine

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantF1s1xK.D87@netcom.com>`

```

To anyone who's ever been in the same room with a
Wang VS computer:


I don't know if I've made an official mention of this
before, but, there's an e-zine for Wang VS systems...

   www.HotVS.com

It's been around for about four months and will be 
filling the void left by the demise of Access to Wang.

Any old Wangers are invited to stop in and see that yes, 
Wang is still in business.  In fact, they're introducing 
a new processor -- the CP18 -- that blows the doors off of 
all prior Wang processors.  Realistically a 1000-user machine.

To see what else is out there try the portal address...

   www.hotvs.com/portal

for links to other Wang-related sites around the world.

Did you know that there's even a Web Server that runs
on a VS?  Not a PC connected to a VS... *on* a VS!

Give it a spin:  

   www.phoenix.net/~tjunker/server-test-203494.html 


In the next few months I'm going to be writing on ways
to tune your applications.... ways to write Cobol so that
your programs get the most out of every CPU and I/O cycle.
```

#### ↳ Re: Wang VS e-zine

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8F92CA407FD22ECB.0726B2090433E35D.C180A1332275DA21@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<dbryantF1s1xK.D87@netcom.com>`

```
David K. Bryant wrote:
> 
> To anyone who's ever been in the same room with a
> Wang VS computer:
> 


I loved the VS--the most under-marketed general-purpose computer.  How
many times did us VS'ers hear the phrase, "Oh, Wang.  You mean it does
something else besides word processing?"  

We migrated off the VS in the bleak years when Wang was abandoning their
customer base and looked to be going down the tubes.  I still look back
nostalgically at what an incredibly integrated, easy-to-use,
easy-to-administer machine it was.  Nice to know things are looking
up...
```

<a id="t-ebdfbabdf3"></a>
## Thread: INCLUDE statement: Y2K Compliance made easier — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### INCLUDE statement: Y2K Compliance made easier

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71llop$bn0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Back in June, there was a discussion of INCLUDE statements of:

  INCLUDE COND=(184,4,CH,GT,C'9212',AND,184,4,CH,LT,C'9302') or
  INCLUDE COND=(184,4,ZD,GT,9212,AND,184,4,ZD,LT,9302)

... which needed to be changed for Y2K compliance.

A solution with Y2C was suggested which people were not happy with because it
was too complicated and unreadable.

DFSORT was listening and we now have a simple, readable solution with our new
full date formats, available via PTFs for DFSORT R13 and DFSORT R14.  To wit:

  INCLUDE COND=(184,4,Y2T,GT,Y'9212',AND,184,4,Y2T,LT,Y'9302')

The specified fixed or sliding century window will be used for the Y2T format
fields and the Y-constants.

There are 6 new full date formats (Y2T, Y2U, Y2V, Y2W, Y2X and Y2Y) for CH, ZD
and PD date fields like yymmdd, yyddd, yymm, yyq, mmddyy, dddyy, mmyy and qyy.
Besides comparing full date fields to full date constants and other full date
fields, you can also sort and merge full dates, transform CH, ZD and PD full
two-digit year dates to CH and PD full four-digit year dates, use full date
formats with COBOL, and more.

For complete information on DFSORT's new generation of Year 2000 features,
visit the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com










-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: INCLUDE statement: Y2K Compliance made easier

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363E6AEB.3F6B@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<71llop$bn0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com wrote:
> 
> Back in June, there was a discussion of INCLUDE statements of:
> 
>   INCLUDE COND=(184,4,CH,GT,C'9212',AND,184,4,CH,LT,C'9302') or
>   INCLUDE COND=(184,4,ZD,GT,9212,AND,184,4,ZD,LT,9302)

By Gadfrey, that sure looks familiar!

> 
> ... which needed to be changed for Y2K compliance.

Aye, that it did.

> 
> A solution with Y2C was suggested which people were not happy with because it
> was too complicated and unreadable.

... but, sadly enough, was the only thing available at the time.

> 
> DFSORT was listening and we now have a simple, readable solution with our new
> full date formats, available via PTFs for DFSORT R13 and DFSORT R14.  To wit:
> 
>   INCLUDE COND=(184,4,Y2T,GT,Y'9212',AND,184,4,Y2T,LT,Y'9302')

How lovely... but... the installation where this was a concern (speaking
as the one who posted the original difficulty) uses SyncSort, not
DFSORT... and they are rather loathe to change packages.

Now, of course, the folks at SyncSort will probably reverse-engineer the
change and make it available... and somewhere down the line *someone*
will say 'Hey, why didn't you do *this*?'

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INCLUDE statement: Y2K Compliance made easier

- **From:** Dave Eastabrook <news@elmbronze.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R$GmrBAyZuP2EwZL@elmbronze.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<71llop$bn0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363E6AEB.3F6B@erols.com>`

```
on Mon, 2 Nov 1998 The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com> wrote
>fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com wrote:
>> 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>will say 'Hey, why didn't you do *this*?'

You're totally right.  Ask Frank if he'd give you an email [1] that says
something like "IBM and DFSORT listened to DD and put this change in but
presently SYNCSORT does not have this facility.".

Not that that will make any difference - you'll still be a dinosaur.

[1].  Dare I say a cheque [2] might be better .....

[2].  Or even a check!

Congrats to Frank and his team anyway - not every company would bother
paying any attention to postings on a newsgroup.

:Dave
```

## Thread: Ultimate Cobol Tool... — 1 msgs

### Ultimate Cobol Tool...

- **From:** Philippe Le Marchand <mkt@raincode.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363D1B9B.5B38B54@raincode.com>`

```
RainCode is now available !!!
------------------------------

Dear cobol experts,

Meet our new product and discover its incredible power.
RainCode is not just a Y2k compliance software: it is a full
COMPLIANCEWARE product, which will allow you to manage your thousands of
cobol code lines very easily.

Whether you want to get rid of unused procedure, check some field lenght
compatibility, modify your predefined coding conventions, removing calls
to unuseful labels, analyse the use of a variable troughout your
program: the limit is what you want to do.

- Auto-generated documentation with appropriate inventories
- Automatic user-defined patches
- Meeting your internal and external guidelines
- Implementing complete metrics programs
- ...

Come and check http://www.raincode.com. Numerous examples, as well as
the product's complete online help are presented.

Or send me an e-mail about your COBOL problem. RainCode can help...

More info coming soon...
```

<a id="t-95864a4cef"></a>
## Thread: Cleveland/Akron Consulting Opportunities — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Cleveland/Akron Consulting Opportunities

- **From:** "Roskos Family" <roskos@stratos.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0cu%1.15$r4.1189206@news.axxsys.net>`

```
My name is David Roskos, and I am manager of consulting for modis, Cleveland
(formerly Datacorp Business Systems, Inc.).  Like everyone else, we are in
need of experienced mainframe programmers.  Unlike everyone else, we are
looking for folks who want full-time employment (salaried positions), with
opportunities to move into other areas of programming (client-server,
client-main, ERP, and more).  If you are interested, send your resume to
roskos@datacorp.com.  You can also call me at our Cleveland headquarters at
1-800-327-3274.
Thank you.


Also, per the group's rules, the rates for our current projects vary
depending on the candidates level of experience and the requirements of the
position.  Current assignments for people with 10 or more years of
experience may be in the 40's and up.
```

<a id="t-1318f876c8"></a>
## Thread: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept — 18 this month / 25 total

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-1318f876c8)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
trblshtr@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> In article <71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> There is no such thing as a bug which can't be exterminated.

This is getting ridiculous.  Bill just loves to put a stick in a 
hornet's nest and see what trouble he can cause.  The standard is not 
intended to be a manual for users.  It is a standard to be used by 
implementors to develop compilers and whatnot.  How many of you 
complainers have actually used it while your are writing your COBOL 
code?  The more redundancy you have in it the more likely it is to be 
wrong.  The skeleton is completely redundant and has almost always had 
some errors in it, even in the last standard.  Also, in previous 
standards the order of the whole thing was somewhat random so there 
was some usefulness in having everything laid out.  In this one the 
standard is almost completely alphabetical and it is very easy to find 
any format you want.  Personally, I have never used the composite in 
any version of the standard going back to the first one.  I never 
trusted it and needed more detail than it had (I have worked on around 
14 different COBOL compilers - that is, maintaining and developing 
them).

Don Nelson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com>`

```
>>   "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>> > The skeleton is now manually duplicated, or manually updated, and will
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>intended to be a manual for users

But, Don, the Cobol vendors (probably) use the composite as
a template for *their* version of the skeleton, which *is* in the
manual. If the standards committee can't get it right, what are
the chances that the vendors can?

I would like to take issue with your statement that the standard
is only for vendors. Put the standard on the internet, make it
comprehensible, and every Cobol programmer will have access
to the holy grail. I bet you that the standards document will be
a great hit and *will* be used. The trouble in the past was
that it was almost impossible to get a copy of the standards
document. With the internet that has all changed.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mBe12.667$vt.1092341@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <71ojo3$3gl$1@news.igs.net> <71olur$sq7$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36437C2C.9552D8A7@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>Do you think that a 'linear' document on the standard would ever be
>used?

The ones available now sure do.  Since the new standard will be such a
significant change in the language, and it has been so long since there
was a new standard, it seems to me that standards documentation will be
a very hot item.  Many people are using much of the new stuff already,
and it hasn't even been released yet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641E5FA.2E00@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <71ojo3$3gl$1@news.igs.net> <364096E5.5749@compaq.com> <36409c73.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Don Nelson wrote in message <364096E5.5749@compaq.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> use that?

The standard is a reference.  Not a user manual.  User manuals have to 
be more "user friendly".  Standards do not and should not be that way.  
They have to be technically correct and exact, which makes them hard 
to read.  Were we to say things like "when you use this one way such 
and such happens ..." the standard would be 5000 pages long and would 
be very difficult to use as in implementation guide.

Railroad track diagrams were started by IBM.  When IBM says jump 
everyone jumps no matter whether it is good or bad (at least they did 
in the old days - not so much any more).  Almost everyone I have 
talked to doesn't like them (lots of this at Tandem where we changed 
our manuals to use railroad tracks because IBM did).  The Committee 
voted that they did not like them either.  Also, you can't represent 
as much with railroad tracks as you can with the tried and true 
methods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Railroad tracks (was *YOU* need to respond ...)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71t41g$q2n@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <71ojo3$3gl$1@news.igs.net> <364096E5.5749@compaq.com> <36409c73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3641E5FA.2E00@compaq.com>`

```

Don Nelson wrote in message <3641E5FA.2E00@compaq.com>...
>Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>>
   <much snippage>

>Railroad track diagrams were started by IBM.  When IBM says jump
>everyone jumps no matter whether it is good or bad (at least they did
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


I certainly can confirm that J4 (and maybe WG4) did consider railroad tracks
and explicitly rejected them for this revision of the Standard.  Personally,
as someone who as worked with them, they are much easier to use (once you
are used to them) and are particularly useful when working with online
documentation.  Also, I have yet to see ANY syntax that can be represented
by the current COBOL-specific format but not by railroad tracks.  (However,
just FYI, the IBM COBOL manuals do NOT use one standard IBM railroad
convention - indication of default - that would make the IBM documentation
better - IMHO.)

Bottom-line: this is a non-issue for this Standard, but may well come up
again for a future Standard.
```

> 
> documentation.  Also, I have yet to see ANY syntax that can be represented

Our writer could not make them work for choice indicators.  She had to

am quite used to them and still don't like them and don't find them 
easier for anything.  But again, that is my opinion and Bill has his.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641e9fc.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <71ojo3$3gl$1@news.igs.net> <364096E5.5749@compaq.com> <36409c73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3641E5FA.2E00@compaq.com>`

```
Don Nelson wrote in message <3641E5FA.2E00@compaq.com>...
>The standard is a reference.  Not a user manual.  User manuals have to
>be more "user friendly".  Standards do not and should not be that way.
>They have to be technically correct and exact,

so do user manuals!

Most vendors actually use TWO manuals:
a Language Reference (which should be 'correct and exact')
and a User's Guide (with the blah-blah stuff).
I was only talking about the reference.

> which makes them hard to read.

since when is it true that correct and exact writing is necessarily hard
to read? It is often hard to *write*, but the power of crystal-clear prose
is immense.
In my experience muddled, vague, and inconsistent writing is harder
to read than correct and exact writing. (Depending, of course, on
what you understand by *read*; I use the word here in the sense
of "grasping what is needed to write useful Cobol programs").

>Railroad track diagrams were started by IBM.  When IBM says jump
>everyone jumps no matter whether it is good or bad (at least they did
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>voted that they did not like them either.

I actually agree with you here. I also think that the old way is better.
I was not really advocating to use rail-road tracks, just
wanted to have the *same* representation everywhere.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363F9C0A.20A6@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the holy grail. 

They have always had access to it if they really wanted it.  However, 
I've only known about a dozen that ever looked at it.  On the net or 
not, there just is not any interest.  What they want is something that 
reflects the implementation they are using.  Even if it is on the 
internet (which an older copy is and future copies will be), it is 
1000 pages.  Not all that easy to read.

> I bet you that the standards document will be
> a great hit and *will* be used.

As I said above, I doubt it very much.  So far almost no one has 
bothered to look at the drafts.

> The trouble in the past was
> that it was almost impossible to get a copy of the standards
> document.

It was really easy.  I provided lots of people with the numbers and 
all to do so, but almost none of them followed up (they probably felt 
that it was too expensive or something like that).

> With the internet that has all changed.

Not really.  Giant documents are not all that easy to use on the net.  
I might mention that if you want the C++ standard from the net, you 
have to pay $18.  When the COBOL standard is finally approved I'm sure 
something like that will apply.  ISO will own the copyright and I 
doubt if they will make it available for free.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The new standards document ( Was *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363fbce2.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <363F9C0A.20A6@compaq.com>`

```
You are correct to state that no-one wades through a 1000-page
LINEAR document. Maybe I'm not old-fashioned enough, but
I truly envisioned a MODERN look and feel to the document.

The new standard will guide us for the next 10-15 years.
It should be a hypertext document with lots of internal cross-
references. An example is the HTML 4.0 specification at
http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-html40.

Other examples are:
http://imatix.com/html/sfl/index.htm
http://www.etk.com/etkfweb/etkfweb.htm

Imagine that you could click on a  word in a list of Cobol reserved
words and instantly be shown the definition of that word.
Everywhere within the definition another concept or Cobol word
is used, it in turn can be clicked on to show what it is and so on.

Clarifying examples might be included, or added at any
time (I know some standards people will shudder at this,
but the hyperlinks just extends the original document -
and don't alter it)

This newsgroup is full of questions as "what does the standard
say about this or that", so people have interests in that. Even
the vendors are beginning to be standards minded (e.g.. IBM).

There is backlash against proprietary extensions. The "write
once, run anywhere" strategy is valid for Cobol as well.

As a hypertext, the standard could be a sort of the Talmud of
Cobol, with the sacred standard text extended by layers and
layers of commentary, notes, tips, and links to vendors, and
such like.

The internet and the hypertext medium are powerful ways
of bringing the gospel to the masses.

I would like to propose a vote. I'm starting three new threads:
1:  I would use a hypertext Cobol standards document
2:  I don't care about a hypertext Cobol standards document.
3:  I only read what my vendor feeds me, if even that

People then vote by posting a simple remark to the thread that
matches their opinion.

Don, I'm only describing a dream. I would like to see you guys
finish the damn thing. Then make the text available and I'll
personally (this may be the sticking point - copyright and
all that) see to it that the hypertext version be produced.

---------------------------------------------
Don Nelson wrote in message <363F9C0A.20A6@compaq.com>...
>Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>No clever quotes here
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71orm5$ood@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <363F9C0A.20A6@compaq.com>`

```
This thread has wondered a bit (so what else is new). I think that all the
readers in this NG, do NOT want the next Standard delayed any more than it
already has been.  However, I think there are two issues that have come up
that I think really need to get communicated to the Standards committees.
If you agree with either (or both) of the following comments, PLEASE send a
note to both the J4 chair and the WG4 convenor at:

   Ann Wallace (WG4 convenor)          NWALLACE@us.ibm.com
   Don Schricker (J4 Chair)                  doncobol@mediaone.net

1) The Standard is NOT just for vendors as an implementation guide.  It is
used by programmers, text writers, and those trying to understand and
promote portable COBOL development.

2) The composite language skeleton is useful as both a syntax summary of
Standard COBOL and as a guide to implementors as to what THEIR summaries
should have.

   ****
As you can tell from one of Don Nelson's earlier posts, I have a reputation
as a "trouble maker" and so my input may well be discounted.  However, if
other COBOL interested people DO communicate to the committees on these
points, I think it may have some impact.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *YOU* need to respond - if you want to insure that the "composite language skeleton" gets kept

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0KV%1.6$Tk.18323@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<71ari8$t8d@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <71da37$nn7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <363F5E6B.624D@compaq.com> <363f61a9.0@news1.ibm.net> <363F9C0A.20A6@compaq.com>`

```
Don, I can't help but think that the reason for seeming lack of interest
is simply that the actual standard document has been so restricted in
circulation for so long that programmers just aren't used to thinking in
terms of having it available.  Personally I would have *loved* to have
had one available all these years.  I finally bought the COBOL 85 doc
and the two addenda a few months ago, and it cost me $200!  Need I say
more about availability?  You would know the truth better than I, but
there appears to me to be a mindset in the standards culture that looks
a lot like elitism.  Forgive me if this is not actually true, but the
effect is very similar, in any case.  As far as I can tell, completely
across the board there seems zilch interest in ANSI at actually getting
the information into the hands of the programmers.  When I tried to get
a copy of the COBOL 85 standard, the access to documents on their web
site is from the dark ages.  Probably to someone intimately familiar
with the standards process, community and terminology, it would have
made sense.  But it presented a formidable, unfriendly interface for
anyone else.  When I finally figured out 'probably' which documents I
wanted and called them, they seemed surprised that I wanted to order
something, and were not completely sure they were prepared to take my
order.  I had to make two orders before it took.  This is *not* the way
to encourage interest in your product.  It reminded me of a typical
care-less government agency or monopoly business.  And compiler vendors
should be very much secondary to programmers in standards.  It is the
programmers who matter.  Who do you think is the most important in
medical standards, the manufacturers or doctors?  Sure, the medical
manufacturers need to know, but if the doctors don't set the pace and
are not solidly in the information loop, something is wrong.  We need
a new mindset from the standards organizations that (for programming
standards at least) see the programmer as the primary final client.
As long as the average programmer gets the kind of response that I
experienced, you are not going to see much enthusiasm.  To you, being
familiar with the process, the people and the orginization, it may
seem easy.  But from the other side, it just ain't so. :-)
```

---

Of course there is another option:  PERCobol ( www.synkronix.com ).

PERCobol is COBOL compiler that enables COBOL to run on the JVM.  It also
allows for easy integration between COBOL and Java.  From what I know,
Synkronix will support OO Cobol in the near future.

David
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** "Charlie Ebert" <kd5ob@theshop.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EwO%1.145$J42.475@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <363839e4.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk> <363ce8ff.5788326@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
My opinion on OOP.

Object Oriented Programming is merely a technique developed for the C world
which was intended to make a relatively UN-structured language MORE
structured.

In the COBOL world, we already got structured coding perfected man.

So we are just putting OOP in there in an attempt to get college professors
and the younger kids to learn COBOL.

OOP and GUI are similar in the aspect that they run hand in hand,,,, so to
speak...
That is unless your careful.

We will talk about BLOBS in the next chapter...

Charlie

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K3R%1.205$LY1.21@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <363839e4.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk> <363ce8ff.5788326@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <EwO%1.145$J42.475@newsfeed.slurp.net> <HAO%1.152$J42.1107@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```

Charlie Ebert wrote in message ...
>I always forget to ADD something also!!!
>
>I tried MS and Borlands examples of OOP in C.

It is likely that you mean C++ since the C Programming Language
has no OOP concepts in the language.

[...]
>Charlie
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Object Oriented Programming is merely a technique developed for the C
world
>>which was intended to make a relatively UN-structured language MORE
>>structured.

It is generally acknowledged that Object Orientation began with Simula.
Simula pre-dates C by about 9 years and C has no OOP concepts in
the language. Your opinion that OOP was "... a technique developed
for C ..." has no basis in fact.

>>
>>In the COBOL world, we already got structured coding perfected man.
>>
>>So we are just putting OOP in there in an attempt to get college
professors
>>and the younger kids to learn COBOL.

On the other hand, those responsible for millions of lines of code might
believe that the possible reuse of some of that code (through inheritance)
might be sufficient justification.

>>
>>OOP and GUI are similar in the aspect that they run hand in hand,,,, so to
>>speak...
>>That is unless your careful.

Since a Graphical User Interface may be written or implemented in a
non-OOP language, I fail to understand your point.

>>
>>We will talk about BLOBS in the next chapter...
>>
>>Charlie
>>
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364069CB.C73CAF40@home.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <363839e4.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk> <363ce8ff.5788326@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <EwO%1.145$J42.475@newsfeed.slurp.net> <HAO%1.152$J42.1107@newsfeed.slurp.net> <K3R%1.205$LY1.21@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
> >>In the COBOL world, we already got structured coding perfected man.

Structured and OO are not at all the same thing.

> >>So we are just putting OOP in there in an attempt to get college
> professors and the younger kids to learn COBOL.

It doesn't work.  COBOL is a poor tool for OO, it is a fine tool for
creating stand-alone applications though.

> On the other hand, those responsible for millions of lines of code might
> believe that the possible reuse of some of that code (through inheritance)
> might be sufficient justification.
 
Designing an effective, reusable OO and an environment where such can be
well maintaned is expensive.  So you had better re-use the code to get a
payback.  In real life, there is very little reuse of in-house code,
much less than 50%.

> >>OOP and GUI are similar in the aspect that they run hand in hand,,,, so to
> >>speak...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-OOP language, I fail to understand your point.

And it is quite possible that you can have OO without a GUI.  Since both
techniques are coming out simultaneously, and often within the same
tools, people confuse them.
```

wrote:

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Let me say - I'm not an OOP proponant - but I think that there were 
other languages developed for OOP BEFORE C++.

I've seen some bugs in MS products that are obvious problems with 
inheritence and polymoprhism gone awry.  A whole new set of bugs is 
introduced.  But C++ is not the ONLY OO language, nor is it the best. 


> In the COBOL world, we already got structured coding perfected man.

> So we are just putting OOP in there in an attempt to get college professors
> and the younger kids to learn COBOL.

Maybe I'm being broken down, but the more I read about OO COBOL, the

> OOP and GUI are similar in the aspect that they run hand in hand,,,, so to

Not really.  I have full GUI systems that have not one bit of OO in 
them.

## Thread: using tictock on MVS — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### using tictock on MVS

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>`

```
I tested Tick tock to age dates for our cobol programs.

It aged dates retrieved by ACCEPT x FROM DATE but not MOVE FUNCTION
CURRENT-DATE.

Has anyone else had the same experience.

Ken
```

- **From:** captainpeachfuzz@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71mgaf$efo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>`

```
Ahoy there matees,

Install TICTOC's LE/370 support zap PMR. COBOL's ACCEPT issues a TIME SVC but
the FUNCTION CURRENT DATE resolves to a local time call to LE/370 services.

Full steam ahead,
The Cap'n

#### ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> I tested Tick tock to age dates for our cobol programs.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

I've had the same experience with ViaValidate from Viasoft.  The problem
seems to be that native COBOL date verbs in IBM OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL
II (and the IGZEDT4 callable routine) use SVC 11 (Supervisor Call) to
get system current date.  But the newer intrinsic function calls
actually use a PC instruction instead (Program Call).

The SVC instruction can be trapped by the date override tool, but the PC
instruction cannot be.  This problem makes it much harder to test the
intrinsic FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE.  Unless these date override tools can
be upgraded to intercept LE/MVS PC instructions, your only option is to
test on a machine or LPAR that had been IPL'ed with a future date.

I don't know if Hourglass has the same problem, but it sounds possible.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** levyconsulting@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<71nag8$flk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au> <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>,
  arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
> Ken Foskey wrote:
> >
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Although I haven't run into any usage of the newer intrinsic function calls
at the client I am now at, this client has HourGlass, and it works well for
SVC 11 calls.  The HourGlass documentation seems to indicate it would easily
handle the newer FUNCTION instructions as well.

The following is from the HourGlass web page at:

http://www.princetonsoftech.com/products/hourglass-tech.htm

Technical Specs

Getting Started

Once activated, HourGlass 2000 intercepts all requests for the date and time
made via SVC 11 or via the TIME LINKAGE=SYSTEM macro.

<note the word "all" in the last sentence>

<snipped some stuff on STCK requests - which are a whole 'nother topic>

The system date can be retrieved in numerous ways externally via utilities or
programming languages. Most programming languages provide a reserved
word or function that returns the system date in a useable format (such as
COBOL's CURRENT-DATE). However, internally there are only three
ways to retrieve this date.

The first, and by far the most common, is via a SVC 11 call. Another less
common method, used mostly by the operating system level code, is the STCK
instruction that returns the number of microseconds since January 1, 1900.
Lastly, a special format of the TIME macro (LINKAGE=SYSTEM) which issues a PC
instruction can be used. This last retrieval method is rarely used since it
wasn't available until MVS ESA Version 4.1.

<more stuff is also on this page - but you get the drift>


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** captainpeachfuzz@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<71ne31$l4b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au> <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>,
  arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:

> I've had the same experience with ViaValidate from Viasoft.  The problem
> seems to be that native COBOL date verbs in IBM OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL
> II (and the IGZEDT4 callable routine) use SVC 11 (Supervisor Call) to
> get system current date.  But the newer intrinsic function calls
> actually use a PC instruction instead (Program Call).

COBOL/370 or COBOL/MVS date/time functions do not use the TIME LINKAGE=SYSTEM
PC call. It's either LINKAGE=SVC for ACCEPT or LE/370 for CURRENT DATE, etc.
LE/370 itself also does not issue a TIME LINKAGE=SYSTEM PC call. It issues an
STCK instruction.

> The SVC instruction can be trapped by the date override tool, but the PC
> instruction cannot be.

Don't know about Validate or other products. We tested TICTOC and it traps
both TIME SVC and PC calls.

>  This problem makes it much harder to test the
> intrinsic FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE.  Unless these date override tools can
> be upgraded to intercept LE/MVS PC instructions, your only option is to
> test on a machine or LPAR that had been IPL'ed with a future date.

Like I said in a previous post, we applied a simple zap to the STCK
instruction and all works fine here - COBOL and PLI.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<3640049D.EC4067B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au> <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net> <71ne31$l4b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
captainpeachfuzz@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> In article <363EB665.58788A2A@worldnet.att.net>,
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> instruction and all works fine here - COBOL and PLI.

I want to thank all the people who corrected my misinformation.  I
leaped to an incorrect conclusion.

In early 1997 I was playing around with an assembler date program to get
current date with 4-digit year.  I'm much better at COBOL than
assembler, and I chose to use a TIME macro with LINKAGE=SYSTEM, because
it was easier to convert the results into zoned decimal numbers.  From
the assembly, I knew that LINKAGE=SYSTEM translated into a PC
instruction rather than an SVC 11.  And I found that ViaValidate would
NOT override the my assembler program, but WOULD override the date for a
plain old COBOL II program, or an assembler program with an SVC 11
instruction.

Several months later I finally had an opportunity to play with
COBOL/MVS.  I wrote a small program that used FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE as
well as the older ACCEPT DATE.  I found that ViaValidate could override
the ACCEPT date, but not FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE!  The same program, using
two different methods, got two very different "current" dates.

My mistake was leaping to the conclusion that FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE used
a PC instruction.  I don't know if the most current version of
ViaValidate can override a PC instruction or handle STCK.

Even though there may be some problems, you can still do some useful
unit-testing with date override simulators.  But you can only test your
applications this way, not your underlying operating systems software.
```

#### ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71mdkb$4iu@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>`

```
The following reply from one of my sources is quite similar to the one from
Arnold.  I think it sort-of explains the problem.

"I do not have access to a system with Tick Tock, but can make the
following comments....

The common time shifter routines insert themselves in the SVC 11 path in
the nucleus.  The products differ primarily in security, features (i.e.
does time freeze / move, does date march correctly with virtual time,
etc), selectivity, and so on.  By intercepting the SVC 11's they should
be able to handle "well behaved" programs which reference time and date
values thru the SVC 11 / TIME LINKAGE=SYSTEM.  OS/VS COBOL does all of it's
time reference thru module ILBODTE, which uses the SVC 11
mechanism.

VS COBOL II also uses the SVC 11 interface, in module IGZEDTE.

LE related functions, including the MOVE FUNCTION xxxx use the
SToreClocK hardware instruction to obtain the system date.  The SVC 11
interceptor cannot grab this - although it can grab TIME FORMAT=STCK...
a closely related MVS service.  Some of the time shifters have specific
patches to the LE module containing the STCK instruction, changing it to
a TIME FORMAT=STCK they can then deal correctly with the LE time/date
related functions.  Messy, but allows the LE code to avoid issuing
SVC's.  By doing this, the LE routines must also deal with the current
GMT offset and need to dig in the CVT to get this.

Bottom line if MOVE FUNCTION is not shifted, either the product is low
end (and does not support LE), or the (ECHHHHHHHH) modification to LE
has not been installed."



Ken Foskey wrote in message <363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>...
>I tested Tick tock to age dates for our cobol programs.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: using tictock on MVS

- **From:** "John Mycroft" <johnm@mycroft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71qc2q$h14$1@news.iconz.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<363DAD5C.3DF8DB08@zip.com.au>`

```
Gday Ken
I see from your email address that you're in Ozzie so, as the Tictoc
distributor for Oz & NZ, I guess I should answer.  Tictoc has no problem at
all with anything that uses SVC 11, TIME, STCKSYNC, LINKAGE=SYSTEM, CICS
ASKTIME, DB2 CURRENT-TIMESTAMP or anything else.  The one thing that shafts
every date simulator is programs that use the STCK instruction as this
doesn't generate an interrupt for the simulator to trap.  This is easily
solved by replacing the STCK with Tictoc's SVC which then can pass control
to Tictoc's simulation routines.  It is a simple zap, overlaying the first
two bytes of the STCK with the SVC instruction - you don't have to extend or
re-link anything.

We provide zaps for about half a dozen products that need this support
(including LE370, LE390, DB2 and some 3rd party stuff) as well as supplying
a macro that you can assemble into any assembler programs you have that use
STCK.  They are all documented in the installation manual, they are on the
install tape and we have them available for emailing if required.

So you're wrong to say that Tictoc doesn't support those verbs - it supports
every COBOL verb and reserved word in both batch and online, inclduing when
DB2 is involved.  Where were you working when you tested Tictoc?  We never
heard from you at the time and I'd like to make sure that the company in
question is not labouring under some incorrect impression about a Y2K LPAR
being an adequate solution to date testing.

Cheers - John Mycroft
www.mycroft.com.au
```

---

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981103160021.24009.00003275@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<71n933$f64$1@supernews.com>`

```
"jeff" <tinman@ync.net>
Date: Tue, 3 Nov 1998 10:00:02 -0600

listed code 
>>
       1500-CREATE-REPORT SECTION.
       1600-CREATE-REPORT.
            OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
            MOVE "N" TO EOF-FLAG
            RETURN EMPSORT-WORK-FILE
            PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "Y"
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD1 AFTER PAGE
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD2 AFTER 5
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD3 AFTER 10
                PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG ="Y"

                MOVE EWR-NAME TO DL-NAME
                MOVE EWR-MM TO DL-BD-MM
                MOVE EWR-DD TO DL-BD-DD
                MOVE EWR-YY TO DL-BD-YY
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1
<<
and commented
>>.
    im write a sort program for school for a lab and i keep get a =
"imperative statement missing when i tell it to perform until eof-flag =
= "y"
    i move "n" to the eof-flag after it read all my records then =
released then to my sort work file
    thanx for the help
<<

With the listed code, the most likely statement to be needing an imperative
statement is the OPEN.

The PRINT-FILE probably already exists, due to previous attempted runs, or
cannot be created (because of failure to meet the SELECT / ASSIGN statement
requirements).

You are learning a good lessson. You will return to the issue a million times. 
If you code an I/O statement of any kind, you definitely need a status code
check. OPEN, RETURN, WRITE all need specific checks.   

You definitely need checks after each and every WRITE.  You will find your self
doing MOVES and performing a common WRITE routine soon, to reduce the number of
WRITE/check status statement sequences.

You need to check the status after every I/O, whether the I/O is performed by
your native COBOL code directly, or by IMS, DB2 (or any SQL) or CICS.  You can
not assume that any initial function like OPEN, just works. You must check it. 
The same is true for CLOSE!

I agree with the other poster who commented to the effect that  a
                PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG ="Y"
within a 
           PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "Y"
is highly suspicious.

An advanced tip here is that it is usually weak to PERFORM until a specific
value.  Instead it is better to PERFORM until not a specific value.
For example,
     MOVE 'N' to EOF-FLAG
     PERFORM UNTIL NOT EOF-FLAG="N" 
This way you can post other 'not okay' values in the flag (like "E" for
unexpected Error), and shunt the loop.  Also allows the program to stop as a
side effect of damaged working storage (such a bug in population of flag or a
wild subscript).

With time you will learn to establish a FILE STATUS field associated with each
file.  You can initialize this, and set up a group of acceptable values as an
88 level. Then perform until not acceptable-condition.  Then anything
unexpected or otherwise educational will halt the program. Recommend that you
use the structure init-okay/perform until NOT okay for everything as a first
approach.

With the amount of I/O you are undertaking, and it is ambitious for an early
program, you would do well to modularize more.  Generally, and this is not
intended to start a grand style debate, just a way to get you out of trouble
for a while, every I/O statement can have its own paragraph.

A classical techinque, called HIPO, isolates each specific Input, Process and
Output to a separately performed paragraph, (not that you have to become an old
geezer, just to code COBOL, but it helps to do one thing at a time).

This approach will help you when you move on to the large scale I/O support
facilities.  Error handling in CICS, DB2 and IMS can get very involved because
a lot can happen each time you let go of control of the executable environment.
 It is much more so today than in the past that the service modules will pass
control back to you rather than abend. So you need to not only understand many
error conditions, but you need to be very fluid in your ability to structure
the program to deal with the unexpected.

Generally, if you have an I/O statement, and you do not follow it with a status
check (and there are no old-style declaratives), then that code is essentially
wrong. You have got to keep coding the status code checks. 

It will be much easier to code and debug I/O paragraphs if you keep all of the
processing of the buffer (MOVES to/from the buffer) isolated to it's own
conditionally performed paragraph.

As an aside, you probably would benefit from the use of explicit scope
terminators.  If you code an inline PERFORM, use an END-PERFORM.  It will help
those who read your code to understand it, and you are the most likely one to
read it.

Best Wishes, 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71o4u3$4s9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71m8i7$18g$1@supernews.com> <71n933$f64$1@supernews.com>`

```

jeff wrote in message <71n933$f64$1@supernews.com>...
its me i read the replys and u way that u need to see the code so here it is

It is a bit hard to know where to start ... the code that you
have shown makes very little sense.  But let's start with
some basics.

The flag that you have named EOF-FLAG never gets changed
to "Y" anywhere in your code sample at all.  Whatever else is
wrong, the code would run forever.  The norm is something along
the line of:

    open input data-file.
    move "N" to EOF-FLAG.
    read data-file at end
        move "Y" to EOF-FLAG
        do something about an empty file here.
    perform write-report until EOF-FLAG is equal to "Y".

write-report.
    do a bunch of writing here.
    read data-file at end
        move "Y" to EOF-FLAG.


There is also no termination anywhere below, which is probably
what is causing you to get the error message.  Sentences end
in periods.  There are none below.  It never ends. The in-line
perform also needs a terminator.

       1500-CREATE-REPORT SECTION.


       1600-CREATE-REPORT.
            OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
            MOVE "N" TO EOF-FLAG
            RETURN EMPSORT-WORK-FILE
            PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "Y"
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD1 AFTER PAGE
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD2 AFTER 5
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM HEAD3 AFTER 10
                PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "Y"

                MOVE EWR-NAME TO DL-NAME
                MOVE EWR-MM TO DL-BD-MM
                MOVE EWR-DD TO DL-BD-DD
                MOVE EWR-YY TO DL-BD-YY
                WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1






    jeff wrote in message <71m8i7$18g$1@supernews.com>...
    im write a sort program for school for a lab and i keep get a
"imperative statement missing when i tell it to perform until eof-flag = "y"
    i move "n" to the eof-flag after it read all my records then released
then to my sort work file

    thanx for the help
    jeff :)
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<71m8i7$18g$1@supernews.com> <71n933$f64$1@supernews.com> <71o4u3$4s9$1@news.igs.net>`

```

…[52 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-99345a7e7b"></a>
## Thread: Accept statement in COBOL370 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** rwaltei1@nycap.rr.ocm (Rich)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
Is it possible to get century by using an ACCEPT statement, or any
other method, in COBOL370.  Our shop recently installed OS/390 and
COBOL370. I find it hard to believe that with this recent of a
compiler,  I am still recieving a six-byte year from an ACCEPT FROM
DATE statement. Thanks.

Rich
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363f926e.745431@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
On Tue, 03 Nov 1998 23:20:45 GMT, rwaltei1@nycap.rr.ocm (Rich) wrote:

>Is it possible to get century by using an ACCEPT statement, or any
>other method, in COBOL370.  Our shop recently installed OS/390 and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Rich

Rich,
Check your documentation. the ACCEPT verb with cobol/mvs or os/390 has
multiple syntax options -- including those with 4-digit years.  there
are also many intrinsic functions for Y2K windowing available. This
compiler is fully Y2K compliant and provides full Y2K facilities for
programmers.    
good luck, 
david
p.s.  yes, the standard ACCEPT format still returns 6 digit dates for
compatibility....
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71o695$hk0$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```


Rich wrote:
> 
> Is it possible to get century by using an ACCEPT statement, or any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rich


Try the following, MOVE FUNCTION WHEN-COMPILED TO curr-date.
Where curr-date is Pic x(21).

Where WHEN-COMPILED is PIC X(21) and it contains:
yyyymmddhhmmsstt+hhmm.
yyyy = year
mm   = month
dd   = day
hh   = hour
mm   = minute
ss   = seconds
tt   = hundredths of seconds
+hhmmm  = Hours & Minutes ahead/behind GMT
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71og1v$96b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
In article <363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>,
  rwaltei1@nycap.rr.ocm (Rich) wrote:
> Is it possible to get century by using an ACCEPT statement, or any
> other method, in COBOL370.  Our shop recently installed OS/390 and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Rich,

With COBOL/370 you would use the CURRENT-DATE FUNCTION which will return the
following information in a 21-Byte area:

CCYYMMDD    ===> Bytes 01-08 (Current-Date as CCYYMMDD)
HHMMSSTH    ===> Bytes 09-16 (Current-Time as HHMMSSTH)
'+' or '-'  ===> Bytes 17-17 (Ahead or Behind GMT)
GMT Offset  ===> Bytes 18-21 (HHMM off GMT)

03  WS-CURRENT-DATE PIC X(21).

MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-CURRENT-DATE.

Also, check out:

03  WS-DATE-CCYYMMDD PIC 9(08).

CALL 'IGZEDT4' USING WS-DATE-CCYYMMDD.

You may also want to check out the following Web Page address for IBM Book
Manager ===> http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/library

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71orm6$ood@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com> <71og1v$96b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
What is included in WOB's note is correct HOWEVER, when using any of the
COBOL for this-or-that compilers (but not the VS COBOL II compiler) using

    Accept my-8-digit-date from Date YYYYMMDD

is preferred over a call to IGZEDT4 (which will work - but is not intended
as a permanent solution).

WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote in message <71og1v$96b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com>,
>  rwaltei1@nycap.rr.ocm (Rich) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>With COBOL/370 you would use the CURRENT-DATE FUNCTION which will return
the
>following information in a 21-Byte area:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Manager ===>
http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/library
>
>HTH....
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accept statement in COBOL370

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71pc8o$974$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<363f8e76.1358555@news.nycap.rr.com> <71og1v$96b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <71orm6$ood@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <71orm6$ood@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> What is included in WOB's note is correct HOWEVER, when using any of the
> COBOL for this-or-that compilers (but not the VS COBOL II compiler) using
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as a permanent solution).

<snip the rest>

Sorry to contradict your statement. I fully agree witch yout vote not to use
IGZEDT4, but the 'DATE YYYYMMDD' is not known in all IBM COBOL for this and
that until the newest one. The COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R1 is the first IBM
compiler whitch accepts this form of accept.

See the COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming Guide:

--- paste from manual ---

4.7.7.5  Accept with 4 Digit Year             � Copyright IBM Corp. 1991

  The Working Draft for Proposed Revision of ISO 1989:1985 Programming
  Language COBOL defines an extension to the Format 2 ACCEPT statement to
  return 4 digits for the year.  Two optional keywords are added to return
  DATE or DAY to return dates in the format YYYYMMDD and YYYYDDD
  respectively.  For example:

      ACCEPT today FROM DATE YYYYMMDD

  would return "19970807" and

      ACCEPT today FROM DAY YYYYDDD

  would return "1997219" for August 7, 1997.

  See IBM COBOL Language Reference for complete syntax details.

--- end of paste from manual ---

Even the recent IBM COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2 does not know this form of accept,
but the FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE works fine:

--- paste from compile listing ---

PP 5688-197 IBM COBOL for MVS and VM   1.2.1
  000001                IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
  000002                  PROGRAM-ID. LECOBOL.
  000003                DATA DIVISION.
  000004                 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  000005                 01 MY-8-DIGIT-DATE PIC X(08) VALUE SPACES.

  000006                 LINKAGE SECTION.
  000007                PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  000008                     ACCEPT MY-8-DIGIT-DATE FROM DATE YYYYMMDD

==000008==> IGYPS2072-S "YYYYMMDD" was invalid.  Skipped to the next verb,
                        period or procedure-name definition.

  000009                     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO MY-8-DIGIT-DATE
  000010                     DISPLAY MY-8-DIGIT-DATE
  000011                     GOBACK.
  000012                END PROGRAM LECOBOL.
--- end of paste from compile listing ---

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-1ea1f60443"></a>
## Thread: Display Statements Cause Program To End — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Display Statements Cause Program To End

- **From:** "Doug Blaze" <dblaze@merchants-fla.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GyJ%1.480$7_.220069912@newsreader.digex.net>`

```
I have an application that is written in MF 4.0.32.  It was developed on a
NT 4.0 machine and later installed on a Windows 95 machine. The NT machine
has the MF Cobol installed on it where the Win95 does not.  I added some
DISPLAY statements to monitor some values and it works fine on the NT
machine (a DOS window pops us with the display information is in it).
However, on the Win95 machine, the program just disappears as soon as it
hits the DISPLAY.  I have to assume that there is something that is
running/installed on the NT machine that isn't on the 95 machine.  Compile
and linking are fine.

For what it's worth, I created a very simple program that has one paragraph,
a display statement and a stop run.  I'm getting back "Error 107".  The book
says "Operation not implemented in this run-time system".  Any ideas?  It
runs fine on the NT machine.
Any ideas?

Thanks,
Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: Display Statements Cause Program To End

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71p8to$bo1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<GyJ%1.480$7_.220069912@newsreader.digex.net>`

```

Doug Blaze wrote in message ...
>I have an application that is written in MF 4.0.32.  It was developed on a
>NT 4.0 machine and later installed on a Windows 95 machine. The NT machine
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>For what it's worth, I created a very simple program that has one
paragraph,
>a display statement and a stop run.  I'm getting back "Error 107".  The
book
>says "Operation not implemented in this run-time system".  Any ideas?  It
>runs fine on the NT machine.
>Any ideas?

Doug, I suspect that you do not have MFSCREEN.DLL on the 95 machine. It is
required for screen and keyboard handling.

Incidentally, chapter 25 of the Object COBOL User Guide has details of the
files you need to ship with an application. If you have any additional
problems it may be worth referring to that.

I hope this helps.



>
>Thanks,
>Doug
>
>
```

---

Why is this book so popular?  The coding examples are so BORING! 
Compare his table program with that in COBOL Unleashed and COBOL for
OS/370 - Power programming, which I received in the mail, and don't even
remember ordering, but I'm glad I did.  I can't use COBOL/370 where I
work, but they're gonna give me access to the compiler in Washington, a
library and files to play with.  I can't wait!  And yes, I had a life
once, but I didn't like it.  

And they're teaching this book in some schools instead of regular
textbooks.  So, in addition to instructors who tell the students that
they HAVE to take COBOL even though it's only used anymore by Old Guys,
and won't be around for very long, they're stuck with a boring book.

<a id="t-dccaed7376"></a>
## Thread: DFSORT Y2K: Full Date Formats for COBOL and More — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### DFSORT Y2K: Full Date Formats for COBOL and More

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71ljgq$8ca$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
DFSORT's new generation of Year 2000 features handle CH, ZD and PD
full dates like yymmdd, yyddd, yymm, yyq, mmddyy, dddyy, mmyy and qyy,
and their special indicators like zeros and nines. You no longer have
to split dates into year and non-year pieces, and you don't need
E61 exits to handle special indicators.  You can now compare dates to
constants and other dates, transform CH, ZD and PD two-digit year
dates to CH and PD four-digit year dates and more!

This new generation of Year 2000 features is available for Release 13
via PTF UQ22533 and for Release 14 via PTF UQ22534 (the APAR is
PQ19684).

Our updated SORT2000 paper explains how to use DFSORT's new full date
formats, as well as the earlier year formats, in the SORT, MERGE,
INCLUDE, OMIT and OUTFIL statements.  You can look up a particular
date and go directly to examples of the DFSORT control statements
you need.

SORT2000 also explains how to use DFSORT's new Year 2000 features with
COBOL, either automatically with COBOL MLE or explicitly without MLE.

You can browse or download SORT2000 from the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com





-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

---
