<a id="t-8cb1e12ce7"></a>
## Thread: ACCEPT x FROM DATE issue — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: ACCEPT x FROM DATE issue

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <pc@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71mgvp$nd@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<1997Jan15.171737.14661@RedBrick.COM> <kudra.853354119@hubcap>`

```
The latest Micro Focus products (3.4, 4.0 and NetExpress) support the new
ACCEPT x FROM DATE syntax which will return a four digit year.  The
syntax is as follows:

...
ACCEPT x FROM DATE YYYYMMDD.
...

Where x is a PIC X(8) or equivalent.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Team Leader, Distributed Computing Support (WinTel)
Micro Focus International.

sallie j kudra wrote in message ...
>phorn@linkline.com (Paul Horn) writes:
>
>>Anyone have a good workaround for the fact that the ACCEPT identifier FROM
>>DATE command gives only two digits for the year? I'm coding some routines
to
>>timestamp output files and would feel sort of stupid if they "expire" in
three
>>years.
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>===========================================================================
=====
>  Think of me what you will          | Sallie Kudra, Applications Analyst
II
>  I've got a little space to fill... | ISD, Clemson University, Clemson, SC
>                 Tom Petty           | kudra@hubcap.clemson.edu
>===========================================================================
=====
```

## Thread: External program call from Netexpressprg — 4 msgs

### External program call from Netexpressprg

- **From:** BOSsysteme@t-online.de (BOSsysteme)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71pp58$ppv$1@news01.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hello all,

i'm new to this group and I think that my question is a very easy one,
procedure and failure report follows:

I'm writing an application in MF-Cobol/NetExpress 2.0 (Win95/98/NT) from
within that app I call a program written in Borland-Delphi 4 using these
lines:

01  command-li        pic x(8) value "delphi.exe"
01  command-line-len  pic x(4) comp-5 value 8.
01  run-unit-id       pic x(8) comp-5 value 0.
01  stack-size        pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
01  flags             pic x(4) comp-5 value 1.
01  tty-cmd           pic x(4) value space.
01  tty-cmd-len       pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
01  status-code       pic xx comp-5.

call "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT"   using command-li
                           by value command-line-len
                           by reference run-unit-id
                           by value stack-size
                                    flags
                           by reference tty-cmd
                           by value     tty-cmd-len
                           returning status-code.


This works up to here but when I perform a ShellExecute from within the
Delphi-Exe the program halts.

Killing the Netexpressprg (by Taskmangager e.g.) the Delphi Exe and the
second called program work fine.
If I start the Delphiprg with 'dubbleclick' it works perfecly.

Another problem in the same situation is that it seems the window of the
calling Netexpressprg is not been updated anymore after the call to the
Delphi-Exe.


Any suggestions, any help would be appreciated.

- What does my call in detail?
- Is there a better way to call external executables?


Thank you in advance.

Florian
```

- **References:** `<71pp58$ppv$1@news01.btx.dtag.de>`

```

8<

> 01  command-li        pic x(8) value "delphi.exe"

Display command-li would show "delphi.e"
The Frog.
```

<a id="t-9c9b0906f4"></a>
## Thread: Question on technique — 42 msgs

### Question on technique

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hello to all!

I have a question.  Is it a good programming practice to check for a
subscript value to be within a valid range (i.e.: If sub > 0 and sub not >
max-value....)? or should I "assume" that the data that calculates the
subscript is always going to be valid and if it is not, can I "blame" the
data?

Thanks for your input.

Chris



-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71puhs$ims$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,  <maxomtech@wiscasset.net> wrote:
>Hello to all!
>
>I have a question.  Is it a good programming practice to check for a
>subscript value to be within a valid range (i.e.: If sub > 0 and sub not >
>max-value....)?

Yes.

>or should I "assume" that the data that calculates the
>subscript is always going to be valid and if it is not, can I "blame" the
>data?

Comes to you a telephone-call at 3:am for a blown-up program do you
*really* care about 'blame'?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kgPdHNCxlQ1g@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Wed, 4 Nov 1998 15:28:05, maxomtech@wiscasset.net wrote:

> Hello to all!
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


If it is generated by a source outside your program control - ie a 
passed month that you are going to use as a subscript to a table of 
month names - you should validate it.  Unless you LIKE getting called 
at 3:00AM to fix it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


maxomtech@wiscasset.net wrote:

> Hello to all!
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

NEVER trust the data. ALLWAYS test.
(Don't even trust the data if its from your own program, that is, if it's
passed from one routine to another.)

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py>`

```
Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py>...

>NEVER trust the data. ALLWAYS test.
>(Don't even trust the data if its from your own program, that is, if it's
>passed from one routine to another.)


I disagree with this.  In fact, a basic principle of decent top down
structure is:  "work on the module you are working on, and trust
yourself to take care of other details in the modules responsible
for them".   If every module has to repeat all the work of every
other module because you write in such a way that no module
can be trusted, then you are just going to have a huge mess with
all sorts of distractions.

I would agree that you should be very careful with incoming data,
and always test it.  But once that you have tested it and ensured
that it conforms, then you *have* to trust it, or rewrite your test.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36422aac.107306618@news>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Thu, 5 Nov 1998 10:35:05 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>I disagree with this.  In fact, a basic principle of decent top down
>structure is:  "work on the module you are working on, and trust
>yourself to take care of other details in the modules responsible
>for them".   If every module has to repeat all the work of every
>other module because you write in such a way that no module

>I would agree that you should be very careful with incoming data,
>and always test it.  But once that you have tested it and ensured
>that it conforms, then you *have* to trust it, or rewrite your test.

I'd recommend using a conditional include for debug builds if your compiler
supports it. Have each module check that its data in received in the format it
expects and complain loudly if not[1]. I've found this allows you to very
quickly find where bad data is entering the system while developing the various
modules.  It also allows you to avoid the overhead of the exhaustive checks in
released builds.


[1] I use DISPLAY MESSAGE BOX with Acucobol-GT because it's very forgiving on
the data formats, which is handy for including "Expected x got y". As usual,
YMMV.
```

```

it

in
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> I disagree with this.  In fact, a basic principle of decent top down
> structure is:  "work on the module you are working on, and trust
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that it conforms, then you *have* to trust it, or rewrite your test.

That may be acceptable for small to medium sized programs. But when you use
standard routines (implemented as copy books), and in large complex programs,
it's very hard to ensure that the data you are receiving is correctly
initialized. It's also a maintenance problem because new programmers don't
always check for side-effects when they change things.
Better go safe. You will sleep better.

And, if you find invalid data in your test, log the error somewhere permanent.
It makes debugging _so_ much easier.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71tf3e$iks$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net> <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py>`

```
Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py>...

>That may be acceptable for small to medium sized programs. But when you use
>standard routines (implemented as copy books), and in large complex
programs,
>it's very hard to ensure that the data you are receiving is correctly
>initialized. It's also a maintenance problem because new programmers don't
>always check for side-effects when they change things.
>Better go safe. You will sleep better.


Well it's true, I seldom work on programs where the source is bigger
than 2 or 3 megabytes, and the copy books include more than 2 or 3
hundred modules.  In fact, I think the biggest program that I have ever
written has been less than 350,000 lines.  It's also true that I seldom
have to deal with programmers that don't check side-effects when they
change things, but mainly because I either re-train them or fire them
if they are incapable of being toilette trained.  When something of that
nature does occur, I also insist that they go back and fix it, rather than
slap a patch in somewhere else in the code.

Maybe that is why the code that was written twenty years ago is still
being sold at a profit, and integrated into new systems.

The place to check for data integrity is when the data enters the
system, not each and every time it is used.  That is a basic
principle of programming, in my opinion.  It is also one of the
basic principles of Cobol, and one of the reasons that the language
is so highly typed.  Business applications *need* that kind of
data integrity, and the larger the project the more important
rigor in managing the programs becomes, not the other way about.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3642D992.EFF37B75@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net> <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py> <71tf3e$iks$1@news.igs.net>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> Well it's true, I seldom work on programs where the source is bigger
> than 2 or 3 megabytes, and the copy books include more than 2 or 3
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> slap a patch in somewhere else in the code.

I'm sorry I left the impression that double-checking the data obliviates the
need for proper programming in the rest of the program. Very not so!

But I do try to program (and teach my programmers) to work with a "paranoid"
attitude. Take Murphys law serious, and expect things not to work. If you set a
variable, and use it somewhere near (lets say, two or three screenfulls of
code), then you would'nt have to test. But if you have a general-purpose
routine, for example, to display a description based on a table lookup, and that
routine is called from various places, then do a check.

BTW, I work mainly with RM/COBOL, and the only two things that can abort a
program (with no posible exception control) are: I/O to unopened files, and
table indexes with zeros values.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71v4uk$857$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net> <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py> <71tf3e$iks$1@news.igs.net> <3642D992.EFF37B75@infonet.com.py>`

```
Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <3642D992.EFF37B75@infonet.com.py>...

>I'm sorry I left the impression that double-checking the data obliviates
the


<S>I think perhaps we should declare a truce on this, and start over.
The original statement was "NEVER trust the data", with an addendum
of even if it is your own program.  Since the thread is on technique, I
jumped on that.  Perhaps I went in the wrong direction though, and should
have said "Never make blanket statements" about technique.

One of the things that I see beginners do that invariably gets them into
trouble on large projects, is not chunking things down small enough. I have
found, at least for myself, that the key to making a large project simple
is twofold.  Firstly, you have to make sure that you break it down into
small enough portions that each is easily understood.  Secondly, you
have to isolate the data/parameters for each of those portions so changing
one portion does not affect another portion.

It is that latter that causes the problems.  Obviously, to some extent,
the modules are related, and equally obviously, they are going to share
data.  The problem then, as I see it, is to design the modules in such
a way that each module is responsible for the data integrity of its
own module.  Other modules should, and need to be able to trust
that data or you are going to get into trouble.

An example would be a simple master file.  The master file update
module should ensure that all data in the record is edited correctly.
That might be a file copy or a subroutine, but it is fairly crucial that
all updating of that file, as well as all data checking, should be kept
in that module.  Outside it, one should be only using the data, not
modifying it.

Now, in fact, we often find that purity of approach impossible. The
update module may become too large to fit into a program that
has to change a master file field, for example.  One is also faced
with time constraints, and sometimes a patch is forced on the
programmer, for all sorts of day to day reasons.

Nevertheless, as one gets away from that isolation, the system
suffers for it.  We have all dealt with patchy programs, and I think
we would agree that the more scattered the patches become,
the more difficult the system gets to maintain and extend.

Your example with an index is actually an excellent one.  Yes,
I would agree, that in code of that sort, one should reset the index
and not assume.  However, that is precisely why I never use
the "indexed by" construct.  I know it is more efficient.  But an
array does not need it; a subscript can be used instead. If I had
to write a patch, I would introduce a *NEW* subscript.  That way,
I am absolutely assured that the act of setting it does not create
a problem somewhere else, AND I maintain the principal of
isolation ... in effect my patch becomes an integrated unit that
does not affect any other units.

I also avoid USAGE statements like the plaque for similar reasons.
Those copy books might very well be running on different platforms.
A slight increase in efficiency is not worth a programming problem
in 99.9% of all applications, and I expect every bit of code I write
to live through at least three or four migrations.  In fact, DOS lasting
as long as it did made me as sloppy as hell, and I am re-learning
a lot of those basics.    ;<(

I trust that makes what I am trying to express a bit clearer?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71vb0h$i44@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net> <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py> <71tf3e$iks$1@news.igs.net> <3642D992.EFF37B75@infonet.com.py> <71v4uk$857$1@news.igs.net>`

```
And all of this is *exactly* what OO Development addresses. 
Using precisely the same techniques, with the addition of they 
are codified into a formal methodology.  People often ask what
OO technology and methodologies buy them - codification of
the good programming practices we already know about.

BTW: I disagree with your philosophy to a degree:       

  (1) You can not ever trust the data. It will always bite you...

  (2) Technique that is not formed into a formal methodology is
      simply someone's job security. Unless they work for me
      that is. Then it can be their ticket to a new career...

-Paul


Donald Tees (donald@willmack.com) wrote:
```

```
you the same thing at a lot less cost in performance.

>And all of this is *exactly* what OO Development addresses.
>Using precisely the same techniques, with the addition of they
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…

have
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<722ivj$8o6@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <71sg0s$fmm$1@news.igs.net> <3641EC1E.B661DEF0@infonet.com.py> <71tf3e$iks$1@news.igs.net> <3642D992.EFF37B75@infonet.com.py> <71v4uk$857$1@news.igs.net> <71vb0h$i44@lotho.delphi.com> <71vg9d$a8i$1@news.igs.net>`

```
No they don't. They don't buy you a lot of stuff that is important
- especially in larger projects. For example, Simple subroutines
don't protect you from data types or data type changes. Nor do 
they serve very well as code repositories. Nor are they easily
and throughly documented in most cases. 

This is not a case where 'Oh well- *anyone* should be able to
understand that code' applies. It is a case where you *know
that code is right, and will perform right, even when 
someone else uses it for a purpose you didn't exactly forsee. 

There is a lto more, but I strongly suggest investing some 
study time in OO mothodologies. You might just decide 
differently. 

-Paul

: >: of even if it is your own program.  Since the thread is on technique, I
: >: jumped on that.  Perhaps I went in the wrong direction though, and should
: >: have said "Never make blanket statements" about technique.
: >
: >: One of the things that I see beginners do that invariably gets them into
: >: trouble on large projects, is not chunking things down small enough. I
: have
: >: found, at least for myself, that the key to making a large project simple
: >: is twofold.  Firstly, you have to make sure that you break it down into
: >: small enough portions that each is easily understood.  Secondly, you
: >: have to isolate the data/parameters for each of those portions so
: changing
: >: one portion does not affect another portion.
: >
: >: It is that latter that causes the problems.  Obviously, to some extent,
: >: the modules are related, and equally obviously, they are going to share
: >: data.  The problem then, as I see it, is to design the modules in such
: >: a way that each module is responsible for the data integrity of its
: >: own module.  Other modules should, and need to be able to trust
: >: that data or you are going to get into trouble.
: >
: >: An example would be a simple master file.  The master file update
: >: module should ensure that all data in the record is edited correctly.
: >: That might be a file copy or a subroutine, but it is fairly crucial that
: >: all updating of that file, as well as all data checking, should be kept
: >: in that module.  Outside it, one should be only using the data, not
: >: modifying it.
: >
: >: Now, in fact, we often find that purity of approach impossible. The
: >: update module may become too large to fit into a program that
: >: has to change a master file field, for example.  One is also faced
: >: with time constraints, and sometimes a patch is forced on the
: >: programmer, for all sorts of day to day reasons.
: >
: >: Nevertheless, as one gets away from that isolation, the system
: >: suffers for it.  We have all dealt with patchy programs, and I think
: >: we would agree that the more scattered the patches become,
: >: the more difficult the system gets to maintain and extend.
: >
: >: Your example with an index is actually an excellent one.  Yes,
: >: I would agree, that in code of that sort, one should reset the index
: >: and not assume.  However, that is precisely why I never use
: >: the "indexed by" construct.  I know it is more efficient.  But an
: >: array does not need it; a subscript can be used instead. If I had
: >: to write a patch, I would introduce a *NEW* subscript.  That way,
: >: I am absolutely assured that the act of setting it does not create
: >: a problem somewhere else, AND I maintain the principal of
: >: isolation ... in effect my patch becomes an integrated unit that
: >: does not affect any other units.
: >
: >: I also avoid USAGE statements like the plaque for similar reasons.
: >: Those copy books might very well be running on different platforms.
: >: A slight increase in efficiency is not worth a programming problem
: >: in 99.9% of all applications, and I expect every bit of code I write
: >: to live through at least three or four migrations.  In fact, DOS lasting
: >: as long as it did made me as sloppy as hell, and I am re-learning
: >: a lot of those basics.    ;<(
: >
: >: I trust that makes what I am trying to express a bit clearer?
: >
: >
: >
: >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py>`

```
>> Hello to all!
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> data?

The principle: test if there is a chance that the value is out of range.
But something like the following would be absurd.

    Perform Varying I from 1 by 1 until I > 60
         IF I > 0 and I < 61
              MOVE ABC TO  WS-TBL(I)
         End-If
    End-Perform
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Darius Cooper wrote:
> 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>     End-Perform

In the 'writing solid code' book  the paranoid comes out.  If you write
the following:

    Perform Varying I from 1 by 1 until I > 60
              MOVE ABC TO  WS-TBL(I)
    End-Perform

Why do you use the '>' and not equals?

The answer is defensive coding.

If you actually use the greater than why don't you check for impossible
results like:

    Perform Varying I from 1 by 1 until I > 60
              MOVE ABC TO  WS-TBL(I)
    End-Perform
d   if I > 61 
d     abend with full debug
d   end-if.

(the d is for compile with debug mode only).

This is a science that warrants study.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298311.65392.14912@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>`

```
In message <<3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> writes:
> 
> In the 'writing solid code' book  the paranoid comes out.  If you write
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> The answer is defensive coding.

Because you want the loop done for the value of 60 ?

Ok, so you really meant ' = 61' or 'WITH TEST AFTER ... = 60'

In Cobol it is valid to change the value of the controlling
variable (it is not in, say, Pascal) and so an EXIT PERFORM
can be simultaed by:

         PERFORM
             VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL I > 60

             IF ( WS-Table-Entry(I) = To-be-Found )
                 MOVE ....
                 MOVE 99           TO I
             END-IF
          END-PERFORM

> 
> If you actually use the greater than why don't you check for impossible
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is a science that warrants study.

Because the result is not impossible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
<3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>...
> In the 'writing solid code' book  the paranoid comes out.  If you write
> the following:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Because if you use equals the 60th element of the table will NOT be
modified.  The > is used because the test in an inline perform occurs
BEFORE the performed code is executed.  You could instead code the
following:

Perform with test after varying i from 1 by 1 until i = 60
            MOVE ABC TO WS-TBL(i)
End-Perform

This will do exactly the same thing.  The > 60 is not used out of paranoia
but out of necessity.


> The answer is defensive coding.
>

No, it's because you want to fill the 60th element of the table, and then
stop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36458B52.3B5A7A1D@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default>`

```
Programmers have also altered the contents of the variable I to break out of
the perform (not recommended). In this situation, one would go into an
infinite loop and corrupt storage (at least until the OS pulls the plug). The
'>' is the way to go.

 Perform Varying I from 1 by 1 until I = 61
               MOVE ABC TO  WS-TBL(I)
               IF ABC = XYZ add 99 to I
     End-Perform

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au>...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364592A8.74ED1342@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <36458B52.3B5A7A1D@ix.netcom.com>`

```
John Trifon wrote:
> 
> Programmers have also altered the contents of the variable I to break out of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Let's try this again with the complete code sample

      Perform Varying I from 1 by 1 until I > 60
               MOVE ABC TO  WS-TBL(I)
               IF ABC = XYZ add 99 to I         (bad later modification)
      End-Perform
D     if I > 61 
D         Crunch
D     end-if

What happens now is the programmer that coded a senseless statement will
be alerted in his first test. If you put in the debugging code into your
routine (that is not productionised for performance reasons) then the
modification will fail the program.

If XYZ never occurs in the test data then the code will work as expected
and the error will never arise, if XYZ occurs in production (D lines
removed) the error never occurs period and you are not called out
overnight.

This is obviously a very trivial example but for instance what happens
when you 'expect' something to be returned in a range eg month =0..12
(where 0 represents error) from a perform.  The code that is modified
could be a long way from the code that is checking so it is not that
obvious as the above sample.

If you look and the language design for Eifel you will see these
concepts built in.  You can define preconditions and post conditions
into you classes (OK call them performs in Cobol).  If compiled in (via
compile options) it will check that the input values are in a certain
range. It is simply program logic not anyhing special so the programmer
still has to define the conditions in code.  The same occurs for post
conditions.

Is this fail safe, NO!  If your input data loads up a table with 15
items and it all works fine and after four years the input data is now
1500 then this testing is pretty worthless.  Programmers always code
check array bounds when they load tables though don't they :->.  In the
same way your testing may never actually hit the special condition that
fails your program with the special debugging statements in and then it
fails with some sort of memory overwrite problem in production (when it
is not trapped by the operating system) and it is impossible to find.

Another thing that made me sit up was when reading 'Writing solid code'
'If you are writing code step through every line with a debugger.'  My
intial thought was no way, after reading his reasons I would definitely
consider doing this.

Read the book it will make old dogs think about their craft, it did for
me.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7275nl$6pn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <36458B52.3B5A7A1D@ix.netcom.com> <364592A8.74ED1342@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message <364592A8.74ED1342@zip.com.au>...

How about
01 I            picture 99.
<G><crunch>

The real problem with all this stuff, is that there is no
real substitute for reading code. You *have* to follow the
logic through, and be aware of what will happen when
the code is executed.  Programming by guess-work
combined with trial and error (good messages or not)
does not really cut it.

*That* is why I consider highly readable code superior
to all the defensive coding coding techniques in the world.
Note that I am not saying do not code defensively.  I am
saying that it is the second line of defense, not the first.


>Let's try this again with the complete code sample
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>D     end-if
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default>...

I would disagree with you here Thane.  (Not on the
example, but with regard to "equality" checks).  I think
it poor form to use an equality check on any perform.

For example:
    Perform abc until end-flag is equal to "Y".
should be:
    Perform abc until end-flag is not equal to "N".

For the same reason, I would always use a "greater than"
regardless of the end check.  If the equality is part of the check
then a "not less than" can be used, or a
((greater than)or(equal to)).  With flags, particularly, one can
then introduce new meanings, for example.

Perform abc until end-flag is not equal to "N".
if end-flag is equal to "E"
    perform error-routine.

It also avoids problems like:
MOVE "y" TO END-FLAG.  The loop still ends correctly.

>> Why do you use the '>' and not equals?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>but out of necessity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36478ED5.7EC6@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

[snip]

> For the same reason, I would always use a "greater than"
> regardless of the end check.  If the equality is part of the check
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOVE "y" TO END-FLAG.  The loop still ends correctly.

[snip]

I would suggest going a step farther: code the test exclusively in
terms of 88-levels.  Code your flag something like this:

 01  FLAGS-AND-SWITCHES.
     05  FILLER               PIC X       VALUE 'N'.
         88  END-OF-FILE                  VALUE 'Y'.
     05  etc...

When you reach end-of-file, use the SET verb (instead of a MOVE) to
note that fact.  Code your loop as follows:

     PERFORM ABC UNTIL END-OF-FILE

That way it is almost impossible to assign an invalid value to the
switch because there is no named variable to MOVE anything to, apart
from the group item.  (I don't know if it's legal to have FILLER at
the 01 level, but that would provide even stronger protection.)

Of course, the test is still a test for equality.  If that bothers
you, just reverse the sense of the 88-level and code something like:

     PERFORM ABC UNTIL NOT RECORDS-REMAINING

At this point the value of defensive programming starts to collide 
with the value of readability, posing a judgement call on which 
reasonable persons may differ.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36492E6B.81287863@att.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net> <36478ED5.7EC6@swbell.net>`

```
"Michael C. Kasten" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> I would suggest going a step farther: code the test exclusively in
> terms of 88-levels.  Code your flag something like this:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> the 01 level, but that would provide even stronger protection.)

Hooray for Michael! I'm in complete agreement. Stop moving values to
switches & SET them. No need to code a VALUE if the OFF value is the
default for the data type, i.e., ZERO for a switch whose PIC is 9. If
you put all the switches in an 01-level called WS-SWITCHES (or something
clever like that), you code:

INITIALIZE WS-SWITCHES

in your initialization routine, and you're off & running. You can always
INITIALIZE the switch again if you're going to use it more than once.

Bill Lynch


Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36508965.7E31418D@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net> <36478ED5.7EC6@swbell.net> <36492E6B.81287863@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

I agree with Michael as well, he always comes up with good coding
insights.

I do wish to comment that initialise can be very confusing with regard
to switches.  I support a program that uses switches all over the place
and it did initialises on various 05 levels at various times to reset
the switches when the state changed.  It is VERY confusing!

Keep the initialise in one place and put comments in the working storage
above the identifiers.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-iNEoUddsbdGL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Mon, 9 Nov 1998 16:57:42, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> 
wrote:

> Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default>...
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Now you are starting to code defensively <G>.  Couldn't resist.  

However, I agree - and Ken e-mailed me - I didn't understand his 
original example - it had a typo- and what he was trying to get at.

One thing that I could not get across to my former co-workers:

They hated my use of inline perfoms, Evaluate and IF... End-If.  It 
was new and different.  Their complaint was that since it was 
unfamiliar it would be hard to debug.  My encouragement was to LEARN 
how to use these things.  I fumbled about a bit when I first started 
using the '85 version of COBOL.  I did some things with inline 
performs that should not have been done that way.  (ie: a 10 page 
inline perform with nested inline performs inside nested if's).  We 
all learn as we go.  But one thing I HAVE learned --- this TYPE of 
code seldom REQUIRES debugging - it works.  

Your assertion that the equality checks in performs should be avoided 
is a good one, and one I subscribe to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<727d5c$fjs$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-iNEoUddsbdGL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>Now you are starting to code defensively <G>.  Couldn't resist.


<S>As I said earlier in the day(and it bears repeating), I do not
object to defensive programming. I just consider it the second
line of defense.  The first line should always be code that is
easy to read, and obvious as to what it does.  All the defensive
measures in the world will not help you if you have a problem
and you cannot figure out what the code is *supposed* to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36492D0D.A89B9273@att.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-iNEoUddsbdGL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> However, I agree - and Ken e-mailed me - I didn't understand his
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> is a good one, and one I subscribe to.

I have to disagree here (in a collegial, non-flaming, way). The reason
COBOL programmers coded UNTIL blob > some-other-blob is because COBOL,
up to but not including the '85 standard, closed the loop at the top.
Meaning, COBOL increments your loop variable, then tests for the limit.
In the example above, ... > +60, you loop 60 times, then bump the
variable to +61, test it and stop, because it's > +60. In many cases
this caused problems, because we couldn't rely on the contents of the
subscript after the PERFORM was finished. COBOL II (completely IBM MVS
oriented) changed all that with the "WITH TEST AFTER" phrase. Here you
code until something = the-limit. Of course, in COBOL II we can also
code UNTIL blob >=  the-limit. 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72c78d$4h3$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36417BFD.16A4B84A@infonet.com.py> <722a7s$jfj$1@news.megsinet.net> <3644E915.D2DD7642@zip.com.au> <01be0aba$aa4388a0$62bc48a6@default> <7276s0$cd8$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-iNEoUddsbdGL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36492D0D.A89B9273@att.net>`

```
I always considered the test before to be an advantage; it
saves having to have a method to break out of the loop.
Take the following:
01 end-flag            picture x value "n".

start-up.
    open etc.
    read etc at end move "y" to end-flag.
    perform main-loop until end-flag is not equal to "n".
    close etc.
    stop run.

```

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> Darius Cooper wrote:
> >
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…

Given that the subject is technique, I would like to point out that using
numbers such as 61 and 60 is generally not recommended. Define a variable with
a name like max-item or such, and a value of 60 or 61. For the purposes of the
above argument, however, this does not need to apply.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3646c722.3996251@news-server>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Wed, 04 Nov 1998 15:28:05 GMT, maxomtech@wiscasset.net enlightened
us:

>Hello to all!
>
>I have a question.  Is it a good programming practice to check for a
>subscript value to be within a valid range (i.e.: If sub > 0 and sub not >
>max-value....)?

Yes.  You should always check subscript values if there is even the
remotest chance they could be out of range.
 
>or should I "assume" that the data that calculates the
>subscript is always going to be valid and if it is not, can I "blame" the
>data?

Never "assume" anything when it comes to data or programming.  In case
you're new to programming maybe you've never heard or seen the quip:

Assume = makes an ASS out of U and ME
Happens to be all too true.


>
>Thanks for your input.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own    

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Q:  What was the first question they asked Linda Tripp at her make over?
A:  Paper or plastic!

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364381F3.76F8530F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I would have to agree with Donald on this one.

If you are talking about raw data then yes you need to validate.  If you
are talking about data generated from a previous program that you
control then no.  For example it is insane to test whether the month is
in the range 1-12 when a previous program calculates the date, if it is
wrong then the previous program should be fixed.  Do not clutter a
program with all sorts of checks.

However:  If you write your code differently then the fix might be
simple.

evaluate month
when 1,2,....
otherwise
	error
end-evaluate.

The other alternative is to test with it in but production without a 'd'
in column 7 will do the trick but watch out for misplacing periods.  The
new standard removes the 'd' flag just when I found it.

If you would like to read up on this read 'writing solid code' this is
about C programming which might totally confuse you if you are a pure
cobol programmer but just read the prose.

Good luck
Ken

maxomtech@wiscasset.net wrote:
> 
> Hello to all!
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on technique

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364715C7.5607C7DD@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<71prq4$vd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364381F3.76F8530F@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey wrote:

> I would have to agree with Donald on this one.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>

If you are talking about data generated in the _same_ program, then maybe you
don't have to control. If the data is from some other program, posibly read from
files, you _do_ have to control. You never can be sure your data is consistent.
Maybe a crashed system, maybe the data came from somewhere else (changing
requirements? data converted from other systems?).

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

## Thread: Interfacing to SQL using Cobol...which compiler is preferred? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Interfacing to SQL using Cobol...which compiler is preferred?

- **From:** ivanru@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71prhj$uoc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
  I am currently using MBP Visual Cobol. It is painful to talk to a SQL DB
because of the amount of code required in a program i.e. binds etc.. I need an
opinion on any other compilers out that will make this process easier.  Any
reports on Fujitsu Cobol 4.0, Microfocus, etc... Thanks for your help...

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Interfacing to SQL using Cobol...which compiler is preferred?

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36407b87.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<71prhj$uoc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Both Microfocus Net Express and Fujitsu Cobol V3/V4 Professional support
conversion of embedded SQL (ie "simple SQL") to ODBC calls by the compiler.
This simplifies writing SQL in Cobol programs. There is also support for
non-ODBC precompilers such as Oracle.

Taking this one stage further, Casegen Cobol for Windows Professional
generates all the Windows and SQL with point & click technology then
generates all the required Cobol and SQL.

See www.casegen.co.uk

Simon Cordingley
```

- **From:** david murray <dmurray@home.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<36412C38.2D197D75@home.com>`
- **References:** `<71prhj$uoc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36407b87.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk>`

```
I agree with Simon, except for maybe the "simple SQL" part.  NetExpress,
Sybase, and Oracle precompilers all support dynamic "simple SQL" also,
so you can build your own on the fly if need be.  If haven't look at
Fujitsu hard enough to see if they do, but if they support SQLDA, they
do.

Dave Murray
```

```
Yes.

I have used the versions of the standard I can find - to good effect. I am
DISTRESSED at the removal of the current standard.  I have the 1.2 draft.

good can come of having the current draft hidden from public view.
```

```
> <363fbcef.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> can be accomplished within the standard.

Leif, don't count this as another vote from me.

This is the most compelling reason I have seen for putting the 
standard in the hands of the programmer.  So they can tell just WHAT 
IS standard.

MF does a fair job at indicating what's standard and what's not - but 
its often cryptic and hard to read.

Fujitsu did a better job by using a yellow highlighter in their docs 
as to what is non standard.
```

<a id="t-079510e083"></a>
## Thread: Data on disk but not in cache (MF on netware 4.11) — 5 msgs

### Data on disk but not in cache (MF on netware 4.11)

- **From:** Scott Dickson <scottdickson@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3640741A.8517C97F@ibm.net>`

```
We are seeing a record returned with all NULLs in a successful read
after successfully writing the record.  The data is being read from
cache, no disk io takes place (as far as we can tell).  The problem
persists from more than one workstation (going after the same record).
Upon rebooting the server, the read now returns the correct data.

The server is a new Dell Poweredge 4200 running dual Pentium II 333,
Netware 4.11 SMP (with smp installed).  Support pack 5b is installed.

The client is running a program developed with Micro Focus Cobol using
there filehandler.  The program has been running for years without this
NULL record problem occuring.  The problem first appeared 4 days after
the new server went online and has occurred 5 times (in a two week
period).

If we had to take a stab, our guess (which may be dead wrong) is that
somehow the cache is not updated even though the disk is.  Upon reading
the record, the OS is attempting to return the record from cache and is
therfore returning the wrong data (NULLs).  Because of a lack of
understanding on what is in control, we have no basis for how this could
happen (or even if it is at all possible).

Anyone have any idea where to begin?

Scott Dickson
```

#### ↳ Re: Data on disk but not in cache (MF on netware 4.11)

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dWwLbPuMBihd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3640741A.8517C97F@ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 4 Nov 1998 15:34:50, Scott Dickson <scottdickson@ibm.net> 
wrote:

> We are seeing a record returned with all NULLs in a successful read
> after successfully writing the record.  The data is being read from
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Scott - I had a similar problem using the MicroFocus product itself - 
never found a solution.

Running a Novell 4.1 server - Windows 95 client, using Novell Client 
32 drivers.

We could compile a program, and if SOME OTHER workstation looked at it
it was fine, but if the workstation that created it, copied it to 
another drive, an "incomplete" version was copied.

Here's how we found out.   When you comile with MF there's a header in
the .INT - type as in TYPE XXXXX.INT and you will see what I mean.  If
a compile is not successful this header is not updated with the 
information you see when you type the file.  

This was happening.  The compile was complete, the header was updated 
- on the Novell disk - other workstations could see the updated header
- but the machine creating the data could not.

It was fun finding this  - started out as corrupt INT files being sent
to users of the software - but looking at them later on the disk, they
were fine.  Just when they were copied quickly to the backup library -
the copy was wrong the orig was OK.

Now, with a DOS client the problem did not exist.

Is any of this similar to what you are seeing?
```

```
The difference (outside of the fact our problem is with data files, not

upon bouncing the server does the problem go away.

I sure would like to know exactly what MF does when it writes and/or reads an
index sequential file.
```

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)

```
On Mon, 09 Nov 1998 17:39:16 -0500, Scott Dickson
<scottdickson@ibm.net> wrote:


>I sure would like to know exactly what MF does when it writes and/or reads an
>index sequential file.

Hmm. from the sound of it, it sounds like MF's isam files are cached
before writing to disk (or OS). This may have been done previous to
the invention of caches built into a network product, or it may be the
compiler was simply *recompiled* without any thought to the new
architecture.
```

## Thread: Error 9/199 — 2 msgs

### Error 9/199

- **From:** "Fernando J. Mart���nez Vico" <cpdlin@mx3.redestb.es>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3640455B.577F5ED3@mx3.redestb.es>`

```
  Iï¿½m running Micro Focus Application Server Ver. 4.1 for Unixware 2.1.2
in a server with Unixware 2.1. In the applications we run under cobol
almost everyday  appears the error 9/199. The consequence of this error
is that users cannot execute the application, and if they are doing, it
may take them out. There is no rule for the aparition of this error.

    Every time the 9/199 appears, it generates a file called like
"MFIOERR.1225" with the program the user was running at the momment it
came and the parameter "errno=25" in all the cases.

    The only solution we have is restart the server.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 9/199

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<729fsh$cpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3640455B.577F5ED3@mx3.redestb.es>`

```
According to my MF 3.2 Error Messages manual, run time error 199 says
"Operating System error code lies outside expected range (Fatal) -- A system
call has returned an unexpected error number which is not documented. --
Contact Technical Support who will help you to discover the specific cause of
this error."

It sounds to me like the file i/o call is getting an error "25" returned from
the OS, and it doesn't know what that error is.

In MF Cobol - any time you get a file error "9/nnn" the "nnn" is a run-time
error and can be found in the manual as such.  In this case, I would believe
what the manual says and call MF Tech Support.

In article <3640455B.577F5ED3@mx3.redestb.es>,
  "Fernando J. Mart�nez Vico" <cpdlin@mx3.redestb.es> wrote:
>   I�m running Micro Focus Application Server Ver. 4.1 for Unixware 2.1.2
> in a server with Unixware 2.1. In the applications we run under cobol
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: sort problem in Cobol MVS — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Paul Hardy <paul.hardy.uhold@pwgsc.gc.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
Hi. I have another problem with a conversion from OS Cobol to MVS Cobol.
This group was very helpful with my VSAM problem, I thought I would pass
this by you.

Both the OS and MVS versions when run in parallel produce the same
results, however in the Cobol MVS version I was getting a CEEOUT
indicating that my Second sort was unsuccessful.(CEEOUT is produced as
the program was not referencing the sort-return special register)  As a
result I put in a display on the sort-return and indeed I received a
0016 on the second sort (now that I reference the sort-return I do not
get the CEEOUT). Unfortunately the error message recieved from DFSORT
(0016) don't exactly shed much light on what is happening.

In the DFSORT messages for the program all indications are that it was
successful. Can anyone shed any light on this, I have our Guru's here
looking into it, but have had no response for  a couple of days, so I
thought I would try all you guru's.... program details follow.....



SELECT SORT-FILE         ASSIGN TO BA41402.



SD  SORT-FILE
    DATA RECORDS ARE SORT-REC.
01  SORT-REC.
    05 SORT-DTL                PIC X(204).
    05 SORT-KEY1.
       10 SRT1-SUB-TYPE        PIC XX.
       10 SRT1-SOURCE          PIC X(4).
       10 SRT1-DSO             PIC X(2).
       10 SRT1-DAO             PIC X(4).
       10 SRT1-ENCL-IND        PIC X.
       10 SRT1-BTCH-NO         PIC X(4).
       10 SRT1-INP-SEQ         PIC X(6).
    05 SORT-KEY2  REDEFINES  SORT-KEY1.
       10 SRT2-BTCH-SEQ        PIC X(5).
       10 SRT2-SEQ-TYPE        PIC X(1).
       10 SRT2-INP-SEQ         PIC X(6).
       10 SRT2-FILLER          PIC X(11).

Procedure..............

PART2-SORT-REBTCH-BTCHS.

    OPEN OUTPUT TMP-FILE.

    SORT SORT-FILE ON ASCENDING KEY SORT-KEY1
        INPUT PROCEDURE  IS A100-READ-SORT-INP-RECS
        OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS A200-WRITE-SRTED-TMP-RECS.

    DISPLAY SORT-RETURN '<---- SORT-RETURN'                    *** zero
return code
    .

    CLOSE TMP-FILE.
    OPEN INPUT TMP-FILE.
    MOVE ZEROS  TO  WS-INP-SEQ.

    SORT SORT-FILE ON ASCENDING KEY SORT-KEY2
        INPUT PROCEDURE  IS A300-READ-REBTCHED-TMP-FILE
        OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS A400-WRITE-REBTCHED-OUT-FILE.

    DISPLAY SORT-RETURN '<---- SORT-RETURN'                    ***0016
return code
    .


Thanks
Paul Hardy
    .
```

#### ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** David_G_Clark@brown.edu (David_G_Clark)
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71qgok$sfn@cocoa.brown.edu>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
I seem to remember a similar problem when upgrading to Cobol II.  It
was caused by the program not returning all the records released before
ending output procedure.   In output section it checked for high-values in
sortfield and branched to end procedure rather than returning records until the 
end-of-sort condition was triggered (which would have been next time return
was issued anyway).  Nevertheless this caused messages about unsuccsessful 
sorts.

	Hope this helps.  




In article <36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>, paul.hardy.uhold@pwgsc.gc.ca says...
>
>Hi. I have another problem with a conversion from OS Cobol to MVS Cobol.
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71sjii$hk9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca> <71qgok$sfn@cocoa.brown.edu>`

```
In article <71qgok$sfn@cocoa.brown.edu>,
  David_G_Clark@brown.edu wrote:
> I seem to remember a similar problem when upgrading to Cobol II.  It
> was caused by the program not returning all the records released before
> ending output procedure.   In output section it checked for high-values in
> sortfield and branched to end procedure rather than returning records until
the
> end-of-sort condition was triggered (which would have been next time return
> was issued anyway).  Nevertheless this caused messages about unsuccsessful
> sorts.
>

Right.  This can cause DFSORT to issue an ICE025A message (RECORD COUNT OFF)
unless option NOCHECK is in effect.  Here's what the DFSORT Messages book says
about the situation for COBOL:

"If a COBOL-invoking program contains an output procedure, make sure the
RETURN statement is iterated until the AT END condition is executed.  This
occurs the next time the RETURN statement is executed AFTER the last record
was returned to you.

If the iteration of the RETURN is controlled by a PERFORM statement, the
PERFORM logic should be controlled by the execution of the AT END clause of
the RETURN statement."

You can access all of the DFSORT books online, including the Messages book,
from the DFSORT home page at URL:

 http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)





-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71r7tl$6fu@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
Everything looks fairly reasonable.  You might want to put a display of the
Sort-Return entry at the beginning and end of your input/output procedures,

  A300-READ-REBTCHED-TMP-FILE
 A400-WRITE-REBTCHED-OUT-FILE

and see where the problem is occurring. By the way, both the COBOL and
DFSort (and SyncSort) products have options to change where your "output"
goes.  You might want to send your COBOL output to one DDname and your Sort
output to another.  If you need information for the options to do this, let
me know and I will research it further.

Paul Hardy wrote in message <36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>...
>Hi. I have another problem with a conversion from OS Cobol to MVS Cobol.
>This group was very helpful with my VSAM problem, I thought I would pass
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Rex Widmer <rwidmer@sound.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36413158.22FB@sound.net>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca> <71r7tl$6fu@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
One common problem in multiple SORTs within the same COBOL program is
that the SORT may be just too clever for it's own good.

I have seen in the past cases where SORT released work-area space at the
end of the input phase of SORT #1 in a multple sort application, and
then failed on the second SORT in the same program.  In the particular
case the sort product was SYNCSORT, and a communication with their
excellent tech support yeilded a $ORTPARM value which prevented this
release of work areas.  

Collisions in the use of SYSOUT by sort and COBOL / LE are also common
trouble.  Special register SORT-MESSAGE or $ORTPARM would allow the
application to direct SORT to use some other DD-name than SYSOUT for
it's messages.  Sharing a DD-name between multiple programs seems to me
to be playing with fire anyway.
```

#### ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Paul Hardy <paul.hardy@pwgsc.gc.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```


Paul Hardy wrote:

> In the DFSORT messages for the program all indications are that it was
> successful. Can anyone shed any light on this, I have our Guru's here
> looking into it, but have had no response for  a couple of days, so I
> thought I would try all you guru's.... program details follow.....
>

HI....follow-up on resolution of sort problem.

OS/VS Run
-----
.
.
ICE080I 0 IN MAIN STORAGE SORT
ICE055I 0 INSERT 4333, DELETE 4332
ICE054I 0 RECORDS - IN: 0, OUT: 0
ICE025A 0 RECORD COUNT OFF
ICE052I 0 END OF DFSORT

No output indicating sort was unsuccessful other than above...'record count
off' statement

MVS
---
.
.
ICE080I 0 IN MAIN STORAGE SORT
ICE055I 0 INSERT 4333, DELETE 4332
ICE054I 0 RECORDS - IN: 0, OUT: 0
ICE025A 0 RECORD COUNT OFF
ICE052I 0 END OF DFSORT

When you do not reference the sort-return special register in your program
you get the following in CEEOUT;

IGZ0026W The SORT-RETURN special register was never referenced, but the
current
         operation in program BA41400J on line number 1007 was unsuccessful.

1007 is the sort statement.

Program
-------

It appeared that the program was checking for a logical end of file and a
physical end of file, since the logical end of file came first the return
statement was never executed for the last time to get to the Physical end of
file....here is the offending code...

b400-paragraph

 RETURN SORT-FILE
     AT END  MOVE  'YES'  TO  END-OF-SORT2
             GO TO  B400-EXIT.
..snippage

IF INP-REC-TYPE  =  ' '
   MOVE WS-INP-REC    TO  WS-TRAILER-REC
   MOVE TOT-RECS-OUT  TO  TRL-REC-CNT
   WRITE REBTCH-REC   FROM  WS-TRAILER-REC
   ADD  1             TO  TOT-RECS-OUT
   MOVE  'YES'  TO  END-OF-SORT2
   GO TO B400-EXIT.

..snippage

b400-exit

where the b400 was executed until end-of-sort2 was = "yes"

To resolve the problem all I had to do was comment out the move 'yes' in the
logical end of file check in order to force the return sort-file for the
last time.....

It is frustrating to know now that this condition always existed in the past
but was not caught because the sort messages were not checked and NO
MESSAGES indicating a 0016 code had been passed in the sort. I have to admit
that had I not gotten the 0016 in CEEOUT I would have assumed that the
program was functioning correct and would not have caught this "logic"
problem.

Again you guys were great, got pointed in the right direction and was able
to figure it out, Thanks to all who gave me helpful hints....

Paul Hardy

> --
> Take .uhold out of my address when responding.....
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ____________________________________________
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71tav4$lof$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca> <3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
In article <3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca>,
Paul Hardy <paul.hardy@pwgsc.gc.ca> wrote:
> > In the DFSORT messages for the program all indications are that it was
> > successful.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

ICE025A IS an ERROR message.  The A means ERROR (I means information).	The
DFSORT Messages book explains what the A and I mean.  For ICE025A, the
Explanation says "Critical." and explains the error situation.	DFSORT passes
back a return code of 16 to the calling program for any A message including
this one.  This all seems sufficient to tell you that there was an error, so
I don't understand why you say the DFSORT messages indicated the sort was
successful or that there were NO MESSAGES indicating a 16 code.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL :-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT/MVS is on the World Wide Web at URL:
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/



-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Paul Hardy <paul.hardy@pwgsc.gc.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364303F8.86A21C67@pwgsc.gc.ca>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca> <3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca> <71tav4$lof$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com wrote:

> In article <3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca>,
> Paul Hardy <paul.hardy@pwgsc.gc.ca> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

  I'm not implying that DFSORT is poor product at all, it is an excellent one.
What I am trying to say is that unless you look specifically at the output above,
which nobody seems to have done when origionally writting this program and it has
been in production for years, nowhere in the outputs does DFSORT indicate a 0016
sort-return code when sort-return is NOT checked in the program (and it should
have been). I know that if you checked the sort-return in the program it would
indicate this but the origional program did not and the runtime cobol did not
display the message as the new cobol does.

In cobol MVS if you do not specify the sort-return register it DOES tell you, via
a CEEOUT message. That is how I found out about the problem, even though it does
not affect the program processing, I just like to have all return codes clean.

IF CEEOUT had not produced the message I probably would have put the program back
in production the way it was, since I was checking the old vs new outputs and they
were identical as were the sysouts and messages (with the exception of CEEOUT).

Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36483001.4775058@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca> <3641FD3B.ECABB6C1@pwgsc.gc.ca> <71tav4$lof$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364303F8.86A21C67@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
Paul Hardy <paul.hardy@pwgsc.gc.ca> wrote:

> I'm not implying that DFSORT is poor product at all, it is an excellent one.
>What I am trying to say is that unless you look specifically at the output above,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>have been).

This is really a standards and good programming practices issue, then
and now.. 

The first COBOL I wrote code in was COBOL "F". The IBM COBOL
programmers guides from then to the present have always stated that
testing the sort-return-code is mandatory and continuing execution
without doing that is unpredictable. 

I think the original problem was that SORT was a called program from
COBOL  It's always the responsibility of the calling program to
determine that the called routine ended correctly. In most older
versions of COBOL, SORT was treated exactly this way. You can't say
IBM made it difficult, SORT only gives two return codes. 0 or 16. <G>

I remember working on a number of old programs where adding the
sort-return-code test made the program instantly fail. Checking old
production runs showed that the SORT  had been returning a return code
of 16 for years and no one had caught it. Often because I didn't have
time to rework that part of the code I had to comment out the check
and leave a note in the code for the next guy hoping he'd have time.

As a systems programmer I often thought that the COBOL and SORT
product teams didn't talk except under duress. Resolving any problem
involving the two problems was always a nightmare. 

Anyway, what really brings this to a head is that newer versions of
COBOL and SORT are much  more tightly integrated and newer versions do
propagate an error message. 

			Scott Peterson
```

#### ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71sisp$g6n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
In article <36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>,
  Paul Hardy <paul.hardy.uhold@pwgsc.gc.ca> wrote:
>     Unfortunately the error message recieved from DFSORT
> (0016) don't exactly shed much light on what is happening.
>

You can have DFSORT show you all of the messages by adding the following to
your job:

//SORTDIAG DD DUMMY
//MYOUT DD SYSOUT=*
//DFSPARM DD *
  OPTION MSGDDN=MYOUT
/*

//SORTDIAG gives you all of the DFSORT messages, including the diagnostic
messages. MSGDDN=MYOUT puts the DFSORT messages in //MYOUT instead of in
//SYSOUT.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
=> DFSORT/MVS is on the WWW at
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: sort problem in Cobol MVS

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36434fc4.10277980@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36409BA9.E84A6BD@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
Paul Hardy <paul.hardy.uhold@pwgsc.gc.ca> wrote:


>Both the OS and MVS versions when run in parallel produce the same
>results, however in the Cobol MVS version I was getting a CEEOUT
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Check your output procedure.  Almost every time I've seen this the
reason has been failure to activate the AT END condition on the RETURN
statement. I've seen cases where people have counted the records and
then looped the output procedure that many times, or where a
high-value record is sorted as an EOF indicator for other reasons and
is then used in the output procedure to trigger exit from the sort
routine.. 

Bottom line, if you have N records, you must do N+1 RETURN statements
so the AT END condition is activated. Try putting a counter after the
release and return statements and display them at the same time you
display the sort return code.

                            Scott Peterson
 correctly.
```

## Thread: New stuff from Objectz — 1 msgs

### New stuff from Objectz

- **From:** "Stephen D. Burns" <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3640C810.63C638E8@boeing.com>`

```
take a look at the Objectz page to see some new neat stuff. 
http://www.objectz.com/

They have a new online magazine called COBOL World.  It has interesting
(and rather short) articles.  They promise 'frequent' updates, but don't
give a frequency.  They said it would be more often than what is printed
in the COBOL Report, which is bi-monthly...

Also click on the COBOL university link to  read about a new internet
school for COBOL.  The first class is an introduction course and costs
$129.  They talk about future classes in more advanced subjects
including object orientation.
```

```
and a year's maintenance (not support, just maint).  What do you get for
it?  A GUI development environment (finally) and the ability to write
CGI directly (though I'm not sure I understand why; everyone I know that
is writing for the web isn't using cobol.  C++, Perl, shell scripts, but
cobol?), and they bundled the Embedded SQL Toolkit (which has all the
ODBC drivers for that guy who's looking for them).

Seems a pretty steep price for someone that just wants to support a
batch-style environment and do it on a 32-bit Windows platform.

Fujitsu's offering the same thing (sans web and OO) for about $900.
```

```
Hi,
We are facing a v.strange problem under AIX 4.2.0 and MF COBOL v.4,
The problem is that during DATA entry some times (usually in a week) all
of a sudden all the data entred during that transaction disappear
without leaving a trace, but normally the data entry carried out
smoothly, this was reported by 4+ data entry operator  at difftrent time
so it I belive it is true, our programmers have check their cobol
programs, I have my self check the system software and Hardware with the
help of IBM but all is ok!, no errors are reported by fsck or AIX error
log. Last day we have lost 3000+ transactions because of this, any ideas
or help or tips will be most welcome
Thanks in advance

Rizwan Abbasi
```
