#### ↳ Re: working storage

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71tdsv$eh7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364229dd.180703@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
No, they are different programs.  Each is a completely separate
entity.

G Moore wrote in message <364229dd.180703@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>ok, if you have two modules
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: working storage

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36424942.B6F@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<364229dd.180703@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <71tdsv$eh7$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> No, they are different programs.  Each is a completely separate
> entity.

Strictly speaking that's true.  If the two variables are in two 
different WORKING STORAGE sections, declared as shown in the original
post, then they are distinct.  The similarity of name and PIC makes no 
difference.

However, there are arrangements whereby two modules may share a common 
variable (depending on what features are supported by your compiler):

1. You declare the variable as EXTERNAL.

2. In nested programs, you declare the variable as GLOBAL.

3. You provide multiple entry points for a single module.  Since
those entry points use the same WORKING STORAGE, they may access the
same variables.  This isn't really a sharing between modules, since
it's the same module.  However, the distinction is not obvious from
the standpoint of the calling code, since the CALL statements look
the same whether the entry points are in the same module or not.

Each technique has its advantages and disadvantages.  In my own work
I use the third approach extensively, and I have never used the other
two.  Others may have different tastes and probably do.

> G Moore wrote in message <364229dd.180703@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
> >ok, if you have two modules
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> >gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<364229dd.180703@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <71tdsv$eh7$1@news.igs.net> <36424942.B6F@swbell.net>`

```
DLL modules.

>> entity.
```

<a id="t-dbdb33f14a"></a>
## Thread: Command-Line arguemnts for MFcobol 4.1 for Solaris — 2 msgs

### Command-Line arguemnts for MFcobol 4.1 for Solaris

- **From:** ggarrett@unconfigured.xvnews.domain (Garry Garrett)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71t5l2$9u4@omantsrv14.csgsystems.com>`

```
Hi!  I'm a Systems Administrator for a Solaris (2.5.1) machine.
We put MicroFocus Cobol 4.1 on it.  The development box for this
project is an AIX box, also with MicroFocus cobol (I would have
made the developlment box have the same O.S., but I'm sure the
powers that be don't understand that all unix isn't the same...).

I had a cobol class about 10 years ago, and haven't touched it
since (which is why I don't follow this newsgroup, so I'd appreciate
e-mail responses if you could; and I would post my e-mail address
here, but every time I have done that on Usenet I get TONS of spam)
so in your response please assume I have no knowledge of cobol.

Here lies the problem:  My programmers have no problems passing
arguments between programs, but they can't seem to read arguments
off of the command line.  The funny thing is, this works fine on
the AIX version of MFcobol that they have.  I'm thinking that I either
need a patch from Sun or from MicroFocus, otherwise the same exact
source code shouldn't work on AIX either.  My question is, has
anyone else out there seen this behavior, and if so, what did
you do (what patches did you get from whom) to fix it?

We have a service contract with Sun (so I can get patches from them)
and we are working on one from MicroFocus (gawd purchasing can be
slow at times).  I would just wait and call MicroFocus, but the
project is at a halt until we can resolve this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Command-Line arguemnts for MFcobol 4.1 for Solaris

<a id="t-ae719f38f4"></a>
## Thread: Help — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help

- **From:** "Radoux Claude" <claude.radoux@euronet.be>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71t7nh$ds2@news3.euro.net>`

```
Hello,
I don't know how to make a loop (like a perform) that stop when a key is
pressed.
Thank for your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lMA02.27$Rk.4883@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<71t7nh$ds2@news3.euro.net>`

```
Radoux Claude wrote:
>
>I don't know how to make a loop (like a perform) that stop when a key
>is pressed.

The answer to your problem is compiler dependent.  There is no standard
COBOL syntax for testing if a key has been pressed.  For example, in
Micro Focus COBOL there are extensions for this.  In some Windows based
compilers you could call a Windows API function.  In some DOS based
compilers you could call a DOS function.  Let us know what compiler and
platform you are working with and we will try to kelp you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298309.66094.20288@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71t7nh$ds2@news3.euro.net>`

```
In message <<71t7nh$ds2@news3.euro.net>> "Radoux Claude" <claude.radoux@euronet.be> writes:
> I don't know how to make a loop (like a perform) that stop when a key is
> pressed.

Good.  Looping while waiting for a keystroke uses up all the
CPU it can get hold of.  This is a very bad thing on reasonable
systems, or even on Windows, where other programs could actually
do something useful with the processor.

If you do actually find a way of doing this then please also
include an OS despatch withing the loop.  This can be done
under DOS or DOS boxes under Windows or other multitaskers
and DOS multiusers using INT 0x2F with AX=0x1680.

I just did a program that scanned a directory looking for
new files (or an abandon signal from keyboard).  It uses
almost zero CPU time while idling.

First check if there is a timeout clause on your ACCEPT
statement.  If not you need to specify the compiler and
environment.

> Thank for your help.
```

## Thread: Microfocus report to NT printer — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus report to NT printer

- **From:** britishvicar@yahoo.com (Karl O)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641e0f4.166324421@news.enter.net>`

```
Help!!

I have a MF COBOL prog that writes to LPT1 and works fine. Now the
printer is redirected to a Windows NT shared printer. The OPEN gets a
status 37. If I TYPE a file to LPT1 it prints just fine....whats my
problem???

Thanks
Karl
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus report to NT printer

- **From:** britishvicar@yahoo.com (Karl O)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3641f0c5.170373624@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<3641e0f4.166324421@news.enter.net>`

```
Thanks anyway...I found the solution. Found an NT configuration that
could be set up.

Thanks

On Thu, 05 Nov 1998 17:33:57 GMT, britishvicar@yahoo.com (Karl O)
wrote:

>Help!!
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Karl
```

##### ↳ ↳ Solution!!!

- **From:** britishvicar@yahoo.com (Karl O)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36432fa7.252007788@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<3641e0f4.166324421@news.enter.net> <3641f0c5.170373624@news.enter.net>`

```
The answer was to change the properties defined for the NT printer.
The is an option to "Print immediately (default) or to "Spool the
entire document before printing"
It appears that OPEN OUTPUT needing exclusive control of the printer
is in direct conflict with the default option.

Hope this helps...it worked for me

Cheers

Karl 

On Thu, 05 Nov 1998 18:39:36 GMT, britishvicar@yahoo.com (Karl O)
wrote:

>Thanks anyway...I found the solution. Found an NT configuration that
>could be set up.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: copy replacing part of words, instead of complete word — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### copy replacing part of words, instead of complete word

- **From:** ruudvisser@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


COPY "IOHANDLE.PRC" replacing ==XXXX== by ==CUST==.

In this copy there are statements like

         Move XXXX       to ANOTHER-FIELD.       <-- is replaced
         Move USER-INPUT to KEY-XXXX.            <-- should also be replaced


I want all XXXX's to be replaced, but the replace only works on the complete
word. Is there any kind of wild-character ???????

please help me (to prevent hunderds of copybooks instead of just 1)



-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: copy replacing part of words, instead of complete word

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3647d015.6286801@news-server>`
- **References:** `<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Thu, 05 Nov 1998 09:39:02 GMT, ruudvisser@my-dejanews.com
enlightened us:

>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own    

You might try the following:

COPY "IOHANDLE.PRC" replacing ==XXXX== by ==CUST==
                                       replacing ==-XXXX== by ==-CUST.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Q:  What was the first question they asked Linda Tripp at her make over?
A:  Paper or plastic!

 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: copy replacing part of words, instead of complete word

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71t41i$q2n@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3647d015.6286801@news-server>`

```
I don't think the post to which I am responding is correct.  Please see
below for corrected (ANSI'85 Standard) solution.

SkippyPB wrote in message <3647d015.6286801@news-server>...
>On Thu, 05 Nov 1998 09:39:02 GMT, ruudvisser@my-dejanews.com
>enlightened us:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>         Move USER-INPUT to KEY-XXXX.            <-- should also be
replaced
>>
>>
>>I want all XXXX's to be replaced, but the replace only works on the
complete
>>word. Is there any kind of wild-character ???????
>>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

COPY "IOHANDLE.PRC" replacing ==:XXXX:== by ==CUST==.

In this copy there are statements like

     Move :XXXX:       to ANOTHER-FIELD.       <-- is replaced
     Move USER-INPUT to KEY-:XXXX:.            <-- should also be replaced

Notes:

   A) This means the COPY member will NOT compile cleanly - if "xxxx" is not
replaced.
   B) The next Standard will have cleaner (specific) syntax for doing
"partial word replacement" (which is what this is).
   C) Instead of the colons ":" you can use opening and closing
parenthesis -- however, many people think that the colon is a "cleaner"
variation.
   D) If you want to know the technical reason why this works, you need to
understand the difference between a "text word" and a "COBOL word" -
something that normally makes little or no difference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: copy replacing part of words, instead of complete word

- **From:** spolacek@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71tcms$nvk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3647d015.6286801@news-server>`

```
In article <3647d015.6286801@news-server>,
  swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB) wrote:
> On Thu, 05 Nov 1998 09:39:02 GMT, ruudvisser@my-dejanews.com
> enlightened us:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 If you have COBOL/MVS you may want to try using the REPLACE command.
This is different from the REPLACE option of the COPY command.

I included the documentation for the REPLACE command.

You would code the REPLACE command as follows:

REPLACE ==XXXX== by ==CUST==.
COPY IOHANDLE.PRC.
REPLACE OFF.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                       *** REPLACE STATEMENT ***

 The REPLACE statement is a compiler-directing statement which  provides
 an  automatic  method  of applying a change to an entire source program
 (i.e.,  without having to manually identify and modify all places  that
 need  to  be  changed).  It  is  especially  handy  for  simple  string
 substitutions.

      Format 1
      --------
        REPLACE ==pseudo-text1== BY ==pseudo-text2==.

 Each occurence of pseudo-text1 is replaced by the corresponding pseudo-
 text2 value.

      Format 2
      --------
        REPLACE OFF.

 The  Format  2  REPLACE  statement  terminates  any  text   replacement
 currently in effect.  If a Format 2 REPLACE statement is not specified,
 a  Format  1  REPLACE  statement  will  remain in effect until the next
 appearance of a Format 1 REPLACE statement or  until  the  end  of  the
 source program.

 The REPLACE statement must be terminated by  a  separator  period  and,
 unless  it  is  the  first  statement  in  the source program,  must be
 preceded by a separator period.

 Pseudo-text1  must  contain  one  or more text words.  Pseudo-text2 may
 contain one or more text words;  alternatively, it may be a "null" item
 (i.e., == ==).

 The Format 1 REPLACE statement may consist of a single BY phrase or  it
 may  be  specified with two or more BY phrases.  Each pseudo-text1 item
 specified is replaced by the corresponding pseudo-text2 item  specified
 in the same BY phrase.

 Pseudo-text1 in the first BY phrase is compared to an equivalent number
 of  contiguous source program text words starting with the first source
 program text word following the  REPLACE  statement.  If  no  match  is
 found,  the  comparison  is  repeated with each successive occurence of
 pseudo-text1.  When all occurences of pseudo-text1 have  been  compared
 and no match has been found, the comparison operation moves to the next
 successive source program text word.

 When a match is found, the corresponding pseudo-text2 item replaces the
 matching pseudo-text1 item.  The comparison  operation  then  continues
 starting  with  the  source program text word which immediately follows
 the rightmost text word that was just replaced.

 The source program segment which follows:

      REPLACE ==RPL== BY ==EMP==.
      01  RPL-RECORD.
          05  RPL-KEY    PIC X(5).
          05  RPL-DATA   PIC X(95).
      REPLACE OFF.

 would be modified as shown below:

      01  EMP-RECORD.
          05  EMP-KEY    PIC X(5).
          05  EMP-DATA   PIC X(95).







-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: copy replacing part of words, instead of

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298309.64264.19109@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In message <<71rrnm$hpj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>> ruudvisser@my-dejanews.com writes:
> 
> COPY "IOHANDLE.PRC" replacing ==XXXX== by ==CUST==.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> word. Is there any kind of wild-character ???????

The replace only works on Cobol words, this is the defined
behaviour.  What you need to do is use a Cobol word instead
of XXXX.  For example (XXXX).  So your copy book would
be:

              MOVE (XXXX)      TO Another-Field
              MOVE User-Input  TO Key-(XXXX)

Then:

            COPY "file"   REPLACING ==(XXXX)== BY ==CUST==.

will do as you require.
```

<a id="t-b19989d894"></a>
## Thread: CICS date forwarding — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### CICS date forwarding

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36425BD6.2693@sprynet.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a way to easily change the date and time that
is returned in the EIBDATE and EIBTIME for CICS programs?
Basically I'd like to do a CICS transaction on the fly that will
allow me to forward the date by a certain number of days and a
certain number of minutes.  This is, of course, for Year 2000
testing.  We do have a separate Y2K LPAR that can be IPL'd to
a particular date, but as far as I can tell it's a time consuming
pain to do that, plus only the systems programmers know how to do
it.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS date forwarding

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364294C3.A17F3AB2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<36425BD6.2693@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know of a way to easily change the date and time that
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Frank Swarbrick

Frank, if you go to my web page at:
    http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/y2kdl.htm
There's a link to an IBM web page where you can download IBM SupportPak
CH15, the Time Machine for CICS.  It is licensed software, but there is
NO CHARGE for it.  You will need PKUNZIP or INFOZIP to decompress it,
but the entire package will fit on 3.5 inch floppy decompressed. 
There's also a manual in Adobe Acrobat format, and one in another
format.  You will need to TSO/ISPF to deblock the runtime library after
transfering it from the PC to the Mainframe.  You may need a systems
programmer to install it for you.

I heartily recommend it.  We used it extensively for unit-testing our
CICS applications starting in 1997.  CH15 works on CICS 3.3, 4.1, or
Transaction Server for OS/390.  It gives you a transaction (CTMC) that
allows you to set the date and time up to 31/12/2099 and a PF key to
restore the system date and time.

Good luck!
```

## Thread: origin of 88 level — 68 msgs

### origin of 88 level

- **From:** Jedi__Master@a.galaxy.far.far.away.com (Jedi)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com>`

```
Does anyone know where the term 88 level originated?  and 77 level?

I understand the meaning and how these are used. What I am searching for
is how they came to be called "88 and 77".  My guess is that it was
arbitrary decision.  However, if anyone has any folklore or even a good
story for the newsgroup. Some facts would be appreciated. (this is not
homework , really). I would especially like to know if there was some
precedent in another language or assembler for the choice of the numbers.

Thanks,

Jedi
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ccbl complier, then how to run it?

- **From:** hartmutschindler@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72u3oo$jcg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <Pine.SOL.3.95.981106231939.10894A-100000@me.udel.edu>`

```
In article <Pine.SOL.3.95.981106231939.10894A-100000@me.udel.edu>,
  Wei Lu <luw@me.udel.edu> wrote:
> Hi, I tried to use the AcuCOBOL in the unix environment, first I use
> ccbl filename, then I have the cbl.out. But I don't know that how I can
> run it and see the running result.
>
>

hi, Wei Lu

you have to use
      runcbl cbl.out

following paramater are avalible:

    -d = enter debugger
    -c filename = alternate /etc/cblcinfig file
    -e filename = file to be open for error messages
    +e filenema = file to wich error messages are appended
    -l = list contents of configuration file in error output
    -o filename = file to be opened for display output
    -vv = print the current version number of runcbl
    -w = supress warning messages when non-numeric data is used as numeric

example:
     runcbl -d cbl.out


Best wishes,
Hartmut Schindler


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648a59c.488482@news3.ibm.net>`

```
vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

>On 10 Nov 1998 06:44:18 PST, tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler) wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure.

Because it almost has been forgotten, and there are other ways to
achieve your programming needs.

A. REDEFINES handles almost all needs, especially at the 01 Level.
B. In my 18yrs of programming, I've very rarely seen it used.
C. Have yet to find a COBOL text book that will even discuss 66 levels
D. Many shops ban it's use, instead use the deliminated REPLACING
clause, when a rename is needed.

Whether or not REPLACING is an ANSI standard, I'm not sure.  But I've
seen it used quite extensively in IBM Mainframe.

>Just keep in mind that certain copybooks et al. are cast in stone an may not be modified
>by anyone....
>

There are always exceptions, such as when the copybook is external and
proprietary, but shop internal copybooks are rarely, if ever, cast in
stone.  But when it does happen, you can still rename your proprietary
copybook, by using your own copybook, and REDEFINE it:

COPY  THEIRCPY
COPY  MYCPY
            REPLACING ==MYLVL01.==
                           BY  ==MYLVL01 REDEFINES THEIR-01.==.

Or if REPLACING isn't available and your copybook's sole reason for
existing is to redefine a specific proprietary copybook, set the 01
level as:  01 MYLVL01 REDEFINES THEIR-01.


And now that you've started, I feel the same way about Level 77s.
They've outlived their usefulness.  In mainframe apps, they were used
to set the field to the next halfword boundary, which helped when
reading Dumps some 15-18yrs ago, before the wide usage of ABEND and
Dump reading tools such as Abend-Aid.

Now, I never have to read Dumps.  Now, I look up the address of the
ABEND, error msg and explanation, value in problem field, which
program, linklib, when compiled, and then scroll through the ABENDed
program's compiled listing, through it's Hexloc of it's MAP, and then
back to the source line that it x-referenced.  Which is 100-fold
better than what it was leafing through an 18" high paper listing of a
Dump, trying to calculate hex offsets.

I can't think of anything that a 77 Level can do, that an 01 Level
can't.

88 Levels should be used in limited quantities and have specific value
naming.  My current pain in my side, is a huge program with well over
2 dozen fields using 88 levels that in turn have anywhere from 3 to 25
(or more) 88 Level value fields.  Trying to keep track of which field
uses which 88 Level can be quite maddening.

Whenever I've used an 88 Level, I always tried to describe the field
value AND the field.  However, most programmers don't do this.


Tim Oxler
TEO Computer Technologies Inc.
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72chb1$j72$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648a59c.488482@news3.ibm.net> <3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
Tim Oxler wrote in message <3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com>...

>And now that you've started, I feel the same way about Level 77s.
>They've outlived their usefulness.  In mainframe apps, they were used
>to set the field to the next halfword boundary, which helped when
>reading Dumps some 15-18yrs ago, before the wide usage of ABEND and
>Dump reading tools such as Abend-Aid.


I've been tempted to respond to this subthread several times.

The description herein may be all well and good if the computing universe is
precisely defined as one in which the terms "halfword boundary" and "ABEND"
are applicable to every machine.

Unfortunately, that's not the computing universe as I see it, and it
certainly isn't the particular galaxy in which I find myself..

In my galaxy:

Using level 77 for alphanumeric items does not provide any functional
advantage over an elementary 01.   And there are some cases in which numeric
77-level items enjoy no advantage over elementary 01-level items or, for
that matter, subfields of a record.

But only 77-level numeric (and, for that matter,. 01-level elementary
numeric, which by and large are treated similarly) may be optimized into
on-stack variables.  Where this optimization is appropriate,  access is
direct (avoiding access through descriptors) and in binary form (regardless
of declared usage, thus avoiding decimal-to-binary conversion).  This
provides significant execution-time performance over the unoptimized
equivalents.

On *REAL* machines, anyway!!!     ;-)

The point of all this is that to propose the elimination of a feature
because it doesn't provide any benefit *on the machines one is familiar
with* strikes me as evidentiary of a somewhat provincial attitude if it can
be shown that the feature provides a benefit on other machines.  Unisys A
Series COBOL74 (and for that matter, COBOL(68) which some users continue to
demand) distinguishes carefully between 77/01-elementary and subfields of a
record, and that distinction is of considerable value to users of those
compilers (and those machines).

        -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

```
Sorry Chuck,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365c8afd.5128566@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648a59c.488482@news3.ibm.net> <3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com> <72chb1$j72$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 11 Nov 1998 09:23:52 -0800, charles.stevens@unisys.com wrote:

[snip]
>The point of all this is that to propose the elimination of a feature
>because it doesn't provide any benefit *on the machines one is familiar
>with* strikes me as evidentiary of a somewhat provincial attitude if it can
>be shown that the feature provides a benefit on other machines.
[more snip]

Agreed.  No feature or standard should be viewed only as it applies to
one machine.

While we certainly cannot forever retain every obsolete feature,
another thing that should be taken into account is the amount of
program conversion that will be necessary just to make existing code
conform to a new compiler.  Although changing such things as 77 to 01
in the level indicator area is not usually a big deal, changes to some
other things, such as 66 levels, (referenced elsewhere in this thread)
often are somewhat tricky.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365e4ef9.5401763@netnews>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648a59c.488482@news3.ibm.net> <3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com> <72chb1$j72$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <365c8afd.5128566@news1.frb.gov>`

```
'Twas Wed, 25 Nov 1998 22:56:46 GMT, when Lookin'@You.2 (WDS) illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>While we certainly cannot forever retain every obsolete feature,
>another thing that should be taken into account is the amount of
>program conversion that will be necessary just to make existing code
>conform to a new compiler.  Although changing such things as 77 to 01
>in the level indicator area is not usually a big deal, <snip>

But it is!  On Unisys A Series, 77 level numeric fields are stored
on-stack, whereas 01 level fields are always off-stack.  Losing this
distinction would seriously impact some existing applications.  

Of course, if J4 abolished 77, Unisys would keep it as an extension.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365c8af8.5123184@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648a59c.488482@news3.ibm.net> <3649926a.4581539@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
On 11 Nov 1998 07:24:23 PST, Tim Oxler wrote:

[snip]
>88 Levels should be used in limited quantities and have specific value
>naming.  My current pain in my side, is a huge program with well over
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>value AND the field.  However, most programmers don't do this.

There are several schools of thought on this one.  I generally use 88
level items instead of coding literals in the procedure code.  This
can reduce maintenance requirements in data validation routines and
the like when values change or new values are added.  In some
situations, confusion can be minimized by having the name contain some
indicator of the value, or at least a useful description.

I agree about the problem often seen in programs where the various 88
level items floating around are difficult to track.  The solution to
this is also in the naming--use some prefix system that ties it to the
associated data name (and, hopefully, to the record name in which the
data name is defined).  Unfortunately, one often has to weed through
such things that were poorly written by someone else.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net>`

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net>...
>On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 08:48:15 -0600, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:
>>Fine with me, and the 77 as well!
>???  There is no reason NOT to use a 7 level, at least not on IBM Mainframe
environment.
>WHat's there to dislike about 77 levels?


I know I should have kept my mouth shut, but since I didn't here goes:

Traditional Cobol has no local variables. Every data-item is global to
the entire program and can be used by code in every paragraph.

This leads sometimes to nasty and hard-to-find bugs, where one
paragraph uses a certain data-item and also performs other
paragraphs. Due to maintenance, some of these other paragraph
end up also using the that data-item for some other purpose,
with not-so-good results.

You can guard against that to a large extent by defining an 01 level
data block with the 'local' variables for the paragraph, complete with
a suitable prefix to indicate that:

01  customer-validation-vars.
      02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
      02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(1).
....

        perform customer-validation
.....

 customer-validation.
      if cust-valid-data-item-1  ....
            perform zip-code-validation      ....
      .

--------

If you use 77 level data-items you lose the benefit of the
above convention and tend to end up with errorprone
constructions like this;

77  temp1     pic x(10).
77  temp2     pic x(1).

A maintenance programmer in need for a temporary 10-character
variable may look thru the code and say: "gee, there is a handy one
to use (the 77 level temp1)" and use it without being aware of its
use elsewhere or of its "time-to-live". You might counter that no-one
in his right mind would do such a thing, but that is just sticking your
head in the sand.

---------

I could go on and on, but do I need to?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```



>
> 01  customer-validation-vars.
>       02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
>       02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(1).

Your code snippit has duplicate variable names, a Freudian slip?  You see, the
problem you have identified is in the choice of data names, not the choice of
level numbers.  Renaming the "77"s  as:
    77  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
    77  cust-valid-data-item-2     pic x(1).
would solve the problem for the hypothetical maintenance programmer.  Since your
argument was fatally flawed by a slip twixt the finger and the key I presume the
"force" was telling you something.  Something like "always use descriptive
variable names, for God gave us life and COBOL gives us 30 characters, use both
to the maximum  ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364a53f8.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net> <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com>`

```

Scott Ramey wrote in message <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com>...
>> 01  customer-validation-vars.
>>       02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
>>       02  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(1).
>
>Your code snippit has duplicate variable names, a Freudian slip?

slip yes, but not Freudian.

>problem you have identified is in the choice of data names, not the choice
of
>level numbers.  Renaming the "77"s  as:
>    77  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
>    77  cust-valid-data-item-2     pic x(1).
>would solve the problem for the hypothetical maintenance programmer.

not really, because 77 levels are supposed to be independent and nothing
prevents the maintenance programmer to insert other items between them.

Imagine that the customer validation code is needed in another program,
then you would just pick up the 01-level and the code and reuse those,
rather than hunt down the 77 levels.  You *could* say that the programmer
is not supposed to insert anything between existing 77-levels with the same
prefix, but then you are just promoting these to a hidden 01-level.

Anyway, as I said I should have kept my mouth shot.
The maintenance problems I'm describing are not hypothetical
(I could never dram something horrible like that up), but are real life
observations. Pain that I have felt myself. If you have felt less pain
and seen no problems, praise the Lord for your luck or genius
(whichever it may be).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S2n22.821$LY1.661@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net> <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com>`

```

Scott Ramey wrote in message <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com>...
>
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Your code snippit has duplicate variable names, a Freudian slip?  You see,
the
>problem you have identified is in the choice of data names, not the choice
of
>level numbers.  Renaming the "77"s  as:
>    77  cust-valid-data-item-1     pic x(10).
>    77  cust-valid-data-item-2     pic x(1).
>would solve the problem for the hypothetical maintenance programmer.  Since
your
>argument was fatally flawed by a slip twixt the finger and the key I
presume the
>"force" was telling you something.
[...]
>
You seem to be confusing small programs versus large programs.

Are you saying that having 100 or 200 well-named data items as
level-77 is less of a problem for the maintenance programmer than
having those same well-named data items included within
well-named groups?

IMHO, there are no truly "independent" data items. A data item is
either associated with a record or table for which a level-01 is appropriate
or the data item is associated with a procedure or group of procedures
for which a level-01 is appropriate.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A011B.376B0887@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net> <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com> <S2n22.821$LY1.661@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
> IMHO, there are no truly "independent" data items. A data item is
> either associated with a record or table for which a level-01 is appropriate
> or the data item is associated with a procedure or group of procedures
> for which a level-01 is appropriate.

Even constants and subscripts should be with the data they are
associated with, and 77 levels don't like that.  I don't believe in
sharing subscripts across tables.  I typically have the following:
 01  SUBCODE-RANGE-MAX          PIC 9(03)   VALUE 50.
 01  SUBCODE-RANGE-COUNT        PIC 9(03)   VALUE ZERO.
 01  SUBCODE-RANGE-IDX          PIC 9(03)   VALUE ZERO.
 01  SUBCODE-RANGE-TABLE.
     05  SUBCODE-RANGES                 OCCURS 50 TIMES.
         10  SUBCODE-RANGE-1    PIC X(05).
         10  SUBCODE-RANGE-2    PIC X(05).

(I load with SUBCODE-RANGE-COUNT, then process with SUBCODE-RANGE-IDX)
Then this is repeated for the next table.  Of course, I prefer to avoid
tables altogether if I can.

I group my EOF-SWITCHES together with 88's, and don't use the same EOF
switch for two different reads.

I just don't write 77 levels and can't think of why I would want to have
an isolated data name at the front of WORKING-STORAGE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72d04u$620$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <3648eddd.0@news1.ibm.net> <36499A0F.E307446D@bdssoftware.com> <S2n22.821$LY1.661@news1.atlantic.net> <364A011B.376B0887@home.com>`

```
It has been unnecessary to position 77-level items before any 01-level items
since the standard revisions of 1973.

    -Chuck Stevens

appropriate
>> or the data item is associated with a procedure or group of procedures
>> for which a level-01 is appropriate.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> It has been unnecessary to position 77-level items before any 01-level items

That's funny.  I learned COBOL sometime before then and used ANSI-68 for
```

- **From:** boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee)

- **Message-ID:** `<364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net>`

```
vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

>On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 08:48:15 -0600, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Level 77 was introduced before the concepts of Structurd Programming
became well known. Once  Structured Programming together with its
implicit consideration for matters such program organization, modular
chesion, inter-modular coupling became accepted, the reason for the
existence of  totally independent, unrelated level 77 fields (which
cannot be time-related, usage-related, locality-related, etc  to any
other field in the program) became non-existent.

Boon Lee
Melbourne PC User Group
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364bfaec.691140@news.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au> <364bee3f.858644@news3.ibm.net>`

```
vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

>On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 10:58:47 GMT, boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee) wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
To me, a structured program implies, among many other things, good
organisation, not only in the Procedure Division, but ALSO in the data
division where related items should be grouped together under a 01
level  for example. There  are very, very few items in a program which
cannot be related to some other item. For example the various switches
used in a program can be grouped under one 01 level    such as :
01 ws-switches.
     03  end-of-filea-sw     pic x(01).
          88 end-of-filea     value 'y'.
    03 end-of fileb-sw      pic x(01).-
          88 end-of-fileb    value 'y'
     -
     -
    etc_

Similarly, all the counters used, all the totals fields  can  be
grouped under   01 levels with the subfields giving a better
description of the field.

 A program which is structured only in the Procedure Division but not
organised well enough in the Data Division is still on the whole not
'structured.'.


Regards
===================================================

Boon Lee
Melbourne PC User Group

E-mail : boonlee@melbpc.org.au

===================================================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c1113.1244689@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au> <364bee3f.858644@news3.ibm.net> <364bfaec.691140@news.melbpc.org.au>`

```
On Fri, 13 Nov 1998 09:48:01 GMT, boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee) wrote:

>vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:
>

>To me, a structured program implies, among many other things, good
>organisation, not only in the Procedure Division, but ALSO in the data
>division
agreed so far

> where related items should be grouped together under a 01
>level  for example.

Agreement ends here <g>  If several data records are processed in one part of the
structured program, they still would be in separate 01 levels.  Also, 01 level might come
in handy to force alignment for BINARY data (COMP-4)
>
>Similarly, all the counters used, all the totals fields  can  be
>grouped under   01 levels with the subfields giving a better
>description of the field.

This contradict your previous idea - Related data should be together.  Just being a
counter doesn't relate one data item to another counter.  Especially counters would be
good candidates for 77 levels
>
> A program which is structured only in the Procedure Division but not
>organised well enough in the Data Division is still on the whole not
>'structured.'.

agreed again

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364be0bf.37518865@news.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au> <364bee3f.858644@news3.ibm.net> <364bfaec.691140@news.melbpc.org.au> <364c1113.1244689@news3.ibm.net>`

```
vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

>On Fri, 13 Nov 1998 09:48:01 GMT, boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee) wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Sorry, I think that you have misunderstood me here. Of couse, if a
file has different record types, they, of course will have to be
described under different 01 levels. When I said 'Data Division', I
meant the Working Storage Section, not the File section. File Records
in the File Section are usaully described by System Designers and are
usually well thought of. However data in the Working Storage Section
are put in by Programmers (me included!) and under pressure of time,
some of them may not have undergone as much thought as they should
have deserved.
>>Similarly, all the counters used, all the totals fields  can  be
>>grouped under   01 levels with the subfields giving a better
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
No, I have not contradicted myself. There IS a relationship between
two fields if they are both counters. I agree that the relationship is
weak, but nevertheless, there is a relationship, and that is, they are
both counters. To me, grouping several counter fields under one 01
level (appropriately named)  is better than having separate level 77
fields, each a counter, which does not IMMEDIATELY convey to a reader
that they are all counters of some sort.

Just my opinion; you are of course entitled to your own opinion.

Best Wishes,


===================================================

Boon Lee
Melbourne PC User Group

E-mail : boonlee@melbpc.org.au

===================================================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Wayne L. Beavers" <wayneb@mail.beyond-software.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36504927.8672EAF1@mail.beyond-software.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au> <364bee3f.858644@news3.ibm.net> <364bfaec.691140@news.melbpc.org.au> <364c1113.1244689@news3.ibm.net>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 13 Nov 1998 09:48:01 GMT, boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee) wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> good candidates for 77 levels

Declaring two fields (counters) within a single 01 level instructs
the compiler that the two fields must be defined in storage as
adjacent fields. Two 77 level items tells the compiler that the
compiler is free to allocate the fields anywhere it wants to. The
compiler can do a better job of optimizing the 77 level items.
When the fields are independent they should be defined as
independent. Unless you think that you can optimize virtual
storage utilization better than the compiler can.

Years ago the compiler would allocate fields in the order that the
were declared. Now the compiler moves everything around to
optimize the code. Years ago you could define a 77 level field as
'Working storage starts here', and if it was the first data item
that would be true. Now the compiler moves it to wherever it
decides is best.

Even though machines are much faster today than they used to be,
it seems to me that you ought not to disable the compiler
optimization capabilities unnecessarily. Even though your program
might be used only once a day today, next month it might be called
a million times per hour.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36505298.B1F4550F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <3648522d.0@news1.ibm.net> <3649a60c.599832@news3.ibm.net> <364abf49.42812781@news.melbpc.org.au> <364bee3f.858644@news3.ibm.net> <364bfaec.691140@news.melbpc.org.au> <364c1113.1244689@news3.ibm.net> <36504927.8672EAF1@mail.beyond-software.com>`

```
> Years ago the compiler would allocate fields in the order that the
> were declared. Now the compiler moves everything around to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> a million times per hour.

It depends.  If reading a dump enables you to do maintenance and get a
broken application up quickly, then it may be that you do NOT allow the
optimizer to move fields around.  Of course, if your environment creates
a formatted dump, this does not apply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 17:52:04, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:

> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Some choice names come to mind... <G>

I had a record defined with multiple levels.  Something like this.  
(Don't want to indicate the business).

01  Data-Record.
... buncha levels here ...
      05  Data-Area-1.
             10  Some-Stuff1 Pic X(20).
             10  Some-Stuff2 Pic X(10).
       05  Data-Area-2.
              10  Some-Stuff3 Pic X(5).
              10  Some-Stuff4 Pic x(10).
       05  Data-Area-C Pic X(5).
       66  Whole-Area Renames Some-Stuff2 Thru Data-Area-C.

I needed to reference the area from Some-Stuff2 thru Data-Area-C.

I know, I could have used reference modification - but I dislike it 
for this task.  When going back and reading the code, it's unclear.  I
prefer to do it in the data division and have a decent name for 
reference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72adnn$g7c$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>
>I had a record defined with multiple levels.  Something like this.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>reference.


While reference modification is nice in a case like this, I would have no
problem sticking with the good old Level 66.

Note that while reference modificaton was introduced in ANSI 1985 COBOL,
Level 66 significantly predates it --  in fact, my copy of "IBM DOS Full
American National Standard COBOL"  (file # S360-24, order # GC28-6394-4),
published May 15, 1972,  indicates that this feature is part of ANSI
X3.23-1968, not an extension.  Unisys A Series COBOL (68) also supports
RENAMES, and doesn't relegate it to the ANSI-74 extensions in the
documentation.

        -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 66 Level (was: Re: origin of 88 level)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364cbb83.10205669@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On 10 Nov 98 22:56:09 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 17:52:04, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:

>> Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>> >On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 06:44:18, tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com wrote:

>> >> Yuk, could we quietly forget the 66 rename clause?
>> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> show us

>
>I had a record defined with multiple levels.  Something like this.  
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I am sure that many instructors and standards-setters would say that
the "correct" way to do this would be to create another 01-level
record that REDEFINES your 01 Data-Record  record, and then define a
bunch of FILLERs for everything except the area defined by Whole-Area.

In many ways, the REDEFINES record could be preferable, particularly
if you had some more "buncha levels" after Data-Area-C, visually
making the RENAMES less obvious.  (The RENAMES must occur at the end
of the record description, even if it is hundreds of lines removed
from the area being renamed.)  This could prevent the RENAMES from
referencing the wrong data if the area that is being renamed is ever
changed.  There is also the potential for problems such as data
alignment, numeric/alpha usage, etc.  All of these problems can be
reduced by proper documentation and comments.

On the other hand, the RENAMES clause can be very useful (properly
documented) where the area being renamed is subject to being changed,
and the referencing code is not.  Obviously, this would usually be a
special situation.  Sometimes, using RENAMES just plain makes
something else easier; just document what you are doing.

In short, like most other things that some say not to do, there are
occasionally (though probably not many) good uses for RENAMES.
Properly used and documented, RENAMES serves its purpose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 66 Level (was: Re: origin of 88 level)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72iki2$aag@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364cbb83.10205669@news1.frb.gov>`

```

WDS wrote in message <364cbb83.10205669@news1.frb.gov>...
>On 10 Nov 98 22:56:09 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> >On Tue, 10 Nov 1998 06:44:18, tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com wrote:


  <much snippage>
>
>In short, like most other things that some say not to do, there are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


"Long ago and far away" when I worked for Micro Focus and represented them
on the J4 (then X3J4) committee, we were talking about features to enhance,
keep, make obsolete, or to make archaic (a new category that calls something
"yucky" but promises that it will be kept for at least one more Standard).
Personally, I suggested (internally) that we put 66-levels in either the
archaic or obsolete categories.  I was told, however, that this was a
"wonderful and useful" feature - and that the Micro Focus compiler (written
in COBOL of course) used them "all over the place."

I never did really understand why the MF people found them particularly
useful, but I do know that when J4 discussed the topic briefly, there was a
general consensus to keep them (and not to deprecate them at all).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 66 Level (was: Re: origin of 88 level)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Brian Annis <bannis@wnec.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364E07E1.549DEFFF@wnec.edu>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364cbb83.10205669@news1.frb.gov> <72iki2$aag@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> Personally, I suggested (internally) that we put 66-levels in either the
> archaic or obsolete categories.  I was told, however, that this was a
> "wonderful and useful" feature - and that the Micro Focus compiler (written
> in COBOL of course) used them "all over the place."

As someone who has occasionally used a 66-level, I would suggest that
"*potentially* wonderful and useful" would be more accurate. While
it's a great idea to be able to "redefine" things in the way that a
66-level does, it has too many restrictions. More freedom in its
placement would be a big help;  why *can't* you put one immediately
after the fields being renamed (but prior to additional elements in
the record), or after a copy module that defines several records
(when the fields you want to rename are *not* in the last one)?
The ability to use other descriptive clauses, like justified right
and usage, would help too.  66-levels should not be thrown away;
they should be enhanced.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 66 Level (was: Re: origin of 88 level)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72r0qi$drb@journal.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643aebc.0@news1.ibm.net> <364851c0.3503017@news.teo-computer.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-PUspRtlzZGrp@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36487d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kebOPdnFdW1Z@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364cbb83.10205669@news1.frb.gov> <72iki2$aag@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <364E07E1.549DEFFF@wnec.edu>`

```
On occasion I've used renames for various purposes but personally
I would much rather have a non-contiguous REDEFINES. An example in
a simplified layout:

01  RECORD-TYPE-1
    05 TYPE-1-KEY.
        10 TYPE-1-KEY-DATA     PIC X(20).
    05 TYPE-1-DATA.
        10 TYPE-1-DATA-INFO    PIC X(100).
    05 TYPE-2-KEY REDEFINES TYPE-1-KEY.
        10 TYPE-2-KEY-DATA     PIC X(20).
    05 TYPE-2-DATA REDEFINES TYPE-2-DATA.
        10 TYPE-2-DATA-INFO    PIC X(100).

On MVS cobol this won't compile because type-2-key does not immediately
precede type-1-key, etc. I realize the obvious answer here is to insert
another 01 and redefine the whole record but this is just an example to
illustrate the point.  Non-contiguous redefines would be very useful in
complicated layouts, they would eliminate the need for "most" 66 levels,
you could put them anywhere and they could have a picture clause. You
can ORG to anywhere in assembler (370). I see no reason why cobol has to
have this contiguous redefine restriction. Maybe someday?
```

#### ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3643D48B.E289D9E7@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com>`

```
Your guess was good. However, 9 had been tacked for pictures of numeric.
Working backwards, 88 came next, and probably was two digits because 01
was two digits. As they say, it was not rocket science.<HG> There was
nothing to guide us. Next one was 77, and then 66. 49 became the maximum
by default. At that time no user could point to any reason to have more
levels, as they could not (at that time) justify more than 30 character
data names or 18 character max money numbers.  We were not thinking
about the fact that we would have to accommodate the common instead of
the period for other countries.  Things happened, ideas were born, and
some made it. Others were left by the wayside. It EVOLVED.


Warren Simmons, Witness


Jedi wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know where the term 88 level originated?  and 77 level?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jedi
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3643D8FF.B9A66F20@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3643D48B.E289D9E7@inficad.com>`

```
This will teach me to re-read after spell checking.

"Warren G. Simmons" wrote:
> 
> Your guess was good. However, 9 had been PICKED(tacked) for pictures of numeric.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jedi

Also, 50 was used for segmentation.

As added information, the Honeywell product used as a sample contained
lots of reserved words and values for numbers. Many felt this limited
the users ability to assign names that fit the application. The 88 level
was a method of introducing lots of words without making them part of
the language. (As I recall, though feebly).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298310.77548.1845@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3643D8FF.B9A66F20@inficad.com>`

```
In message <<3643D8FF.B9A66F20@inficad.com>> "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com> writes:
> 
> Also, 50 was used for segmentation.

So was 00-49 and 51-99.  It just happens that those below 50
were permanent and above were overlayed (with variations for
certain environments).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298310.77346.1678@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3643D48B.E289D9E7@inficad.com>`

```
In message <<3643D48B.E289D9E7@inficad.com>> "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com> writes:
> Working backwards, 88 came next, and probably was two digits because 01

Nice guess, except for the teensey problem that 88s were added
15 years after 77s.
```

#### ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com>`

```
In message <<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com>> Jedi__Master@a.galaxy.far.far.away.com  writes:
> 
> I understand the meaning and how these are used. What I am searching for
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> precedent in another language or assembler for the choice of the numbers.

88 levels came much later than 66 and 77s - in fact in ANSI74.
I suspect that '77' was chosen because this is the highest
value in octal.  01 - 50 were used in record layouts, leaving
51 - 77 as good choices able to fit into a single character
value even on a 6 bit machine (in the compiler tables).

Choosing the high end for special values seems reasonable.

66 was probably added after 77.  88 carried on in '74 when
it probably wasn't such an issue to cater for 6 bit character
values and the compiler could in any case 'waste' a few
instructions replacing '88' with something that would fit.

And yes, I have used Cobol on 6 bit character machines.

Brought to you by the number 8,388,607 and the magic word
'XE13'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36454997.1182875@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) wrote:


>
>88 levels came much later than 66 and 77s - in fact in ANSI74.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm sorry, you are wrong on this. I can guarantee you that at least 2
prior versions of the standard contained 88 levels. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36466301.701FE026@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36454997.1182875@news3.ibm.net> <364644C2.B4FF3B23@hotmail.com>`

```
Level 66 is used for the RENAMES:
01 some-group.
     02  field1 pic x.
     02  field2 pic x.
     02  field3 pic x.
66 one-name RENAMES some-group.
66 another-name RENAMES field2 thru field3.

Segmentation is also a rarely used feature, even in the olden days. Today,
if you code it, the COBOL for MVS simply performs a  syntax check; has no
effect on  program execution.

Chris wrote:

> Scott Peterson wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jedi
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981109005316.14590.00000518@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364644C2.B4FF3B23@hotmail.com>`

```

I nevr heard of _fields_ participating in segmentation.

The level numbers in WORKING-STORAGE have absolutely nothing to do with the
segment level numbers in SECTION headers in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649A0EF.F5FA17D9@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36454997.1182875@news3.ibm.net> <364644C2.B4FF3B23@hotmail.com>`

```
I don't think octal had anything to do with it.  Back then most of the
machines were IBM S/360, Burroughs, Univac, and what not, were hex based,
used 8 bit bytes.  The choice of "77" was probably more closely related to
77 Sunset Strip or ez typing than anything else.  "66" and "88" just
continued the heritage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DDi22.1120$pz6.1350631@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36454997.1182875@news3.ibm.net> <364644C2.B4FF3B23@hotmail.com> <3649A0EF.F5FA17D9@bdssoftware.com>`

```
Scott Ramey wrote:
>I don't think octal had anything to do with it.  Back then most of the
>machines were IBM S/360, Burroughs, Univac, and what not, were hex based,
>used 8 bit bytes.

The main Burroughs line in those days was the B2500/B3500 series, later
called Medium Systems.  They were decimal machines.  The actual machine
addresses were decimal (packed BCD), the op codes were decimal, etc.
As you would expect, dumps were very easy to read.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>

```
> The main Burroughs line in those days was the B2500/B3500 series, later
> called Medium Systems.  They were decimal machines.  The actual machine
> addresses were decimal (packed BCD), the op codes were decimal, etc.
> As you would expect, dumps were very easy to read.

Yep, I forgot that.  I considered the MCP a godsend after mucking around with
S/360 DOS for a few years.  I loved those 5' high head per track disks, the
bullet proof 10 cps teletypes for the SPO, and device assignments that made
sense.  On the other hand, there were those 4 spindle tapes that broke every
other day, and Burroughs maintenance, or lack thereof.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72pnmi$oc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <3298310.71325.25038@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36454997.1182875@news3.ibm.net> <364644C2.B4FF3B23@hotmail.com> <3649A0EF.F5FA17D9@bdssoftware.com> <DDi22.1120$pz6.1350631@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <364E226E.49D5B149@bdssoftware.com>`

```
Scott Ramey wrote in message <364E226E.49D5B149@bdssoftware.com

<snip>

>... On the other hand, there were those 4 spindle tapes that broke every
>other day, and Burroughs maintenance, or lack thereof.


Yes, I remember the "Maytag" tape drives, and they did have some reliability
problems,. but in all fairness to Burroughs the price-per-tape-unit was hard
to beat, because for four tape drives there was no need to have more than
one capstan motor, one reel motor, and one vacuum source!  This was, of
course, in the days of pinch rollers ...

I count this device as analogous to the IBM 7330 tape drives used so often
on 1401's, or the 2415 drive beloved of 360/20 and 360/30 users.   We
replaced the 7330's with 729III drives not long after installing our 1401's
...

And in both cases -- IBM and Burroughs -- larger, faster and more reliable
drives were available, if you (or your management) wanted to spend the money
...

       -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364714F6.271BA1EA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com>`

```
Jedi wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know where the term 88 level originated?  and 77 level?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> precedent in another language or assembler for the choice of the numbers.

Just guessing.  Using level numbers for these meanings is easy for the
compiler and fits in with the structure of the rest of the code.  But
you don't want numbers which will be confused with standard level
numbers.  So you pick high numbers (above 49) which aren't multiples of
5, which are easily recognizable.  So they picked 66, 77, & 88 as these
easily visible numbers.  (Does anybody use 66 anymore?)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: origin of 88 level

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<727cj1$cpj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jedi__Master-0611981921010001@dialup90.jcn1.com> <364714F6.271BA1EA@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <364714F6.271BA1EA@home.com>...

>> Does anyone know where the term 88 level originated?  and 77 level?

>Just guessing.  Using level numbers for these meanings is easy for the
>compiler and fits in with the structure of the rest of the code.  But
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>easily visible numbers.  (Does anybody use 66 anymore?)

If I had to guess, I would guess that the double digits were more
a result of the fact that my generation could not type than anything
else<G>.  "keyboarding" indeed.  harumph.
```

## Thread: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also. — 77 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
My new book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours will be published 
in early December.  They want me to choose a "preview" chapter to put 
up on the Macmillan web site - for you all to read.

So which one should I put up?  

Here are some of the ones I think might be good candidates.  Tell me 
what you think - before Monday - and I'll tell them to use that 
chapter.

(this list does not correspond to chapter numbers)

1.  Advanced Reporting - control breaks are here. - I get more e-mail 
about how to do this than anything else.
2.  Master File Updating - we seem to have lots of questions here 
about that.
3.  Tables
4.   Date Processing (Including the Easter Calc - thanks Judson)
5.  Sorting
6.  Evaluate
7.  String and Unstring

What do you all think?
```

#### ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7202l0$g3j@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
My vote would be for "date processing" - as this has such a high profile in
the COBOL world these days.
```
