Are STRING and UNSTRING that new, or were just never used much?  In
COBOL class ten years ago, our instructor pointed out STRING and
UNSTRING, but skipped over it like it wasn't worth learning.  Which I
think was a shame; I love to use STRING (I don't think I've ever
unstrung).

kitty
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36462c3e.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net>`

```

Kitty Carr wrote in message <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net>...
>SkippyPB wrote:
>> I would say STRING and UNSTRING as these are relatively new COBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>unstrung).


No they are not that new, but for a long time they could not be used in
CICS (they allocated memory with a GETMAIN) so people didn't use
them in taht very important environment. Last I heard is that it is
now OK to use them under CICS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<726msl$1fs$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote in message <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net>...

>Are STRING and UNSTRING that new, or were just never used much?  In
>COBOL class ten years ago, our instructor pointed out STRING and
>UNSTRING, but skipped over it like it wasn't worth learning.  Which I
>think was a shame; I love to use STRING (I don't think I've ever
>unstrung).


I was a bit puzzled over that post, as I have been using them for
years.  I wrote an assembler in Cobol about 15 years back that
was almost nothing but STRING and UNSTRING.  It ran on a
Decsystem-20 (or maybe a 10).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3647AD08.8B4@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
> Are STRING and UNSTRING that new, or were just never used much?  In
> COBOL class ten years ago, our instructor pointed out STRING and
> UNSTRING, but skipped over it like it wasn't worth learning.  Which I
> think was a shame; I love to use STRING (I don't think I've ever
> unstrung).

It's my recollection that STRING had some problems in OS/VS COBOL under
MVS.  The target field was not space filled when it was larger than the
sending fields.  This may not have been a problem with STRING, it may
have been a problem with programmers understanding how STRING worked. 
So many installations simply banned it because it created more problems
than it solved. 

01  RESULT        pic x(10).

STRING '123' DELIMITED BY SIZE
       '456' DELIMITED BY SIZE
       INTO RESULT.

RESULT then contained '123456' followed by 4 undetermined characters
(whatever happenned to be in those storage locations), NOT spaces as
many people might think.  STRING is not a substitute for MOVE, and the
proper breakdown of group items into elementary items in
working-storage.

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <3647AD08.8B4@netbox.com>`

```
It sounds like the problem was not the STRING verb, but in not
initializing the output field.  We print a lot of different forms with
addresses and use STRING to:

MOVE SPACES TO NAME-ADDR-STRING.
MOVE FIVE-DIGIT-ZIP TO FIRST-5-DIGITS.
IF ZIP-PLUS-FOUR EQUAL SPACES OR ZEROS
    MOVE SPACES TO HYPHEN
                   LAST-4-DIGITS
ELSE
    MOVE '-' TO HYPHEN
    MOVE ZIP-PLUS-FOUR TO LAST-4-DIGITS.
STRING CITY DELIMITED BY '     '
    ', ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
    STATE-CODE DELIMITED BY SIZE
    '  ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
    NINE-DIGIT-ZIP DELIMITED BY SIZE
INTO NAME-ADDR-STRING.

We also use this in our CICS programs.  Before we used STRING, we used
subscripts and indexes to check every position in a field to determine
the length of the data.  How else would you do this? 


Bob Berman wrote:
> 
> Kitty Carr wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> ----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <3647AD08.8B4@netbox.com> <364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
> It sounds like the problem was not the STRING verb, but in not
> initializing the output field.  
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> INTO NAME-ADDR-STRING.

I agree with your point of initializing the target field, however many
programmers did not, thinking that STRING acted like MOVE.  But in your
example, why not simply define NAME-ADDR-STRING with elementary items of
CITY, STATE-CODE, & NINE-DIGIT-ZIP  and use MOVE. Your target field is a
fixed length field.  It would seem that for forms printing, you would
want these fields to end up in the same position, as in the window area
for enveloping & mailing ?  Do you then UNSTRING NAME-ADDR-STRING into
the detail print lines ?  All this for a variable length city name ?

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <3647AD08.8B4@netbox.com> <364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net> <364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com>`

```
The address fields of the incoming record are defined as:

03  NAME-ADDR-LINE-1            PIC X(36).
03  NAME-ADDR-LINE-2            PIC X(36).
03  NAME-ADDR-LINE-3            PIC X(36).
03  CITY                        PIC X(20).
03  STATE-CODE                  PIC X(2).
03  ZIP-CODE.
    05  ZIP-CODE-5              PIC X(5).
    05  ZIP-PLUS-4              PIC X(4).

If I used MOVE for city, state and zip, it would look like this:

Opp                 AL  352153543      
Birmingham          AL  352150000    (sometimes the last 4 digits come
in as zeros or spaces)

I use STRING so it will look like this:

Opp, AL  35215-3543      
Birmingham, AL  35215

I don't UNSTRING this into a detail print line, I STRING it into a
36-pos field (same length as the address lines) and move this field to a
detail print line.  And yes, all this for a variable length City name
and to print the zip code properly.  It looks better. 


Bob Berman wrote:
> 
> Kitty Carr wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> ----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net> <364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net>,
Kitty Carr  <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>The address fields of the incoming record are defined as:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Birmingham, AL  35215

Perhaps you might consider like:

STRING  CITY DELIMITED BY SPACES, 
        ', '  DELIMITED BY SIZE,
        STATE-CODE, '  ', ZIP-CODE-5, ZIP-PLUS-4
         DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO someplace.

Dealing with how to insert a '-' depending on a value of ZEROES for
ZIP-PLUS-4 is an extra-credit assignment, of course.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net> <364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net>,
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Huh??  I was explaining why I used STRING instead of MOVE.  I handled
the '-' by moving the input zip to a W-S field:

01  NINE-DIGIT-ZIP.
    03  FIRST-5-DIGITS       PIC X(5)    VALUE SPACES.
    03  HYPHEN               PIC X       VALUE SPACE.
    03  LAST-4-DIGITS        PIC X(4)    VALUE SPACES.

In STRING routine:

MOVE ZIP-CODE-5 (or whatever I called it) TO FIRST-5-DIGITS.
IF ZIP-PLUS-4 EQUAL SPACES OR ZEROS
    MOVE SPACES TO HYPEN
                   LAST-4-DIGITS
ELSE
    MOVE '-' TO HYPEN
    MOVE ZIP-PLUS-4 TO LAST-4-DIGITS.
STRING  (city, comma, state)
    NINE-DIGIT-ZIP DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO something.

Your way was better (shorter) as I as putting STATE-CODE DELIMITED BY
SIZE, '  ' DELIMITED BY SIZE, NINE-DIGIT-ZIP DELIMITED BY SIZE on three
separate lines, instead of one line as you did, so thanks for the
edification/education.  But as Demosthenes once said, (I think in 1938,)
"Education is the third greatest cause of human misery in the world. 
The first, of course, is life."

kitty
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net>,
Kitty Carr  <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>the '-' by moving the input zip to a W-S field:

Oh, don't worry... I gets confustigated easily.

>
>01  NINE-DIGIT-ZIP.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>edification/education.

Just a matter of style/shop standards/prejudices... I try to write
'clean-reading' code... how about this:

MOVE SPACES TO someplace.
IF ZIP-PLUS-FOUR = ZEROES OR SPACES
    STRING  CITY DELIMITED BY SPACES,
        ', '  DELIMITED BY SIZE,
        STATE-CODE, '  ', ZIP-CODE-5
         DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO someplace
ELSE
    STRING  CITY DELIMITED BY SPACES,
        ', '  DELIMITED BY SIZE,
        STATE-CODE, '  ', ZIP-CODE-5, '-', ZIP-PLUS-4
         DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO someplace.

*Now* comes the next knotty question... depending on the quality of your
data records either *with* +4 or *without* +4 will predominate; one might
easily argue that in the future there will be more *with* +4 than
*without* so the statement above is 'inefficient' since more data will
have to pass through both IF and ELSE, burning more CPU cycles.

This being correct (that the above example burns more CPU), then... what
reason can you give a PHM for coding it this way rather than using a
negative conditional which would be more efficient?

DD
```

> From Dilbert-land, a pointy-haired manager... so, what is a benefit in
> coding it the less-efficient way (assuming a majority of records which
> *do* contain the +4 characters) rather than using the negative
> conditional which is more efficient?

You - of all people - should know that NEGATIVE CONDITIONS confuse the heck
out of  programmers who come into the code later.  I am the undisputed
*KING* of the negative condition.  At my last place, there was a guy that
was so bugged by them he would take them OUT of the programs, making them
VERY confusing.  (At least to me - The King).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default>...
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Some COBOL coders just never seem to understand that the conditional
expression
      IF A NOT = B AND A NOT = C
is the Boolean equivalent of
      IF NOT (A = B OR C)
so they try to avoid compound negative conditionals altogether.

I prefer to put such compound conditional expressions within parentheses to
improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses to
separate out potentially confusing conditional expressions?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981116142149.04161.00001508@ng155.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<365074C2.71502D04@att.net>`

```
There ain't no such thing as a COBOL programmer confused or not about negative
logic. It is the associativity of the NOT operator that causes a dismay second
only to a flabbergasting change in standard per same. I think it is agreed that
parentheses dissolve the amibiguity as well as ameliorate the paresthensia of
those who are supercilious about unexplicated text.

Best Wishes, 

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72q1a7$3nt$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default> <OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...

>      IF A NOT = B AND A NOT = C
>is the Boolean equivalent of
>      IF NOT (A = B OR C)


I always write it out not only with the parentheses, but also on separate
lines that reflect the actual tests that I am doing (as opposed to the
boolean equivalent, which I grant one could calculate and put into the
code, but I think confuses the issue, as well as forcing the reader to
"uncalculate" the result in order to actually figure out what is going on)
end-sentence.  (I am told this is required for run-on sentences under
the new standard)<G>

So I would write the above as:

    if ((a is not equal to b)
         and
        (a is not equal to c))
          do-something-here.

>I prefer to put such compound conditional expressions within parentheses to
>improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

time and don't want to have to sacrifice TOO many trees in doing so<g>.)

to
>>improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses to
>>separate out potentially confusing conditional expressions?
```

Donald Tees wrote in message <72qn2c$4qt$1@news.igs.net>...
>Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>


My first thought on reading your prior comment and example was that you
probably worked in an 80x24 world and didn't do many printouts.  It seems I
was wrong, you are thriving in a 4x80x24 world<g>.  BTW, my use of the terms
80x24 is just a sort of generic reference to viewing a listing on a screen -
with WinDoze and hi-res, we can actually see more lines of code in a single
window nowadays, and a lot more in 4 windows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72vqlk$92v$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default> <OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03> <72q1a7$3nt$1@news.igs.net> <OYHFMwaE#GA.199@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <72qn2c$4qt$1@news.igs.net> <u8NGOkzE#GA.274@upnetnews03>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...

>My first thought on reading your prior comment and example was that you
>probably worked in an 80x24 world and didn't do many printouts.  It seems I


I find that one screen is not enough.  Actually, I had four.  A few
weeks back I decided to go high res, and went out and bought a 19"
screen and an electronic four to one switch for keyboard/mouse/screen.
I hooked all four computers up to a single screen/etc.  An it is
not enough ... I am either going to have to hook up another screen
or get some ink.  By the time you get two IDE's going, and maybe a DOS
window, there is just not enough screen area.

I think that if you do not like listings, then you pretty well need two
screens.
I can get them with the switch, but it makes it hard to read one and
translate to the other. I think I am going to hook up one 15 incher
just for that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Obm2Jg2E#GA.323@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default> <OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03> <72q1a7$3nt$1@news.igs.net> <OYHFMwaE#GA.199@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <72qn2c$4qt$1@news.igs.net> <u8NGOkzE#GA.274@upnetnews03> <72vqlk$92v$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Many years ago in my COBOL development career, I used to tell people that I
viewed the world and handled my programming problems 24 lines at a time.  I
have always resisted doing printouts (except for the final version of a
program for hard-copy archival purposes, but even that excuse for printing
program listings has gone away with availability of CD writing technology.)
The Sierra Club should commend both of us for all the trees we have
saved<g>.

Dennis J. Minette <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote in article 

> >>improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses to
> >>separate out potentially confusing conditional expressions?

Yes - with a caveat.  The DEC/VMS compiler sometimes behaves "strangely"
with complex conditions within parentheses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3650ed81.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default> <OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03> <72q1a7$3nt$1@news.igs.net> <OYHFMwaE#GA.199@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <01be11d1$513f69e0$43bc48a6@default>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be11d1$513f69e0$43bc48a6@default>...
>Dennis J. Minette <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote in article
>> >>improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses to
>> >>separate out potentially confusing conditional expressions?
>Yes - with a caveat.  The DEC/VMS compiler sometimes behaves "strangely"
>with complex conditions within parentheses.


other compilers too (and not only with conditionals). Below is a clip from
MATHPK
(of the ETKPAK collection http://www.etk.com/download ):

041700 EVALUATE-LOGARITHM-APPROX.
041800     COMPUTE MANTISSA-X = ONE-HALF + TRANSF--X * (LOG-COEFF1
041900                                   + TRANSF-XX * (LOG-COEFF3
042000                                   + TRANSF-XX * (LOG-COEFF5
042100                                   + TRANSF-XX *  LOG-COEFF7
042200                                                         )))
87/05/27

the strange parentheses in line 042200 have to be on a line by themselves,
ideally, they should be at the end of line 042100, but some version of
MicroFocus
on some Unix box back in 1987 abended during the compile if one moved the
parentheses up...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be11ea$d647c600$c5bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default> <OZxSBuWE#GA.274@upnetnews03> <72q1a7$3nt$1@news.igs.net> <OYHFMwaE#GA.199@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <01be11d1$513f69e0$43bc48a6@default> <3650ed81.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```


Leif Svalgaard <leif@ibm.net> wrote in article
<3650ed81.0@news1.ibm.net>...
> Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be11d1$513f69e0$43bc48a6@default>...
> >Dennis J. Minette <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote in article
> >> >>improve readability.  Do you also advocate  the use of parentheses
to
> >> >>separate out potentially confusing conditional expressions?
> >Yes - with a caveat.  The DEC/VMS compiler sometimes behaves "strangely"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> other compilers too (and not only with conditionals). Below is a clip
from
> MATHPK
> (of the ETKPAK collection http://www.etk.com/download ):
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the strange parentheses in line 042200 have to be on a line by
themselves,
> ideally, they should be at the end of line 042100, but some version of
> MicroFocus
> on some Unix box back in 1987 abended during the compile if one moved the
> parentheses up...

What I have seen with VMS COBOL is that with conditionals (Ands and Ors) is
that the parenthesis seem to be ignored.  This has meant that some code
ported TO the dec does not behave the same as it did on the other platform,
and some code ported FROM the DEC behaves differently because parenthesis
in the "wrong" place were ignored and the conditions behaved properly on
the DEC.

But this *is* an off the cuff remark - I might be wrong about what is
really happening - but every time I have encountered it I have just fixed
it to work and moved on - I've been in a bit of a hurry lately.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eX8BjezE#GA.150@upnetnews03>`

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3650ed81.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote in message <01be11d1$513f69e0$43bc48a6@default>...
>>Dennis J. Minette <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote in article
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I suspect one could find idiosyncracies  in almost any compiler, so the
example of requiring all contiguous closing parentheses on a separate line
is not to surprising.  BTW, was your  TRANSF--X  dataname  a typo?  Dashes
are legal in constructing a dataname, but I don't believe I've ever seen two
in a row like
that.
```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36533bc7.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3650ed81.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>>>other compilers too (and not only with conditionals). Below is a clip
from
>MATHPK
>>>(of the ETKPAK collection http://www.etk.com/download ):
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>the strange parentheses in line 042200 have to be on a line by
themselves,
>>>ideally, they should be at the end of line 042100, but some version of
>>>MicroFocus
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>


It is not immediately intuitive to me that a double dash in a dataname is
justified within the context of your mathpak example.  Perhaps I did not
spend enough time studying and puzzling over it.  In any case, I was just
observing that I had not seen that construct used in the past.  But, then
again, there are probably LOTS of things that I have not seen or experienced
before.  As long as it makes sense to you, and it compiles and works, that
is all that really counts, isn't it?

BTW, I see a line in your mathpak.cob that is at odds with your observation
that trailing parentheses were required by a particular DEC/VMS compiler to
be placed on a separate line.

048300                                   + TRANSF--W))))
93/08/20

Was the problem you previously cited related to having an odd number of
trailing parentheses?  Or was the prior DEC/VMS compiler problem fixed in
the 6-year interim?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CXX32.48$956.95200@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <01be1127$617e0c80$14bc48a6@default>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>The Goobers <docdwarf@home.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>heck out of  programmers who come into the code later.

I have heard this said a lot, but will have to admit that I have never
understood it.  To me, if you understand logic, you understand logic.

Negative logic, positive logic, what difference does it make?  Logic
is the stock and trade, the very essence, of programming.  If you can't
stand the heat, get out of the kitchen. :-)  If a programmer can't read
and write logical expressions as easily as they understand their native
language, then they should practice until they can.  If they can't learn,
then they should get another job.  To me, this is an absolute minimum
requirement for basic programming competency.

I agree that logic should not be overly complex, but often the simplest
expression is negative.  I go for the simplest and most direct expression.
The way that seems to most clearly and simply to express the the intent.
If it is equally simple and clear to express either way, I will use
positive logic. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36555B26.6268@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <364B2CFC.40CD@bellsouth.net> <72f5i7$20c$1@callisto.clark.net> <364BC5FD.530B@bellsouth.net> <364F832B.5B9D9A78@home.com> <3654F41F.1C2C@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
> 
> The Goobers wrote:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> most part, we receive them without the extra four.

Ahhhhhh, there you go... it was mentioned in the thread by Someone
Showing Off that negative conditional should be avoided; even Highly
Skilled Coders have stumbled over the rules of NOT... AND.

Since one write code for the archetypical Two Year Programmer one does
one's best to avoid *anything* which smacks of confusion; the difficulty
is that most things which are easier for humans to deal with are harder
for the machine to digest (e.g., machine code)... and *that* is the
Eternal Balance.  The code which is easier for the maintenance
programmer to understand and fix consumes more CPU cycles; a PHM may
well know that these two things must be balanced yet, to reinforce
Control, whine that your solution could be Made Better if negative
conditionals are used... and you, in response, should be able to sigh
and say that this is the age-old Battle between Machine Efficiency and
Human Interaction and that the reason you never became a Manager was
that you believe the *machine* should be making things easier for the
*humans*, not the other way around.

'Machines *work*... People *think*.'

DD
```

CITY might have embeded spaces (eg WISCONSIN RAPIDS), so you might wan't
this instead:

STRING  CITY DELIMITED BY '<2 spaces>',
        ', '  DELIMITED BY SIZE,
        STATE-CODE, '  ', ZIP-CODE-5, ZIP-PLUS-4
         DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO someplace.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<##1GNPpD#GA.169@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364941B2.6488@bellsouth.net> <364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com> <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net> <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net> <72fcmi$rv6@musky.state.wi.us> <MPG.10b5085123f7cd29989694@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>In article <72fcmi$rv6@musky.state.wi.us>, tmj@spam.com says...
>> CITY might have embeded spaces (eg WISCONSIN RAPIDS), so you might wan't
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>more words that make up the city name.


In the coding snippet below I have handled the general problem of city names
comprised of 2 or 3 parts, each separated by one or more spaces, by first
using UNSTRING to break down the city name into it's constituent parts and
then using STRING to reassemble them into an intermediate WS item,
someplace, to be ultimately printed/written as the city-state-zip line.  I
have also included a solution for the _extra credit_ quiz question posed by
DD regarding zip-plus-4 formatting that handles the situation where values
of spaces or zeros are encountered .

01  CITY-SPLIT.
       03  CITY-SPLIT-1         PIC X(20).
       03  CITY-SPLIT-2         PIC X(20).
       03  CITY-SPLIT-3         PIC X(20).
01  FULL-ZIP                       PIC X(10).
01  SOMEPLACE              PIC X(36).

       MOVE SPACES         TO CITY-SPLIT
                                                    FULL-ZIP
                                                    SOMEPLACE
       UNSTRING CITY DELIMITED BY " " INTO
                            CITY-SPLIT-1
                            CITY-SPLIT-2
                            CITY-SPLIT-3
       END-UNSTRING
       IF ZIP-PLUS-4           > ZEROS
             STRING ZIP
                            "-"
                           ZIP-PLUS-4 DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 INTO FULL-ZIP
             END-STRING
          ELSE
            MOVE ZIP TO FULL-ZIP
       END-IF
       IF CITY-SPLIT-3 > SPACES
             STRING CITY-SPLIT-1 DELIMITED BY " "
                             " "                    DELIMITED BY SIZE
                            CITY-SPLIT-2 DELIMITED BY " "
                            " "                     DELIMITED BY SIZE
                            CITY-SPLIT-3 DELIMITED BY " "
                            ", "
                            STATE-CODE
                            "  "
                            FULL-ZIP         DELIMITED BY SIZE
                     INTO SOMEPLACE
               END-STRING
           ELSE
           IF CITY-SPLIT-2 > SPACES
                  STRING CITY-SPLIT-1 DELIMITED BY " "
                                  " "                    DELIMITED BY SIZE
                                 CITY-SPLIT-2 DELIMITED BY " "
                                 ", "
                                STATE-CODE
                                "  "
                                FULL-ZIP         DELIMITED BY SIZE
                       INTO SOMEPLACE
                 END-STRING
              ELSE
                  STRING CITY-SPLIT-1 DELIMITED BY " "
                                 ", "
                                STATE-CODE
                                "  "
                                FULL-ZIP         DELIMITED BY SIZE
                       INTO SOMEPLACE
           END-IF
      END-IF
      .

>> docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <72etf5$ei$1@callisto.clark.net>...
>> >In article <364A7158.5051@bellsouth.net>,
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981111233018.18121.00000195@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com>`

```

In article <364A4583.3DC6@netbox.com>, Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com> writes:

>why not simply define NAME-ADDR-STRING with elementary items of
>CITY, STATE-CODE, & NINE-DIGIT-ZIP  and use MOVE

The United States Postal Service does not like lots of spaces in the address.
At our shop we ended up writing an Easytrieve-Plus macro that collapses out
trailing spaces so one is left with a space between city and state, and (if I
recall correctly) two spaces between the state and zip code. We were told that
the OCR equipment that paints the bar codes on the envelopes does not like
three spaces.
Mark A. Young
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>

Bob Berman wrote:
> 
(snap)
> It's my recollection that STRING had some problems in OS/VS COBOL under
> MVS.  The target field was not space filled when it was larger than the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> working-storage.

This is still true (in an MVS-COBOL II environment), it's the
programmer's responsibility to initialize the target field before the
STRING (I've been coding so many STRINGs that I'm starting to acquire
the habit of initializing a field before I MOVE to it <g>). I don't
expect this to change.

Bill Lynch

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee)
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364957cc.12578009@news.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <3647AD08.8B4@netbox.com>`

```
Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com> wrote:


>
>It's my recollection that STRING had some problems in OS/VS COBOL under
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
STRING works on a character by character basis, not on a whole field
basis;  as such it should not be surprising that the extraneous
characters(if any) at the end of the field are not affected by the
command.
===================================================

Boon Lee
Melbourne PC User Group

E-mail : boonlee@melbpc.org.au

===================================================
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>

> > I would say STRING and UNSTRING as these are relatively new COBOL
> > verbs and have a lot of potential use.
> >
(snip)
> 
> Are STRING and UNSTRING that new, or were just never used much?  In
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I think it's the latter. I remember a good friend saying how powerful he
thought STRING was in the mid-70's (when I had no idea what it was, I
was still coding Assembler). I don't know when it became legal to use in
a CICS program, but my application uses STRING freely.

program in which I wanted to break a DSN up into its component pieces -
UNSTRING was perfect here, DELIMITED BY '.'.

Regards to all,
Bill Lynch

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net>`

```
You can tell a mainframer from a PCer<G>.  Without
"UNSTRING COMMAND-LINE-IN" I don't think I could
write a program!  It is also by far the easiest way to deal
with comma-delimited files ... a mainstay of PC life.

Or how about:

UNSTRING FILESPEC DELIMITED BY ":"
    INTO
    DRIVE-NAME FILENAME.
UNSTRING FILENAME DELIMITED BY "."
    INTO FILENAME FILE-EXTENSION.

William Lynch wrote in message <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net>...
>Kitty Carr wrote:
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36470af5.3649545@news-server>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
My experience with STRING and UNSTRING is nil.  I've been programming
Cobol for over 20 years mostly in the VSE arena but during the last 6
years in the MVS arena..all mainframe.  In college I was taught Cobol
at the IBM ANS S/360 level.  STRING and UNSTRING were not present
then.  I just checked my text book which has an appendix of reserved
words and STRING and UNSTRING were not listed.

In my professional career I've worked with Cobol-D and Cobol-F and
everything there after.  None of the applications I've ever worked on
had these verbs and I never got a new manual every time a new Cobol
was released in the mainframe world.  

Hence, to me these are new verbs.  I really wasn't aware they had been
around as long as some of you state which is why I stated that they
are relatively new.

Sorry if that was wrong or misleading.

Cheers,







On Sun, 8 Nov 1998 07:32:17 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
enlightened us:

>You can tell a mainframer from a PCer<G>.  Without
>"UNSTRING COMMAND-LINE-IN" I don't think I could
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
If man evolved from apes why do we still have apes?

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364BA709.8E44977C@att.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net> <364b3a23.12066640@news.freeserve.net>`

```
"Shaun C. Murray" wrote:
> 
> On Sun, 8 Nov 1998 07:32:17 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> scm@enterprise.net        PGP Key available

Not true - we may have a platform issue here. I was UNSTRINGing an MVS
DSN in the format:

xxxxxxxx.xxxxxxxx.xxxxxxxx

MVS DSNs most commonly have three nodes, in the ISPF standard. The limit
for a DSN is 44 characters, with no node more than eight characters,
concatenated with periods. So my UNSTRING verb looks like:

        UNSTRING FULL-DSN DELIMITED BY '.' INTO
                DSN-NODE1 COUNT NODE1-CNT
                DSN-NODE2 COUNT NODE2-CNT
(etc., where the -CNT fields are defined as BINARY SYNC.)
	END-UNSTRING

This separates the DSN into its nodes and tells me the length of each
one, just what I need if I want to disable the file and alter the DSN
(which is exactly why I wrote the code).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981109005315.14590.00000517@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net>`

```

In article <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net>, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> writes:

>I don't know when it became legal to use in
>a CICS program, but my application uses STRING freely.

In VSE/ESA, STRING and UNSTRING became legal with COBOL for VSE, which uses the
Language Environment. It became legal because the Language Environment routines
for CICS use a different way of allocating memory that is CICS-friendly.

The STRING and UNSTRING verbs were illegal in CICS for FCOBOL (DOS/VS COBOL)
and COBOL II because the runtime libraries did GETVIS (the MVS equivalent would
be GETMAIN, not the EXEC CICS GETMAIN, but an operating system GETMAIN) to
acquire storage for a work area and, since the program never ended, the storage
would be held on in case it was needed later. But during a program compression,
the program (and its pointer to the GETVISed memory) would be thrown out, and
the next time the program was executed, it did not have any pointers to that
GETVISed area, so under even moderate program compression rate, GETVIS storage
would eventually be eaten up.

One of our vendors did this and the way we eliminated the memory creap was by
making the faulty programs resident.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>

- **References:** `<36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <19981109005315.14590.00000517@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
Mark0Young wrote:
> 
> In article <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net>, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> writes:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> making the faulty programs resident.

Hmm, we are a VSE shop with COBOL II and we using STRING and UNSTRING.  I've
not heard of any problems with it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981110010951.14596.00000759@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3647AAA8.48E4@sprynet.com>`

```

In article <3647AAA8.48E4@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
writes:

>Hmm, we are a VSE shop with COBOL II and we using STRING and UNSTRING.  I've
>not heard of any problems with it.

If you had zero compressions, or if the program was compiled with RMODE(ANY)
and loaded into 31-bit GETVIS (from where it would never get deleted), then you
wouldn't have seen the GETVIS creap.

But if the COBOL II program is compiled in RMODE(24), or gets loaded into the
DSA (insufficient or no 31-bit GETVIS available), and one experiences lots of
program compressions, one would see lots of GETVIS creap, usually in small
increments over time.

Of course, it was never an issue in batch.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365236AF.9B@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3647AAA8.48E4@sprynet.com> <19981110010951.14596.00000759@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
Mark0Young wrote:
> 
> In article <3647AAA8.48E4@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course, it was never an issue in batch.

Hmm, interesting.  And definately worth knowing.  All our programs are 24
bit, so perhaps it could be a problem if we used STRING and UNSTRING a lot.
We have one program that uses UNSTRING a great deal, but the program is only
run maybe once or twice an hour, if that, and of course PROD CICS is cycled
every night.  I recently (earlier this year) wrote my first CICS programs from 
scratch, and I use STRING in a few places, but only for error messages that 
never get called (unless something is really screwy).  I'll definately have
to remember this.

Did you say that COBOL for VSE solves this problem?  Well, if we ever get it
up and running that will be good...  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981121212344.25467.00000631@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<365236AF.9B@sprynet.com>`

```

In article <365236AF.9B@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
writes:

>Did you say that COBOL for VSE solves this problem?  Well, if we ever get it
>up and running that will be good...  :-)

Yes, COBOL for VSE solves the problem. More precisely, the Language Environment
(which COBOL for VSE uses) solves the problem by using memory management
routines that are CICS-friendly. Actually, two separate sublibraries are used
for LE (SCEEBASE for common and VSE-specific routines; SCEECICS for
CICS-specific routines), which is a change from previous COBOL libraries where
one library was used for both batch and CICS and thus library routines that
needed memory would do so in a VSE-compatible but CICS-hostile manner.

Also, it apprears that not all STRING nor UNSTRING routines are equal; some
constructs require the use of routines that end up needing to acquire temporary
memory for their work, and some don't. Sorry, I don't have the details, but I
recall seeing it mentioned on the VSE-L mailing list.
Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **References:** `<365236AF.9B@sprynet.com> <19981121212344.25467.00000631@ngol04.aol.com>`

```

Mark0Young wrote in message
<19981121212344.25467.00000631@ngol04.aol.com>...
>
>In article <365236AF.9B@sprynet.com>, Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
>writes:
>
>>Did you say that COBOL for VSE solves this problem?  Well, if we ever get
it
>>up and running that will be good...  :-)
>
>Yes, COBOL for VSE solves the problem. More precisely, the Language
Environment
>(which COBOL for VSE uses) solves the problem by using memory management
>routines that are CICS-friendly. Actually, two separate sublibraries are
used
>for LE (SCEEBASE for common and VSE-specific routines; SCEECICS for
>CICS-specific routines), which is a change from previous COBOL libraries
where
>one library was used for both batch and CICS and thus library routines that
>needed memory would do so in a VSE-compatible but CICS-hostile manner.
>
>Also, it apprears that not all STRING nor UNSTRING routines are equal; some
>constructs require the use of routines that end up needing to acquire
temporary
>memory for their work, and some don't. Sorry, I don't have the details, but
I
>recall seeing it mentioned on the VSE-L mailing list.
>Mark A. Young

Mark,
  I am a little confused by your post.  I know MVS a lot more than VSE, but
I thought that all the changes that you are talking about actually were
introduced with VS COBOL II and not just with LE.

I know that for MVS, there are two sets of run-time libraries for VS COBOL
II (one for CICS and one for everything else) - and I thought that the CICS
ones did remove the STRING/UNSTRING (and a few other "getmain"/"getvis")
problems under CICS.

Now, as indicated in another thread, I am NOT recommending the use of VS
COBOL II (for MVS or VSE), but did think that the problems you are talking
about were DOS/VS COBOL problems that were solved by VS COBOL II - and did
not require an LE solution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981124013120.25361.00001205@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<739q5b$2i9@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```

In article <739q5b$2i9@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:

>did think that the problems you are talking
>about were DOS/VS COBOL problems that were solved by VS COBOL II - and did
>not require an LE solution.

Not in VSE--the STRING and UNSTRING commands in DOS/VS COBOL II still had
GETVIS problems under CICS. It wasn't until LE that the GETVIS problem was
solved.

Mark A. Young
```

in existing systems (Y2K and all), If the goal is to sell

chapter to use as the preview for people that might be
interested in buying.

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<01be0ab9$d32caae0$62bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <721tfo$f42$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <36448694.0@news1.ibm.net>`

Leif Svalgaard <leif@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36448694.0@news1.ibm.net>...

> Of course, if date processing would be their main reason for
> buying the book, having it all freely available in the preview
> might negate the need to buy the book <g>


The chapter on dates concentrates on date related processing and NOT Y2K
remediation - this is not a get rich off of Y2K venture - this is a help
people to learn COBOL venture.  The date chapter is pretty neat though - as
is the chapter on Tables - E-mail is running in favor of that right now.
```

<a id="t-8cba8fa964"></a>
## Thread: Begginer's question!! — 12 msgs

### Begginer's question!!

- **From:** "Filipe Martins" <Silnox@Mail.Telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```

I saw a MicroFocus Cobol File that had the following definition (printed by
Micro Focus Rebuild (/n switch) utility):

File : FSTFOAR.DAT
  Organization - Indexed
  Recording mode - Fixed
  Record length -   114
  Keys description :
  Key           Start             Length
    0:              1                +15
    1:              6                +10
                     1                 +5
As I am new to Cobol, my question is:
        How this is done in Cobol ?

Please answer me, if you can.

            Thanking you in advance
                        Filipe Martins
```

#### ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
       program-id. fstfoar.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select fstfoar-file
               assign to "FSTFOAR.DAT"
               organization indexed
               access dynamic
               record key primary-key
               alternate alt-key = key-field-2 key-field-1.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd fstfoar-file
           recording mode fixed.
       01 fstfoar-record.
         05 primary-key.
           10 key-field-1          pic x(5).
           10 key-field-2          pic x(10).
         05 rest-of-record         pic x(99).
       procedure division.
           open output fstfoar-file
           close fstfoar-file
           stop run
           .

File : FSTFOAR.DAT
  Organization - Indexed
  Recording mode - Fixed
  Record length -   114
  Keys description :
  Key           Start             Length
    0:              1                +15
    1:              6                +10
                    1                 +5

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

The 'alternate' clause above must be some new-fangled
extension or up-and-cumming standards feature.
Or is it just a MicroFocus feature? and since when?
Please enlighten me. Thanks.

>       data division.
>       file section.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

by
>>Micro Focus Rebuild (/n switch) utility):
>>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZkW02.1765$Tk.1712537@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>
>Rick Smith wrote in message ...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Please enlighten me. Thanks.

It's a Micro Focus extension, *sorely* needed.  Version 1.2 of the new
standard has a similar feature:

    ALTERNATE RECORD KEY alt-key SOURCE IS key-field-2 key-field-1.

I hope they leave it in there.  READ PREVIOUS, also *sorely* needed, is
in there too.  I'm concerned they are going to take PREVIOUS out, and
to me that would trivialize INDEXED files for real database use.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00

```

>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>I hope they leave it in there.

key including the PRIMARY key.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-VkPNdde9dDtd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net> <ZkW02.1765$Tk.1712537@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sat, 7 Nov 1998 11:40:41, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> I hope they leave it in there.  READ PREVIOUS, also *sorely* needed, is
> in there too.  I'm concerned they are going to take PREVIOUS out, and
> to me that would trivialize INDEXED files for real database use.


The last draft I was able to obtain before they removed it from the 
site still contains the "split key" and the read previous.  I 
anticipate, however, that the split key manipulation will be an 
optional item as some indexed file access methods will not tolerate it
(ie VSAM).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<727m02$a4p@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net> <ZkW02.1765$Tk.1712537@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-VkPNdde9dDtd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Sat, 7 Nov 1998 11:40:41, "Judson McClendon"
><judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Both split key and read previous will (probably) be in the next Standard but
BOTH will probably be in the "processor dependent" list - which is a
replacement for the old "hardware dependent" list - but now basically means
it is optional.

As to "vsam doesn't support it" - I want to remind you that the COBOL
Standard has (in the past) placed requirements that implementors had to
"build to".  For example, the VS COBOL II option of "SIMVRDS" was a method
of providing variable length relative files BEFORE vsam supported it (on
MVS).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-eRzw0sOfJkug@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net> <ZkW02.1765$Tk.1712537@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-VkPNdde9dDtd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <727m02$a4p@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 9 Nov 1998 21:14:07, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> As to "vsam doesn't support it" - I want to remind you that the COBOL
> Standard has (in the past) placed requirements that implementors had to
> "build to".  For example, the VS COBOL II option of "SIMVRDS" was a method
> of providing variable length relative files BEFORE vsam supported it (on
> MVS).

That's true - but the "processor" dependent area now will allow 
vendors to claim something is processor dependent in order not to 
provide support - something I know you have sounded off about before. 
 I want to call additional attention to that fact.  It defeats 
Standard COBOL.  

While I agree there WILL be some things that are TRULY - what I would 
rather call "Platform" dependent - the latitude that is allowed in 
this area should be severly limited.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QdvdOpf9JhX2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 7 Nov 1998 02:18:10, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:

> 
> Rick Smith wrote in message ...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It's been available in MicroFocus COBOL since 2.5 and probably before.
 It varies from the new proposed standard only slightly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<727fu8$rc7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<720066$8jb$1@duke.telepac.pt> <nWN02.386$LY1.340@news1.atlantic.net> <3643adef.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QdvdOpf9JhX2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>> The 'alternate' clause above must be some new-fangled
>> extension or up-and-cumming standards feature.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> It varies from the new proposed standard only slightly.


There is an equivalent in Fujitsu but phrased slightly differently.
I know that you know about it Thane, but perhaps others do not.

    SELECT DTRK-FILE ASSIGN TO DTRK-IDENTIFICATION
       ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
       ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
       FILE STATUS IS DTRK-STATUS
       RECORD KEY IS DTRK-KEY
       ALTERNATE KEY IS
                                DTRK-SHOW-FLAG, DTRK-TARE-DATE
                                DTRK-IN-TIME, DTRK-KEY
       ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS DTRK-DISPATCH-KEY
                                WITH DUPLICATES.

The main difference is that the MF version has a name
given to the key, while the Fujitsu version does not. I think
the Fujitsu method is the one in the new standard, though
I think it inferior.  It makes it a lot more awkward to use,
as the read has no name to reference ... you have to
list the components of the key.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Begginer's question!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

wrote:

> The main difference is that the MF version has a name
> given to the key, while the Fujitsu version does not. I think
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

this area, but it is done differently than MF does it.
```

<a id="t-30897f6c27"></a>
## Thread: Cobol on the AS/400 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### Cobol on the AS/400

- **From:** "William R. Fink" <bfink@apci.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71v4b1$89d@mhstl1.monsanto.com>`

```
I am very new to the as/400.  I am having a VERY difficult time getting my
cobol program to work.  I just want to create a physical sequential file and
access it via COBOL.

Can someone please tell me how:
    1) To create a sequential file
    2) Define the file in my COBOL select and FD
    3) How to run the program so it knows where the file is

I have programmed on MVS, Tandem, Unix, and Windows...the AS/400 is driving
me nuts!!!
(if you don't want to help me, can you point me in the direction?)

Bill
bfink@apci.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on the AS/400

- **From:** "Bruce Anderson" <BruceA@SpaceStar.Net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B26B9647-D9802@192.168.2.21>`
- **References:** `<71v4b1$89d@mhstl1.monsanto.com>`

```
Bill the AS400 uses externaly defind files, the files for input
and output must exist before the compiler will process your source.
You will need to lern all about the OS/400 before you can do
any programming. The AS400 is simply too different from a file
based OS for you to make any progress.
BA-

Bruce Anderson
Computer programming student at DCTC
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tools to make CICS BMS mapping easier

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-1,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36431500.2849809@news-server>`
- **References:** `<712tnd$65h$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3637020b.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 11:28:53 -0000, "Simon Cordingley"
<simonc@casegen.co.uk> enlightened us:

>Hi
>Our Casegen for CICS product does BMS screen painting and lots more. If you
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There was a product I use to use on the mainframe called MAPGEN.  I
don't remember the company that made it, but you might find it via a
search on the Internet.  Not sure if they had an MVS vsersion or not.
The version I used was on VSE.  It was a great product for doing BMS
maps and even gave a hardcopy representation of what the screen would
like like before you assembled and linked and cataloged the generated
BMS macroes.  

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Q:  What was the first question they asked Linda Tripp at her make over?
A:  Paper or plastic!

 Steve
```

documentation from Micro Focus. Does anybody know, which versions of
Micro Focus support CORBA?

to start such a project?

Frank Pohlmann

<a id="t-c774184689"></a>
## Thread: Prison vs Work — 2 msgs

### Prison vs Work

- **From:** croaker@access.digex.net (Frank Ney)
- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649b839.4756922@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
Dedicated to those of us on the Y2K Death March:


 IN PRISON...You spend the majority of your time in an 
             8x10 cell. 
 AT WORK...You spend most of your time in a 6x8 cubicle. 
 

 IN PRISON...You get three meals a day. 
 AT WORK...You only get a break for 1 meal and 
           you have to pay for it. 
 

 IN PRISON...You get time off for good behavior. 
 AT WORK...You get rewarded for good behavior with more work. 
 

 IN PRISON...A guard locks and unlocks all the doors for you. 
 AT WORK...You must carry around a security card and unlock 
           and open all the doors yourself. 
 

 IN PRISON...You can watch TV and play games. 
 AT WORK...You get fired for watching TV and playing games. 
 

 IN PRISON...You get your own toilet. 
 AT WORK...You have to share. 
 

 IN PRISON...They allow your family and friends to visit. 
 AT WORK...You cannot even speak to your family and friends. 
 

 IN PRISON...All expenses are paid by taxpayers with no work 
             required. 
 AT WORK...You get to pay all the expenses to go to work and 
           then they deduct taxes from your salary to pay for
           prisoners. 
 

 IN PRISON...You spend most of your life looking through 
             bars from the inside wanting to get out. 
 AT WORK...You spend most of your time wanting to get out 
           and go inside bars. 
 

 IN PRISON...There are wardens who are often sadistic. 
 AT WORK...They are called supervisors.
 

 IN PRISON...You have unlimited time to read e-mail jokes.
 AT WORK...You get fired if you get caught.

 ___________________________________________________________
 The Original Email Joke of the Day! http://www.joker.org
 
 To SUBSCRIBE to the Joke of the Day send an email to:
 subscribe@joker.org
      
 To UNSUBSCRIBE from the Joke of the Day, and to live the
 rest of your life in humorless mediocrity, *forward* this 
 email to: unsubscribe@joker.org
 ___________________________________________________________

Frank Ney  WV/EMT-B  N4ZHG  LPWV  NRA(L) GOA CCRKBA JPFO
Sponsor, BATF Abuse page  http://www.access.digex.net/~croaker/batfabus.html
West Virginia Coordinator, Libertarian Second Amendment Caucus
Fan Guest of Honor, Technicon 16  http://www.technicon.org
- --
"Pinky, are you pondering what I'm pondering?"
"Uhh, I think so, Brain, but how are we going to get all of the computers to
fail all at the same time?"
```

#### ↳ Re: Prison vs Work

- **Date:** 1998-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981106120942.17748.00000057@ng99.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3649b839.4756922@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```

.


Frank J. Boll

<a id="t-b3e03a6577"></a>
## Thread: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<724g10$db0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>What would you say is "religious use of
the scope delimiters" ?

I would define religious use of anything as use
by rule. (as opposed to use when one decides
on a completely different basis ... does it make
the code read better?  ... is it REQUIRED to
make the compiler recognize the construct
correctly ...)

If every "IF" must end with an "END-IF" then use
of the scope indicators is 'religious'; however if
every "IF" statement must simply be easy to read
then they are being used correctly (in my opinion).

If
    (every "IF" must end with an
"END-IF")
then
    (use of the scope indicators is
'religious');
however
if
    (every "IF" statement must simply be
easy to read)
then
    (they are being used correctly (in
my opinion))
    end-if.

It is what you are use to. In a specific
context a given literary device may either
add to the readability or obscure it, depending
on circumstances and the skill of the writer.

>How would you characterise using a full-stop wherever it is
>possible to do so even if it is not needed for any purpose
>such as terminating a scope ?

Now that is an interesting question.
I consider

This-is-a-paragraph.
      perform first-step
      perform second-step
      perform third-step
      perform four-step.

and

This-is-a-paragraph.
      perform first-step.
      perform second-step.
      perform third-step.
      perform four-step.

To have subtly different meanings, though only to the
programmer and not to the compiler. I would assume
with the first, that the following would be true:


This-is-a-paragraph.
      perform first-step
      perform second-step
* note no periods.  For some reason the programmer wanted
*this to be kept to-gether
      perform third-step
      perform four-step.

Whereas with the second, I would have no compunction in
changing the code as follows:

This-is-a-paragraph.
      perform first-part-that-i-am-going-to-expand.
      perform second-part-that-i-am-going-to-expand.

first-part-that-i-am-going-to-expand.
      perform first-step.
      perform second-step.

second-part-that-i-am-going-to-expand.
      perform third-step.
      perform four-step.
```

## Thread: Help w/ Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 3.0 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help w/ Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 3.0

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3644A07A.1D82@sprynet.com>`

```
I've already written to Fujitsu about this, but I thought I might get a
quicker answer here.

I got a copy of PowerCOBOL 3.0L10 included with the book COBOL Unleashed.
I have installed it, but when I try to bring it up I get a message that
says "F5BA1243 Cannot open registry information."  Any ideas?  I'm guessing
it's something really simple...
```

#### ↳ Re: Help w/ Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 3.0

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EUf12.690$vt.1114040@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3644A07A.1D82@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>I've already written to Fujitsu about this, but I thought I might get a
>quicker answer here.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it's something really simple...

I believe there is a problem with the Fujitsu files on the CD.  I think
somebody posted a message about that a couple of weeks ago, but I don't
remember if there is a quick fix.  You can download the Fujitsu compiler
files from www.adtools.com.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help w/ Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 3.0

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3645d448.4619746@news-server>`
- **References:** `<3644A07A.1D82@sprynet.com> <EUf12.690$vt.1114040@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sun, 08 Nov 1998 12:12:20 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:

>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>>I've already written to Fujitsu about this, but I thought I might get a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>files from www.adtools.com.

Judson is correct.  The Fujitsu compiler shipped on the Cobol Unleased
is either incomplete or not compatible with Casegen.  In either case,
you should got to www.adtools.com and download version 3.

Casegen, in the person of Simon, has corresponded with me about this
and that was his suggestion.  I've done the download but haven't had
time to see if it cures the problems up or not.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
If man evolved from apes why do we still have apes?

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Help w/ Fujitsu PowerCOBOL 3.0

- **From:** "Fujitsu COBOL Support" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72gqkj$da5@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3644A07A.1D82@sprynet.com>`

```
The CD in the back of "COBOL Unleashed" contains an incomplete copy of
PowerCOBOL.

Go to our website www.adtools.com and order the CD directly from us.   If
you are really serious about creating GUI's in COBOL, the version 4 product
supports OCX and Active X controls.
```

Simon

#### ↳ Re: Where to find a COBOL compiler?

- **From:** "Joe Coffman" <joec@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%h612.824$Qf1.641@news12.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0a9c$7041f7e0$9de10fcb@szandberg>`

```
Check out the Fujitsu web site.

Simon Zandberg wrote in message <01be0a9c$7041f7e0$9de10fcb@szandberg>...
>I am simply looking to download a COBOL compiler to work under
>MS-DOS/Win95.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
