###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where to find a COBOL compiler?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364aab66.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<01be0a9c$7041f7e0$9de10fcb@szandberg> <36489295$0$12767@nntp1.ba.best.com> <36489796.0@news1.ibm.net> <364a13aa$0$12756@nntp1.ba.best.com>`

```
David Sanders wrote in message <364a13aa$0$12756@nntp1.ba.best.com>...
>Please download the latest trial version at:
>www.synkronix.com/educational/educational.html
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>support@synkronix.com

this is not a bug report. It is a progress report. PERCOBOL finally
executes the following program correctly.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    TSORT2.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD
000500                THE ROUTINE 'COMB SORT' IS A REMARKABLE N*LOG(N)
000600                BUBBLE SORT ROUTINE INVENTED BY STEPHEN LACY AND
000700                RICHARD BOX (BYTE MAGAZINE APRIL 1991, P315),
000800                IT IS NOT STABLE AND TABLES WITH SOME PARTIAL
000900                ORDER ARE SORTED FASTER THAN RANDOM TABLES.
001000
001100 DATE-WRITTEN.  91/04/17
001200     -REVISED:  98/11/12.
001300
001400 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
001500
001600 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001700 SOURCE-COMPUTER. ALMOST-PORTABLE.
001800 OBJECT-COMPUTER. ALMOST-PORTABLE.
001900
002000 DATA DIVISION.
002100
002200 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
002300
002400 01  RANDOM-VARIABLES.
002500     02  RANDOM-PRECISION        PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +9999999.
002600     02  RANDOM-INTEGER          PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +1234567.
002700     02  RANDOM-MODULUS          PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +2099863.
002800     02  RANDOM-MULTIPLIER       PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +1005973.
002900     02  RANDOM-INCREMENT        PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE  +443771.
003000     02  RANDOM-QUOTIENT         PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE   ZERO.
003100     02  RANDOM-PRODUCT          PIC S9(15) COMP  VALUE   ZERO.
003200
003300 01  ARRAY-VARIABLES.
003400     02  DUPLICATION             PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +3.
003500     02  ARRAY-SIZE              PIC S9(7)  COMP.
003600     02  ARRAY-MAX               PIC S9(7)  COMP  VALUE +8000.
003700     02  ARRAY-INDEX             PIC S9(7)  COMP.
003800     02  ARRAY-STORAGE.
003900         03  ARRAY-ITEM                     OCCURS 8000 TIMES.
004000             04  ARRAY-KEY       PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004100             04  ARRAY-DATA      PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004200
004300 01  SORT-VARIABLES.
004400     02  PROBE-INDEX             PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004500     02  UPPER-LIMIT             PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004600     02  PREV-PROBE              PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004700     02  JUMP-SIZE               PIC S9(7)  COMP.
004800
004900     02  SWAP-ITEM.
005000         03  SWAP-KEY            PIC S9(7)  COMP.
005100         03  SWAP-DATA           PIC S9(7)  COMP.
005200
005300     02  SWAP-INDICATOR          PIC X.
005400         88  NO-MORE-SWAPS                  VALUE SPACE.
005500
005600 01  TIME-VARIABLES.
005700     02  YYMMDD                  PIC 9(6).
005800     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  YYMMDD.
005900         03  YEARS               PIC 99.
006000         03  MONTHS              PIC 99.
006100         03  DAYS                PIC 99.
006200
006300     02  HHMMSSHH                PIC 9(8).
006400     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  HHMMSSHH.
006500         03  HOURS               PIC 99.
006600         03  MINUTES             PIC 99.
006700         03  SECONDS             PIC 99.
006800         03  HUNDREDTHS          PIC 99.
006900
007000     02  THE-TIME-IN-MSECS       PIC 9(9).
007100     02  BEFORE-IN-MSECS         PIC 9(9).
007200     02  AFTER-IN-MSECS          PIC 9(9).
007300
007400 01  CHECK-VARIABLES.
007500     02  NBR-OUT-OF-ORDER        PIC S9(7)  COMP.
007600     02  NBR-NOT-STABLE          PIC S9(7)  COMP.
007700
007800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
007900 BEGIN-OF-PROGRAM.
008000     DISPLAY "Enter Array Size=" NO ADVANCING
008100     ACCEPT ARRAY-SIZE
008200
008300     PERFORM GENERATE-SORT-ARRAY
008400     PERFORM COMB-SORT-THE-ARRAY
008500     PERFORM CHECK-IF-SORTED
008600
008700     ACCEPT SWAP-INDICATOR
008800     STOP RUN
008900     .
009000
009100*******************    GENERATE SORT ARRAY    ********************
009200
009300 GENERATE-SORT-ARRAY.
009400     MOVE 1234567 TO RANDOM-INTEGER
009500     PERFORM GENERATE-RANDOM-DIGIT
009600       VARYING ARRAY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
009700         UNTIL ARRAY-INDEX > ARRAY-SIZE
009800     .
009900
010000 GENERATE-RANDOM-DIGIT.
010100     PERFORM RANDOM-FUNCTION
010200
010300     COMPUTE ARRAY-KEY (ARRAY-INDEX) =
010400       RANDOM-INTEGER * ARRAY-SIZE / RANDOM-MODULUS / DUPLICATION
010500
010600     MOVE ARRAY-INDEX TO ARRAY-DATA (ARRAY-INDEX)
010700     .
010800
010900 RANDOM-FUNCTION.
011000     COMPUTE RANDOM-PRODUCT  = RANDOM-INCREMENT
011100                             + RANDOM-MULTIPLIER * RANDOM-INTEGER
011200     COMPUTE RANDOM-QUOTIENT = RANDOM-PRODUCT    / RANDOM-MODULUS
011300     COMPUTE RANDOM-INTEGER  = RANDOM-PRODUCT
011400                             - RANDOM-QUOTIENT   * RANDOM-MODULUS
011500     .
011600
011700*****************     COMB SORT THE ARRAY    *********************
011800
011900 COMB-SORT-THE-ARRAY.
012000     PERFORM TAKE-TIME-BEFORE
012100
012200     MOVE   "SWAP"   TO SWAP-INDICATOR
012300     MOVE ARRAY-SIZE TO JUMP-SIZE
012400
012500     PERFORM COMB-SORT
012600       UNTIL NO-MORE-SWAPS
012700         AND JUMP-SIZE NOT > 1
012800
012900     PERFORM TAKE-TIME-AFTER
013000     .
013100
013200 TAKE-TIME-BEFORE.
013300     PERFORM TAKE-THE-TIME
013400     MOVE THE-TIME-IN-MSECS TO BEFORE-IN-MSECS
013500     .
013600
013700 TAKE-THE-TIME.
013800     ACCEPT HHMMSSHH FROM TIME
013900     COMPUTE THE-TIME-IN-MSECS = (((HOURS * 60 + MINUTES) * 60
014000                               + SECONDS) * 100 + HUNDREDTHS) * 10
014100     .
014200
014300 COMB-SORT.
014400     COMPUTE JUMP-SIZE = (10 * JUMP-SIZE + 3) / 13
014500     PERFORM BUBBLE-SORT
014600     .
014700
014800 BUBBLE-SORT.
014900     MOVE SPACE TO SWAP-INDICATOR
015000     COMPUTE UPPER-LIMIT = ARRAY-SIZE - JUMP-SIZE
015100
015200     PERFORM BUBBLE-UP-ITEMS
015300       VARYING ARRAY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
015400         UNTIL ARRAY-INDEX > UPPER-LIMIT
015500     .
015600
015700 BUBBLE-UP-ITEMS.
015800     COMPUTE PROBE-INDEX = ARRAY-INDEX + JUMP-SIZE
015900     IF ARRAY-KEY (ARRAY-INDEX) > ARRAY-KEY (PROBE-INDEX)
016000         MOVE ARRAY-ITEM (ARRAY-INDEX) TO SWAP-ITEM
016100         MOVE ARRAY-ITEM (PROBE-INDEX) TO ARRAY-ITEM (ARRAY-INDEX)
016200         MOVE  SWAP-ITEM               TO ARRAY-ITEM (PROBE-INDEX)
016300         MOVE "SWAP" TO SWAP-INDICATOR
016400     .
016500
016600 TAKE-TIME-AFTER.
016700     PERFORM TAKE-THE-TIME
016800     MOVE THE-TIME-IN-MSECS TO AFTER-IN-MSECS
016900     COMPUTE THE-TIME-IN-MSECS = AFTER-IN-MSECS - BEFORE-IN-MSECS
017000     DISPLAY "milliseconds = " THE-TIME-IN-MSECS
017100     .
017200
017300***********************   CHECK IF SORTED   **********************
017400
017500 CHECK-IF-SORTED.
017600     MOVE ZEROES TO NBR-OUT-OF-ORDER, NBR-NOT-STABLE
017700     PERFORM CHECK-IF-IN-ORDER
017800       VARYING ARRAY-INDEX FROM 2 BY 1
017900         UNTIL ARRAY-INDEX > ARRAY-SIZE
018000
018100     IF NBR-OUT-OF-ORDER > ZEROES
018200         DISPLAY "Not sorted, " NBR-OUT-OF-ORDER " out of order"
018300     ELSE
018400         DISPLAY "OK sorted "
018500         IF NBR-NOT-STABLE > ZEROES
018600             DISPLAY "but not stable " NBR-NOT-STABLE
018700     .
018800
018900 CHECK-IF-IN-ORDER.
019000     COMPUTE PREV-PROBE = ARRAY-INDEX - 1
019100     IF ARRAY-KEY (PREV-PROBE) > ARRAY-KEY (ARRAY-INDEX)
019200         ADD 1 TO NBR-OUT-OF-ORDER
019300     ELSE
019400     IF ARRAY-KEY (PREV-PROBE) = ARRAY-KEY (ARRAY-INDEX)
019500         IF ARRAY-DATA (PREV-PROBE) > ARRAY-DATA (ARRAY-INDEX)
019600             ADD 1 TO NBR-NOT-STABLE
019700     .


execution time for ARRAY-SIZE = 8000 is
PERCobol:    2,945,000  miiliseconds    (hundredths of seconds on ACCEPT
always zero)
Realia v3.105:          170 milliseconds    ( 17,000 times faster)
Fujitsu v3.10:               50 milliseconds

On 32-bit systems you can increase the maximum size for ARRAY-SIZE.
I did that with Fujitsu to 9,999,999. This produced a DLL of size 80,037,376
bytes. I'm impressed that it complied and even more that Micro$oft's linker
could handle a DLL of that size. Even more impressive is that the program
actually runs. Here are the times in *milliseconds* to sort various array
sizes:
size 10,000            60 msecs
size 100,000     1,160
size 1,000,000  14,830
size 5,000,000  84,480

It is amazing that it sorts 5 million items in less than 1.5 minute.

This program with such a large table would not run in the java VM,
which is effectively a 16-bit system for data areas.

Now, one could argue that this test is a very atypical Cobol program,
that *real* Cobol programs do I/O and simple moves only. ACUCobol
makes such a claim and PERCobol will undoubtly too. One hears
the advise: don't use Cobol for non I/O bound applications,
use C++ or some other real programming language, but I just can't by
that.

Looking at the generated java code, it appears that the Cobol program
is NOT translated into jave, but that rather is interpreted by a generated
java program, which in turn is interpreted by the VM. I'm not sure if that
is the way to go.  The argument *for* a java solution is (apart from its
obvious fad-appeal) that the code will run anywhere (somewhat doubtful
as this has not been demonstrated - it is largely an unfulfilled promise).
A better way of making the code run anywhere would seem to insist
that all the vendors produce standard-compliant compilers without
extensions and funny stuff. (sigh)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where to find a COBOL compiler?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364b629f$0$12762@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0a9c$7041f7e0$9de10fcb@szandberg> <36489295$0$12767@nntp1.ba.best.com> <36489796.0@news1.ibm.net> <364a13aa$0$12756@nntp1.ba.best.com> <364aab66.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

> Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Actually, PERCobol uses a runtime library much as any COBOL compiler
would use a runtime library.  We keep the code in the Java side as
close as possible to the original COBOL code in order to help ensure
accuracy.  Also, that allows us to optimize the runtime library on the
known basis of COBOL-like statements rather than generalized Java
statements.  (This form of optimization has only begun for the next
release.)  Some of the standard Java API's are _markedly_ slower
than the equivalent code written for more special-purpose applications.
If Java code not dependent upon our COBOL runtimes were produced,
the generated Java code would forever be dependent upon the
slower API's.  Currently, our COBOL runtimes use the slower runtimes,
but we are transitioning to our own special-purpose API's which are
_much_ faster; this transition is invisible to the user as it is hidden
behind a layer of abstraction, allowing code produced now to run
faster in the future with only a runtime swapout.

David
```

<a id="t-1fa2534c9a"></a>
## Thread: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help! — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

- **From:** "co33y" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Nk712.397$5n1.2954706@news.magma.ca>`

```
Hello,

I'm a first year programming student just starting out with COBOL, and just
completed my second program, free from syntax or logic errors.
My problem is that I can't print my output to my printer.  When I assign my
output to "CON", it is displayed on the screen properly, but when assigned
to "LPT1:", or PRINTER, nothing gets printed.
My printer is a bi-directional Lexmark and is assigned to LPT1 and works
fine with any other printing tasks.
I'm using the MicroFocus for Windows version of COBOL.
Does anyone know what I could do to rectify this?  Thank you for your time.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iGf12.687$vt.1110423@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Nk712.397$5n1.2954706@news.magma.ca>`

```
Try this:

     SELECT print-file-name  ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
     ...
     FD  print-file-name.
     01  ...

Works for me.
```

Unfortunately, my problem is that it doesn't print when I use the

statement.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<724q5h$i0o$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Nk712.397$5n1.2954706@news.magma.ca> <iGf12.687$vt.1110423@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <cGj12.483$5n1.3256972@news.magma.ca>`

```
Assigning a file to PRINTER, under Windows95, assigns it
to the default windows printer.  If there is no Windows default
printer that may be the problem.

You can also assign it to a specific printer (i.e.:
\\machinename\printername)
in quotes, of course.  That is Fujitsu, BTW, but I suspect that
a similar methodology applies to other compilers.

co33y wrote in message ...
>Hi,
>Thanks for the reply.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "co33y" <no@spam.com>

if it makes a difference.  If I remember correctly (and it has been
a couple years) that tells it to use system calls instead of BIOS
calls for the printer.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<725gge$1tv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Nk712.397$5n1.2954706@news.magma.ca> <iGf12.687$vt.1110423@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <cGj12.483$5n1.3256972@news.magma.ca> <724q5h$i0o$1@news.igs.net> <kem12.487$5n1.3326184@news.magma.ca> <7258lk$nmd$1@news.igs.net> <4Up12.496$5n1.3427767@news.magma.ca>`

```
On the command line, or in the PIF that starts it.  It is
a *run* time option, and should be in your manuals
somewhere.  I found an example in an old batch file,
and it loks like:
CD\area
adsn +P    <--- this stars the program.
If I remember correctly, without the +P the program would
not actually print until I exited from the DOS window.  With it, it
printed as soon as the file was closed.  There may be a
problem with running a DOS program in a windows
environment.

co33y wrote in message <4Up12.496$5n1.3427767@news.magma.ca>...
>Thanks again Donald.  Quick question however; where do I include the +P
>option?  Sorry, I'm very new to COBOL and programming in general.  Thanks
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981108194315.17371.00001198@ng75.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<4Up12.496$5n1.3427767@news.magma.ca>`

```
Hi,

The +P is used in the SET COBSW=+P.  I used to use this under MS-DOS with
MicroFocus 3.2.    You can put this in the autoexec.bat.  I am not sure the
effect in Win95.

Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't print in MicroFocus COBOL... please help!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<725gr7$91b$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<4Up12.496$5n1.3427767@news.magma.ca> <19981108194315.17371.00001198@ng75.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote in message <19981108194315.17371.00001198@ng75.aol.com>...
>
>The +P is used in the SET COBSW=+P.  I used to use this under MS-DOS with
>MicroFocus 3.2.    You can put this in the autoexec.bat.  I am not sure the
>effect in Win95.


I did not know you could do it that way, I always used it
on the command line.  It does make a big difference in Win95
with older DOS code.  Without the P switch, nothing gets
released to the spooler until the program exits.  With it,
it gets released when the print file is closed.  Since my
programs print stuff like bills of lading one page at a time,
without ever exiting back to the OS, the difference is fairly
crucial<G>.
```

<a id="t-5b4655ca49"></a>
## Thread: 13 year old neds HELP! — 3 msgs

### Re: 13 year old neds HELP!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<3UX02.1800$Tk.1740410@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3642e07d.0@kastagir.senet.com.au> <01be0987$546fca80$47ca0dd0@default> <71uv40$qq2@falcon.le.ac.uk> <FiH02.88$lw.9068@news.shore.net> <I%H02.287$Rk.255921@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <77M02.212$lw.13411@news.shore.net>`

```
wetboy wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>: wetboy wrote
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I said C++.

You said "Proceed straight to C and C++."

>: the lingua franca of the computer world than French is of
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>greater proportion).  A lot of the rest is Java/Visual J++.

And you think the few *hundred* people involved in the journals are
more significant than the *hundreds of thousands* of programmers?  You
and the journalists are putting the cart before the horse.  They are
simply spouting the P.C. line, and so are you.  P.C. has never been,
nor ever will be, the definition of what is actually happening, only
what some people 'think' is happening, or what they 'think should'
happen.  Academia did not create COBOL, has never liked COBOL, and
has never understood COBOL and why it is important for business. They
have been announcing COBOL's demise 'any day now' for forty years.

>: the language world (English is the 'lingua franca' today).  There is
>: probably more new COBOL code being written today than C,
>
>Well, there certainly have been a lot of COBOL *patches* cranked
>out in the past few years.

Most of the COBOL programmers and consultants I know are spending more
time writing new code than fixing Y2K or maintaining old code.  It varies
from person to person and place to place, of course, but there is a
huge amount of new COBOL development being done, and much of it is being
held up because of the Y2K effort.  Even after 2000 there will be a great
need for COBOL programmers.  Why do you think Y2K was so big a deal in
the COBOL community?  It's because COBOL is so successful and stable that
no other language has a significant number of 20-30 year old programs that
are still in production.  This is success and a strength, not a failure.

>: not to mention
>: the gobs of Visual Basic being cranked out.  C is only third in C/S new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Where are you getting your "facts"?

Among other sources, from obscure places such as the front page articles
in the Wall Street Journal of Thursday, April 30, 1998 and Info World
June 29, 1998, and from a 1996 study in the Client/Server Market Report,
from which I quote "In fact, COBOL and 4GLs will see significantly
increased use in client/server settings, and COBOL is right behind Visual
Basic as the leader in terms of penetration ... ."  What much of academia
is oblivious to is the serious concern in the business world about the
skyrocketing costs and reliability problems encountered with the newer
gee-whiz technologies.  Currently, about 45% of new projects are never
implemented because of cost and time overruns and failures to meet design
goals.  Much of the blame for this falls on attempts to use the newest,
complex technologies whether they are justified or not.  The effect on
the bottom line is becoming very noticeable.  Many very large companies
(see Wall Street Journal article above) are beginning to rethink their
strategies, and a lot of them are reverting to older, more cost effective,
reliable technologies, such as COBOL.  This has been going on for a while,
and the trend toward C/C++ and C/S for everything and everybody has begun
to shift back to cheaper more reliable methods.  The Info World article
above highlights the fact that companies are realizing that PC based
servers are practically toys in performance and reliability when compared
to mainframes.  The very fastest PC servers can't begin to match the
throughput of a mainframe.  And the same goes for reliability.  Companies
used to almost 100% uptime on their mainframes are facing far less
reliability in their PC based servers.  These facts have spurred a real
resurgence in the use of mainframe based servers for large enterprise
networks.  This is one of the reasons why, by the time of the 1996 survey,
COBOL came in as a close second only to VB as the C/S development language
of choice, with C/C++ a more distant third.  And COBOL use is growing, not
declining, as many people suppose.  The 1980's shift away from COBOL has
reversed in the 1990's, and only a part of it is due to Y2K.  Actually,
in part Y2K has helped to show companies just how much their businesses
have been profiting from COBOL all along.  COBOL was doing the job so
quietly, smoothly and effectively that many senior officers had almost
forgotten COBOL, thinking it was no longer relevant.  But as companies
struggle with the difficulties of implementing the costly, complex and
fragile newer gee-whiz technologies, they are realizing that this is far
from the case.  But academia still hasn't gotten the message about COBOL,
even after forty years.  Perhaps they never will.

As an interesting aside here, I recently saw a posted comment from the
editor or publisher (don't remember which) of the C User's Journal.
He said that even though he lived and breathed C/C++, he used a COBOL
system to process the subscriptions and circulation for the magazine.
He said (I'm quoting from memory): "I don't need an abort from an
uninitialized pointer just when I am ready to send out thousands of
magazines."  Academia has never understood that this is where business
lives, every day.  COBOL is simple, reliable, cost effective, stable,
and very efficient at what it does.  Those are COBOL's strengths.
COBOL is not good for everything, but for what it does well, nothing
else even comes close.
```

#### ↳ Re: 13 year old neds HELP!

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<727ika$13hk$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3642e07d.0@kastagir.senet.com.au> <01be0987$546fca80$47ca0dd0@default> <71uv40$qq2@falcon.le.ac.uk> <FiH02.88$lw.9068@news.shore.net> <I%H02.287$Rk.255921@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <77M02.212$lw.13411@news.shore.net> <3UX02.1800$Tk.1740410@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon skrev i meddelelsen
<3UX02.1800$Tk.1740410@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>...
>wetboy wrote:
>>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[109 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Indeed, I believe that C is much more likely to die than COBOL. I work in a
Danish insurance company. Recently, it was decided to completely abandon C
(and C++). We will be running PL/1, Java and COBOL.
And COBOL is the number one language for new applications. If we need some
fancy GUI interface, well, we may do that in Java. But probabbly only the
interface. It's interesting to note that one of the first questions asked
when introducing Java is "how do you call COBOL code?".

I would never try to program a game in COBOL and I would never try to make a
business application i C or C++. And even though my personal favourite is
Object Pascal (Delphi), I can see why it could never take the place of
COBOL. There are just too many things you can screw up in Pascal. And I
believe that C has even more problems.

Kennet
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: My Homework

#### ↳ Re: Unbderstanding tables in COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KSC12.39$RM6.52559@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36464FBF.70A0@letterbox.com>`

```
The Chameleon wrote:
>I know a lot of you guys don't want to help a poor college student do
>his homework but I need help in understanding tables.

Think of a table (any item which contains an OCCURS clause) to be several
copies of that item, all with the same name.  Because they all have the
same name, you must have another way to differentiate between them.  This
is done by appending a 'subscript' (or 'index', see below) to references
to the item.  The term 'subscript' is taken from math, where variables of
the same name but different incarnations are indicated by subscripts.

   a    = (a  + (n / a )) / 2
    i+1     i         i

A subscript is always an integer that indicates which one of the items
of the same name are being referenced.  Since computer languages (with
the notable exception of APL) are usually written using plain text,
true subscript fonts are not available, so COBOL uses parenthesis (C
and Pascal use square brackets) to designate the subscript.  You can
think of the table as a row of boxes, each with the same name, and the
subscript designates which of the boxes is being referenced.  In this
example:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...

   MOVE MY-ITEM(subscript) TO ...

The value of (subscript) determines which of the 20 MY-ITEM elements is
being referenced.  'subscript' can be a numeric literal, a numeric data
item, a numeric data item +/- a numeric literal, or an index, or an
index +/- a numeric literal.  It must always evaluate to an integer
between 1 and the limit in the OCCURS clause (20 in this example).
Indexes are a special type of subscript.  Indexes are associated with,
and must be used only with, a particular table, and are defined by the
INDEXED BY clause on that table:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...

This creates an index named MY-INDEX associated with the table MY-ITEM.
The specific internal format of indexes is left to the implementor, but
the general idea is that an index can be in a form other than an integer
representing the 'occurrence number' of the entry in the table.  It is
essentially a construct for permitting a form which can be manipulated
more efficiently by the CPU.  The details vary by implementation, and
are not important here.  There are a few differences in the way indexes
are used, as opposed to subscripts.  One is that you must use the SET
verb to change the contents of an index.  Instead of

   MOVE 1 TO MY-INDEX
   ADD 1 TO MY-INDEX
   SUBTRACT 2 FROM MY-INDEX

you would use, respectively

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1
   SET MY-INDEX UP BY 1
   SET MY-INDEX DOWN BY 2

You can also set and change the value of an index by using the VARYING
clause of the PERFORM or SEARCH statements.

   PERFORM ... VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MY-INDEX > 20

If you declare a table with an index you can also use the SEARCH verb
to search the table.

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1.
   SEARCH MY-ITEM
      AT END ...
      WHEN MY-ITEM(MY-INDEX) = ...

If your table is completely populated and the values are in ascending or
descending order, you can use the SEARCH ALL syntax (see ASCENDING and
DESCENDING clauses of OCCURS), which may do a non-linear search (such as
binary search) of the table, resulting in much faster execution.  You
also do not have to set the initial value for the index before executing
SEARCH ALL.

Note that when you reference an individual table element, you must use
a subscript or index to indicate which element you refer to.  But when
you reference the table as a whole, such as in the SEARCH statement,
you do not use a subscript or index.

The power and usefulness of tables and subscripting is best understood
in the context of loops.  It is the use of these two concepts together
which permits writing one set of code which can operate on a number of
different elements.  Consider a program that tested some sort of code
to be valid, and there were 500 different codes.  Without the use of
subscripts, you would probably need to individually code 500 IF tests,
or 500 EVALUATE ... WHEN clauses.  What if there were 5000 different
codes?  What about 50,000?  With a table, you can load all the values
into the table, either through VALUE clauses in Working Storage if the
table is static, or from a data file.  Then just use a short SEARCH
statement or loop to test the codes against the values in the table.

If your table has subordinate entries like this

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-1 ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-2 ...

Then each of the subordinate items MY-SUB-ITEM-1 and MY-SUB-ITEM-2 are
referenced using subscripts or indexes in exactly the same way as the
item MY-ITEM.  The difference is that you may not reference any of the
subordinate items as the object of the SEARCH verb.  You can reference
subordinate items in the WHEN clauses of a SEARCH.

Two dimensional tables merely carry subscripting one step farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...

Each of the 20 copies of MY-ITEM contains one MY-ITEM-A and 10 copies
of MY-ITEM-B.  There are 200 total copies of MY-ITEM-B in the entire
structure.  A reference to MY-ITEM or MY-ITEM-A is resolved by the use
of one subscript.  But if you reference MY-ITEM-B, you must use two
subscripts, the first to designate which of the 20 MY-ITEMS, and a
second to designate which of the MY-ITEM-B entries is intended within
that MY-ITEM.  Both subscripts are enclosed in the same parenthesis,
optionally separated by a comma.

   MY-ITEM-B(14, 6)
or
   MY-ITEM-B(14 6)

This references the 6th MY-ITEM-B, inside the 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a grid of boxes, with the first subscript indicating
the row and the second subscript indicating the column.

Additional levels of subscripting merely carry this concept farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A ...
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B1  OCCURS 5 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B2 ...

   MY-ITEM-B1(14, 6, 3)

References 3rd MY-ITEM-B1, in 6th MY-ITEM-B, in 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a 3D rectangle of boxes, with the first subscript
indicating the row, the second subscript indicating the column, and the
third subscript representing the level.  There are 20 * 10 * 5 = 1000
total copies of MY-ITEM-B1 in the structure, and 20 * 10 = 200 copies
of MY-ITEM-B2.

Two dimensional tables are used occasionally, three dimensional tables
are pretty rare, and you almost never see four or more dimensions.  I
won't even attempt to describe a method for visualizing a table with
more than three dimensions. ;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Ibm Cobol set for Aix or Microfocus Object Cobol Dev. Suite for UNIX ?

- **From:** jtpage33@aol.com (JTPage33)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981110213334.20955.00002310@ng116.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0c28$73887360$2e00d8d4@GIAVAVAS>`

```
I had to make that same choice.  My team went with the Micro Focus compiler. 
At first I wasn't sure it was the right choice, however it allowed us great
flexability in combining C with Cobol, as well as taking advantage of UNIX (env
variables).  We are currently using MF 4.1 on a AIX 4.2 install.  There is a
lot of different tools you will receive with MF, some you will use and some you
may not.  
I would accept any questions you may have feel free.
Jamie
SR/DEV
Venator Group
```

##### ↳ ↳ Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

- **From:** bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0c28$73887360$2e00d8d4@GIAVAVAS> <19981110213334.20955.00002310@ng116.aol.com>`

```
We just purchased an AIX box and installed the Micro Focus Developer Suite.
My "mission" is to port COBOL programs developed with Micro Focus COBOL in
Windows 95 to the AIX box.  I have no UNIX experience.	I'm trying to draw
parallels between our Windows 95 run-time system and AIX. 1.) PARM PASSING -
We execute our programs and pass parms into linkage via a Visual Basic
front-end.  I understand you run a program on AIX by typing it's name.	How
do you pass the parm ? 2.) EXTERNAL FILES - We SET our files to the system
via a bat file (that executes before the GUI comes up).  Is there such a
thing as a bat file (with SET commands)on AIX ?  Would it be run the same way
as a program - type it's name ? 3.) PACKAGING - On Windows 95, we linked our
programs into DLLs (with the Visual Basic being the exe which called the
first DLL which, in turn, calls other DLLs).  Would we still create DLLs and
have an "executable" program to call the DLLs ?


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981118151833.22086.00000986@ng17.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
 bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
Date: Tue, 17 Nov 1998 22:12:24 GMT

asked 

>> We just purchased an AIX box and installed the Micro Focus Developer Suite.
My "mission" is to port COBOL programs developed with Micro Focus COBOL in
Windows 95 to the AIX box.  I have no UNIX experience.	I'm trying to draw
parallels between our Windows 95 run-time system and AIX. 1.) PARM PASSING -
We execute our programs and pass parms into linkage via a Visual Basic
front-end.  I understand you run a program on AIX by typing it's name.	How
do you pass the parm ? 2.) EXTERNAL FILES - We SET our files to the system
via a bat file (that executes before the GUI comes up).  Is there such a
thing as a bat file (with SET commands)on AIX ?  Would it be run the same way
as a program - type it's name ? 3.) PACKAGING - On Windows 95, we linked our
programs into DLLs (with the Visual Basic being the exe which called the
first DLL which, in turn, calls other DLLs).  Would we still create DLLs and
have an "executable" program to call the DLLs ?
<<

You have a lot of work ahead of you. I admire your courage!

I do not see a cross post listed for your current inquiry, so I encourage you
to take note of 
  comp.unix.programmer
and other related newsgroups which will get you connnected to AIX experts. 

Your questions are definitely welcome here, the interdisciplinary aspects of
your environment are instructive for all those looking forward to the
convergence challenge that is before us all.

A few meager comments is all I can offer.  The SET commands, if I understand
you, are setting environment variables. I may be going too far to say that
environment varibales started in UNIX, but for sure Mr. Bill copied them from
UNIX into DOS (and its descendants, WindowsOn&On).

UNIX makes very heavy use of environment variables. You won't have any trouble
here, but there can be a few things to learn.

First let me comment on the .BAT file concept. That kind of thing is present in
nearly all operating systems.  In UNIX the concept is known as scripts. But
first you need to know that the operating system will execute from the command
line either a command directly entered or a shell that is to read and interpret
a script named on the command line.

A script file usually contains an identifier of the shell it needs encoded as a
comment on its first line. (In the absence of such a comment, there is a
default shell).

You will come to know that there are three basic shells the Bourne Shell, the
Korn Shell and the C Shell.  Generally, they are similar.  They process a
syntax very much like a simplified programming language. And the environment
variables are the variables the shell script syntax uses.

However, you will find that UNIX shells are more like an execution of MAKE in
DOS.

DOS .BAT files have an nasty way of exiting stage left with no return should
you name another .BAT file as a command. UNIX shells just 'shell' to the next
shell script with the intention of returning control to the original shell.  

And these shell invocations fully establish entirely new executable
environments, which environmens are then POPped, as it were, upon return to the
invoker.

And exactly what happens when the environment is established varies from Bourne
to Korn to C shell. This you need to get a handle on right away.  In some cases
your exisiting envrionment variables are not simply instantiated automatically
into the next lower shell, but are exported to it. There is a difference.

The syntax of the UNIX shells are somewhat the same. They are very advanced
compared to DOS .BAT files.  You will be able to convert, but you need to
really understand the syntax; the effort will be largely manual.

Your best bet is to simplify wherever you can, to reduce the amount you must
learn to get this thing up in the new environment.

Even if you have "no UNIX experience", don't worry, there is plenty of help
around. The AIX systems are excellent. Usually there are mountains of tutorial
matterial online. You should have Infoseek (pardon me if that is not the exact
name).  It is useful, and has very powerful search capability.  The man pages
are also very complete in AIX.

Get help early to make sure you have all necessary packages installed and 
files systems mounted for the man pages and Infoseek.

I think you know this, but I'll say it for completeness, AIX is very standard
UNIX so nearly any of the books on UNIX will be useful to you as a beginner.
(It does not have to be AIX specific, you will find all the specifics you need
right on the machine). 

You also asked about parms.  Again DOS is modeled on UNIX for parm processing.
But really, the whole thing is up to your runtime environment. The compiler
documentation should clarify how the parms are passed into your COBOL program.
But you will need to describe the actual situation with the BASIC front-end, if
that survives, to get comments on its combination with COBOL.

There is a possibility that your DLL implementation strategy is dead, but I'm
going beyond my knowledge here.

Also FYI, Microsoft Visual BASIC, is not an IBM Visual Age product. Exactly
what BASIC do you plan to use on the AIX box?

Beware of the use of quotes (single or double) in parms, that is a learning
curve unto itself. You could get variance in behavior across the UNIX shells,
and maybe none of it would match what you did in DOS .BAT files with quotes on
parms.

The standard for parms in DOS is to encode a slash in front, as in /parm, in
UNIX the standard is a dash infront, as in -parm.  You will find that many DOS
programs accept -parms (UNIX format) as well as /parms (DOS format).  But not
all. This was to help bridge the work place to UNIX programmers. (This is
especially true with developer programs like compilers, linkers, debuggers).
UNIX  systems defintely do not do this, because the slash looks like a
directory name, /dir/subdir/file.nam.  So if your parm expressions survive,
they will have lots of little fixes.

There is a large amount of UNIX tutorial information on the net.   If you need
URLs ask here or on the UNIX specific newsgroups.

UNIX has a steep learning curve. You and your manager need to understand it
will take time because UNIX is vast! Yet the part of UNIX you need is probably
small. So keep; asking yourself about that when you get bogged down; what is
the minimum part need to make progress?

How much use does your application make of Windows95 itself? Does Micro Focus
offer adapters, or converters to the X Windows system?  

Best Wishes, 




















Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981119182110.09599.00000152@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
On: Thu, 19 Nov 1998 20:37:20 GMT
asked these follow up questions (I have rearranged the sequence of some
snippets)...

>>
We're using Microsoft Visual Basic as a front end for Windows 95/NT.  We're
not going to have a front end on the UNIX.  I need to be able to run my
application from the command line.
...
I hope the DLL strategy isn't dead !  I've yet to get the Micro Focus software
installed, but I've got to compile my programs into some format.  If not DLLs,
then EXEs that call EXEs ?

<<
It seems to me that you are on the right track here on the bigger issue. You do
realize that you will have some system restructuring to do. That alone sould
help characterize the amount of time you need to estimate for this.

You will have system construction options that you can combine ... I am veering
off my familiar path here so I won't attempt to be exact. But you should be
able to hard link submodules , and the compiler should certainly allow you to
spawn other processes waiting or not waiting for their completion.

Relating this back to your original questions then, with the BASIC compiler
technology out of the picture, getting parm values and environment variable
values into (atleast your mainlines) is going to be simple cookbook work from
the COBOL compilers documentation.


Again from bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
On: Thu, 19 Nov 1998 20:37:20 GMT

>>
...Let me clarify. When I wrote that we SET our files to the system, I meant as
in SET TFPGMLOG=C:\XFRMRV51\MFORGW\TFPEDIT\TFPGMLOG.TXT where you tell the
system the logical name (or "DDName") and the physical name (or path).	In
other words, how do I tell my program the name (and location) of my input and
output files ?	Do I understand you correctly that this can be done from a
script ?  And, I supppose that this script could then also contain the name
of my executable program followed by -parms.
...
<<

Generally you can do anything from the script that you can do from the command
line. Just like a btach file.  So you can invoke a command. You can set an
environment variable.  You can invoke a command (program) passing myriad
parameters; some file names, some switches, and the script shell will even
resolve command line textt $ENVYOUNAMEIT to what ever you set,as well as do all
the filename globbing that DOS copied from UNIX.


You can do the exact equvalent of 
" SET TFPGMLOG=C:\XFRMRV51\MFORGW\TFPEDIT\TFPGMLOG.TXT "
in UNIX.

How you are hooking TFPGMLOG in as the DDNAME is not clear to me (that may be
the Compiler that does that magic). But it is not difficult to believe that a
similar hook is going to be available to you with the UNIX compiler. That is
actually a _convention_ of your compiler, not an OS feature.

You have long file names, which for complex programs, in itself tends to lead
away from explicit entry of all the file names on command lines (whether
entered directly or from within a script). Although UNIX is fully capable of
passing file names in as parameters.

Another way to do this (and you can combine all of it), is to create files with
list of file names. A portion of your posts suggested to me that a small part
of your system does this, but even if not done yet you could consider it. You
would need application code to read these 'control' files and do specific
things with the file names. But really that is what shells are for. So my point
is, that if you had this type of functionality in your BASIC front end, or even
in some of your COBOL application initialization logic, then convert it to
shell scripts.

In a small way what BASIC on DOS machines does (only in part now) is that it
provides the text substitution logic of a UNIX shell, the text scanning and
replacement function of UNIX commands grep and sed and the popular program perl
(which can do sockets like some DOS BASICs).

Any serious orientation effort in UNIX will get you to grep and sed.  If you
hace anything like a control file that survives in your restructured system,
you may not need much more than that.

But actually I would be radical.  Simplify, big time. This is to reduce the
amount of learning you must do to get to the end. Focus on one shell, as your
compiler vendor seems to be leading you to.  Try to do every configuration and
setup with shell scripts (this so you don't have to learn too many tools).

Generally, these are just notions, not a golden rules: if something comes out
of the old BASIC front-end code, put it into a script, do not place it into
COBOL.  

Try to avoid changes to the COBOL code. Atleast do not add environment
management to the COBOL programs (although extract it from COBOL source
programs as possible).



Again from bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
On: Thu, 19 Nov 1998 20:37:20 GMT

>>
....
I understand that the AIX initially comes up with the Korn shell, but the
Micro Focus documentation says to use the Bourne shell.
...
<<

You will find that shells are like computer languages in that people can be
very emotional about them. Don't get too drawn into the preference war. There
are real differences. Your plan, IMHO, should be to stay simple. Try to use one
only for no other reason than to limit the number of learning curves.

But a thing you need to grasp is that shell scripts invoke other shell scripts
(with eventual return back up the stack). These invokations just look like
commands in the scripts, but they are major junctions.

Each time a script starts it can use a different shell, at that exact point the
conventions for passing environment variables can be somewhat different from
Bourne to Korn to C shell (this is just a caveat). Basically UNIX systems spend
all day bouncing around from shell to shell, it is all wonderful, lots of fun,
but just be aware that the environment variables can be tricky, both on the way
in and the way out.

It is important to know for example that environments can be copied upon
entering a shell.  You could then proceed to manipulate the heck out of your
environment variables for your COBOL system run.  Adding and changing variable
that represent file names. Now everything is done, we are ready, ... script
ends, ... shell has no more work, ... shell ends, ... environment is popped!,
... no lasting effect, ... you bound back to the higher script under another
shell (or just a different instantiation of the 'same' shell), ... all your
vars are gone.

(This goes on within make in DOS, but not really in .BAT files, where the
system just careens forward, never popping back, and never losing the effects
accumulated in the environment..)

So you end up inexorably considering configuration files (perhaps slightly more
frequently known as control files in COBOL realms outside of UNIX), which have
files names and possibly parm text.  These files, when editted (actually piped
into temporary work files), will 'permanently' store your 
changes for the current run.

This leads to grep and sed.

Life is magnificently fluid in UNIX, resource control file (*.rc) and .config
files are basically the answer to the popping environment phenomenon (that is
just a genralization for the current discussion).  Note that you can 'edit'
(with grep and sed)  script files in any number of ways just before executing
them.

But keep in mind the notion of simplification.  Just because UNIX can pop and
environment, does not mean that you have to do that.  Just get it all clear
before it burns you. Truth is that you could create a cascade of scripts to
mimick the brain dead simplicity of DOS .BAT files, ... just keep descending
into deeper scripts, passing the environment in the manner you are comfortable
with.  Design your script so that there is nothing to return to after the
invokation of the lower script (that is no more commands in the higher script).

You don't have to limit yourself to this technique. But you are doing a
conversion, you need to get this thing up: a cascade down scripting sequence
can help you get started. You can become more of a virtuoso later.

Anyway, I know I have said too much already, and it is dinner time so that's
all you get.  Hope it helps some. Please continue to post and let us know what
dragons show up.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981118151833.22086.00000986@ng17.aol.com> <731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Before I comment on individual items, can I just recommend that you
purchase
a book on Unix and read up on the basic environment. It will answer a
lot of
your questions in more detail than you will get from Usenet.

bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> Let me clarify. When I wrote that we SET our files to the system, I meant as
> in SET TFPGMLOG=C:\XFRMRV51\MFORGW\TFPEDIT\TFPGMLOG.TXT where you tell the
> system the logical name (or "DDName") and the physical name (or path).

Yes, you can do this in Unix shell. The Bourne Shell for this is

TFPMLOG=/path/path/path/path/file.ext
export TFPMLOG

(the "export" command says that sub-processes will see this variable -
without
it only the shell you typed it into will see it. Unix shells use the
same
syntax for local variables and "environment" variables).

In Korn Shell, you can just say:

export TFPMLOG=/path/path/path/path/file.ext

Note that you may need to use the "DD_" prefix on variable names
depending on
your COBOL ASSIGN clause (this is specific to Micro Focus COBOL).

> And, I supppose that this script could then also contain the name
> of my executable program followed by -parms.

Yes. Note that a "shell script" is really little more than a
non-interactive
command line shell, so you can try all of this stuff yourself
interactively on
the command line:

$ sh
(creates a new shell)
$ XYZ="abc"
$ sh
(creates another new shell)
$ echo $XYZ
(nothing echoed XYZ is a local variable in our parent shell)
$ exit
(return to previous shell)
$ echo $XYZ
("abc" echoed)
$ export XYZ
(declare XYZ as an "environment variable")
$ sh
(creates new shell)
$ echo $XYZ
("abc" echoed - it was inherited this time)
$ XYZ="def"
$ exit
(return to previous shell)
$ echo $XYZ
("abc" echoed - environment variables are only inherited "forwards").
$ exit
(return to login shell)

> We're using Microsoft Visual Basic as a front end for Windows 95/NT.  We're
> not going to have a front end on the UNIX.  I need to be able to run my
> application from the command line.

You just need to write an executable front end which does the job of
tying the
app together, much like the VB exe does.

> I understand that the AIX initially comes up with the Korn shell, but the
> Micro Focus documentation says to use the Bourne shell.

Korn shell can be thought of as a superset of the Bourne shell. Examples
are
given in Bourne shell because that's pretty much guaranteed to exist,
and the
syntax will work in either. Forget the C shell (csh) IMHO.

> I hope the DLL strategy isn't dead !  I've yet to get the Micro Focus software
> installed, but I've got to compile my programs into some format.

You don't (as far as I can see) say what version of AIX you are using.
This has
a bearing. The standard for this sort of thing on Unix is "SVR4 Shared
Objects"
(which usually have the extension ".so"). However, AIX 4.1 only supports
a
proprietary "shared library" mechanism which has simiarities with DLLs.
From AIX 4.2 onwards, SVR4 shared objects are supported (as well as the
AIX-only
"shared libraries").

With regards to the support for these files in Micro Focus COBOL, the
product
you have will support the AIX "shared libraries", but not directly - you
need
to drive the system linker yourself to build them (use "man ld" to see
the 
manual page for the system linker). It is not straightforward.

The next release of Micro Focus COBOL (currently in Beta and due out on
certain
platforms next year) provides full support for SVR4 shared objects and
makes
creating them incredibly simple. I cannot say whether AIX is a supported
platform.

It sounds like you've got your work cut out just learning the Unix side
of
things and putting together the front end etc. A reasonable way to
proceed
might be to compile your COBOL code to .GNT format until you get things
together, *then* worry about packaging to shared library format - you
might be
in a position to take advantage of the newer Micro Focus release by
then, if
it is released on AIX. At the very least, you'll be more familiar with
Unix
and will not have such a problem fathoming out the AIX "shared library"
format.

Cheers, Kev.

PS. The above information is a personal (but informed) opinion and is in
no way
    an official statement on behalf of Micro Focus. Micro Focus reserves
the
    right to prove me wrong!
    Interested parties should contact their sales representative for an
    official response to any specific questions (maybe ask to be put on
the
    Beta programme).

    In addition, my comments regarding versions AIX and which versions
support
    which facilities are personal opinion - I strongly encourage people
to
    contact IBM if they have questions regarding such matters.

    In short, don't believe everything you read on Usenet ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<733rdi$568$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981118151833.22086.00000986@ng17.aol.com> <731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk>`

```
In article <36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk>,
Kevin Digweed  <ked@mfltd.co.uk> wrote:
> Before I comment on individual items, can I just
> recommend that you purchase a book on Unix and read up on
> the basic environment. It will answer a lot of your
> questions in more detail than you will get from Usenet.

For an amusing compendium of suitable references, search the
Web for the Loginataka by Eric S. Raymond.
>
> bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[ discussion elided -- dlc ]
>
>Note that you may need to use the "DD_" prefix on variable names
>depending on
>your COBOL ASSIGN clause (this is specific to Micro Focus COBOL).
>

It works like this:  in your MF COBOL program:

    SELECT FILE-NAME ASSIGN EXTERNAL "MYFILE"

and in your Bourne or Korn shell:

    DD_MYFILE='/home/myname/myfile"    ; export DD_MYFILE
    cobrun MYPROG

See the COBOL System Reference Manual chapter "External File Name
Mapping" for the details (you can even READ the output of shell
scripts; e.g., DD_MYFILE='</home/myname/my_input_script').
>
[ discussion elided -- dlc ]
>
>> I hope the DLL strategy isn't dead !  I've yet to get
>> the Micro Focus software installed, but I've got to
>> compile my programs into some format.
> 
[ discussion elided -- dlc ]

Going with shared objects with the current compiler
is not for the faint of heart, although if you are
only going to be targeting AIX vis-a-vis other UNIX
flavors, then it's much eaiser, but you will have a
learning curve.  See the $COBDIR/docs/dynlink.1 text
file for more specific information.  However, it is
much easier to go with dynamic linking:

   cob -i myprog.cbl

runs the checker that builds an intermediate file,
myprog.int that can be interpretively executed by any MF
COBOL Run Time System (RTS) on any platform (SLOWLY).

   cob -u myprog.int

runs the Native Code Generator (NCG) that builds a
generated file, myprog.gnt, a processor specific
version of the program that can be native mode
executed by any MF RTS on the same processor; e.g.,
Windows NT or SCO UNIX on a Pentium box.

Both .int and .gnt files are executed by the cobrun
command that fires up the RTS and scans the directories
defined in your COBPATH environment variable to find
the .int or .gnt file; e.g.,

   cobrun myprog

I hope this is helpful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting MF COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<733sa7$72a$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<72ssbu$ibg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <731vhu$2a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36554E02.8DEA7046@mfltd.co.uk> <733rdi$568$1@saltmine.radix.net>`

```
In article <733rdi$568$1@saltmine.radix.net>,
David L. Craig <g1dlc@Radix.Net> wrote:
>
>Going with shared objects with the current compiler
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>much easier to go with dynamic linking:

Oops.  I meant go with dynamic loading.  See the
COBOL System Reference Manual chapter "Linking."

Also, when I said any MF RTS could run your program,
I should have pointed out they need to be the same
release as your checker/generator.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ibm Cobol set for Aix or Microfocus Object Cobol Dev. Suite for UNIX ?

- **From:** jtpage33@aol.com (JTPage33)

I forgot one thing,  Oracle has a developers suite for cobol, that will allow
you to plug in a Run-Time system provied by oracle, for MF, that will support
all their calls with the exception of OCI, but that can be linked in.
This may be important to you if you use MF debugger animator.
again:

Jamie

#### ↳ Re: Ibm Cobol set for Aix or Microfocus Object Cobol Dev. Suite for UNIX ?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72b01v$cpp@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0c28$73887360$2e00d8d4@GIAVAVAS>`

```
Well, I would recommend IBM VisualAge COBOL very highly.
Especially if you are moving twoards or from a mainframe
or AS/400 machine. It tuns great under AIX and gives you
the same kind of GUI builders you find under OS/2 or Windows
NT, (and is compatible with OS/2, NT, VM, and MVS COBOL.) 

It works great with multi-langauge calls, C, Fortran, and PL/1,
as well as with CICS, MQ Series, and DB2. 

-Paul


Giampi (giavava@nch.it) wrote:

: Bye Giampi !

<a id="t-4c81592c7b"></a>
## Thread: ACCEPT FROM COMMAND-LINE in MF Cobol — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### ACCEPT FROM COMMAND-LINE in MF Cobol

- **From:** Frank Pohlmann <Frank.Pohlmann@baeurer.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364736F8.27B94309@baeurer.de>`

```
With the Workbench 3.4, I use ACCEPT PARAMETERS FROM COMMAND-LINE. It
works without problems. Now I used the Workbench 4.0 and I didn't get
any PARAMETERS from the command-line.
Can anybody give me a hint what has changed fom 3.4 to 4.0 or anything
else?

Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: ACCEPT FROM COMMAND-LINE in MF Cobol

## Thread: Assembler Training **promotional note** — 1 msgs

### Assembler Training **promotional note**

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981109094857.23668.00001003@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Regarding the recent thread about changing courses from C++ to COBOL, Assembler
came up as a worthy option. I would just like to pass on an opportunity to get
some OS/390 Assembler training early next year.

I am currently refurbishing my Assembler classes from a 7-day intro and 3-day
intermediate to two 5-day courses. At the same time I am integrating the new
hardware instructions into the main course flow and updating the information
about the Assembler to reflect the most recent version of HLASM.

A client of mine has scheduled an offering of these two weeks in Denver (Jan
25-29 for the first week, Feb 8-12 for the second week), tuition is $1,250 per
week. I'll update the outlines on our web site in a few weeks when the
revisions are in place. In the meantime, the current outlines (course codes
C212 and C220) can give you a pretty good idea of the content.

And now, back to COBOL...


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-dcfad4202b"></a>
## Thread: Printing under Unix — 1 msgs

### Printing under Unix

- **From:** "Newsgroups" <Domenyk.Newman@MCL-Group.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<910627070.23820.0.nnrp-09.9e98ccda@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Please help me!
I have written a program in Acucobol which is printing a form to a laser. It
is done in 3 passes using PCL to control the output. The first pass is
printing the layout of the form using line characters. The 2nd is printing
the box headings in a variety of fonts etc and the 3rd pass is printing the
actual data. The application is running on an AIX box and is used by a
number of remote sites using their own printers. 1 site is printing to the
laser from AIX via a Novell server, and it prints OK. Another site are
printing from the AIX to an HP laser directly, through a JetDirect card. The
output is slightly diffferent. The 2nd and 3rd passes seem to be padding the
output lines with spaces. If the print job is captured to disk and these
edited, the output is OK.
Has anybody got any ideas what I can do, without affecting the output on the
other printer. Is it a programmatic thing or is it a hardware issue. Any
guidance would be appreciated.
Regards,
D Newman
Medhurst Computers Limited
Email: Domenyk.Newman@MCL-Group.com
```

## Thread: COBOL to C conversion tools — 1 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-da3910d8c7)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to C conversion tools

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36472dc0$0$12769@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<70nrmk$a0r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7127aq$gu5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
If you can handle the code in Java rather than C, Synkronix
(www.synkronix.com) has a  COBOL compiler that allows your
COBOL to run on the Java Virtual Machine.  It allows easy
integration with other Java programs.  The nice thing about this
compiler is that your business logic doesn't get altered.  If it is standard
COBOL code, it will run right on the JVM, no tweaking required.

David


dean@deskware.com wrote in message <7127aq$gu5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

<a id="t-a4493252db"></a>
## Thread: -- system call? — 3 msgs

### -- system call?

- **From:** "Emulov" <emulovNOSPAM@sin.khk.be>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<727m8b$fnr$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```
lo ya'll,

I am a C fanatic, but COBOL is part of my education at college...

We work with CA-Realia II Workbench 3.0, under Windows 95/98. My question
for now is: how can I invoke a DOS-system call?

I know under C you just put system("here comes the system call"); but in
COBOL it is done how?

Thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: -- system call?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36476110.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<727m8b$fnr$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```
Emulov wrote in message <727m8b$fnr$1@xenon.inbe.net>...
>lo ya'll,
>I am a C fanatic, but COBOL is part of my education at college...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL it is done how?

There is a distinction between an API call and a system command.
A 'system call' is too ambiguous. Do you mean a call of an API function
or a system command?
As you state your example 'system(...)' it seems that you mean a command
(such as "dir" or "cd \xxx\ccc") as opposed to a DOS API which is an
interrupt 21h function.

Assuming you want a system command you could do
move "dir" to command-line
call "DOS_EXEC" using command-line

But you got to be a bit more precise.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: -- system call?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364761a8.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<727m8b$fnr$1@xenon.inbe.net> <36476110.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I was a bit too quick here, see below:

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36476110.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Emulov wrote in message <727m8b$fnr$1@xenon.inbe.net>...
>>lo ya'll,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>But you got to be a bit more precise.

There are two calls:
DOS_EXEC using prog-name command-line
DOS_EXEC_COMMAND using command-line
```

<a id="t-e1ae5bb6b4"></a>
## Thread: AUSTRALIAN TELON JOBS — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### AUSTRALIAN TELON JOBS

- **From:** "Peter Walsh " <peter@cca.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be0bac$d20cfea0$880315cb@peter.cca.com.au>`

```
Apologies for posting this job requirement in technical based news groups.

As we are having trouble filling these roles I have come here looking for
people with TELON skills who would like to expand their careers whilst
enjoying a fantastic travel experience in beautiful Australia.

Computer Consultants International, a world wide consulting firm, needs
experienced Telon contractors for one of our major clients.   You must have
experience using TELON and have good technical,  functional and
communication skills.   

The jobs are contract roles, typically of at least 12 months duration, pay
would be around $50 per hour..

CCI handles immigration formalities being a Government pre-approved
business sponsor.  We provide travel to Australia and initial accommodation
upon arrival.  You will find CCA to be a caring consulting organisation,
keeping in touch with what you are doing and giving assistance to resolve
problems
should they arise.  We are also well experienced in matters relating to
taxation - we believe it is important for you to maximise your net
disposable income while enjoying the Australian lifestyle.

We require people URGENTLY and will be interviewing by phone and/or in
person as soon as possible.   If you are interested in combining the
advancement of your career with an excellent travel opportunity, then
contact me now.

I look forward to hearing from anyone that may be interested.

Thanks and Regards,

Peter Walsh

E-mail : peter@cca.com.au
```

the resulting class it says "class not found"
even though it is there. Still working on it...

Just to show hat the resulting java looks like,
here is first the cobol program, then the java:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    TLEN.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  98/10/30
000600     -REVISED:  98/10/30.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  STRING-HANDLING.
001900     02  STRING-LENGTH           PIC S9(5)  COMP.
002000     02  STRING-PIECE-NBR        PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002100     02  THE-STRING.
002200         03  THE-STRING-CHAR     PIC X      OCCURS 1024 TIMES.
002300     02  STRING-PIECES           REDEFINES  THE-STRING.
002400         03  STRING-PIECE        PIC X(256) OCCURS 4 TIMES.
002500
002600 01  NUMBER-HANDLING.
002700     02  CHAR-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002800     02  EDITED-NUMBER           PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.99.
002900     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES EDITED-NUMBER.
003000         03  EDITED-NUMBER-CHAR  PIC X      OCCURS 13 TIMES.
003100     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES EDITED-NUMBER.
003200         03  EDITED-NUMBER-DIGIT PIC 9      OCCURS 13 TIMES.
003300
003400     02  NUMERIC-VAR             PIC 9(11)V99 COMP.
003500
003600 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
003700 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
003800     MOVE 17269.53 TO EDITED-NUMBER
003900     DISPLAY EDITED-NUMBER
004000     MOVE ZEROES TO NUMERIC-VAR
004100     PERFORM GET-DIGIT
004200       VARYING CHAR-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
004300         UNTIL CHAR-NBR > 13
004400
004500     DIVIDE 100 INTO NUMERIC-VAR
004600     MOVE NUMERIC-VAR TO EDITED-NUMBER
004700     DISPLAY EDITED-NUMBER
004800
004900     MOVE ZEROES TO THE-STRING
005000     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
005100     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
005200
005300     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
005400     MOVE "X"    TO THE-STRING-CHAR (800)
005500     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
005600     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
005700
005800     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
005900     MOVE "X"    TO THE-STRING-CHAR (600)
006000     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
006100     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
006200
006300     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
006400     MOVE "X"    TO THE-STRING-CHAR (400)
006500     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
006600     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
006700
006800     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
006900     MOVE "X"    TO THE-STRING-CHAR (200)
007000     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
007100     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
007200
007300     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
007400     MOVE "X"    TO THE-STRING-CHAR (1)
007500     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
007600     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
007700
007800     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
007900     PERFORM GET-STRING-LENGTH
008000     DISPLAY STRING-LENGTH
008100
008200     STOP RUN
008300     .
008400
008500 GET-STRING-LENGTH.
008600     MOVE 1024 TO STRING-LENGTH
008700     PERFORM OMIT-TRAILING-SPACES
008800       VARYING STRING-PIECE-NBR FROM 4 BY -1
008900         UNTIL STRING-PIECE-NBR < 1
009000            OR STRING-PIECE (STRING-PIECE-NBR) NOT = SPACES
009100
009200     IF STRING-LENGTH > ZERO
009300         PERFORM CHECK-STRING-FOR-SPACE
009400           VARYING STRING-LENGTH FROM STRING-LENGTH BY -1
009500             UNTIL THE-STRING-CHAR (STRING-LENGTH) NOT = SPACE
009600     .
009700
009800 OMIT-TRAILING-SPACES.
009900     SUBTRACT 256 FROM STRING-LENGTH
010000     .
010100
010200 CHECK-STRING-FOR-SPACE.
010300     EXIT
010400     .
010500
010600 GET-DIGIT.
010700     IF EDITED-NUMBER-CHAR (CHAR-NBR) IS NUMERIC
010800         COMPUTE NUMERIC-VAR = NUMERIC-VAR * 10
010900                 + EDITED-NUMBER-DIGIT (CHAR-NBR)
011000     .

// Java Imports
import com.synkronix.cobol.*;
import java.util.*;
import java.sql.*;

/**
 * Generated by PERCobol(TM) COBOL to Java Cross-Compiler<br>
 * PERCobol(TM) is Copyright 1996-1998 <a
href="http://www.synkronix.com">Synkronix, Inc.</a><p>
 * COBOL PROGRAM-ID tlen
 * WHEN-COMPILED: 19981110091126000000000
 * @version 2.0
 */
public class tlen extends cobolThread
{
    public int majorPercobolVersion() { return 2; }
    public int minorPercobolVersion() { return 0; }
    /**
     * @return Program/Class name in Java style.
     */
    public String getProgramName()
    {
        return "tlen";
    }

    // Allocate Cobol style memory
    static private Memory memory=new Memory(getMemorySize());	// Cobol
shared memory
    private final Memory localMemory=new Memory(getMemorySizeLocal());	//
Cobol non-shared memory
    static private Vector entryPoint=new Vector(32); // Entry points of new
instances
    static private IO SYSTEM=IO.allocateIO("SYSTEM");
    static private IO CONSOLE=IO.allocateIO("CONSOLE");
    static private IO SYSERR=IO.allocateIO("SYSERR");

    static public synchronized void addEntry(int n)
    {
        entryPoint.addElement(new Integer(n));
    }

    static public synchronized int getEntry()
    {
        if(!(entryPoint.isEmpty()))
        {
            int n=((Integer)(entryPoint.firstElement())).intValue();
            entryPoint.removeElementAt(0);
            return n;
        }
        return 0;
    }

    static public boolean traditionalCobolTerminated=false;
    public void run()
    {
        this.run(-1,getEntry());
        if(!traditionalCobolTerminated)
        {
            if(Utilities.isApplet())
            {
                applet_init();
                applet_start();
            }
            else
            {
                application_init();
            }
            traditionalCobolTerminated=true;
        }
    }

    public void run(int setCurrentThread)
    {
        this.run(setCurrentThread,getEntry());
    }

    static public tlen newThread()
    {
        return newThread(0);
    }

    static public tlen newThread(int entryParagraph)
    {
        addEntry(entryParagraph);
        tlen newInstance=new tlen();
        newInstance.start();
        return newInstance;
    }

    public void newThreadThis(int entryParagraph)
    {
        addEntry(entryParagraph);
        tlen newInstance=new tlen();
        newInstance.EventData=EventData;
        newInstance.EventObject=EventObject;
        newInstance.start();
    }

    static public void runInstance()
    {
        addEntry(0);
        tlen newInstance=new tlen();
        newInstance.start();
        try
        {
            newInstance.join();
        }
        catch(Throwable e)
        {
            ;
        }
    }

     //---------------\\
    // COBOL PARAGRAPH \\____ BEGIN-PROGRAM ____
    public final int begin_program_Label=1;

    public int begin_program()
    {
        edited_number_OF_number_handling.move(new Display("17269.53"));

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(edited_number_OF_number_handling.toString());
        numeric_var_OF_number_handling.move(((Display)Variable.ZEROES));
        char_nbr_OF_number_handling.move(new Display("1"));
        while(!((char_nbr_OF_number_handling.compare(new Display("13"))>0)))
        {
            if((performResult=perform(get_digit_Label))<0) return
performResult;
            char_nbr_OF_number_handling.add(new Display("1"));
        }
        sizeError=false;
        sizeError|=numeric_var_OF_number_handling.div(new
Display("100")).isSizeError();

edited_number_OF_number_handling.move(numeric_var_OF_number_handling);

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(edited_number_OF_number_handling.toString());
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(((Display)Variable.ZEROES));
        if((performResult=perform(get_string_length_Label))<0) return
performResult;

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(string_length_OF_string_handling.toDisplayString(
));
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(Variable.SPACES);

the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(800).move("X");
        if((performResult=perform(get_string_length_Label))<0) return
performResult;

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(string_length_OF_string_handling.toDisplayString(
));
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(Variable.SPACES);

the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(600).move("X");
        if((performResult=perform(get_string_length_Label))<0) return
performResult;

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(string_length_OF_string_handling.toDisplayString(
));
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(Variable.SPACES);

the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(400).move("X");
        if((performResult=perform(get_string_length_Label))<0) return
performResult;

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(string_length_OF_string_handling.toDisplayString(
));
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(Variable.SPACES);

the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(200).move("X");
        if((performResult=perform(get_string_length_Label))<0) return
performResult;

sizeError=!CONSOLE.display(string_length_OF_string_handling.toDisplayString(
));
        the_string_OF_string_handling.move(Variable.SPACES);

the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(1).move("X");

performResult;

));

));

        return 0; // fall through

    // COBOL PARAGRAPH \\____ GET-STRING-LENGTH ____
    public final int get_string_length_Label=3;

    public int get_string_length()
    {
        string_length_OF_string_handling.move("1024");
        string_piece_nbr_OF_string_handling.move(new Display("4"));
        while(!((string_piece_nbr_OF_string_handling.compare(new
Display("1"))<0) ||
(string_piece_OF_string_pieces_OF_string_handling.subscript(string_piece_nbr
_OF_string_handling.toInt()).compare(Variable.SPACES)!=0)))
        {
            if((performResult=perform(omit_trailing_spaces_Label))<0) return
 performResult;
            string_piece_nbr_OF_string_handling.add(new Display("-1"));
        }

if((string_length_OF_string_handling.compare(((Display)Variable.ZEROES))>0))
        {

string_length_OF_string_handling.move(string_length_OF_string_handling);

while(!((the_string_char_OF_the_string_OF_string_handling.subscript(string_l
ength_OF_string_handling.toInt()).compare(Variable.SPACES)!=0)))
            {
                if((performResult=perform(check_string_for_space_Label))<0)
return performResult;
                string_length_OF_string_handling.add(new Display("-1"));
            }
        }
        return 0; // fall through
    } // END PARAGRAPH

Display("256")).isSizeError();
        return 0; // fall through
    } // END PARAGRAPH

    public final int check_string_for_space_Label=5;

    } // END PARAGRAPH
