```
IBM)  pay  yearly  dues  and  in  return  are  included  in   the
Foundation's promotion efforts, literature, directories, etc. and
have  their  company  and  product  information  posted  on   the
Foundation's web server. The URL for the server is

Many  people  have  contributed  to  this  FAQ  in  each  of  its
iterations. In the past an ongoing list of these people have been
included  within  the  FAQ.  At this time,  i  am  stopping  this
practice, although I ,in no way, want to deprecate all  the  work
and  input  that these people have provided. I have continued  to
identify people whom I am quoting (directly or indirectly) and do
still  hope  that everyone who has input for this  document  will
continue to provide it.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.
FAQ availability details.
Added section on Commercial COBOL Products.
Removed 'what happened to Realia?' section.
Removed references to Wang, as I can find no information  on  any
Wang COBOL Products.
Checked all URLs, and updated/added as appropriate.
Removed reference to IBM 'COBOL Products WWW site'.
Added  entries  in  'GUI Tools' section for  Micro  Focus  Dialog
System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting
  �    Updated OO COBOL Section
  �    Add URL for IBM COBOL Web site
  �    Added information on "COBOL for Dummies"
  �    Added initial Y2K section
  �    Added information on using the newsgroups
  �    Added information on PCYACC

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
  �    Started adding Logos
  �    Removed the detailed list of FAQ contributors
  �    Fixed erroneous Standards information
  �    Corrected information about Liant (who acquired RM)
  �    Added information on Amiga freeware compiler
  �    Added information on Siber tools
  �    Corrected CA company and product information
  �     Added information on where to turn for information not in
     the FAQ
  �    Rephrased the information on what can/should be put in the
     newsgroups.
  �    Revised the information on the CobCy project/product
  �    Updated Micro Focus information
  �    Added URL for "Dice" for those looking for COBOL jobs.
  �    Added information on getting a "COBOL Tutor"

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x
  �    Added and corrected information on Fujitsu
  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.
```

## Thread: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS? — 10 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Sterling Software (CoolGen, formerly IEF) pretends that one has to recompile
all components of an application when upgrading to an LE version of COBOL
under CICS.

Correctly , if you consult the CICS-manual, but not true, if you consult the
several COBOL- and the LE-manuals!

Even in the newest CICS-manual I have at hand (CICS Transaction Server for
OS/390 Application Programming Guide Release 2, Document Number SC33-1687-01)
you read the following:

<paste from manual>
1.2.1.6  Mixing languages
   A run unit is a running set of one or more programs that communicate with
   each other by COBOL static or dynamic CALL statements....
<snip>
   Because CICS does not support a mixture of language levels in a run unit,
   a COBOL run unit can contain only:
   -   COBOL programs compiled with the same compiler
   -   ASSEMBLER ..........
<snip the rest>

So far, Sterling is right. Only VS COBOL II <or> COBOL for MVS & VM in one run
unit!

But every other manual contradicts that statement! For example the newest
COBOL manual (COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming Guide Version 2 Release 1,
Document Number SC26-9049-02):

<paste from manual>
4.10.1.10  Calls under CICS
You can make calls to and from VS COBOL II, COBOL for MVS & VM, and
COBOL for OS/390 & VM programs.
<snip>
COBOL for OS/390 & VM, COBOL for MVS & VM, and VS COBOL II programs
cannot call or be called by OS/VS COBOL programs.  EXEC CICS LINK must
be used instead.
<snip the rest>

Same in the Programming Guide of COBOL for MVS V1R2, same in the Programming
Guide of Language Environment V2R4.

Now, what shall we do? Are we to believe the majority of the manuals? Are we
to be extremely prudent and hold two separately compiled versions of every
module and two versions of DB2 package? BTW we have no OS/VS COBOL, but only
VS COBOL II V1R4M0 modules in production.

TIA for your opinions of this matter!

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b36b23cc3a7f5b989681@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

In article <72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch 
says...
> Sterling Software (CoolGen, formerly IEF) pretends that one has to recompile
> all components of an application when upgrading to an LE version of COBOL
> under CICS.
> 
<SNIPO>
> 
> Now, what shall we do? Are we to believe the majority of the manuals? Are we
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Daniel

Hi Daniel,

  I would think you would have to recompile at least to migrate to 
an LE version of Cobol. Since you are upgrading the compiler I am 
assuming that you are also upgrading the version of CoolGen/IEF at 
the same time?? If you are from my past experience with the product
I would re-gen the code and compile, link and bind to make sure that
everything works. At least that is the procedure we would follow when 
we migrated to a newer version of IEF.  

Good Luck,
Jeff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981111141223.29247.00000141@ng117.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10b36b23cc3a7f5b989681@news.earthlink.net>`

```
You are in pretty good shape, and you probably can do all of the contemplated
combinations without maintaining separate loadlib versions. 

Since you do not have any of the really old compiler stuff around, you do not
have a syntax change issue and you do not have the ILBxxxx service module calls
to deal with.  Since you are at COBOL II or better, then you are dealling with
IGZxxxx service modules, and one way or another they should be found in your
CICS environment under OS/390 LE.

(A brief aside: considering the assertion that you have COBOL II can be tricky,
since it is possible to have migrated to COBOL II with compile options to parse
COBOL 74 syntax.  Some organizations do that to buy time to convert source code
to COBOL 85 later ... times up. If your shop has done that you DO have a
potentially hefty source code conversion issue front and center.  I think the
compile parm is COMPR2. That name reflects a compiler syntax regression to a
Release 2 of a compiler that predates COBOL II.)

So for you things should be easy to do, but sometimes hard to talk about.
You state in part
>> Sterling Software (CoolGen, formerly IEF) pretends that one has to recompile
all components ...
<<
I encourage you to not be so harsh on them.  Really what we are all dealing
with together here is a Tower of Babel effect that takes hold in the
application projects when we talk about this.

Many COBOLers do not have a thorough understanding of the linkage technology,
and the run-time environment that came in with COBOL for MVS, is essoteric for
sure.  Project management personnel typically are not current technically.
Frequently they can not understand.

What you are dealing with is a situation that may require you to relink.
Specifically, the intermediate product COBOL for MVS allowed a MIXRES option
alternative (which you may not have ever used), that is not viable in the new
OS/390 LE. You need to go RES (this is actually for COBOL service submodules).
This impacts mostly batch.

Use of RES can create compile parameter combination challenges, specifically
relating to static and dynamic calls. You then proceed to try to converse about
the difference between static/dynamic calls and static/dynamic links and the
merry-go-round never seems to end.

Eventually, you get to a simple, but sometimes expensive position. You will
perhaps conclude that you are dealing with a software regeneration issue: some
relinking, some recompiling.

In the new world, the softer the link, the better.

Vendors are now trying to stay away from the intricacies. It is safest to say
'recompile.' The general issue is to 'regenerate systems'.  Many shops,
however, do not have standalone link procs enumerated, so there could be some
work ahead to get to a postition where you can just re-link.

On the CICS side, the major consideration is AMODE and RMODE, since your CICS
stuff is probably already RES. Generally this is an optimization consideration,
you want to get everything above the line if you can. The vendor's software may
have certain requirements in this regard.

An important, and not so obvious problem here is that you may have to test many
more modules than you change! It is especially difficult to convince upper
management of the need for testing resources in this area.

COBOL for MVS allowed Language Environment. COBOL for OS/390 requires it.  One
important implication of re-linking a module (or regenerating a whole system)
is that you now have new COBOL service modules (possibly dynamically configured
by systems programming) as the foundation of your executable environment. For
critical systems it IS necessary to prove that that environment will work for
your programs as coded and linked. 

Despite the cost of testing, it is almost alway easier to find the problems in
test then in production (especially with CICS or other transaction oriented
systems that could drive thousands of damaged records onto files within a very
short period of time).

LE/390 allows system programmers to pull strings, to set parameters across all
modules. This can impact CICS programs carte blanche.  It is a good idea to do
atleast minimum system testing of systems regenerated for LE.

In short, you are in good shape because it sounds like you do not have a source
code conversion issue. You may need some relinking, and you may benefit most by
concluding early that you need to be able to regenerate entire systems, not
just a few modules.
And finally, for critical systems, you need to observe your modules in the
LE/390 environment in test mode at a low level of detail.
 
Best Wishes,
















Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote:
> 
> Sterling Software (CoolGen, formerly IEF) pretends that one has to recompile
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> several COBOL- and the LE-manuals!
...
> Now, what shall we do? Are we to believe the majority of the manuals? Are we
> to be extremely prudent and hold two separately compiled versions of every
> module and two versions of DB2 package? BTW we have no OS/VS COBOL, but only
> VS COBOL II V1R4M0 modules in production.
> 
...

Based on actual experience, there are combinations that will get you
into trouble, though it depends on a number of factors and I don't
remember all the details.  After getting burned on several applications
after changeover to COBOL for MVS, we found the safest rule was to
demand that COBOL subroutines that were to be linked with a CICS COBOL
program must have been recompiled with the new compiler prior to
recompiling any invoking CICS COBOL program with the new compiler.  The
problem may have only occurred in cases where the subroutine loadmod
included some old COBOL run-time CSECTS, but I'm not sure.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org>`

```
Combining the two is VERY "doable" - but does require some care (and
certainly you need to make certain that you were using ALL "res"
applications with VS COBOL II - but that was supposed to be done any way).

Can you point me to some specific place in the CICS manuals that says that
this has a problem?

Joel C. Ewing wrote in message <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org>...
>daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote:
>>
>> Sterling Software (CoolGen, formerly IEF) pretends that one has to
recompile
>> all components of an application when upgrading to an LE version of COBOL
>> under CICS.
>>
>> Correctly , if you consult the CICS-manual, but not true, if you consult
the
>> several COBOL- and the LE-manuals!
>...
>> Now, what shall we do? Are we to believe the majority of the manuals? Are
we
>> to be extremely prudent and hold two separately compiled versions of
every
>> module and two versions of DB2 package? BTW we have no OS/VS COBOL, but
only
>> VS COBOL II V1R4M0 modules in production.
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364E0081.D3BA407D@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Combining the two is VERY "doable" - but does require some care (and
> certainly you need to make certain that you were using ALL "res"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joel C. Ewing wrote in message <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org>...
...
> >
> >Based on actual experience, there are combinations that will get you
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >included some old COBOL run-time CSECTS, but I'm not sure.
...

No.
Our read of the manuals convinced us it was OK or we wouldn't have gone
into production mode with COBOL for MVS -- and for the most part is was
OK.  But after hitting two similar problems after one week, both having
the simple solution of recompiling a support subroutine, we came to the
conclusion that it was much simpler to lay down the rule that such
subroutines be recompiled first.  The problem only surfaced in the CICS
environment and only occurred when the main program was "higher-level"
than the subroutine.  My suspicions are that this may have been more
related to run-time environment in effect when the subroutines were
linked rather than the actual compiled code, but frankly the recompile
correction was cheaper than spending much time analyzing the load
modules and then trying to explain to applications development in terms
meaningless to them when they would or would not have a problem. 

Finding nice clear rules explicitly laid out in a manual can give you a
warm fuzzy feeling, but having it in writing doesn't guarantee
correctness.  Empirical evidence in your own environment is the only
ultimate authority.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732di9$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <364E0081.D3BA407D@acm.org>`

```

Joel C. Ewing wrote in message <364E0081.D3BA407D@acm.org>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
  <much snippage>
>
>Finding nice clear rules explicitly laid out in a manual can give you a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Finding it documented in a vendor's manual means that you should be able to
get the vendor (not your application programmers) to provide a fix when it
is NOT working.  This is fine when at the beginning of a lengthy
development/maitenance phase, but not very useful when called at 3am in the
morning with production "dead in the water".  My recommendation is to ALWAYS
(even at 3am in the morning) let the vendor know when there product is not
working as documented - HOWEVER, to also fix (find a circumvention) for the
problem at hand.

P.S.  I have already refered the other post with the documentation from CICS
that seems to conflict with the COBOL documentation to the "appropriate"
people at IBM.  I am waiting for a reply that I will share when I get it.

>--
>Joel C. Ewing, Fort Smith, AR        jcewing@acm.org
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3655009B.DA73263A@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <364E0081.D3BA407D@acm.org> <732di9$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
...
> Finding it documented in a vendor's manual means that you should be able to
> get the vendor (not your application programmers) to provide a fix when it
> is NOT working.  This is fine when at the beginning of a lengthy
...

Finding it documented gives you a defensive position from which to fire
the first salvo at the vendor, but you may still end up losing the war. 
When presented with a conflict between manuals and code IBM (and I
suspect other vendors) has historically elected to either (1)insist the
code is "working as designed" (WAD response) and create a documentation
APAR to alter the documentation, or (2) admit the code has a problem and
either create a correction APAR or make a committment to rectify the
problem in some future release.  A WAD-documentation change is a much
simpler solution to implement from the vendor's standpoint; so even when
you feel your case is clearcut, forceful persistence may be required to
convince the vendor to commit to a code change (and to work your way
past the "database searchers" to get to someone who understands the
product).  This resistance is not totally unjustified:  the empirical
behavior of product code is itself a form a "documentation", and some
customers may have dependencies on that behavior, even where it
conflicts with manuals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72oms2$s8d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<72bq04$brk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <364CF86D.43DE8010@acm.org> <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <72j09e$khf@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> Combining the two is VERY "doable" - but does require some care (and
> certainly you need to make certain that you were using ALL "res"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip the rest>

Refer to:

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHCAP32

<manual cover>
CICS Transaction Server for OS/390

CICS Application Programming Guide

Release 2

Document Number SC33-1687-01

Program Number
5655-147
<end of cover>

<paste from above manual>
1.2.1.6 Mixing languages

A run unit is a running set of one or more programs that communicate with
each other by COBOL static or dynamic CALL statements. In a CICS environment,
a run unit is entered at the start of a CICS task, or invoked by a LINK or
XCTL command. A run unit can be defined as the execution of a single entry in
the processing program table (PPT) even though for dynamic CALL, the
subsequent PPT entry is needed for the called program.

Because CICS does not support a mixture of language levels in a run unit, a
COBOL run unit can contain only:

     COBOL programs compiled with the same compiler
     Assembler language routines

CICS supports only COBOL-to-COBOL and COBOL-to-assembler calls.

However, a CICS transaction can consist of many run units, each of which can
be at a different language level. This means that a single transaction can
consist of programs compiled by different compilers (including non-COBOL
compilers), provided that programs compiled by different compilers
communicate with each other only using LINK or XCTL commands. <end of paste>

The point is 'COBOL programs compiled with the same compiler'! This statement
contradicts every other COBOL- and LE-manual to this topic.

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mix VS COBOL II / COBOL for MVS in CICS?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981116104829.04685.00001189@ng132.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<72oms2$s8d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
"Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
On  Sat, 14 Nov 1998 16:13:21 -0600
got the quarry in the cross hairs with 
>> ... , but frankly the recompile
correction was cheaper than spending much time analyzing the load
modules and then trying to explain to applications development in terms
meaningless to them when they would or would not have a problem. 
<<
 
We are dealing with cost factors. It is cheaper if a project understands from
the begining the need to REGENERATE the software wholesale.

IBMs products are truely amazing in their ability to stretch old software into
new environments. But each application run unit needs to be caste. 

That WOULD necessitate parallel libraries in some shops. But caste applets in
environments that mix dynamic and static calls is babel, babel, babel, ... and
the meter is running on the consultants, the full time staff, and the millenium
event threshold approaches. Recompile.

As an aside, because, really, avoiding the discussion bog is the most important
thing, but just to put a lid on the old compilers. Keep in mind that a shop
that tries to get old-ly compiled source code into the new millenium must bring
the compiler itself into 1/1/2000.  Have you tested that? Have you tested the
compilers, assemblers, linkers, macros and PROCs on the Y2K machine. That is
different from the application itself.  

If you even contemplate parallel libraries as suggested by the original
postings of this thread
then you most believe that the old compiler(s) will make it across. You are on
your own if you try that.   If there is a lot of software that you want to be
old-ly compiled, then you are at big risk.  If there is only a small amount of
code, then convert; recompile; stop wasting time. 

It is not nice to thing of any shop that is confronted with that type of
decission, but it is much more painful to watch the babel bog syndrome.
Recompile. Do it! 

As of now, we have about twelve productive month left considering the effect of
holidays.
```

```
Equally important, if you are not a consultant, contractor or independent
software developer, is finding (and using) a style that your _boss_ is
comfortable with (if you want to remain employed.)  Most, or at least many,
organizations have some level of mandated coding style so there are
relatively fewer style choices remaining to be made by subordinate
programming staff members.


Dennis J. Minette
CCCO
```

## Thread: Standard Label Tape — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standard Label Tape

- **From:** "Ruben Steck" <r.steck@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>`

```

Can anyone point me in the right direction?

I am trying to find a written syntax for both the header on a standard
labeled tape and a COBOL file descriptor.

If you can help, that'd be great.

Ruben Steck
r.steck@worldnet.att.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard Label Tape

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A0CED.33716B71@home.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>`

```
I remember using an Amdahl operating system called ASPEN.  I really
liked it, but it never went gold.  It had a help system which discussed
labeled tapes as being something which one used when writing tapes for
companies which used "water cooled computers".
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard Label Tape

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364a0da6.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>`

```
The whole syntax?  I assume you mean record layout/organization?  I'll also
assume you're talking about an IBM 3480 or related tape, since "standard
labels" are found there most often.

I can't remember the whole thing, but here goes . . .  (I'll omit discussion
of Tape Marks)

The first thing on an SL tape is the 6 byte volume serial number.  This
field should have the same value as the physical label on the outside of the
tape.
The second thing is the 80 byte Header One record.
The third thing is the 80 byte Header Two record.

These are collectively considered the "tape label", although there is more.

The last thing on a tape will either be an End of File or an End of Volume
record.  End of File for the end of a dataset, unless that dataset is the
last dataset on a tape (there can be more than one dataset on one tape, and
there can be more than one dataset on more than one tape; i.e. huge dataset
1 goes into four and half tapes, and dataset 2 goes onto the last half of
tape five and then onto a couple of more tapes, etc.), in which case you get
End of Volume.  On top of this, there is an End of Volume at the end of
every tape in a multi-volume dataset (i.e. dataset 1 from above gets an EOV
label at the end of tape 1, tape 2, tape 3, tape 4, and an EOF half way
through tape 5 where dataset two begins; thereafter, dataset 2 gets an EOV
label at the end of tape 5, tape 6, and tape 7, where it ends and leave an
EOV label because it is the last dataset on the tape).

EOF labels and EOV labels come in sets just like the Header labels.  There
is an EOF 1 and an EOF 2, and an EOV 1 and EOV 2, and they are always found
in pairs, just like Header labels.

The Header labels of successive volumes in a multi-volume dataset contain
information about the immediately previous tape in the series.  The volser
field at the beginning of successive volumes in a multi-volume dataset does
not change (that is to say, it sold have the same value as the physical
label on the cartridge).


If you need the exact record formats, I can look up where to find them and
tell you, or photocopy a complete description if you don't have access to
that information.  Email me if you need them.



Ruben Steck wrote in message <01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>...
>
>Can anyone point me in the right direction?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>r.steck@worldnet.att.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard Label Tape

- **From:** "Wayne L. Beavers" <wayneb@beyond-software.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A1E70.BD8@beyond-software.com>`
- **References:** `<01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>`

```
Ruben Steck wrote:
> 
> Can anyone point me in the right direction?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> r.steck@worldnet.att.net

There is an IBM pub named something like "Tape Labels". Can't recall off
the top of my head.

If all you want to do is read them then all you need to know is that
they are 80 byte records. You may not be able to access them from a
program because many shops do not allow you to code Label=BLP in the
JCL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard Label Tape

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D0A10.6C945C6B@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<01be0dba$bbe24940$11764e0c@old>`

```
Ruben Steck wrote:
> Can anyone point me in the right direction?
> I am trying to find a written syntax for both the header on a standard
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> r.steck@worldnet.att.net

See
http://www2.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DGT1M302/CCONTENTS?SHELF=DGT1BK23
for current version of "IBM DFSMS Using Magnetic Tapes", specifically
Chapter 2 on IBM Standard Labels and Chapter 3 on ISO/ANSI Standard
Labels.

Reasonable operating systems designed for commercial use automatically
create standard labels on output tapes and analyze standard labels on
input tapes to improve the reliability of data interchange via tape. 
When receiving a tape from a non-technically oriented user of another
installation, any information provided about the physical format of the
tape may be more wishful intent rather than actuality.  If the tape uses
standard labels, these provide the information needed to verify you have
the correct data set and to correctly access the logical records on the
tape, in spite of misinformation provided by its creator.  Correct
interpretation of fields within a record may still be a problem, but at
least you can get to the records.
```

<a id="t-5bfd143e8b"></a>
## Thread: Multi Column Display Issue — 2 msgs

### Multi Column Display Issue

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364a046e.21648117@news.abraxis.com>`

```
For debugging (and just to watch some output on screen) I put display statements
at two locations in one program:  whenever two different performs are carried
out.  The second group (of displays) executes sometime after the first group is
done.  Without any attempt to format the output on screen they're written in the
same very tall pile starting at column 1 line 1.  

I tried to format the output so that the first display statement was set
explicitly to start at line 1 column 1 and the second to start at line 1 column
40.  The screen variables are set by default to 80 x 24 (in the Fujitsu 3.0, and
unknown on Aix--MF v.3??).  Both compilers choked and didn't finish compiling.
I tried DISPLAY AT and variations.  I think the Fujitsu documentation didn't
really specify syntax to allow this or I couldn't figure it out and there is
some documentation in the class's textbook ('From Micro to Mainframes' which is
essentially dedicated to MicroFocus) but nothing exactly about this sort of
trick.  

Is it possible to do this?  (example follows)
'Preciate your opinions!  TS


output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
++++++*******Dontcha*just*hate*this*antispamming*stuff??********++++++
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

#### ↳ Re: Multi Column Display Issue

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@getridofspam.worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72e6gr$935@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<364a046e.21648117@news.abraxis.com>`

```

OldUncleMe wrote in message <364a046e.21648117@news.abraxis.com>...
>
>Is it possible to do this?  (example follows)
>
>output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later


Simple way would be to replace the first DISPLAY with a MOVE,
saving 'output stuff 1' in working storage,
then display 'output stuff 1' (from the save area), some blanks,
and 'output stuff 2', using one DISPLAY statement.
```

<a id="t-97fdce1e11"></a>
## Thread: Career Development Opportunities in Ireland and the UK — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Career Development Opportunities in Ireland and the UK

- **From:** "PKS" <vcasey@Pks.ie>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be0d57$eee5b560$6a01330a@vcasey.pks.ie>`

```
Situated on the grounds of the University of Limerick, PKS Systems
Integration (Ireland) Limited has spent the last two years establishing its
reputation in the remote delivery of high quality software maintenance
services to our Fortune 500 clients.

A subsidiary of PKS Systems Integration Inc in Phoenix, we are one of the
most exciting companies operating in the dynamic information services
industry.  PKS' software engineering centres around the world currently
employ over fifteen hundred software engineers skilled in mainframe, PC and
client server technologies.  Our ultimate parent company is Level 3
Communications Inc, a global telecommunications and information services
company.  Level 3 is NASDAQ quoted with a market capitalisation of US$12
billion.

Having proven our capabilities, primarily in the Year 2000 arena, we are
now gearing up to deliver to the same high standards in software
development, feature enhancement, general maintenance and reengineering. 
Our success to date has resulted in heavy demand from our clients for our
legacy systems renewal, application migration, re-platforming and
re-engineering services. 

Poised for significant growth, we are keen to recruit the following
personnel:

Software Engineers

Software Engineers (1 to 4 years' experience) and Senior Software Engineers
(5+ years' experience) mainly in the area of IBM mainframe COBOL
application development and maintenance. 

You should have reasonable knowledge of a mix of CICS, batch and DB2
systems, in both program design and development. 

If your main development experience is on IDMS, IMS or non-MVS, suitable
training will be provided.  As a minimum, you must be fluent in COBOL. 

Some of the services in our portfolio are designed to meet the needs of
clients, migrating legacy applications to the client server world (WEB
enabling, re-platforming, etc).  To meet the needs of the business, you
must be willing to retrain in non legacy technologies. 

We have a wide range of in-house and external training resources in such
areas as C/C++, TCP/IP, object technology and visual programming. You must
share our enthusiasm for developing your own career as well as the clients'
applications.

Technical Support Specialists

You will be part of a team that continues to enhance and support our
constantly expanding network infrastructure, providing service excellence
to our end-users. 

Experience is essential in a number of the following: PC, LAN, WAN, TCP/IP,
Windows NT, Unix and telecommunications.  

A degree/diploma in computer/electronic engineering is required as well as
a minimum of two years' experience in a similar role.

As part of our operations and technology group, you will be trained in
leading edge enterprise management software tools to proactively manage our
LAN/WAN network infrastructure from our command centre. 
 
Technical Consultants

This is a very challenging and rewarding role.  Responsible for designing
and implementing enterprise management software solutions at our client
sites in Ireland and the UK, your contribution will be a significant factor
to the growth of our Network Integration Services division.  

You will get the opportunity to work with blue chip multinationals and
explore and develop experience in new platforms, application software and
telecommunication technologies. 

You should have at least two years' experience in network management and
ideally be certified in Novell or NT.

It is essential that you have experience in LAN/WAN Windows NT and TCP/IP. 
Experience in CA-Unicenter TNG, HPOpenview or Microsoft SMS is desirable
but not essential.  

Full training will be provided where necessary. 


If you are willing to travel, some of these positions require periods of up
to six months in the US at one of our offices close to New York.  Health
care, accommodation, transport and living allowances will be provided.

Interested candidates should apply in writing enclosing a detailed CV to

Orla O'Connell
Recruitment Manager
PKS Systems Integration (Ireland) Limited
National Technological Park
Limerick

e-mail		ooconnell@pks.ie		
Telephone	061 333066
Fax		061 333068
Web		www.pksis.com
```

<a id="t-e2813b083c"></a>
## Thread: My homework — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### My homework

- **From:** The Chameleon <sarvec@letterbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
Getting desperate here.
Straight out of Sam's teach yourself Cobol in 21 days.
01 TABLE-STATE-RECORD OCCURS 50 TIMES.
   05   TABLE-STATE-CODE       PIC XX.
   05   TABLE-STATE-NAME       PIC X(2).
And my Fujitsu compiler complains:
OCCURS CLAUSE CANNOT BE SPECIFIED FOR LEVEL 01 OR LEVEL 77 ITEM.
Help?
```

#### ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649B480.69206ADB@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
The book is using a non-conforming extension. To quote IBM:

"To code a table, give the table a group name and define a subordinate
item
(the table element) that is to be repeated n times:

     01  table-name.

         05  element-name OCCURS n TIMES. "
"
The OCCURS clause cannot be specified in a data description entry that:

   * Has a level number of 01, 66, 77, or 88."

Just change 01 TABLE-STATE-RECORD OCCURS 50 TIMES.
to:
01 TABLE-STATE-RECORD.
         02       OCCURS 50 TIMES.
             05   TABLE-STATE-CODE       PIC XX.
             05   TABLE-STATE-NAME       PIC X(2).


The Chameleon wrote:

> Getting desperate here.
> Straight out of Sam's teach yourself Cobol in 21 days.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Help?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649C09B.7402FC2F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com> <3649B480.69206ADB@ix.netcom.com>`

```
John Trifon wrote:
> 
> The book is using a non-conforming extension. To quote IBM:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Help?

If he is copying code, he may wish to keep the full functionality of
being able to address TABLE-STATE-RECORD (5).
```

#### ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b37ef3b280cd3c989682@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

In article <3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>, sarvec@letterbox.com says...
> Getting desperate here.
> Straight out of Sam's teach yourself Cobol in 21 days.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Guess what,

    01 TABLE-STATE.
       05  TABLE-STATE-RECORD OCCURS 50 TIMES.
           10 TABLE-STATE-CODE   PIC X(02).
           10 TABLE-STATE-NAME   PIC X(02).

This will work just fine.
Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649C019.791BF251@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
This is a compiler dependent error.  It bugs me that I have the same
error with IBM 370, but there are some people on this NG who like that
limit.

Just change the code:
01  TABLE-STATE-TABLE.
    02  TABLE-STATE-RECORD OCCURS 50 TIMES.
        05   TABLE-STATE-CODE    PIC XX.
        05   TABLE-STATE-NAME    PIC X(2).

The Chameleon wrote:
> 
> Getting desperate here.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Help?
```

#### ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72chca$ft$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
One of the rules is "read what the error message says".
If an occurs cannot be used with an 01 level, try making
it an 02 level.  That rule, BTW, I am told is standard. Many
compilers do allow occurs on an 01, so the example
is non-conforming, but probably runs on most compilers.

I find it annoying, as I do not see any need for the 01 level rule.
However, if you go back a few weeks in this NG, you will find
a long argument over that very point.

A correction is below.

The Chameleon wrote in message <3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>...
>Getting desperate here.
>Straight out of Sam's teach yourself Cobol in 21 days.
>01 TABLE-STATE-RECORD.
     02  table-state-item OCCURS 50 TIMES.
  >   05   TABLE-STATE-CODE       PIC XX.
  >   05   TABLE-STATE-NAME       PIC X(2).
>And my Fujitsu compiler complains:
>OCCURS CLAUSE CANNOT BE SPECIFIED FOR LEVEL 01 OR LEVEL 77 ITEM.
>Help?
```

#### ↳ Re: My homework

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-u5SYRswbdJV3@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3649A774.7E89@letterbox.com>`

```
On Wed, 11 Nov 1998 15:04:20, The Chameleon <sarvec@letterbox.com> 
wrote:

> Getting desperate here.
> Straight out of Sam's teach yourself Cobol in 21 days.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Help?

This is correct - some compilers tolerate it, but the current standard
says it's a no no.  Add an intermediate 03 level in there with the 
occurs clause on it.
```

<a id="t-7cd997f53e"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus Libraries for Unix — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus Libraries for Unix

- **From:** "Izhar Arie" <izhar@tadsys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72c7ec$j64$1@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
Hello all,

I'd like to know where can I find any libraries for MicroFocus
running on Unix.
The version we use is 3.2 for Solaris Sparc 2.6.
I searched the microfocus site but couldn't find anything.

Is there a way for calling sytem calls from COBOL?

Thank for the help,

Izhar
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Libraries for Unix

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3649d91a.10704067@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<72c7ec$j64$1@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
Izhar,

If you use the standard rts, you  add C programs to the Cobol runtime
and call them from COBOL.  The microfocus web site has some good info
on calling C programs, and also how to create your rts.

I use Real World Accounting software, and needed to write some C
programs to do Credit Card authorization.  Real World comes with a
custom pre-built rts, so I could not call the C programs directly from
C.  Instead I did a system call to run my C program.  I passed data
between the two using an ASCII file.  Something like:

	Write data into ASCII file
	Close ascii file
	Call "SYSTEM" USING "myprogram"
	Open ASCII file

Paul


On Wed, 11 Nov 1998 16:42:52 +0200, "Izhar Arie" <izhar@tadsys.com>
wrote:

>Hello all,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

## Thread: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol — 37 msgs

### C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

- **From:** Rauno Kaunismaa <raunok@commhyd.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au>`

```
I hope someone out there would be kind enough to help me with a
difficult problem, for me that is.
I've got a task of converting an existing, very old Cobol system to
Windows platform. The system was compiled with an older version of MF
Cobol.
I have Version 3.2.20(1994). I can't get any help from MF and can't
justify cost of support.
I hope someone's encountered this weird problem before.
It is to do with Signed Integers, i.e. PIC S99999.
They don't display properly nor do they compare using > and < operators.

When you run the program, the comparison interprets everything as
negative.
I've fiddled with Settings in Cobol.dir, such that I have set
CHARSET"ASCII"
and SIGN"ASCII" which are defaults anyway, but no luck.
When I write the signed values to a file and inspect their Hex values
they
don't seem to correspond with the settings, i.e. ASCII.

Thanks for your help

Rauno
```

```
>difficult problem, for me that is.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<910862784.4199.0.nnrp-11.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au>`

```
Is this data you are converting?  This sounds like big endian/little endian.
If I remember correctly,  most machines (in my experience) store long
integers as bytes 1,2,3,4 but on pcs (and others) they store them as
3,4,1,2.  If you are transferring data without converting then long integers
will be mis interpreted.  I.e.  X'00FF' would be interpretted as X'FF00'.

Rick


Rauno Kaunismaa wrote in message <364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au>...
>I hope someone out there would be kind enough to help me with a
>difficult problem, for me that is.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>, 
redsky@ibm.net says...
> On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 08:05:28, Rauno Kaunismaa <raunok@commhyd.com.au> 
> wrote:
> > 
<SNIP> 
> Can you post some of the data values?
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Just a thought,

   From my experience it seems that Cobol is not taken
seriously as a program language, and I know this is not
a revelation to a lot of you. It seems that programmers
new to Cobol try to convert it to a language that they
are more familiar with, and it happens to be C or C++.

   I guess the sixty four thousand dollar question is
what will it take to make Cobol a *popular* language??
And I guess the other question is do *we* want a 
popular language or just a sturdy get the job done type
of language?? Finally, will the changes in the new standard
help Cobol stand up to the perceived superior languages
like C, C++, Perl, etc???

   I for one would not mind seeing some new features
included in Cobol but I still like it for it stability.

Any thoughts???
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364B20CA.CC89078@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net>`

```
I'm going to limit my response to only one suggestion because this topic can
go on and on and on . . .  Aside from stability, one of the strengths of
COBOL is that you have programs 20+ years old still maintainable.  This is
because there is a well defined standard and compelling reasons for compiler
vendors to comply with the standard.  Some major changes in requirements
have occurred over the years, GUI being one of them.  GUI has been around
for awhile so the requirements for a suitable interface is getting pretty
well defined.  If I could code a suitable GUI interface within the
strictures of ANSI COBOL I could probably rest assured this code would be
serviceable for a decade or more and I would get a good return on my
investment.  Somewhere, somehow, it usually comes back to $$$.  That's my 2
cents worth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>,
>redsky@ibm.net says...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><SNIP>
<and SNIP again>

>Just a thought,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jeff,

I don't know, what would make the language more popular?

New features?

Well, I have wanted the ability to put something like

IN RANGE (1-10000)

after an IF and a variable like

IF field1 IN RANGE (1-10000),
or
IF field2 IN RANGE (4032, 165, 243, 395, 1-5, 8-10)
or
IF field3 IN RANGE (A-Z), [correctly handling EBCDIC]
or
IF field4 IN RANGE user-defined-range, [any of the above, or any combination
of the above]
etc.

I haven't seen what VALIDATE will do, so I'm not sure if this is the same.
In any event, this is minor and similar to SEARCH (avoids manually fiddling
around with arrays, indexes, and AT END and WHEN, and the comparison fields;
but lacks the fine control)

Hmm, I think dynamic allocation of memory is coming in the next standard.

Intrinsic functions add a lot of capability.  But how about more of them?
This shop is still stuck on the '85 standard, so I haven't used them before.
Are user-defined functions allowed?  If they aren't, maybe allowing them
would be good.


It is difficult to simply come up with something and point to it and say,
"This!  This will make COBOL more popular."
```

```
combination
>> of the above]

Thane,

Yes.  88-levels are another way to duplicate this.
But I have been teaching and augmenting the knowledge of several new COBOL
users around here, and trying to communicate the 88-level uses is one thing,
but getting some people to start thinking that way is tough.

I believe that an IN RANGE would be more, how do I say it, "understandable"
to people with minimal previous programming experience.  However, I also
don't believe it would add anything _new_ to the language (other than easier
understanding, it just seems more like "plain english" than either 88s or
SEARCH).  I did say it was minor.

(Oh, I'm also hoping that I won't start an understandibility firestorm here
(and I want that anti-gravity belt, too)).  :)
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-cmjjn77zCktg@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <MPG.10b5017c906f7d56989693@news.earthlink.net>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 19:31:01, JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff 
Farkas) wrote:

> What I would really like to see is a day when the question of
> converting Cobol to C would be irrelevant because Cobol would
> have enough features to meet the needs of more programmers. 
> 

Jeff,

I think this harkens back to some previous posts as to WHY this is 
done.  I think it's because GUI programmers have come to us from other
language disciplines.  They convert from COBOL to C (or attempt to) in
an effort to get to something they know and understand.  When pressed,
many admit that the COBOL system was more reliable and capable than 
the new "C" or "VB" system.  

If these were COBOL programmers, they would find a way to achieve the 
GUI without wholesale conversion from COBOL.  The entire fiasco can be
chalked up to the shortage of COBOL programmers!

-------------------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** petervz@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```

>
> IF field1 IN RANGE (1-10000),
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc.


How about writing your own subroutine, and call it thus:

CALL "INRANGE" USING BY REFERENCE INRANGE-SWITCH
                     BY VALUE <condition>
                     BY VALUE <parameters>
IF INRANGE-TRUE
       .....
       .....
END-IF
.





-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d9vH2UDvTO7C@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 13 Nov 1998 07:27:29, petervz@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> 
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF

It's really not necessary.  Here are two ways to check to see if a 
character is within the specified range.

01  Test-Char Pic X.
       88  In-Range Value "A" thru "Z".

Move Char-To-Test to Test-Char.
If In-Range
  ......

The next way:

Evaluate Char-To-Test
   When "A" thru "Z"
      ......
   When Other
      .....
End-Evaluate

Or how about a moving range?

01  Low-End Pic X Value "A".
01  High-End Pic X Value "Z".

...

Evaluate Char-To-Test
    When Low-End Thru High-End
      .....
    When Other
      .....
End-Evaluate

If you want to test for J-K instead must move "J" to low-end and "K" 
to High-end.

----------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d9vH2UDvTO7C@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> It's really not necessary.  Here are two ways to check to see if a
> character is within the specified range.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> to High-end.

All of these techniques give results which are dependent on the
character set.  In ASCII, A-Z is nothing but upper case letters.  In
EBCDIC, such a range includes miscellaneous bits of trash.  Other
character sets may, in principle, be even more perverse -- what if
the alphabet is encoded in reverse order, or otherwise scrambled?

Offhand the only simple (but tedious) way to code such a range test
portably is to enumerate all the eligible characters, whether in an
88-level, an EVALUATE, or whatever.

I think the original wish was for some mechanism which was both
succinct and portable.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72in0g$1d1@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d9vH2UDvTO7C@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net>`

```

Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> It's really not necessary.  Here are two ways to check to see if a
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html

Michael,
  I am confused by your comment.  I went back and looked at the original
post (from Chris Osborne and dated Thursday, November 12, 1998 10:32 AM) and
it seems to assume that the range is the compiler's current collating
sequence (or at least that is how I read it).  Furthermore,  If you WANTED
to use another collating sequence it is easy enough (well sort-of) to create
a user-defined sequence (or use ASCII if you are running under EBCDIC - or
in many cases to use EBCDIC if you are running under ASCII - if your
compiler supports this as predefined).  It is true that the Standard doesn't
let you "dynamically change" collating sequences, but you can call a
sub-program to do your test with whatever collating sequence you want.  My
assumption, however, is that if you are trying to do this range test with a
specific collating sequence then you PROBABLY want the rest of your logic to
use it too.  (Not always true - but true most of the time)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D0E0A.4F45@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d9vH2UDvTO7C@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net> <72in0g$1d1@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>        88  In-Range Value "A" thru "Z".

[snip ]

> >All of these techniques give results which are dependent on the
> >character set.  In ASCII, A-Z is nothing but upper case letters.  In
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> use it too.  (Not always true - but true most of the time)

I keep forgetting about user-defined collating sequences, since I
have never needed to use one.

Chris Osborne was proposing some syntax for range tests, but it isn't
clear exactly what he meant by "correctly handling EBCDIC".  Is his
proposed:

	IF field3 IN RANGE (A-Z)

...supposed to restrict the test to alphabetic characters?  Is it
significant (or just an oversight) that he does not enclose the letters 
in quotes?  Could we code:

	IF field3 IN RANGE (A-9)

...or should that be:

	IF field3 IN RANGE ("A"-"9")

Such a test would be plausible for EBCDIC but dubious for ASCII.

If someone uses something like the proposed syntax to test for "A-Z",
the casual reader is likely to assume that the code is looking for
upper case capital letters.  In the absence of user-defined collating
sequences, that assumption will not be portably and reliably true 
unless the syntax is defined with considerable subtlety.  The 
interaction of this subtlety with user-defined collating sequences, 
to me at least, would only multiply the confusion.

In the absence of user-defined collating sequences, Thane's suggested
tests will work differently for EBCDIC vs ASCII, will they not?   To me,
his 88-level means: any character c such that "A" <= c <= "Z".  In
the case of EBCDIC, that range includes some non-alphabetic characters.
If not, please educate me.

I'm not referring to exotica such as an MVS program processing an ASCII 
file -- just ordinary stuff.  The EBCDIC version is not likely to be a 
very useful range unless you supplement it with a test for ALPHABETIC.

I've never liked using THRU clauses in 88-levels.  If I were
ever to use one it would probably be on a numeric variable where
the meaning of the range would be clearly defined, independent of
the character set.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364dafeb.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```

Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <364D0E0A.4F45@swbell.net>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
>> Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <364CC9B5.1215@swbell.net>...
<snip>
>Chris Osborne was proposing some syntax for range tests, but it isn't
>clear exactly what he meant by "correctly handling EBCDIC".  Is his
>proposed:
><snip>

Just a stray thought, EBCDIC breaks the letters of the alphabet into three
different sets (c1 to c9, d1 to d9, and e2 to e9; instead of the ascii 41 to
5a).  Of course the person who wrote the compiler to handle this collating
sequence would handle that fact.  I have a habit of stating the obvious.  I
try to filter these sort of things out, but sometimes they slip through.


If I could think of at least one thing that would make COBOL on MVS more
pleasent, I would have the compiler listings from the IBM '85 compiler print
diagnostic messages in only 80 columns, instead of 132.  For some parts of
the listing, long lines are important.  But for the messages at the end,
there is no reason to string them out beyond column 80, where they pass the
edge of an 80 column display.
I haven't used the later IBM mainframe compilers, can anyone tell me if they
still do the same thing?
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364E49B6.6A08FBD7@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <72gn11$gs7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-d9vH2UDvTO7C@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> It's really not necessary.  Here are two ways to check to see if a
> character is within the specified range.
>
>  <snip - examples using condition name and evaluate>

There is a third way to check if a character is in a valid range (or if
all characters in a field come from a class of valid characters):

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
SPECIAL-NAMES.
    CLASS VALID-HEX-DIGIT IS '0123456789ABCDEF'.
    CLASS NON-BLANK IS '0' THRU '9',
                       'a' THRU 'z',
                       'A' THRU 'Z',
                       '!@#$%&*()-+='.

IF FIELD-A IS VALID-HEX-DIGIT...
IF FIELD-A IS NON-BLANK...

For IBM COBOL compilers, this requires COBOL II or higher.

And, by the way thane, nice web page!
```

```
For the INRANGE statement you could probably use something like this -
provided your compiler supports it:

01   INRANGE-FLAG		PIC 9(5).
     88  INRANGE-VALUES		VALUES 1 THRU 10000.
01   INRANGE-FLAG-2		PIC 9(04).
     88 INRANGE-VALUES-2	VALUES 4032, 165, 243, 395, 1 THROUGH
5, 				       8 THROUGH 10.
     IF INRANGE-VALUES .... ETC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ What "characters" are "alphanumeric" (was C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72kmbg$m6o@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <364DD6D6.51A9CA8E@home.com>`

```
Given the recent discussions about the EBCDIC alphabetic characters not
comprising a discrete (?) series of hex values, I thought that I would let
the newsgroup know about an interesting discussion going on (not yet
resolved as far as I know) at J4 and WG4.

The question is whether or not the next Standard should require, allow, or
be indifferent to the question of whether the alphanumeric character set (or
as it is now known "repertoire") is a complete set of ALL the hex values
that fit into a specific "size" of data (usually a byte - but not required
to be).

In other words, can an implementor say that X'00' thru  X'10' and X'12' thru
X'FF' comprise the alphanumeric character set - but that X'11' is NOT a
valid alphanumeric character in their implementation?  Obviously (as
discussed in previous threads), the current "official" (little used) ASCII
character set is from X'00' thru X'7F' - but does *not* include X'80' thru
X'FF' - and J4 isn't about to disallow that.  On the other hand, no known
(or at least known to me) current character set has "holes" in the middle.

One reason that this becomes important is that the next Standard (like many
implementations) is supporting hex literals.  However, the question is
whether X'02'  represents a specific bit-pattern (as most of us would
expect) or whether it really represents the 3rd (X'00' is 1st) valid
alphanumeric character.  The problem with using the former interpretation is
that the Standard has never before talked about bytes or bits; the next
Standard is going to include "bytes" (without specifying how many bits there
are in a byte) - but so far (I think) has avoided any mention of bits.

Some of you wonder WHY it takes so long to get a "correct" Standard.  It is
this type of problem that takes so long to resolve in a truly portable way.
It is easy for any specific vendor to define hex literals - because they
know the byte/bit definitions of their operating systems - but when the
Standard tries to create a "generic" definition, this type of thing is
remarkably hard to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What "characters" are "alphanumeric" (was C programmer Needs Help with Cobol (Just a thought).......

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lick$rgo$1@newsreader2.core.theplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <MPG.10b4d2e18f90fe90989691@news.earthlink.net> <364b2953.0@news3.uswest.net> <364DD6D6.51A9CA8E@home.com> <72kmbg$m6o@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <72kmbg$m6o@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>, William M. Klein says...

>The question is whether or not the next Standard should require, allow, or
>be indifferent to the question of whether the alphanumeric character set (or
>as it is now known "repertoire") is a complete set of ALL the hex values
>that fit into a specific "size" of data (usually a byte - but not required
>to be).

Wouldn't this cause a problem with DBCS? Particularly EBCDIC with Shift-Out/In 
characters amd valid 'alphanumeric' characters occupying multiple bytes or 
indeed half a character being treated as alphanumeric. Sounds like a nasty 
kettle of fish I don't want to think about.

I seem to recall being able to test IS ALPHANUMERIC on PIC G's with DBCS A-z in 
there. Could be recalling wrongly though.

Also it would classify hankaku katakana (single byte Japanese) as alphanumeric 
where is currently isn't AFAIK. I think it'll break programs if the definition 
of 'alphanumeric' is changed.


>
>In other words, can an implementor say that X'00' thru  X'10' and X'12' thru
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>character set is from X'00' thru X'7F' 

Hmm, I've always thought of ASCII as x20 to 7F myself as the lower 31 
characters change from codeset to codeset and even between the same codeset on 
different manufacturers PC's. Eg. IBM codepage 932 sticks draw characters in 
that range whereas 437 has card symbols and smiley faces.


[snippage]
>
>Some of you wonder WHY it takes so long to get a "correct" Standard.  It is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>remarkably hard to do.

I'd say leave it as it is. Support Unicode.
```

```
existing COBOL implementations (both those with and without DBCS support).
```
