```
On Thu, 19 Nov 1998 16:56:42 -0800, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>My guess is that this is going to cause a complete NIGHTMARE for some of the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the new Standard in its "fully conforming mode".

Having worked in Japanese development at MicroFocus, implemented the
DBCS EBCDIC support and worked on various OEM's compilers I can think
of plenty of people in Japan that would like it working in "fully
conforming mode". Japan being the place where the term "zero defects"
comes from. 

I do hope MicroFocus at least are aware of the implications, National
Language support often gets put off as it's not considered a core
requirement. I hope someones tracking this.

Delphi 4 for instance only just got Bi-Directional support.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kcF22.1277$Lj2.1197830@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 08:05:28, Rauno Kaunismaa <raunok@commhyd.com.au>
>wrote:
>
<<SNIP>>

>I'm about to go on a Tirade - this is not aimed at you:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

because...

* the Windows API is documented for C programmers, meaning you have to have
to be able to translate from C to COBOL in order to use it.

* all the self-help books, courses, etc. that are readily accessible are for
visual basic, java, and C++.

* schools are churning out programmers who learned the Windows API using
java, C++, VB and their corresponding development tools. It costs time and
money to retrain them to use COBOL development tools.

* most COBOL support for Windows is provided by vendors who insist on
locking you into their proprietary "simplified" interface, rather than
giving you direct access to the objects provided by Microsoft.

* it is cheap and easy to buy VB and JBuilder and bang out a program with
little or no prior training. Try doing that with products provided by Micro
Focus, Fujitsu, Flexus, etc.

* investing any amount of time or money building a GUI program is a waste of
resources. The target keeps moving. 4 years ago it was Presentation Manager
and Win3.x, then it was Win32, then browser-based clients with Java and/or
Javascript. The pragmatic approach is to decouple the presentation layer
from the business logic and data access. Develop the presentation layer in
whatever gets the job done, and use COBOL for the rest.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-JybVGDE66PdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <kcF22.1277$Lj2.1197830@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 17:49:04, "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com> wrote:

\> * the Windows API is documented for C programmers, meaning you have
to have
> to be able to translate from C to COBOL in order to use it.
> 

Ok.  Granted. 

> * all the self-help books, courses, etc. that are readily accessible are for
> visual basic, java, and C++.
> 

Not anymore.

> * schools are churning out programmers who learned the Windows API using
> java, C++, VB and their corresponding development tools. It costs time and
> money to retrain them to use COBOL development tools.
> 

Also rapidly changing - but granted.

> * most COBOL support for Windows is provided by vendors who insist on
> locking you into their proprietary "simplified" interface, rather than
> giving you direct access to the objects provided by Microsoft.
> 

This one is true and not true.  There are 3rd party tools that let you
have the GUI, accomplish your goals of isolating it from the UI, and 
support virtually all compilers  - http://www.flexus.com for example. 
There are others also.

> * it is cheap and easy to buy VB and JBuilder and bang out a program with
> little or no prior training. Try doing that with products provided by Micro
> Focus, Fujitsu, Flexus, etc.
> 

It's doable with the Flexus tools.  Easy.  (Easier than VB!)

> * investing any amount of time or money building a GUI program is a waste of
> resources. The target keeps moving. 4 years ago it was Presentation Manager
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes the target is moving.  Your point is well made, and actually kinda
sorta supports my position.  I see query after query about converting 
the DATA so these other tools can access it directly - that's not 
necessary.  Keep the data access in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364B39FE.C40DF900@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-F0Lq13kBtUuL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <kcF22.1277$Lj2.1197830@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-JybVGDE66PdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```


> This one is true and not true.  There are 3rd party tools

Part of the value of a programming language is the stability of it's syntax.
There is great value in knowing that what you code today will compile tomorrow and
the year after regardless of the operating system.  When you have to turn to 3rd
party tools to accomplish a goal you loose this value.  If you were able to do a
decent GUI using the screen section and/or accepts & displays, and these were all
ANSI standard, then COBOL would increase in value.

I broadly categorize languages into what I call "standard" and "non-standard"
languages.  Fortran, C, C++, and COBOL are examples of standard languages.  VB,
the xBASE languages would be non-standard.  When you use the non-standard
language, *OR* a 3rd party tool,  you are at the mercy of the compiler vendor who
has an economic interest in continuing sales.  Simply put, you can expect your
code to become obsolete as the vendor encourages you to upgrade.  They more or
less have to do this to stay in business, there's nothing evil about that.
However, as a developer I am primarily interested in getting the greatest return
on my investment and if I can use one of the so called "standard" languages I'm
more assured of my code not becoming obsolete in the next few years.
```

#### ↳ Re: C programmer Needs Help with Cobol

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364ca4ca.2658205@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364A96C8.4F24E7B9@commhyd.com.au>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 19:05:28 +1100, Rauno Kaunismaa
<raunok@commhyd.com.au> wrote:

>I've got a task of converting an existing, very old Cobol system to
>Windows platform. The system was compiled with an older version of MF
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>They don't display properly nor do they compare using > and < operators.

>When you run the program, the comparison interprets everything as
>negative.


>I've fiddled with Settings in Cobol.dir, such that I have set
>CHARSET"ASCII"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't seem to correspond with the settings, i.e. ASCII.

Hmm. Well, without looking at the code, I am not sure what to say.
The first thing is, a pic variable on one mainframes compiler may be
different than a pic variable on a PC.

Thus,

01 somevar pic s99.

might have different byte orders. The next problem is it may be stored
in packed decimal format on the mainframe, and display or binary
format on the PC.

I'd start by looking through all the code, and replacing unknown
variable types with the default mainframe-compiler type.

thus,

somevar pic s999.

would become

somevar pic s999 usage packed decimal.

or

somevar pic s999 usage comp-6.

or comp-5 or comp-4 or whatever.

I don't really know what comp-number refers to what machine, but after
a few tries, you should find the default data-storage type.


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

---

<a id="t-1e73c729b8"></a>
## Thread: VB5 Strange Behaviour When Calling Cobol DLL — 10 msgs

### VB5 Strange Behaviour When Calling Cobol DLL

- **From:** "Paul Galbraith" <paul_galbraith@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vb.general.discussion
- **Message-ID:** `<#raUxAgD#GA.339@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
I have a DLL that I've created using Micro Focus Cobol (v4).  I'm calling a
routine in the DLL that is expecting a large string (> 1k) to be passed by
reference.  When I call this routine from a Visual Basic 5 program, I get a
lot of strange behaviour.  Often the entire VB IDE just shuts down without a
word of warning.  Other times (varied by changing the size of the string
parameter that is being passed) I get a VB warning saying that I'm passing
the wrong number of parameters, or using the wrong calling convention to
reach the DLL.  I am as certain as I can be that the data area being passed
from VB, and the data area expected by the Cobol DLL, are identical.

What I'm not certain of is that I'm using the correct call convention.  Does
anyone know the specifics of VB5's calling convention?  Does it push
parameters onto the stack left-to-right, or right-to-left (although with
only 1 parameter in my case, this shouldn't matter)?  Does it expect the
called routine to clean up the stack?

It gets stranger, though:  if I reduce the size of the string enough, it
works!!  I can't figure out why.  Using several different Cobol DLL's that
I've created, each of them has a different, but very specific (e.g. 683
bytes in one case), size of string that it will accept.  If the string is
even 1 byte longer, the whole thing bombs.

And it gets worse:  In VB, I have some code like this:

   Declare Sub CobolDLL Lib "MyCobol.DLL" Alias "MyCobol"

   Dim MyParmString as MyParmStringType

   MyParmString = "Stuff"
   call CobolDLL_X(MyParmString)

   Sub CobolDLL_X(ParmString as MyParmStringType)
      call CobolDLL(ParmString)
   End Sub

This above code causes the behaviour I've mentioned above.  However, if I
call CobolDLL directory, instead of indirectly going through the routine
CobolDLL_X, I have no problems!  Does anyone know what's up with that?

Any ideas would be greatly appreciated!  Thanks,

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: VB5 Strange Behaviour When Calling Cobol DLL

- **From:** "grayhawk" <na>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vb.general.discussion
- **Message-ID:** `<364b7958.0@diana.idirect.com>`
- **References:** `<#raUxAgD#GA.339@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
vb passes parameters using the pascal calling convention.

assuming : Declare Sub CobolDLL Lib "MyCobol.DLL" Alias "MyCobol"

you can't use: call CobolDLL(ParmString)

assuming :  Type MyParmStringType
                        Parm1 as string * 1
                        Parm2 as string * 80
                        Parm3 as string * 5
                     End Type

you can't use : MyParmString = "Stuff"

er.. which version of vb are you using ?

>
>   Declare Sub CobolDLL Lib "MyCobol.DLL" Alias "MyCobol"
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Without thinking about this more than a few seconds ...... be aware that
internally VB5 aligns structure members on 4-byte boundaries, so variables
of the above type actually take 92 bytes in memory. Is your Cobol compiler
set to pack the same way?
```

```
I'm quite certain that the Cobol compiler is not aligning on DW

maybe it unaligns the data in a separate workspace before pushing the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VB5 Strange Behaviour When Calling Cobol DLL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Tony Lavender" <promedia@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vb.general.discussion
- **Message-ID:** `<Oh57LTLE#GA.306@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<#raUxAgD#GA.339@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com> <364AD584.1E2D@netbox.com> <eBUVg7jD#GA.378@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <ul5SYlpD#GA.339@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com> <O#UHNfxD#GA.293@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
<<...internally VB5 aligns structure members on 4-byte boundaries...>>

>!! This is useful information !!  Are you sure about this?  I'm using VB5.
>I'm quite certain that the Cobol compiler is not aligning on DW
>boundaries....Also, I know that VB does some internal conversion
(especially
>to convert Unicode to ASCII) before calling any subroutine/function, so
>maybe it unaligns the data in a separate workspace before pushing the
>address on the stack?
>
Yes, I'm sure about it. I think you're probably correct, though, that VB
unaligns the data before making a DLL call. So, it may not be an issue for
you.

I ran into the alignment problem in a convoluted kinda way - I had found
that VB5 will not do an "OPEN xxx FOR BINARY ACCESS READ" on a file that's
on a network device which is shared read-only (as opposed to full), so I
wrote a small C routine in a DLL that opens a file and gets binary data. I
was "getting" it into a VB user-defined-type containing a mix of ints,
longs, and fixed strings....

Unlike a regular VB "Get", which transparently unpacks the binary data and
puts it into the correct variables in a structure, my function ("MyFileGet")
puts the raw data directly into memory beginning at the start of the
structure. Because of the internal 4-byte alignment of the structure
members, some of the data ends up in the wrong place.

I did some research and found out that the 4-byte alignment is documented.
There's no compiler option to change it.
```

#### ↳ Re: VB5 Strange Behaviour When Calling Cobol DLL - vbdem.zip (0/1)

- **From:** grwallace@taynet.co.uk ()
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72rril$7no$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<#raUxAgD#GA.339@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
"Paul Galbraith" <paul_galbraith@csi.com> wrote:

>I have a DLL that I've created using Micro Focus Cobol (v4).  I'm calling a
>routine in the DLL that is expecting a large string (> 1k) to be passed by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>from VB, and the data area expected by the Cobol DLL, are identical.

>What I'm not certain of is that I'm using the correct call convention.  Does
>anyone know the specifics of VB5's calling convention?  Does it push
>parameters onto the stack left-to-right, or right-to-left (although with
>only 1 parameter in my case, this shouldn't matter)?  Does it expect the
>called routine to clean up the stack?

>It gets stranger, though:  if I reduce the size of the string enough, it
>works!!  I can't figure out why.  Using several different Cobol DLL's that
>I've created, each of them has a different, but very specific (e.g. 683
>bytes in one case), size of string that it will accept.  If the string is
>even 1 byte longer, the whole thing bombs.

>And it gets worse:  In VB, I have some code like this:

>   Declare Sub CobolDLL Lib "MyCobol.DLL" Alias "MyCobol"

>   Dim MyParmString as MyParmStringType

>   MyParmString = "Stuff"
>   call CobolDLL_X(MyParmString)

>   Sub CobolDLL_X(ParmString as MyParmStringType)
>      call CobolDLL(ParmString)
>   End Sub

>This above code causes the behaviour I've mentioned above.  However, if I
>call CobolDLL directory, instead of indirectly going through the routine
>CobolDLL_X, I have no problems!  Does anyone know what's up with that?

>Any ideas would be greatly appreciated!  Thanks,

>Paul


I have been working with VB calling COBOL for some time now, and the
first and most important thing I have found is that you must ensure
that any parameters passed have a length of a multiple of 4.

This even goes as far as passing numeric data. If you want it to work,
and work reliably, then convert all numbers to usage display items
with a multiple of four bytes, and do the same with the UDT in your VB
program. Ensure that your linkge section and the UDT match exactly,
and you will be OK.

We also have a number of utilities which will convert a COBOL FD
copybook into VB UDT's with all the support code which goes along with
them.

The attatched .ZIP may give you some ideas

Best regards,


Gordon Wallace.
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ...!!! Please.........

- **From:** "Mark Undrill" <marku@affirm.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<910870342.22176.0.nnrp-07.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

sat wrote in message <2Dp22.6844$bt4.4643592@news.teleport.com>...
>How is 0 stored in this PIC Clause 9(8)V99S


This is not valid COBOL.
>
>will it store it as     0,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In PIC S9(8)V99 zero is stored as 000000000{ on most platforms that I've
ever worked on.

In PIC S9(8)V99 SIGN TRAILING SEPARATE zero is stored as 0000000000+.

HTH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ...!!! Please.........

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uRCKuQoD#GA.240@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<2Dp22.6844$bt4.4643592@news.teleport.com> <910870342.22176.0.nnrp-07.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

Mark Undrill wrote in message
<910870342.22176.0.nnrp-07.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk>...
>
>sat wrote in message <2Dp22.6844$bt4.4643592@news.teleport.com>...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>


The answer to this question may depend on the compiler implementation.

A direct move of ZEROS to a signed PIC could result in either an _unsigned_
zero value or a _positive_ zero value (presumably so that a test of the
resultant field for NEGATIVE would not yield a TRUE value.)

I've experienced COBOL compilers where adding a positive quantity to a
negative quantity with an identical absolute value would result in a
_negative_ signed zero amount (where a test for NEGATIVE would yield a TRUE
value even though the absolute value was zero.)

There can also be difficulties encountered in moving ZEROS to a group item
which contains subordinate signed numeric PICs - I have experienced this in
Tandem COBOL compilers.

Dennis J. Minette
CCCO
```

```
these anomolies on Honeywell 2000, Honeywell 6000 or Data General CS series
computers.  I recall making those findings in the course of debugging
conditional statements in various COBOL programs that were coming up with
unexpected results on signed data items having an absolute value of zero.

>nor negative.


The COBOL world is definitely not all Blue<g>.

>Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ...!!! Please.........

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36508896.BA5740E4@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<2Dp22.6844$bt4.4643592@news.teleport.com> <910870342.22176.0.nnrp-07.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk> <uRCKuQoD#GA.240@upnetnews05> <364BAC5D.BD7F4E06@att.net> <OeiDkC0D#GA.142@upnetnews05>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote:

> William Lynch wrote in message <364BAC5D.BD7F4E06@att.net>...

8<

> >In the IBM mainframe world from the S/360s on zero is neither positive
> >nor negative.
>
> The COBOL world is definitely not all Blue<g>.

The COBOL standard defines the sign condition as

                         {POSITIVE}
   expression-1 IS [NOT] {ZERO    }
                         {NEGATIVE}

which should give it's result depending on the numeral value, either > 0, = 0
or < 0, independant from the stored sign-bit-pattern when value = 0. So this
condition tests for *absolute* place on the "number line" (below, on or above
the origin).

The standard defines part of the relation condition as

   expression-1 IS [NOT] {EQUAL} expression-2 (expr-2 may be ZERO)
                         {=    }

which should give result of comparing TWO values. So this condition test for
*relative* place on the "number line".

Rï¿½sumï¿½:
IF VAR = ZERO
IF VAR IS = ZERO
IF VAR IS EQUAL TO ZERO
are all relational tests
IF VAR ZERO
IF VAR IS ZERO
are both sign tests

All five (should) return the same result.

I wonder if: When someone sees IF VAR IS ZERO i.s.o. IF VAR = ZERO wheter that
person realises these are syntactically and semantically different types of
conditions.

COBOL.FROG
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ...!!! Please.........

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uCzN39aE#GA.258@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`

```

COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote in message <36508896.BA5740E4@IMN.nl>...
>Dennis J. Minette wrote:

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>which should give it's result depending on the numeral value, either > 0, =
0

this
>condition tests for *absolute* place on the "number line" (below, on or
above
>the origin).
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>which should give result of comparing TWO values. So this condition test
for
>*relative* place on the "number line".
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I wonder if: When someone sees IF VAR IS ZERO i.s.o. IF VAR = ZERO wheter
that
>person realises these are syntactically and semantically different types of
>conditions.


I have never used the IF VAR [IS] ZERO form of conditional expression, and
actually was not even aware of it (I've only been programming in COBOL since
1967.)  Most of the grief I have experienced has been with NOT NEGATIVE
tests on zero values carrying a negative sign.  These situations were
apparently caused by arithmetically arriving at a zero value of VAR from the
negative side of VAR (e.g., ADD 11 to VAR, where VAR has an initial value
of -11.)

>COBOL.FROG
>--
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Hello, how to convert this ? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** Andrew <mandrake007@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
Hello,

Can anybody tell me Equivalents of Mainframe Dataset size units of
Blocks, Tracks and Cylinders in Bytes ?

Thanks,
Andrew.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-e950Die9zsqo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 13:45:03, Andrew <mandrake007@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Hello,
> 
> Can anybody tell me Equivalents of Mainframe Dataset size units of
> Blocks, Tracks and Cylinders in Bytes ?
> 

It's all device dependant.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364BAD81.903D5F37@att.net>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-e950Die9zsqo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 13:45:03, Andrew <mandrake007@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's all device dependant.

As Thane said, it's all device dependant; but you can forget about 512
bytes per sector on the mainframe. The physical entity is the block,
which contains one or more logical records. IIRC, the 3380's & 3390's
have 15 tracks per cylinder. The no. of bytes used per track depends on
the block size (i.e., there's a fixed interrecord gap). BTW, MVS
supports allocating datasets in MB nowadays, too.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** "John Mycroft" <johnm@mycroft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<910922424.783919@news.iconz.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
Very rough rule of thumb (like give or take 200%!!!)

1 block = 512 bytes (if you're referring to a hardware block - a block of
records could be anywhere from 1 byte to 64K)
1 track = 40K
1 cylinder = 20 tracks

Like I say, very rough, totally device dependent and these numbers don't
represent any known device!
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364ceeda.1013327@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 14:45:03 +0100, Andrew <mandrake007@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hello,
>
>Can anybody tell me Equivalents of Mainframe Dataset size units of
>Blocks, Tracks and Cylinders in Bytes ?


No, not reallly...

the size of a track is depending on the physical architecture of the disk drive, i.e.
track size is different on 3330, 3350, 3370, 3380, 3390 and all the other disk drives

A Cylinder is the set off all tracks that have the same distance from the spindle.  In the
338003390 disk drives 15 tracks make one cylinder.

The blocksize, then, is depending on the record format:

Recfm=F	Blocksize = LRECL (which is logical record length)
RECFM=FB	Blocksize is some multiple of LRECL
RECFM=VB	Blocksize is 4+multiple of (4+LRECL)

HTH

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3651aac4.4188305@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 14:45:03 +0100, Andrew <mandrake007@hotmail.com>
wrote:

>Can anybody tell me Equivalents of Mainframe Dataset size units of
>Blocks, Tracks and Cylinders in Bytes ?

No. There are no equiviliants in Byte size, without knowing the hard
drive specs, OS, ect.

A better method to determine an datasets size, though, is
finding the total number of records, and multiply it by the maximum
record size.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, how to convert this ?

- **From:** "Ryan Roberts" <ryanr@fgi.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lghm$kfo$1@supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<364AE65F.2D126E17@hotmail.com>`

```
Hi Andrew, in my shop I use the following estimated sizes:
one page of output (file or report) = 8,712 bytes (characters per line *
lines per page.  In my case, 132 cpl * 66 lpp)
one track = 121,968 bytes (roughly 14 pages per track)
one cylinder = 1,829,520 bytes (roughly 15 tracks per cylinder)

As for block sizes, they are usually determined by record lengths and are,
therefore infinitely variable.  I hope this helps.

-Ryan-
__________________
ryanr@fgi.net
__________________

```

<a id="t-893839d69a"></a>
## Thread: HELP!!! with Multi Column Display — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP!!! with Multi Column Display

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364b6cc7.2974458@news.abraxis.com>`

```
For debugging (and just to watch some output on screen) I put display statements
at two locations in one program:  whenever two different performs are carried
out.  The second group (of displays) executes sometime after the first group is
done.  Without any attempt to format the output on screen they're written in the
same very tall pile starting at column 1 line 1.  

I tried to format the output so that the first display statement was set
explicitly to start at line 1 column 1 and the second to start at line 1 column
40.  The screen variables are set by default to 80 x 24 (in the Fujitsu 3.0, and
unknown on Aix--MF v.3??).  Both compilers choked and didn't finish compiling.
I tried DISPLAY AT and variations.  I think the Fujitsu documentation didn't
really specify syntax to allow this or I couldn't figure it out and there is
some documentation in the class's textbook ('From Micro to Mainframes' which is
essentially dedicated to MicroFocus) but nothing exactly about this sort of
trick.  

Is it possible to do this?  (example follows)
'Preciate your opinions!  TS


output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later 
output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP!!! with Multi Column Display

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364B8105.473B@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<364b6cc7.2974458@news.abraxis.com>`

```
OldUncleMe wrote:
> 
> For debugging (and just to watch some output on screen) I put display statements
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> output stuff 1 here first                output sfuff 2 here later

I don't believe you can do this.  In IBM mainframe COBOL, each issuance
of a DISPLAY advances a line, and the only way to get 2 (or more) on a
line is to
DISPLAY VARIABLE-1 '         ' VARIABLE-2,  and I don't think this is
what you are looking for.  DISPLAY was not intended to produce pretty
pages.

An alternative might be to store the items to be displayed in a table,
and then output the table at the end-of-job. But this is a lot of work,
just for debugging output.

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP!!! with Multi Column Display

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GDOcd4QOzV3R@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<364b6cc7.2974458@news.abraxis.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Nov 1998 23:19:07, NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe) 
wrote:

> For debugging (and just to watch some output on screen) I put display statements
> at two locations in one program:  whenever two different performs are carried
> out.  The second group (of displays) executes sometime after the first group is
> done.  Without any attempt to format the output on screen they're written in the
> same very tall pile starting at column 1 line 1.
```

<a id="t-55b45f5be9"></a>
## Thread: How Many COBOL Programs Does It Take To Change A Light Bulb? — 1 this month / 21 total

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-55b45f5be9)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Many COBOL Programs Does It Take To Change A Light Bulb?

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ecok$smh$1@garnet.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<360d4947.0@news3.uswest.net> <19980927144444.28784.00003041@ng95.aol.com>`

```
The following is specific to the IBM COBOL 85 compilers on the MVS platform     
(VS COBOL II, COBOL 370, COBOL for MVS and VM and COBOL for OS390 and VM).   
   
1.  The maximum length of a record on a variable length record file must     
agree with the length of the record as described in the Data Set Control     
Block (or tape label).  Thus a record described to COBOL as having a maximum     
length of 2000 bytes should have a record length (LRECL) in the DSCB of 2004.      
There is a way around this limitation because JCL provided LRECL overrides     
file (DSCB/ tape label) LRECL so provided the maximum length of the records     
on the file is not greater that 2004, coding LRECL=2004 will get around the     
problem for non-VSAM files.  Unfortunately there is no similar bypass for     
VSAM.   
   
2.  IBM claims it is enforcing the COBOL standard.  My opinion of that     
requirement and the inability to override it is anger and frustration.  I     
would like and have had use for the ability to point an "FD VARYING-FILE     
RECORD VARYING FROM 0 to 32760 DEPENDING ON VARYING-FILE-RECORD-SIZE." to any     
file in the shop, both VSAM and non-VSAM with fixed, variable or undefined     
records and have the program read it.  This matches one of the functions     
requested in the SHARE/GUIDE Language Futures Task Force White Paper which     
dates back to the early 1980's.  Indeed if we had an easy way to describe     
bridging mechanisms in JCL (inadequate equivalent of Burroughs WFL, AS400     
OCL, etc.) so that programs could process files which differed from what the     
program believed was truth, it would have eased fixing the year 2000 mess.     
This was also defined as highly useful in the white paper.     
>    
>    
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>Using COBOL For MVS (NOCMPR2) without assembler subroutines, it looks     
like   
> >if   
> >>one wants to copy variable length files with LRECLs ranging from 1 to     
32768   
> >>bytes, one would need 32768 separate programs to avoid File Status 39     
open   
> >>errors - even using ...DEPENDING ON clauses - since COBOL seems to demand   
> >that   
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>Someone (knowledgeable, not guessing) please tell me what I'm doing     
wrong,   
> >or   
> >>it's time to round up the lynch mob for the Standards folks!   
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Other stuff is going on, but for the purposes of this issue, just assume     
you   
> have to read records from variable length files (different LRECLs) using     
ONE   
> program compiled with COBOL For MVS.    
>    
> The program is to be used with all the variable length files within a     
system,   
> and therein lies the rub. For the COBOL For MVS program to work, it looks     
like   
> the maximum LRECL in the program must agree with the physical files, or a   
> failure occurs at run-time.   
>    
> An earlier post indicated this was the case. I hope someone can prove   
> otherwise, but I'd tend to agree based upon my coworker's efforts and the     
COBOL   
> for MVS documentation.    
>    
>    
> 

Clark F. Morris, Jr.   
CFM Technical Programming Services   
Bridgetown, Nova Scotia, Canada   
cmorris@fox.nstn.ca   
on assignment at morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

<a id="t-a44ab4438d"></a>
## Thread: Which compiler supports -INC — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Which compiler supports -INC

- **From:** cstump@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72i9pa$t5s$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Anyone know which compiler for MVS Cobol supports the -INC directive for
copies/includes? It appears to be legal even in columns 1-7. I have seen this
in some non-IBM compilers before (Honeywell and DG I think).

Thanks for the help,

Chuck Stump

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ila8$anm@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72i9pa$t5s$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
No IBM mainframe compiler (that I know of) supports -INC directly.  It is
supported by Librarian (unless I have it confused with Panvalet - one
uses -INC and one uses ++INCLUDE - but they both work basically the same.)

In most cases, these statements are handled by a "preprocessor" step.
However, CA (which owns them both) also supports a mode where members can
actually be handled as if they were COPYLIB members (via the COPY
statement).

Finally, with the newer compilers (I think anything after VS COBOL II,
Release 3.0) it is also possible to code (or have code) which is handled via
an EXIT compiler-option.  This to could "intercept" (and handle) -INC or
++INCLUDE statements.

A few other "tidbits" of related information:

A) Micro Focus provides compiler support for -INC and ++INCLUDE statements
directly (as an emulation of the mainframe products). Other PC vendors (CA?
IBM? Fujitsu?) may also support this syntax - I don't know.

B) All IBM mainframe compilers (at least OS/VS COBOL and above) support
"INSERT" "DELETE" and "REPLACE" statements. (This replace is NOT the new
ANSI'85 standard replace - which can cause confusion).  Although I know of
this feature, I personally have only run into one shop that ever used it.


cstump@my-dejanews.com wrote in message <72i9pa$t5s$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Anyone know which compiler for MVS Cobol supports the -INC directive for
>copies/includes? It appears to be legal even in columns 1-7. I have seen
this
>in some non-IBM compilers before (Honeywell and DG I think).
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D0D76.9C17615F@att.net>`
- **References:** `<72i9pa$t5s$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
cstump@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> Anyone know which compiler for MVS Cobol supports the -INC directive for
> copies/includes? It appears to be legal even in columns 1-7. I have seen this
> in some non-IBM compilers before (Honeywell and DG I think).

As Bill Klein wondered, the "-INC" feature/function is supported by
Librarian and it is resolved in the Librarian step. This means you can
-INC procedure division code containing CICS commands and not have a
problem; until you decide to migrate to Endevor or some other software
which wants COPY statements (which get resolved in the COBOL step).

Bill Lynch 

BTDTBTTS (been there, done that, bought the T-shirt)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981114133218.11454.00001126@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364D0D76.9C17615F@att.net>`

```
><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: Which compiler supports -INC
>From: William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

One company I know of "saved" money by dumping PANVALET because "you can just
use COPY statements". They ran into a little trouble later when they tried to
compile SQL and CICS statements which can be ++INCLUDE'd prior to the
preprocessor, but not COPY'ed at compile time...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YSJ32.4099$FY5.10843186@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<364D0D76.9C17615F@att.net> <19981114133218.11454.00001126@ng-fr1.aol.com> <364dd119.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>Come, now, it is a trivial matter to write your own mini-preprocessor
>to include the +INCLUDE files.

And it would be easy to write a preprocessor to convert the cobol to C++ to
pascal and then back to assembler for faster run times.......

on and on it goes. ++includes and -includes are not part of COBOL. Using
nonstandard COBOL syntax only complicates maintenance. If it is not a hard
task to expand ++includes in a preprocessor, then why not use the COPY
statement which is standard COBOL syntax? Then even an idiot will be able to
figure out what is going on.

I know this sounds like a trivial thing, but adding such complexities to
COBOL codes makes things that much harder to research.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364f6fa9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364D0D76.9C17615F@att.net> <19981114133218.11454.00001126@ng-fr1.aol.com> <364dd119.0@news1.ibm.net> <YSJ32.4099$FY5.10843186@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
>>Come, now, it is a trivial matter to write your own mini-preprocessor
>>to include the +INCLUDE files.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement which is standard COBOL syntax? Then even an idiot will be able
to
>figure out what is going on.


The problem was that yet another preprocessor had to process the program
because it contains embedded SQL statements, and it was my understanding
that the SQL preprocessor did NOT expand copy books, so one had to put
common SQL statements in the include file. Otherwise, I, of course, agree
with you that what you can do in standard Cobol should not be done with
non-standard means.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Which compiler supports -INC

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981121220502.25467.00000635@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981114133218.11454.00001126@ng-fr1.aol.com>`

```

In article <19981114133218.11454.00001126@ng-fr1.aol.com>, theodp@aol.com (Theo
DP) writes:

>They ran into a little trouble later when they tried to
>compile SQL and CICS statements which can be ++INCLUDE'd prior to the
>preprocessor, but not COPY'ed at compile time... 

The SQL preprocessor can be used for a number of statements by using

   EXEC SQL
     INCLUDE membername
   END-EXEC

Unfortunately, it won't work for CICS statements if the program isn't going
through the SQL preprocessor.

Mark A. Young
```

<a id="t-67450df2d6"></a>
## Thread: Who is the Standard for? — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
For those who read some of the previous posts in which it was pointed out
(correctly) that I have a definite reputation in J4 (and WG4) as someone who
"likes to poke a stick into a hornets nest and twist it around", the
following shouldn't come as much of a surprise.  However, I thought that
everyone (who is interested in the next COBOL Standard) might be interested
in the following document that I just submitted to J4.

As always if you disagree with me OR agree with me, I think that you should
send your input to J4 via its chair at
     das@microfocus.com

My complete input document follows (it is only medium long):

November 13, 1998                                      J4/98-0587


SUBJECT: (Quality Review) - Who is the Standard for?

FROM:       William M. Klein (wmklein@ix.netcom.com)

REFERENCES:

     1.   Base document CD 1.4, June 1998

COMMENTS:

During  several  informal conversations both in person  and  via
Usenet  Newsgroups, it has become clear to  me  that  I  have  a
different view of the "target audience" of the Standard than  do
several  others involved (often more intimately than I)  in  the
Standard�s  development  process.  Although  this  may  seem   a
somewhat  esoteric or hypothetical issue for this late  in  this
Standard�s development, I truly believe that spending  a  little
(not  too much) time on this issue can easily save time  in  the
long run.  I know that there are several outstanding issues that
I  would  have very different opinions on than I do,  should  it
become  clear that I have misunderstood who the current audience
is.

The  view  with  which I do NOT agree is that  the  Standard  is
primarily  (possibly  exclusively - but  I  haven�t  heard  that
expressed in so many words) for the vendors and implementors.

Although,  I certainly agree that vendors and implementors  will
(and do) need to use this document, I have never thought of them
as  its  PRIMARY target.  Now, I don�t think that there  is  any
question  that  the  COBOL language itself (as  opposed  to  the
Standard  document) is intended for the "public" -  but  I  have
always  believed they were also the primary target  audience  of
the document itself.

I  know  that many text writers, course developers,  and  cross-
platform  oriented programmers and developers have in  the  past
and  continue to use the existing Standard.  However, it  is  my
belief  that  with  this next Standard, the  built-in  increased
cross-platform  and  cross-language  capabilities  of  the   new
Standard,  and the growing interest in "open systems"  that  the
next  Standard  will be the reference manual  for  significantly
more  programmers.  Of course, the Standard isn't now and  never
will  be  a  "Programmer�s  Guide" -  but  it  is  an  excellent
reference manual - and I think should be enhanced as such.

Should  WG4  give direction that they agree with  me  that  this
document is not and should not be targeted primarily at vendors,
then  I can think of at least two (outstanding) issues that this
might impact:

1)   Should the composite language skeleton be kept (and made as
  accurate as possible)?  Obviously, if you agree with those who
  think  that  the primary target for the Standard  document  is
  vendors and that programmers will only (or primarily) use  the
  reference  manuals  that the vendors supply  (and  that  those
  developing courses and text books will just HAVE TO WAIT until
 some vendor comes out with a document to use), then this section
  of  the Standard does seem to be "nice" but not necessary.  On
  the other hand, if you are trying to create a document intended
  for  authors, programmers, course developers, etc (as well  as
  implementors), then its usefulness seems apparent.

2)    Many  terms  have changed in this Standard  from  previous
  Standards (for example, removal of "verbs;" "headers" becoming "markers;"
"increment and decrement values" becoming something that  I still don�t
understand; "COBOL character set" becoming  "COBOL  character  repertoire").
Again, if  the  Standard  is  targeted primarily at vendors, it is probably
reasonable to let
  them (and their documentation writers) try and find and figure
  out all the terminology changes.  However, if the Standard  is
  supposed  to be useful for authors and course developers  (and
  programmers who try and communicate with others familiar  with
  different  products  and platforms), then  it  really  becomes
  imperative to highlight each and every one of these changes in
  the "Substantive Changes - not affecting" annex.

It  is  my  guess that there may be other issues where I  simply
have not understood the J4 recommendations that would make sense to me,
should it turn out that the WG4 direction is to produce a "vendor-targeted"
document.   (In  fact,  I  think  the  entire
concepts annex could be removed or at least pruned, should  this
be  true.)  Similarly, if clear cut direction is given to J4  to insure
that this turns out to be as useful a document (still  a reference  manual
and not a programming guide) targeted  at  the entire COBOL-oriented public,
then I think this might make  them reconsider some previously made
recommendations and to  reorient some of their current work.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#8pHR5BE#GA.105@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>...
>For those who read some of the previous posts in which it was pointed out
>(correctly) that I have a definite reputation in J4 (and WG4) as someone
who
>"likes to poke a stick into a hornets nest and twist it around", the
>following shouldn't come as much of a surprise.  However, I thought that
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>understand; "COBOL character set" becoming  "COBOL  character
repertoire").
>Again, if  the  Standard  is  targeted primarily at vendors, it is probably
>reasonable to let
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>and not a programming guide) targeted  at  the entire COBOL-oriented
public,
>then I think this might make  them reconsider some previously made
>recommendations and to  reorient some of their current work.
>
>


Right on, Bill!  Changing terms of reference in the Standard (as you noted
in 2. above) will only serve to confuse existing COBOL users, who are in
fact are, or should be, the primary beneficiaries of the Standard document.
BTW, what are _verbs_ now going to be called? And who in their right mind
would exchange the universally understood  word _set_ for the flowery
replacement word, _repertoire_ (an anglophobic francophile maybe?)
Well-understood terminology already included in  the current Standard should
NOT be arbitrarily changed without a GOOD reason, or it should be liberally
documented and foot-noted if such changes are actually implemented.

Is there any URL where the framework of the ever-evolving new COBOL Standard
can be viewed?  I believe I've seen some postings in c.l.c. to the effect
that the most current draft of the new Standard is being withheld from
public availability.  I've always heard that the only thing that thrives in
the dark is mushrooms, so the evolution of the new Standard should NOT be
allowed to proceed in the dark.

Please, in the best interests of all COBOL programmers, continue poking that
stick into the hornet's nest, it might scrape some of the obfuscation out of
the hornets' eyes and cause them to see more clearly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The French have an Academy for their language.  In France if you
have a sign with "improper" French, you can be fined -- you can be forced 
to take down the sign &c &c

Now if the French Academy were to say, "the people of France have 
NO need to know what is standard French,  the police will enforce that"
then you would expect a vociferous outcry "unfair, unfair!"

If the COBOL standards document(s) is/are NOT for Cobol programmers, 
then . . . who can expect anyone to be able to write "standard" Cobol?  Is
it that people are expected to code and then see what the compilers object
to?

I must be missing something, it seems self-evident to me that the FIRST,
FOREMOST and PRIMARY audience of any language standards documents
are the USERS of the language!  So what am I missing?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#VJTb8EE#GA.213@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```

Kevin G. Rhoads wrote in message
<01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95>...

It is sad but true that many program coders (note that I did NOT say
programmers)  rely on compilers to do all their syntax checking for them and
are then unhappy that the compilers will not also flag logic errors for
them.  Desk-checking ones own code seem to be long gone practice in these
days of virtually instantaneous compilations (compared to the old days of
submit-a-batch-compile-job then wait-until-after-production-for-printout.)

>I must be missing something, it seems self-evident to me that the FIRST,
>FOREMOST and PRIMARY audience of any language standards documents
>are the USERS of the language!  So what am I missing?
>--

The influence of  vendor personnel and academics that permeate the standards
organizations?
Is there any corollary here among
'those that can, do, and those that can't teach'
and
'those that can, program, and those that can't write standards'?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <kerrye@ms.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364F05EC.312CCA7F@ms.com>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <#VJTb8EE#GA.213@upnetnews03>`

```
My recollection of the membership of the ANSI COBOL committee
is that there are few, if any, "academics" on it.

There are, however, a number of "users."

The "unfortunate" situation of vendors controlling the standards process
is a fact of life; it is similar to big money controlling the political process.
They are the ones who have to implement the Standard, probably at
"great" expense to them.  They are the ones who have to market it
(with a few exceptions: what competition does IBM have on its big iron
for the COBOL compiler market? (Except for promoting a new version
to replace an old?))  Of course, they are slugging it out among them-
selves to get "their" features accepted as "Standards," but, in the end,
it is a somewhat "democratic" vote.  Look at how long it has taken IBM
to get the Committee to accept "GOBACK" as a standard (even though
there have been perfectly acceptable (and standard) ways of accomplish-
ing the same thing for decades)!  And even that won't be published until
-- what? -- 2000 or 2001.

Sure, one could argue that it is the users rather than the vendors who
should dictate the standards.  But we live in the Real World.  Users
might want a compiler that is *REALLY* English-like (remember
that? it was a "design point" for the language back in the '50s), but
who is going to write those compilers?  Maybe shareware compilers
would arise, but would "Big Business" actually want to bet their
fortunes on those software "products"?

Kerry Ellis
Independent Systems Programming Consultant
[the views expressed are mine alone and not those of my client]


Dennis J. Minette wrote:

> Kevin G. Rhoads wrote in message
> <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95>...
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> >krhoads@NO_SPAM.cmpnetmail.com
```

```
>My recollection of the membership of the ANSI COBOL committee
>is that there are few, if any, "academics" on it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

process.

>"great" expense to them.  They are the ones who have to market it
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

forced
>> >to take down the sign &c &c
>> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >then . . . who can expect anyone to be able to write "standard" Cobol?
Is
>> >it that people are expected to code and then see what the compilers
object
>> >to?
>>
>> It is sad but true that many program coders (note that I did NOT say
>> programmers)  rely on compilers to do all their syntax checking for them
and
>> are then unhappy that the compilers will not also flag logic errors for
>> them.  Desk-checking ones own code seem to be long gone practice in these
>> days of virtually instantaneous compilations (compared to the old days of
>> submit-a-batch-compile-job then
wait-until-after-production-for-printout.)
>>
>> >I must be missing something, it seems self-evident to me that the FIRST,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The influence of  vendor personnel and academics that permeate the
standards
>> organizations?
>> Is there any corollary here among
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have lots of problems with the Standard committee (as regular readers of
this NG know) and wish there were more "users" on it (at this weeks J4
meeting, 4 of 12 "members" were represented by users - which isn't too bad).
HOWEVER,  I want to make one thing very clear,

In my experience, there has been little (not none - but truly a minimal
amount) of vendors fighting for features or definitions of features - JUST
because their current implementations support it.

As the person who PROPOSED the addition of "GoBack" into the next Standard
(and later proposed that the function get the name "goback"),  I can tell
you that I have never (and do not) currently work for IBM!

In fact, the biggest problem with vendors and the current work of the
committee is that so much is being made "non-portable" by calling it
"implementor defined" or "processor dependent" (which now basically means
"optional").  However, it was two of the user representatives at the recent
J4 meeting who made a real point of saying,  "I don't think this should be
defined in the Standard, just let my implementor figure out how to do it
best on their machine with their operating system."  (This really shocked
and depressed me - but didn't completely surprise me.)

One other comment that I need to make about the increased "non-portability"
of Standard COBOL is that I understand well the origin of much of it.  One
of the major goals of this Standard is to provide both the functionality of
and inter-operability with the C programming language.   One of the
historical advantages of COBOL over C was that the run-time results of COBOL
were always much more portable than those of C (although the rest of the
world has called C "portable").  Once you try to add these non-portable C
functionality and interfaces to COBOL, you are "stuck" with making them
equally un-portable in COBOL - just so that you can correctly interface with
all the non-portable C implementations.  I am not saying that this "change"
in COBOL is good, but I do see it as (probably) necessary in a world where
most interfaces and APIs are defined for the C-oriented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00

```
For all of those who replied to my original note and its thread,  I will
posting a "report" on my time at the J4 meeting in Carmel (hopefully in the
next day).  HOWEVER, on this particular topic J4 took a vote and decided
OVERWHELMINGLY that the Standard does
     *NOT*
have "users" as one of its primary target audience.

I consider this "useful" (if depressing) information when judging and
evaluating the work that comes out of that group!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b8ca5b87998d339896a8@news.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95>, 
T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM says...
> The French have an Academy for their language.  In France if you
> have a sign with "improper" French, you can be fined -- you can be forced 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What I think we are seeing unfold is a power struggle between those who 
have control of the standards and those who simply want to have access to 
them. I don't see any reason why the standards are not made accessible
to the public. Granted this is not light reading but I feel that the 
majority of individuals who whish to access them will make the effort
to interpret them. 

I think there is a simple fear that once the masses have a chance to
read the standards they might want to contribute the the development of 
future versions. So what is the problem with that? Well, it would clearly 
undermined the power that the current individuals have and they do not 
want to give this in the near future. 

Granted I could be reading far to much into this issue but I don't know 
of any reason why *we* should not at least be allowed to read the 
standards that *we* must make our code comply to. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UfG32.3344$RM6.3707364@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <MPG.10b8ca5b87998d339896a8@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff Farkas wrote:
>T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM says...
>> The French have an Academy for their language.  In France if you
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>standards that *we* must make our code comply to.

Jeff, it may be more a 'status' thing than an actual 'power' thing, though
the two are obviously closely related.  Also, though you are quite correct
that the current standards documents are tough reading, I believe that to
be completely unnecessary, and a cousin to 'bureaucratese'; more a matter
of custom and form than of function.  I have been writing computer system
technical documentation for a long time, with lots of feedback from users.
And I have never found it needful or useful to resort to such indirect,
unclear language as seen in the standards.  I agree with Donald Tees; clear
and precise are not mutually exclusive, or even at odds.  Indeed, to be
'functionally precise', it should be clear.  What good is 'precise' that
is not understood because it is unclear?  Not to say that writing standards
grade documentation is easy.  Just that it could be made a lot simpler,
clearer, easy to understand, and still be precise and efficient.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732gng$bi9@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <01be1046$b9c1e600$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <MPG.10b8ca5b87998d339896a8@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
   <some snippage>
>
>What I think we are seeing unfold is a power struggle between those who
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

  <more snippage>

There is a BIG difference between "access to the Standard" and a
user-targeted Standard.  The issue of "easy access to the COBOL Standard" is
related not to its target audience but to an existing and outstanding
"fight" over whether the text of the Standard is "validly" copyrighted or
not.  If you have a copy of the '85 Standard (or any of its amendments), you
will notice that there is both:
  -  a copyright notice on the outside cover
         and
  - an acknowledgement that anyone can copy and distribute it in any way
they want - as long as they acknowledge the source

For at least 10 years that I know of this has been an open issue with J4,
WG4, ANSI, and ISO as to whether this does or does not constitute a "valid
copyright".  Even without the acknowledgement, the current Standard
documents (in its history section) that it is DERIVED from the CODASYL JOD -
and that was never copyrighted.  Therefore, there are additional reasons to
question the ANSI claim of a copyright.  HOWEVER, there is reasonable
evidence that ISO will *not* make a new Standard (for anything - computer
language or nail size) unless they can copyright it (and SELL copies of it).
Therefore, there are a number of people in the COBOL Standards group are
scared to death that anyone will ever make it clear to ISO (and/or ANSI)
that no future version can be "restricted" to THEIR copy.

Bottom-line: for those of you interested in a hyper-text version (or even in
creating courses or text books that include extensive quotes from the
Standard), I suggest that you write to ANSI and make it clear that you do
NOT accept that they have a valid copyright on the current Standard or any
document derived from it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who is the Standard for?

- **From:** RandallBart <Barticus@att.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3653F134.58494B8F@att.spam.net>`
- **References:** `<72jbem$3e9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> The  view  with  which I do NOT agree is that  the  Standard  is
> primarily  (possibly  exclusively - but  I  havenï¿½t  heard  that
> expressed in so many words) for the vendors and implementors.

I agree with you.  Furthermore, every shop with more than X programmers
(where X is left open to debate) should have a senior programmer who has
read the Cobol standard.  Whoever writes the company coding standards
should be familiar with the Cobol standard.  Even the best implementor
manuals don't clearly identify all extensions and implementor dependant
features.

> 1)   Should the composite language skeleton be kept (and made as
>   accurate as possible)?  Obviously, if you agree with those who
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   implementors), then its usefulness seems apparent.

Even accepting that most programmers never see the standard itself, every
word of the standard influences the derivitive documents they see.  If a
language skeleton is useful to Cobol programmers (has anyone claimed that
it isn't) then the job of assembling it from the main text ought to be
done once.  

In a small way it is my fault that the language skeleton is on the
chopping block.  I submitted a large number of points of action against it
in CD 1.4.  Perhaps if I had submitted only half of them, then the level
of change would have seemed more palatable to J4, then I could have
submitted the rest of them against CD 1.5.  Now that I've found most of
the errors, correcting and mmaintaining the language skeleton would
require the effort of only one person (me), and would not have significant
impact on the critical path. 

> It  is  my  guess that there may be other issues where I  simply
> have not understood the J4 recommendations that would make sense to me,
> should it turn out that the WG4 direction is to produce a "vendor-targeted"
> document.

To target the document to the implementors is a mistake, but historically
they are the ones represented on the committees, so their view prevails. 
The standard ought to be written for the programmers, with the
implementors almost as an afterthought.

Much of the problem is that programming language standards are developed
under a system developed for other types of standards.  The earliest
standards were weights and measures.  Though everyone uses them, it
doesn't make much difference exactly how big a gallon or a barrel or a
meter is, as long as everyone can refer to the same standard.  Later came
quality standards (how large is a large olive), manufacturing standards
(plugs and jacks, screw threads), and interface standards (MIDI, SCSI).

Who is the "consumer" of the SCSI standard?  I have plugged SCSI cables
into SCSI devices, but I have not read the SCSI standard, not do I intend
to.  The only people who need to read the actual SCSI standard are
engineers designing devices.  Even the engineer is usually happy that
there is a standard pin ordering, and spends little time ruminating over
how a different ordering would make his job easier.

But this is not the world the Cobol standard (or any programming standard)
lives in.  When the 1974 Cobol standard said that "ADD A TO B GIVING C"
was an invalid statement, it was not enough that every implementor knew to
give it a syntax error, but a million programmers had to learn (usually
after half an hour of staring in disbelief at an error message) that it
was invalid.  When the 1985 standard corrected this anomaly, they didn't
do it for the implementors, they did it for the programmers. 

It is wrong for the committee to put the implementors ahead of users.  The
paucity of user input in the standard development process is a major cause
of this.  At this point, I want to see the current draft pushed out the
door as soon as possible, even with a few of the existing warts.  But the
Cobol standard development process is flawed; we need to create a new
process for subsequent Cobol standards, and it should be centered around
the users, not the implementors. 
```

<a id="t-63bc6200bd"></a>
## Thread: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL) — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Preface,
   I am NOT advocating the use of NEXT SENTENCE in new code. However, given
several recent threads, I thought I would pass this along.

It is absolutely true (as pointed out in a previous thread) that the current
(and draft) Standard explicitly prohibit the use of NEXT SENTENCE  in an IF
statement that is concluded by END-IF (also true for SEARCH and END-SEARCH).
This means that the following code (although allowed as an extension in many
compilers) is "non-conforming" source code.

If whatever > something
     Perform aRoutine
Else
     Next Sentence
End-If
Perform anotherRoutine
  .

However, as I pointed out to J4 in an interpretation request (which they
confirmed was true), the following is fully conforming source code (with an
explanation following it)

If whatever > something
     Perform aRoutine
Else
     Copy NextSentence.
End-If
Perform anotherRoutine
  .

Where the NextSentence copy-member includes the following code (which is
ALWAYS logically equivalent to a "NEXT SENTENCE" statement)

Move 1 to Always-one
If Always-one < 2
    If Always-one = 1
         Next Sentence
    Else
         Continue
Else
    Continue
End-If

OK, you may ask WHY is this "legal" (ANSI conforming) source code. The
reason is that the Standard states that an "ELSE" always terminates an
"internal" conditional statement (IF or otherwise).  The Standard also says
that you can't use an END-IF to terminate an IF with a NEXT SENTENCE but it
does NOT state that you can't use an END-IF to end an IF that has a nested
IF with a NEXT SENTENCE.

FYI, I came up with this technique (and interpretation request) when I was
trying to convince ANSI to remove the restriction against END-IF with NEXT
SENTENCE in the next Standard.  I think it is silly that the "logic" can be
included in a conforming source program when the code cannot.  However, J4
(and/or WG4) disagreed and the restriction is still included in the draft of
the next Standard although many (most?) compilers don't enforce it.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>`
- **References:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```

> 
(snip)
> 
> If whatever > something
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> End-If

I think I see a period/full stop after the "Copy NextSentence". Isn't
this a syntax problem?

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72j5cf$43l@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>...
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch

No, it is actually required (and can easily cause confusion).  A "correct"
COPY statement *must* end with a period (full-stop) - but the entire COPY
statement (and any REPLACING - along with REPLACE statements - which also
need periods) are fully "resolved" before any syntax analysis is done.  I
know that this looks AWFUL to have a period in the middle of a statement -
but 'tis the way it works!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>

<72j5cf$43l@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>...
>>
>>William Lynch wrote in message <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am in enough trouble with J4 and WG4.  If you want to suggest this, then
please send a note to

    das@microfocus.com

(Actually I don't think it is that bad of an idea - but there may be a
problem if you had a

    Copy whatever replacing aWord by end-copy

Is that a syntax error or are you really trying to replace the COBOL word
"aWord" by another COBOL word "end-copy"?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#mN0KPCE#GA.257@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net> <72j5cf$43l@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <ezjnH8AE#GA.150@upnetnews03> <72l0a3$f86@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <72l0a3$f86@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>No, it is actually required (and can easily cause confusion).  A
"correct"
>>>COPY statement *must* end with a period (full-stop) - but the entire COPY
>>>statement (and any REPLACING - along with REPLACE statements - which also
>>>need periods) are fully "resolved" before any syntax analysis is done.  I
>>>know that this looks AWFUL to have a period in the middle of a
statement -
>>>but 'tis the way it works!
>>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>


That is a pretty far-out example of where END-COPY could go wrong.
END-COPY, if implemented in the Standard, would be a _reserved word_ and I
could not envision a replacement situation such as you have presented.
Would this be a case of nested COPY statements?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lhh5$f70$11@newsreader4.core.theplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>`

```
In article <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>, William Lynch says...
>
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>this a syntax problem?

Nope. COPY Statements are supposed to have a period after the file. 

The other legal next sentance before an end-if situation I thought of was...

if whatever > something
    perform aroutine
else
    next sentance
end-if.
display "next sentance"

Which I've seen a few times as a quick fix. I don't use that myself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732dib$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72im3e$bcs@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net> <72lhh5$f70$11@newsreader4.core.theplanet.net>`

```

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message
<72lhh5$f70$11@newsreader4.core.theplanet.net>...
>In article <364D0E19.958EA28A@att.net>, William Lynch says...
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>The other legal next sentance before an end-if situation I thought of
was...
>
>if whatever > something
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Although this last example compiles cleanly on many (most?) compilers it is
explicitly illegal according to the Standard.

P.S.  Since my original note, I did discover that J4 and WG4 *have* placed
"NEXT SENTENCE" into the "archaic" category.  This doesn't mean that it will
go away in the next or next after that Standard, but it does mean that they
are at least saying that it isn't a "nice thing to do"!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF (and Standard COBOL)

_(thread depth: 4)_


#### ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72inoq$1ub@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A few comments (interspersed within your post)

patbean@help.worldnet.att.net wrote in message
<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>...
>I am a college student taking advanced business programming ... my
>class has been working on the same program since the beginning of
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>

   shouldn't your JCL have
         //GO.TRANSRPT  DD   SYSOUT=A
         //GO.SRTRPT    DD   SYSOUT=A
         //GO.ERRPT     DD   SYSOUT=A

but much more importantly, where are you sending your DISPLAY and SORT
output to?  What is your OUTDD compiler option set to? (probably the default
of SYSOUT)  If so, does your GOCOB already allocate it? (If not, you
should - although some software releases will do this for you).   You
probably need a SORTOUT DD card as well - and when you look at that, you
will see why your SORT is failing (or at least get a better clue).



>1
>  STMT NO. MESSAGE
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>***************************************************************************
****************
>
> 000337                000-MAIN-CONTROL.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>500-VALIDATE-AFTER-SORT.

  You aren't showing us the 500-VALIDATE... output procedure.   My guess (as
discussed in another recent thread) is that you are not executing your
OUTPUT logic in a "full loop" so the AT END of your RETURN isn't being
executed - but that is just a guess without seeing the full code.

>   000343                        DISPLAY 'END OF SORT STATEMENT'.
>   000344
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>   000367      1                       PERFORM 126-REC2-PROC

    Does this routine ever do a RELEASE (or does anything)?


>   000368                    END-IF.
>   000369
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>   000382
```

```
WS-REC2-SP-CTR
```
