```
   000406      1                  MOVE 0 TO WS-REC2-SP-CTR
   000407      1                     PERFORM 250-RELEASE-REC2
   000408                    END-IF.
   000409                    DISPLAY 'VALIDATE RECORD TYPE 2'.

ALL-REC-HEAD-LINE2-WS

   000429                    MOVE HLD-FIRST-NAME   TO FIRST-NAME-EM-DL

ERROR-MESSAGE-DL
   000431
   000432                    WRITE ERROR-RECORD FROM
ERROR-RPT-DETAIL-LINE
   000433                        AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
   000434                        ADD 1 TO ERR-LINE-COUNT-WS.
   000435
   000436                211-WRITE-REC2-ERROR.

   000439                    MOVE SPACES TO LAST-NAME-EM-DL
   000440                    MOVE SPACES TO FIRST-NAME-EM-DL
0  000441                    MOVE SPC-HOME-PHONE-EM TO
ERROR-MESSAGE-DL
   000442
   000443                    WRITE ERROR-RECORD FROM
ERROR-RPT-DETAIL-LINE
   000444                        AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
   000445                        ADD 1 TO ERR-LINE-COUNT-WS.

   000448                    DISPLAY 'RELEASE REC 1'.
   000449                    RELEASE WS-REC1 FROM HLD-REC1.
   000450
   000451                250-RELEASE-REC2.
   000452                    DISPLAY 'RELEASE REC 2'.
   000453                    RELEASE WS-REC2 FROM HLD-REC2.

   000455                400-EXIT.
   000456
   000457                    EXIT.

ARE-THERE-MORE-SORT-REC-WS.

HOME-PHONE-DL
   000477      1
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72j0td$l0l@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72inoq$1ub@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <364ee4c8.8647469@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```

patbean@help.worldnet.att.net wrote in message
<364ee4c8.8647469@netnews.worldnet.att.net>...
>here is the entire program .. it is going to be written to three
>different reports ...  I was trying to post the parts that I thought
>were needed ....
>
>
   <program snipped>

A *cursory* glance at your program shows no obvious problems.  My
suggestions are:

A) Make certain that you have a SORTOUT DD allocated and look at what the
sort messages are.  (If you don't understand them, post them here)

B) Put a DISPLAY of the SORT-RETURN register (with a literal to tell you
where you are)
    Before and after the SORT statement
    Before and after each RELEASE statement
    Before and after each RETURN statement

Once you know exactly where the SORT is having its problem, it should help
you locate the problem.

P.S.  Does your college let you use any "interactive" debugger (such as
COBTEST, Xpediter, SmarTest, etc?).  If so, this too may help you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3654dd0c.14974311@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72inoq$1ub@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <364ee4c8.8647469@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72j0td$l0l@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:


>
>B) Put a DISPLAY of the SORT-RETURN register (with a literal to tell you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There's no point in doing this. SORT does not return intermediate
result codes. The SORT-RETURN special register is only set at the end
of the sort. The only valid reference to SORT RETURN during the sort
isthat a sort can be told to terminate at any time by using 
"MOVE 16 TO SORT-RETURN-CODE." 

		Scott Peterson.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D9AD9.B980C3A0@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72inoq$1ub@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <364ee4c8.8647469@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
There are several problems with the  program. First, at lines 466/467, if
the condition is not met, you will not process anything, then execution
will continue at line 484. Without seeing your data, this is the most
likely cause of your run-time error.

Your code is also processing your input file after & SORT-REC after EOF.
Basically, when you read/return a record, having reached end of file, your
record area is undefined. You then continue to use the record area. The
UNTIL THERE-ARE-NO-MORE-RECORDS will not be tested until the PERFORM
evaluates it. This is done either at the beginning (the default) or the
end (WITH TEST AFTER).
This change will correct this problem:
copy line 363 before 356 :
     000363                    PERFORM 125-READ-INPUT.
     000356                    PERFORM 120-PROCESS-IN-RECORD
     000357                        UNTIL THERE-ARE-NO-MORE-RECORDS.

move 363  after line 375.
Same issue in  310-VALIDATE-RECORD.

patbean@worldnet.att.net wrote:

> here is the entire program .. it is going to be written to three
> different reports ...  I was trying to post the parts that I thought
> were needed ....
> [snip]

>    000355
>    000356                    PERFORM 120-PROCESS-IN-RECORD
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

> 000459                500-VALIDATE-AFTER-SORT SECTION.
>    000460
…[186 more quoted lines elided]…
>    000609                    EXIT.
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72iv9v$o91$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
    Check the SORT-RETURN Special-Register at the start of your Output
Procedure for a non-zero value. If you've tested the RETURN-CODE
Special-Register before, you'll know the proper syntax. If I remember
correctly, a value of 16 indicates an unsuccessful Sort/Merge. Check the
appropriate manual for other non-zero values, their meanings and severity
levels. It looks like one aspect of this exercise is for the student to
react properly in an unsuccessful Sort scenario. If this were a Job Stream
with 100 steps and this error occurred during step 002, consider the impact
this could have on the next days business should the SORT-RETURN be
non-zero.

    FYI, the SORT-RETURN Special-Register in an IBM Mainframe environment is
actually the Register 15 value returned by DFSORT or some other third-party
Sort/Merge. RETURN-Code is also a value returned by a Called Sub-Program to
the Calling Program. Please note that although registers are inherently
4-bytes, both of the above Special-Registers are viewed by COBOL as 2-bytes,
halfword-binary.

    Good Luck....

WOB,
Atlanta

>seems to be able to help us solve the problem ...

<Rest Snipped>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364DBC06.1A823B75@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72iv9v$o91$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
WOB wrote:

>     Check the SORT-RETURN Special-Register at the start of your Output
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> levels.

Some comments from a DFSORT guy:

You can find all of the DFSORT return codes in "DFSORT Messages, Codes
and Diagnosis Guide", online at URL:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ICECM104/CCONTENTS

If you want to make sure you see the DFSORT messages from a COBOL
program, add the following to your JCL:

//SORTDIAG DD DUMMY
//DFSPARM DD *
   OPTION MSGDDN=MYOUT
/*
//MYOUT DD SYSOUT=*

SORTDIAG tells DFSORT to write all messages, including diagnostic
messages.  This makes sure the messages are written even if some other
parameter has been used to suppress them.

DFSPARM can be used to supply additional or overriding control
statements to DFSORT.  You can even overrride the SORT statement.  The
name DFSPARM may have been changed at your site via the ICEMAC MSGDDN
parameter.  You can use ICETOOL DEFAULTS to list all of your site's
defaults, including MSGDDN, as explained at URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/storage/software/sort/srtmadfl.htm

MSGDDN=MYOUT tells DFSORT to write its messages to MYOUT.  The default
for DFSORT's message data set name is SYSOUT, not SORTOUT as some people
have suggested.  SORTOUT is the default for DFSORT's output data set
name.    The  SYSOUT DD might also be used for COBOL messages, so using
the MSGDDN DD makes sure the DFSORT messages are separated from the
COBOL messages.  (If COBOL messages are not going to the SYSOUT DD, you
can just put in a SYSOUT DD and not use MSGDDN=name.)

MYOUT DD  will show the DFSORT messages.  If DFSORT passed RC=16 to the
calling program, you'll see an ICExxxA error message.  You can look it
up online in "DFSORT Messages, Codes and Diagnosis Guide" using the URL
above.

For more information about DFSORT, visit the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL
:-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364EE6C9.FC8CC16D@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <72iv9v$o91$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <364DBC06.1A823B75@ibm.net>`

```
Frank Yaeger wrote:

> DFSPARM can be used to supply additional or overriding control
> statements to DFSORT.  You can even overrride the SORT statement.  The
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.storage.ibm.com/storage/software/sort/srtmadfl.htm

Oops.  That should read "PARMDDN" instead of "MSGDDN".  PARMDDN is the
parameter for changing the DFSPARM name. MSGDDN is the parameter for
changing the message data set name.  Sorry if I confused anyone.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL
:-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT/MVS is on the World Wide Web at URL:
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "Ryan Roberts" <ryanr@fgi.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lhui$qt8$1@supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Although I'm not sure if it will fix your problem, I find the placement of
your "close files" statement somewhat unusual.  With that in mind, I
recommend moving line 358 'perform 190-close files' to about line 344,
placing it just above your 'stop run' statement.  Although your program
should automatically handle opening and closing your sort file, your
placement of a "close files" statement in your input procedure may be
affecting the sort file.

Additionally, even though they're not listed below, I'll assume you have
properly used and placed your 'release' (to sort file) and 'return' (from
sort file) statements.  Be sure to check these.

-Ryan-
____________________
ryanr@fgi.net
____________________
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981115132604.07525.00000998@ng154.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
patbean@help.worldnet.att.net
On 14 Nov 1998 01:34:46 GMT

posted
>>
I am a college student taking advanced business programming ... my
class has been working on the same program since the beginning of
september and we are all getting the same error message and no one
seems to be able to help us solve the problem ... ...
<<

Did you say "advanced"? I mean seriously! We are glad to help. Really! It IS a
privilege. 

But are you actually saying that you have a problem with an internal sort for
more than five weeks,and as a student in an 'advanced' class,  you can't even
begin to think of a way to get the stingy old machine to cough up as much as a
hint about the problem?

Well, okay then. Lets pretend. Let us assume that you have this kind of problem
in the real world. You take the source code to the nearest senior programmer.
What will she say?

We can only imagine, since all of this is so difficult. But she might say
something like, "You have I/O statements in this program. You have not coded
FILE STATUS for thosse files. Code FILE STATUS for those files.  After each I/O
statement, I know this is hard, ... I will help you, because I care, ... after
EACH, that is E-A-C-H I/O statement test the status code."

She may then proceed to add, "I am a busy person, but I will mention to you
that when I took INTRODUCTORY programming, they encouraged me to test the
status code after EACH I/O statement. ... this habit really helped when I moved
into those absolutely awesome rocket science access methods like SQLs, IMS and
CICS, ... I mean it was RAD, we could actually catch an error right there when
it happened, rather than later on when it would be confusing."

And she might add as she tossed the boring listing back at you, "OPEN is an I/O
statement. Check the status code aftrer every I/O statement. CLOSE is an I/O
statement. Check the status code after every I/O statement. Junior programmers
operate under the incredibly expensive assumption that coding status code
checks takes longer ... " there could then be the possibility that a light
would go on within your brain as she risisted filling the pause with derisive
laughter and adds ... "it is usually not a good idea to close a file twice." 

After such an encounter, it might be reasonable to believe that not all
problems were identified for you.  But it would be reasonable to believe that
you can find all I/O problems if you test the FILE STATUS after every I/O verb.
If you code a status code check after every I/O statement you write, you will
save a great deal of time. 

And naturally you should display (or dump to an error file) any unexpected
status code. The structure of a procedure division in COBOL is mostly just a
reflection of the file access relationships and I/O status code driven
exception handling.  The movement of the data and the manipulation of values is
minor work.

Business data processing is I/O processing. The shape and surface of the
procedure division is dominated by I/O status management. 

SORT and MERGE are I/O verbs.  A procedure division withou a check of
SORT-RETURN should be assumed to be an error,  until proven otherwise. A
procedure division with OPENs, READs, WRITEs, and CLOSEs which do not have
immediate status code checking, is not really worthy of review. Perhaps a
beginning student might beleive that is reasonable, but not a beginning
professional.  

So, do you want to look forward or backward at this 'advanced' stage? Think
about what you are doing.  It is like you are in your car, you have a problem
and you are not even willing to look at the instrument panel. I mean all those
lights and dials: Gosh that is rough stuff!

In a prefessional environment you can not wait five weeks to possibly, maybe
find some indicator readout.  When the problem happens, that day, that night,
that very minute, millions are at stake. You most resolve problems NOW, not
five weeks from now.

Exactly how is it, that you can have a teacher who allows you to think you are
doing something 'advanced', and you have not been instructed to test the status
codes? What is that?

It blows my mind! You have I/O statements and you are not even checking to see
that they work before you proceed. What did y'all do thar' in yer beginers'
class?

Before you advance to the PROCEDURE DIVISION, have a look at the ENVIRONMENT
DIVISION. It is part of a 'complete' program.

Hope your next five weeks are more enlightening.















Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72nenq$hue$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <19981115132604.07525.00000998@ng154.aol.com>`

```
Come on now ... you know that correct academic methodology
involves changing the problem to fit the preconceived solution,
not the other way around ...

A properly designed course material program is *not* supposed
to work.  It is supposed to make it appear that business does not
know what it is doing, so that the professor can get a grant to
solve the problem that s/he just invented.

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981115132604.07525.00000998@ng154.aol.com>...
>patbean@help.worldnet.att.net
>On 14 Nov 1998 01:34:46 GMT
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Did you say "advanced"? I mean seriously! We are glad to help. Really! It
IS a
>privilege.
>
>But are you actually saying that you have a problem with an internal sort
for
>more than five weeks,and as a student in an 'advanced' class,  you can't
even
>begin to think of a way to get the stingy old machine to cough up as much
as a
>hint about the problem?
>
>Well, okay then. Lets pretend. Let us assume that you have this kind of
problem
>in the real world. You take the source code to the nearest senior
programmer.
>What will she say?
>
>We can only imagine, since all of this is so difficult. But she might say
>something like, "You have I/O statements in this program. You have not
coded
>FILE STATUS for thosse files. Code FILE STATUS for those files.  After each
I/O
>statement, I know this is hard, ... I will help you, because I care, ...
after
>EACH, that is E-A-C-H I/O statement test the status code."
>
>She may then proceed to add, "I am a busy person, but I will mention to you
>that when I took INTRODUCTORY programming, they encouraged me to test the
>status code after EACH I/O statement. ... this habit really helped when I
moved
>into those absolutely awesome rocket science access methods like SQLs, IMS
and
>CICS, ... I mean it was RAD, we could actually catch an error right there
when
>it happened, rather than later on when it would be confusing."
>
>And she might add as she tossed the boring listing back at you, "OPEN is an
I/O
>statement. Check the status code aftrer every I/O statement. CLOSE is an
I/O
>statement. Check the status code after every I/O statement. Junior
programmers
>operate under the incredibly expensive assumption that coding status code
>checks takes longer ... " there could then be the possibility that a light
>would go on within your brain as she risisted filling the pause with
derisive
>laughter and adds ... "it is usually not a good idea to close a file
twice."
>
>After such an encounter, it might be reasonable to believe that not all
>problems were identified for you.  But it would be reasonable to believe
that
>you can find all I/O problems if you test the FILE STATUS after every I/O
verb.
>If you code a status code check after every I/O statement you write, you
will
>save a great deal of time.
>
>And naturally you should display (or dump to an error file) any unexpected
>status code. The structure of a procedure division in COBOL is mostly just
a
>reflection of the file access relationships and I/O status code driven
>exception handling.  The movement of the data and the manipulation of
values is
>minor work.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>about what you are doing.  It is like you are in your car, you have a
problem
>and you are not even willing to look at the instrument panel. I mean all
those
>lights and dials: Gosh that is rough stuff!
>
>In a prefessional environment you can not wait five weeks to possibly,
maybe
>find some indicator readout.  When the problem happens, that day, that
night,
>that very minute, millions are at stake. You most resolve problems NOW, not
>five weeks from now.
>
>Exactly how is it, that you can have a teacher who allows you to think you
are
>doing something 'advanced', and you have not been instructed to test the
status
>codes? What is that?
>
>It blows my mind! You have I/O statements and you are not even checking to
see
>that they work before you proceed. What did y'all do thar' in yer beginers'
>class?
>
>Before you advance to the PROCEDURE DIVISION, have a look at the
ENVIRONMENT
>DIVISION. It is part of a 'complete' program.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
From what it looks like you simply have and error with your sort.

Due to what I perceive as complexity in your program I recommend the
following:

1) Verify all DDNAMEs manually. This probably isnt the problem...but if
COBOL references a DD which is not defined in the JCL it will dynamically
allocate a temp DDNAME and DSN which will delete itself after the JCL/PROC
step completes.

2) Place a display statements after each paragraph label. Run program.
Manually follow code through program. 9 times out of 10 this solves problems
for me.

3) if possible, convert sort to an externale sort. EXTRACT FILTER SORT
REPORT. 4 STEPS TO EASIER PROGRAMMING.
- step 1 should extract all information into a flat file with little or no
selection criteria rformatting as necessary, usually COBOL program.
- step 2 should filter off uneeded information, typically I use SORT
- step 3 should sort the information into the order(s) you need
- step 4 should generate a report. simple reports can be done with sort,
more complex would use COBOL or EZtrieve or DYL280
****see DFSORT/SYNCSORT manual referencing SECTIONS/HEADER/TRAILER
statements for reporting. See OUTFIL statement for generating more than 1
report from 1 input file


I am not trying to be insulting, just trying to help simplify the problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com>`

```
"Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:


>3) if possible, convert sort to an externale sort. EXTRACT FILTER SORT
>REPORT. 4 STEPS TO EASIER PROGRAMMING.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>more complex would use COBOL or EZtrieve or DYL280

I don't think this suggestion is doing anyone a favor. There is no
'programming efficiency' in parsing data 4 times to extract and
produce a report. This approach was generally used either by people
who didn't trust COBOL sorts (often justified back in COBOL "D" or "F"
days) or were extremely limited on memory. Then the tradeoff was
sometimes justified. 

In any version of IBM COBOL later than VS-COBOL the I/O efficiency is
so much better for a COBOL SORT  that with any significant amount of
data this read/write/read/write/read/write/read/report that you
suggest is simply wasteful. Worst case, do the extract and sort in one
step and then the reporting if you want to use a different
language...or write the whole thing using E15/E35 sort exits and let
sort do most of the work for you. All you have to write is a couple
little subroutines. 

                            Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net>`

```

>
>I don't think this suggestion is doing anyone a favor. There is no
>'programming efficiency' in parsing data 4 times to extract and


Nowhere did I say it was more efficient. Efficiency is needed on slow
computers with limited memory. IBM mainframes are neither of these.

I said simpler because obviously these ADVANCED COBOL students were having
serious problems. When a problem becomes too complex ONE SUGGESTED way of
solving it is to simplify the program.

>In any version of IBM COBOL later than VS-COBOL the I/O efficiency is
>so much better for a COBOL SORT  that with any significant amount of
>data this read/write/read/write/read/write/read/report that you
>suggest is simply wasteful. Worst case, do the extract and sort in one
>step and then the reporting if you want to use a different

I often combine the extract and the sort, but I was trying to simplify the
problem, not complicate it. All of these steps could be written in COBOL and
all with small easily maintained and easily understood programs.

>language...or write the whole thing using E15/E35 sort exits and let
>sort do most of the work for you. All you have to write is a couple
>little subroutines.


When talking about waste and efficiency you have to remember that the work
at some point in the future must be completed. That is the project must end
with a functional, not necessarily 100% efficient. I agree my process may be
inefficient, but once the basic procedures are broken down to their basics
they are much easier to develop, maintain, and implement. This is what we
are supposed to do.

The question was to help find a solution to a problem. Critiquing my
suggestion does not help solve the problem. Obviously you are more concerned
with criticizing my style rather than solving the problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uIs$vqcE#GA.153@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net> <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com>...
>
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem, not complicate it. All of these steps could be written in COBOL
and
>all with small easily maintained and easily understood programs.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with a functional, not necessarily 100% efficient. I agree my process may
be
>inefficient, but once the basic procedures are broken down to their basics
>they are much easier to develop, maintain, and implement. This is what we
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>suggestion does not help solve the problem. Obviously you are more
concerned
>with criticizing my style rather than solving the problem.
>

Learning from constructive criticism should be one of the primary goals of
the c.l.c. newsgroup.  One of the benefits of a properly organized design
and development process should be some measure of refinement and
simplification of the initial project design outline. Some rookie COBOL
programmers (and there do seem to be more and more of them lately) regularly
lurking in this ng might seize upon your outline of one possible solution as
an itemized gospel of the way these things MUST or SHOULD be done.  Reading
later rebuttals, refinements and philosophical discussions on a particular
problem in a given thread can lead to expanded thinking and better
programming practices among the regular lurkers here in c.l.c.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365e79f7.24391983@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net> <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com>`

```
"Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:

>

>Nowhere did I say it was more efficient. Efficiency is needed on slow
>computers with limited memory. IBM mainframes are neither of these.
>
Bad programming practices are bad programming practices. I've  spent
far too much time helping programmers unlearn techniques learned when
people trained on a couple of hundred records and then used the same
techniques on a million plus records.  

>I said simpler because obviously these ADVANCED COBOL students were having
>serious problems. When a problem becomes too complex ONE SUGGESTED way of
>solving it is to simplify the program.
>

Another is to examine the environment and read the error messages
before rewriting the program. 

>
>When talking about waste and efficiency you have to remember that the work
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Within reason. However, I challenge the idea that in the hands of a
competent programmer that your methodology would truly be easier to
develop, maintain or implement.  It may harken back to some of the old
modular programming ideas, but maintainability is more a function of
lines of code than anything else. 

>The question was to help find a solution to a problem. Critiquing my
>suggestion does not help solve the problem. Obviously you are more concerned
>with criticizing my style rather than solving the problem.
>

As stated above, the concern should be with debugging methodology
rather than redesign. There are apparently a couple of logic errors in
the original program, combined with a severe lack of knowledge of sort
workings. Neither requires your approach for resolution.  In fact,
your approach ignores the problem by coding around it rather than
resolving it.

As far as criticizing your style, it deserved criticism.  Deal with
it. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OMM42.283$QZ5.168345@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net> <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com> <365e79f7.24391983@news3.ibm.net>`

```
>Bad programming practices are bad programming practices. I've  spent
>far too much time helping programmers unlearn techniques learned when
>people trained on a couple of hundred records and then used the same
>techniques on a million plus records.

I regulary use this method on hundreds of thousands of records with
significant decease in CPU usage when compared to internal sorts. I have
developed this over several years in the work place. Granted there may be
better ways of doing this but I am not familiar with them.

If you are referring to their style...that is another story.


>Another is to examine the environment and read the error messages
>before rewriting the program.
>


I did examine error messages, but without data files and JCL error messages
don't get you very far.

>As stated above, the concern should be with debugging methodology
>rather than redesign. There are apparently a couple of logic errors in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>resolvig it.

The first 2 suggestions were based on DEBUGGING METHODOLOGY within the
information that was given. I have never tried to rewrite a program in this
manner. But the program they wrote and most of the programs I have ever
worked on are very different. They obviously have alot of time to waste and
suggesting a rewrite when you have the time is often a better solution.

Without seeing the data files, JCL, and knowing the specifics of the rest of
the job process these were the geusses I gave. Having these things makes a
problem such as this much easier to solve. Specifically using display
statements, if a dbugger is not available. I fully expected one of the first
two suggestions to solve the problem. Rewriting an entire program is clearly
a last resort. I simply suggested a process which has proven time and time
again the shortest and easiest way for me to develop a report generating
program.

I may have overemphasised it as the EASIEST, maybe I should have said
EASIEST time and time agin for me to program a report generating program.

>
>As far as criticizing your style, it deserved criticism.  Deal with
>it.


If you offerred constructive criticism I would agree, but there is little
constructive criticism in your comments. Just ridicule. Anyone can ridicule
anothers work. Offer a better solution or don't say anything at all.

Obviously you think you had a better solution, but you failed to offer it.
Your other posting simply criticized anothers suggetion.

So what would you suggest they do?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3654a32f.3836977@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net> <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com> <365e79f7.24391983@news3.ibm.net> <OMM42.283$QZ5.168345@storm.twcol.com>`

```
"Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:

>I regulary use this method on hundreds of thousands of records with
>significant decease in CPU usage when compared to internal sorts. I have
>developed this over several years in the work place. Granted there may be
>better ways of doing this but I am not familiar with them.
>

There are many other measures than CPU usage. In fact,by itself, it's
very misleading. You need to look at a number of measures. But
regardless of your claim above, there is no way that passing the data
four times with COBOL I/O routines feeding an external sort will match
the efficiency of one pass of the data with an internal sort unless
the number of records is minimal.

>
>I did examine error messages, but without data files and JCL error messages
>don't get you very far.
>
I did and other people did find enough to make suggestions...and I
wasn't referring to JCL error messages. I was referring to SORT error
messages which were included. 

>
>The first 2 suggestions were based on DEBUGGING METHODOLOGY within the
>information that was given. I have never tried to rewrite a program in this
>manner. 

Not a good one from any viewpoint I've ever seen.

>But the program they wrote and most of the programs I have ever
>worked on are very different. They obviously have alot of time to waste and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is a need for external sort at times. But the reaons for those
are primarily based on limited resources (as previously mentioned). I
will tontinue to disagree with you that this is an appropriate
generalized way to develop code in the late ninties.  

>I may have overemphasised it as the EASIEST, maybe I should have said
>EASIEST time and time agin for me to program a report generating program.
>
That's a different issue. Lots of times reports are better generated
in a different language. But that doesn't force the use of an external
sort. To me, this sounds like someone who found a technique that works
and just uses it over and over regardless of applicability. 

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The first suggestions I would have made have already been suggested.by
others. Fix the logic errors and then see what happens. Their
fundamental concept is not in error. 

Second, the point of what I wrote was not adddressed to their problems
but to the problems I see in your proposed solution which I regard as
even more incorrect. Display statements are one thing, but the rest,
no way. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UD452.48$NF5.240662@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<364dd7d1.5327538@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <0jK32.4101$FY5.10858514@storm.twcol.com> <364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net> <8C242.4342$FY5.11548957@storm.twcol.com> <365e79f7.24391983@news3.ibm.net> <OMM42.283$QZ5.168345@storm.twcol.com> <3654a32f.3836977@news3.ibm.net>`

```

>That's a different issue. Lots of times reports are better generated
>in a different language. But that doesn't force the use of an external
>sort. To me, this sounds like someone who found a technique that works
>and just uses it over and over regardless of applicability.


Once again you are stretching what I said into one more ridicule. Saying a
process works for me time and again never implies that I use it "over and
over regardless of applicability. Any descent programmer finds things which
work and uses them. To be successful you have to know when to use what
technique. You have stretched my general technique into a strict policy
which I would never recommend. No set process works for every situation in
programming. All situations are slightly different. A good programmer knows
when to stop making generalizations and look at the details and figure out
what is the best way given a particular situation, regardles of what is his
or her favorite way of doing things.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT-RETURN not referenced

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981116145338.04161.00001517@ng155.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364f843b.4703513@news3.ibm.net>`

```

Okay, Class. 
Class.
Class!!!!

Now it is agreed, we will use advanced COBOL, EZtrieve as well as DYL280, and
naturally there will be an external sort with full exploitation of Exit Fifteen
and Exit Thrity-Five on your final exam. But now ...

Now a pop quiz!

We have an external sorts, that's right, sort_s plural, jammed between two
pairs of COBOL programs.  SORT#1 must transform a fixed length input file to a
variable length output file, and SORT#2 has to transform a variable length
input file to a fixed length output file.

Got it?! Good!!!!  Now for extra credit why is one more advanced than the
other?

Worst Wishes, 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

---

## Thread: getting at the beginning of a file ! Please help — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### getting at the beginning of a file ! Please help

- **From:** CRAPPIE@mail.dma.be
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c9462.3035537@news.ping.be>`

```
I'm having a little problem with my cobol program. I have CA-Visual
realia cobol, under windows 98
I have to read one file multiple times, but each time, i have to start
over at the beginning. I though the record position would reset using
a simple close statement, but when i try to read my file the second
time, cobol doesn't get any input. Can someone tell me how to get the
record positioner back in the beginning of the file  ?

Thanks

Sparkie
CRAPPIE@mail.dma.be
```

```
> realia cobol, under windows 98
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
<365091D8.FCCD299B@IMN.nl>...

>

Sparkie is a student learning COBOL - my offer to help yielded a complete
-nearly running properly program, along with the test data files and his
programs actual output.  This is homework, but it was a GREAT attempt - not
a "please do my homework" for me.  That's me vouching for Sparkie if any
future questions arise.

Now the problem actually was a simple logic error.  An EOF switch was used
that was not reset between executions of the open/read/close loop.
```

#### ↳ Re: getting at the beginning of a file ! Please help

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)

```
>a simple close statement, but when i try to read my file the second
>time, cobol doesn't get any input.
```

<a id="t-f76898762b"></a>
## Thread: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also. — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c5719.85016226@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net> <364b3a23.12066640@news.freeserve.net> <364BA66A.9F0C3CE7@att.net>`

```
On 13 Nov 1998 03:23:59 GMT, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:
>
>Not true - we may have a platform issue here. I was UNSTRINGing an MVS
>DSN in the format:

Well of course. Donald originally posted about PC filenames and
presumed DOS naming conventions ie. 8.3. or at least only having one
period in there.

Other PC OS's allow more than one period and longer filenames and
extensions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72hng9$q2d$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net> <364b3a23.12066640@news.freeserve.net> <364BA66A.9F0C3CE7@att.net> <364c5719.85016226@news.freeserve.net>`

```

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <364c5719.85016226@news.freeserve.net>...

>Well of course. Donald originally posted about PC filenames and
>presumed DOS naming conventions ie. 8.3. or at least only having one
>period in there.


No, I did not.  I posted about the UNSTRING being used more frequently
on PC's than on mainframes, then gave a very simple example of UNSTRING
usage.  If you want to get into command-line parsing, then there have
been dozens of books written on the subject.

UNSTRING is by far the simplest (and most powerfull) Cobol verb
for attacking such problems ... the point of the post.  If you are going
to write a program that parses thousands of lines out of a file, there
may well be a more efficient way of doing it.  However, for the amount
of time involved in coding, for something as simple and infrequently
used as a command line in, then UNSTRING is by far the most
simple ... the point of my second post.

Now if you want to get into every type of command line that can
be analysed, then that is a completely different topic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c711c.91675272@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1eYkZVRMQGVF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36444656.4A73@netbox.com> <36477e29.4966572@news-server> <36464210.4D58@bellsouth.net> <36464CFC.3FECF140@att.net> <726nan$2d0$1@news.igs.net> <364b3a23.12066640@news.freeserve.net> <364BA66A.9F0C3CE7@att.net> <364c5719.85016226@news.freeserve.net> <72hng9$q2d$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Fri, 13 Nov 1998 11:44:00 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <364c5719.85016226@news.freeserve.net>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>been dozens of books written on the subject.

You wrote...

"You can tell a mainframer from a PCer<G>.  Without
"UNSTRING COMMAND-LINE-IN" I don't think I could
write a program!  It is also by far the easiest way to deal
with comma-delimited files ... a mainstay of PC life."

and then...

UNSTRING FILESPEC DELIMITED BY ":"
    INTO
    DRIVE-NAME FILENAME.
UNSTRING FILENAME DELIMITED BY "."
    INTO FILENAME FILE-EXTENSION.

Nothing about frequency of use on the PC. The example being very PC
and indeed DOS specific.

btw, there are also Runtime calls to split and join filenames in most
PC compilers I know which make that task easier.

>
>UNSTRING is by far the simplest (and most powerfull) Cobol verb
>for attacking such problems ... the point of the post.

Not in my experience or preference.

>  If you are going
>to write a program that parses thousands of lines out of a file, there
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>simple ... the point of my second post.

Not in my experience. It's too simplistic as it relies on you knowing
how many parameters a command line will have or having slack space. It
also makes having command line parameters in any order or switches
conjoined dificult to process.

Pity we don't have argc / argv type syntax in COBOL to be honest.

>
>Now if you want to get into every type of command line that can
>be analysed, then that is a completely different topic.

ok. but you have to be aware of the limitations of UNSTRING as a verb
for command line parsing which you seem to be Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some advice - COBOL beginners - read also.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72icnf$mho$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <364c711c.91675272@news.freeserve.net>...

>Not in my experience. It's too simplistic as it relies on you knowing
>how many parameters a command line will have or having slack space. It
>also makes having command line parameters in any order or switches
>conjoined dificult to process.


Actually, all those things can be handled by unstring.
```

<a id="t-ac96a06754"></a>
## Thread: Date Windowing problem — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Date Windowing problem

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Came across an interesting date windowing problem recently.

A client was using date windowing in their software as a quick fix for
Y2K. So they'd changed their software to say dates before '93' were
treated as 2093 and after were 1994 which was when their software
first went in. They tested everything, worked perfectly and so shipped
it.

Then users in Saudi Arabia started complaining. It's 1491 currently in
Saudi!

So, be careful with your date windowing routines people.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981113161402.00292.00001137@ng124.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Shaun writes ... 

>Came across an interesting date windowing problem recently.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>So, be careful with your date windowing routines people.

Interesting. But this is not just a windowing problem. All the literature says
that the final Y2K remediation is to convert to dates with four digit years.
But 1491 is a four digit year.

How does DB2 work with this?

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364CA54F.70F5E03A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net> <19981113161402.00292.00001137@ng124.aol.com>`

```
> >Then users in Saudi Arabia started complaining. It's 1491 currently in
> >Saudi!
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> But 1491 is a four digit year.

Except the problem would be the same moving from 1899 to 1900 as long as
the old dates are two characters long.  The only way this is different
is that a three digit fix won't work at millenium changes.  But it would
work at other century changes!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72liit$rgo$2@newsreader2.core.theplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net> <19981113161402.00292.00001137@ng124.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981113161402.00292.00001137@ng124.aol.com>, S Comstock says...
>
>
>Interesting. But this is not just a windowing problem. All the literature says
>that the final Y2K remediation is to convert to dates with four digit years.
>But 1491 is a four digit year.

In this case they used windowing because of an embedded system. Using 4 digits 
meant new hardware.

>
>How does DB2 work with this?

No idea. System didn't use DB2.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364CE346.D03A7439@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Assuming the system date format is correct, using a sliding window
method based on the system date handles these type of situations. 

"Shaun C. Murray" wrote:
> 
> Came across an interesting date windowing problem recently.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> scm@enterprise.net        PGP Key available
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364D0C28.E85658A1@att.net>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net>`

```
"Shaun C. Murray" wrote:
> 
> Came across an interesting date windowing problem recently.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Saudi!

And they're clearly many years from having to deal with the year 2000. I
guess the first question concerning dates is "Are we discussing the
Christian/Western calendar?".

So, did they pick a different pivot year, or another consultant?

Bill Lynch :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Windowing problem

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lha7$f70$10@newsreader4.core.theplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<364c5a4a.85833281@news.freeserve.net> <364D0C28.E85658A1@att.net>`

```
In article <364D0C28.E85658A1@att.net>, William Lynch says...
>
>"Shaun C. Murray" wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>And they're clearly many years from having to deal with the year 2000.

Well yes, but 1500 is coming up ;-)

> I
>guess the first question concerning dates is "Are we discussing the
>Christian/Western calendar?".
>
>So, did they pick a different pivot year, or another consultant?

It was a tricky one. The software on the PC was easy enough. Problem was the 
video edit controller it was attached to. Eprom in that went in there in 93 
(hence the 93 pivot date) when the first embedded version was done. They have 
to replace eproms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe COBOL decompile

- **From:** jeff@jakfield.xu-netx.com (Jeff York)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3652d8ad.270925520@news.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<364BA2EC.B0CD6567@worldnet.att.net> <PVa32.2185$EC2.3007101@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <364EB861.39EB70F2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net wrote:

>Writing a new program is of course one option. However, since with the
>exception of one or two of the specific functions, it appears to be
>compliant, we were checking other options. 

I would have thought that having systems reliant on a piece of code
that no longer has an extant source is lunacy in the extreme.
Rewriting is surely the only safe option.
```

<a id="t-62886d119f"></a>
## Thread: DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts

- **From:** Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>`

```
We are using DFSORT Release 13 for Y2K changes. Support for both the
external and internal sorts is required. We are having a problem with
programs contains more than one internal sort. We have been unable to
make this work with out program changes.

Our site has overridden the name DFSPARM with a site specific name. 

For a single internal cobol sort, no program code changes have been
required. For programs with more than one internal sort, we have found a
work around which requires program code changes related to sort control.
With out these changes, DFSORT fails to pick up the override sort parms
from the JCL stream. 

The DFSORT write up we have describes the procedure to use for multiple
internal sorts. We have not had success with the method.

Is anyone doing DFSORT 13 Y2K multiple internal sorts for two digit year
values without programn changes? If so, any assistance would be
appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72hk2j$944$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>,
  Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net wrote:
> ...
>
> The DFSORT write up we have describes the procedure to use for multiple
> internal sorts. We have not had success with the method.
>

Are you using the latest write up on the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmycbl.htm

or the equivalent write up in a hardcopy version of my SORT2000 paper?

Are you substituting the name you use for DFSPARM wherever the write up says
DFSPARM?

Are you using R14, or R13 with PTFs UN90139 and UQ05520?

If so, then please send me offline a description of what you're doing and what
result you're getting so I can help (send to fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com).

As far as I know the write up is correct and the methods it describes do work,
but if something is wrong, I'd like to investigate it and fix it.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL
=> DFSORT/MVS is on the WWW at
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72hl8o$acp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>,
  Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>
> The DFSORT write up we have describes the procedure to use for multiple
> internal sorts. We have not had success with the method.
>

I reread your post and it seems to me that the most likely problem is that
you are using DFSORT R13, but do NOT have PTF UQ05520 installed.  UQ05520 is
needed for the FREE=CLOSE support that the described procedure for multiple
internal sorts depends on.  With the FREE=CLOSE support, DFSORT will use the
first set of DFSPARM statements for the first internal sort, the second set
of DFSPARM statements for the second internal sort, and so on. (Without the
FREE=CLOSE support, the first set of DFSPARM statements will be used for
every internal sort.)

So please make sure that you have PTF UQ05520 installed.  If that's not the
problem, please send me more details offline.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL
=> DFSORT/MVS is on the WWW at
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364DB190.8960C148@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net wrote:

> The DFSORT write up we have describes the procedure to use for
> multiple
> internal sorts. We have not had success with the method.

I assume you're referring to the "DFSORT writeup" in my SORT2000
document.  You can find the latest version at URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmycbl.htm

It describes a method using DFSPARM with FREE=CLOSE.  As far as I know,
this method does work (I've tested it).  However, there are a couple of
caveats stated in the SORT2000 document:

1) JES3 does not allow the use of duplicate DFSPARM DD statements if the
DD statements request JES3 or jointly-managed devices.  So if you have
JES3, you may not be able to use this method.
2) If you're using DFSORT R13, PTF UQ05520 is needed for the FREE=CLOSE
support that the described procedure for multiple internal sorts depends
on.  With the FREE=CLOSE support, DFSORT will use the first set of
DFSPARM statements for the first internal sort, the second set
of DFSPARM statements for the second internal sort, and so on. (Without
the FREE=CLOSE support, the first set of DFSPARM statements will be used
for every internal sort.)  So please make sure you have PTF UQ05520
installed if you're using DFSORT R13.  (DFSORT R14 has this support in
the base.)
3) If your installation is using a different name for DFSPARM, you must
use that name instead of DFSPARM wherever DFSPARM is mentioned.

If your problem isn't caused by any of the above, then please send me a
note offline (fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com) describing exactly what you're doing
and the results you're getting and I'll try to help.

(I tried to post a response via DejaNews, but I don't see it on the
actual newsgroup, so I'm posting directly.  Sorry, if I end up with
redundant posts.)

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL
:-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT/MVS is on the World Wide Web at URL:
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DFSORT Y2K options for Internal Sorts

- **From:** Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364EB908.A1A104DB@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<364BA1AD.1A8ECC53@worldnet.att.net> <364DB190.8960C148@ibm.net>`

```
Thanks for your assistance. I will be checking with our sort software
specialist Monday regarding your comments. Will let you know the
results. 



Frank Yaeger wrote:
> 
> Steve.D.h@worldnet.att.net wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>      http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

### OBJECT STUFF

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<72hmfo$phu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I am floundering in attempting to get a handle on the
object oriented stuff.  I cannot even get a simple
example to work the way that I expect it.  Maybe
someone here can help me out.

What I would like to do, as a simple learning problem,
is invoke a DLL object from a simple Cobol program. To
create the object DLL, I am using PowerCobol 4.  I have,
in a DLL (at least it looks like I do) a very simple object
which is a form called "MAIN", with at least two methods.
One is called "OpenSheet", which should open the form
and one called "Deactivate" which should close the form.

In the main program, written in Cobol97, I have one statement.

    INVOKE MAIN "OpenSheet".

If I declare MAIN to be an object reference like so:

01 MAIN        USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE.

Then the program compiles and links, but fails execution
on a null pointer error.  I presume that I need main to also
be declared as a class, etc., but do not know how to do it.

What do I try next?
;<(
```

## Thread: MF Cobol and Longfilenames — 3 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1998-10](1998-10.md#t-c221a33ada)._

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol and Longfilenames

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eiI2ug0D#GA.78@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<3639d008.69617294@news.mci2000.com>`

```
Doug,
     We had a similar problem in that we have our users enter a "temporary"
directory for working files.  If there are no spaces (or as MF likes to say
"the path is not spacey"), it works fine but when we get a space in there it
doesn't like it.  What we did was take the path they enter, put a quote (")
on the front and back and we were able to use the directory without any
problems.  Try that and see if it works.

bill


dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote in message
<3639d008.69617294@news.mci2000.com>...
>I've got an application written in MF Cobol 4.0.32 that is using
>InstallShield 5.1 Professional for the installation package.  I've
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Doug
```

- **Message-ID:** `<Odf34h0D#GA.142@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<3639d008.69617294@news.mci2000.com>`

```
Doug,
      We had a similar problem in our application and we found that by
putting a double-quote (") on the front and back of the path, we were able
to use it without any problems.  Try that and see if it works.

bill

<3639d008.69617294@news.mci2000.com>...
```

<a id="t-daa75827e6"></a>
## Thread: Dialog 3.0.01 Question — 2 msgs

### Dialog 3.0.01 Question

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#RitLp0D#GA.142@upnetnews05>`

```
I am using Dialog 3.0.01 along with NetExpress 2.0.  I need to show two
windows and have the two entry fields on the first window enabled (they are
the only objects on that window) and have all of the objects on the second
window disabled.  If I use DISABLE-OBJECT WIN2 it will disable all of the
objects on WIN2 with the exception of the pages on a tab control and that is
where my problem comes in.  Since the pages won't disable, the user can
click on them with the mouse and the LOST-FOCUS event is triggered in my
entry field on the first window.  I've tried disabling the tab control, the
individual pages, as well as the entire window and nothing seems to work.
Is there a way to disable the pages?  Right now I have it coded to not show
the second window until the user finishes with the first window, but it
makes the screen look crappy when I do that.  If anyone can give me a
suggestion, I'd appreciate it.

bill
```

#### ↳ Re: Dialog 3.0.01 Question

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be0f8e$cc9f09a0$99bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<#RitLp0D#GA.142@upnetnews05>`

```


Bill Wood <beavis27@email.msn.com> wrote in article
<#RitLp0D#GA.142@upnetnews05>...
> I am using Dialog 3.0.01 along with NetExpress 2.0.  I need to show two
> windows and have the two entry fields on the first window enabled (they
are
> the only objects on that window) and have all of the objects on the
second
> window disabled.  If I use DISABLE-OBJECT WIN2 it will disable all of the
> objects on WIN2 with the exception of the pages on a tab control and that
is
> where my problem comes in.  Since the pages won't disable, the user can
> click on them with the mouse and the LOST-FOCUS event is triggered in my
> entry field on the first window.  I've tried disabling the tab control,
the
> individual pages, as well as the entire window and nothing seems to work.
> Is there a way to disable the pages?  Right now I have it coded to not
show
> the second window until the user finishes with the first window, but it
> makes the screen look crappy when I do that.  If anyone can give me a
> suggestion, I'd appreciate it.

Are you sold on Dialog?  There are alternatives  - that work so much better
- and still support the tabs you are using - e-mail me if you want to hear
more about it.

Now, to help you - maybe - Each "Page" with a tab inside the window has an
object handle, or name.  Use the Disable-Object for each of them.  My
dialog may be getting rusty - we had another way to handle this that
involved making the FIRST window a Modal dialog box - but that opens a
whole different can of worms.
```

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

- **From:** peter.derichs@rocketmail.com (Peter)
- **Date:** 1998-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b6171ed82253c7989682@news.wxs.nl>`

```
I'm planning the conversion of several large applications with databases 
from MF COBOL 3.2 to Fujitsu COBOL V4. To avoid have to logon twice I 
want to use the network login name as login name to the applications 
where database rights are given.
So now I need to get the network-logon-name in my programs with as little 
delay as possible.
I tried the Windows-registry, but it isn't conclusive: If the user 
doesn't logon, the registry still comes up with the information of the 
previous logon. It takes a (relative) long time to get the information 
from the registry. The 'Powerreg' solution -supplied by Fujitsu- takes 
even longer.

Has anyone an idea??????


Peter Derichs.
```

<a id="t-13cdde6c43"></a>
## Thread: Open Letter to Mr. Woodward.... — 21 msgs

### Open Letter to Mr. Woodward....

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b7767d2d1d382b9896a2@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Greetings Mr. Woodward,

   It seems that you and several of your colleges are still 
interested in establishing a new news group for the discussion
of Acucorp's Acucobol product. Since you did not want to
respond to all of the post that's were made concerning the initial
RFD, comp.lang.cobol.acucobol, I thought I would post an open
message and you can respond to this or email me if you whish. In
addtion if anyone else has any thoughts on this subject I would like
to hear them.

    Mr. Woodward, I think that the idea of a group for Acucobol is
not an entirely a bad idea and in fact it has some merit. Even though
I did not fully agree with the RFD's rational I think the real problem
was in he name of the news group, comp.lang.cobol.acucobol. It is 
some what vague and it needs to be more descriptive of what the group
is for. So I suggest that the name should be the following:
comp.lang.cobol.acucobol.usage. 

     By adding the term usage it clearly defines that the news group
is for the discussion of the usage of Acucobol. It eliminates the 
possible confusion that this might be another general Cobol discussion
group. IMO, this might reduce the negative responses to the initial
idea and the level of support just might increase.

     This is a potential win/win situation. You get a group to discuss
Acucobol and the group comp.lang.cobol is not affected by the 
establishment of a new news group.

I look forward to hearing any and all thoughts on this subject.
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Letter to Mr. Woodward....

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364dbe11.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10b7767d2d1d382b9896a2@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff Farkas wrote in message ...
>Greetings Mr. Woodward,
>    Mr. Woodward, I think that the idea of a group for Acucobol is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>comp.lang.cobol.acucobol.usage.


As AcuCobol is almost a platform onto itself, may I suggest to put it
with the other platforms in comp.sys.AcuCobol if a NG is the way to
go. IMHO, it would be better for AcuCobol simply to have a BBS-style
entry on their main website as everybody else has.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Letter to Mr. Woodward....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10bde7c2b8da35949896ac@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.10b7767d2d1d382b9896a2@news.earthlink.net> <72u4h1$k4t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <uG1DkozE#GA.247@upnetnews03> <01be1355$725d5620$5901df80@enterprise> <efP#Ck2E#GA.307@upnetnews03>`

```
In article <efP#Ck2E#GA.307@upnetnews03>, dennis_minette@email.msn.com 
says...
> What is "the big 8", some jargon for usenet groupies?<g>
> 

Well, it refers to the non alt prefixed news groups, comp etc.
The rules for setting up a "big 8" is a lot more complex than
establishing an alt news group. Check out the following:
http://angelfire.com/tx/calame/createnewsgroups.html.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Letter to Mr. Woodward....

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<731a87$73n$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It strikes me that if people just want to do something,
it does not matter much.  A quick scan of the newsgroups
will find at least 5000 of them that have had no traffic
except spam for at least a year.  Just move into one.

They could just rename them channel000000 thru
channel999999, let people use them at will,
and the system would run a lot more smoothly.

'Course I am an anarchist at heart ...


Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>How about ... alt.schindlers.list?  So the name of any new newsgroup DOES
>matter, and should not unnecessarily detract from or fragment any existing
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

In article <01be15ac$4171fc60$9d4f6ccb@xxxx>, 
scottw@southpacfinance.com.au says...
> Would anyone have any objections if I re-submitted the Acucobol RFD with a
> new name of
> 
> comp.sys.acucobol
> 
For what it is worth, I like this one Scott. I think it would be
a good idea to resubmit the RFD. The only change I would suggest
in the RFD is to stress the differences that exist between Cobol
and Acucobol. By doing this it would help justify the creation of
a news group because it is significantly different from Cobol and
its users require a forum to discuss the use of the product.

> Here are some other thoughts as well
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

<a id="t-8a5d811253"></a>
## Thread: OT?: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? — 51 msgs

### OT?: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

- **From:** 100.162651@germanynet.de (Eckhard Prinz)
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de>`

```
How to convert a number (year) to Latin numerals:
 1999 = MCMXCIX is ok 
- but is 
 1999 = MIM false ?


Is there any fixed rule for converting or is it
(as I think) possible to use different expressions?

Where to find the rule - which Newsgroup?

BTW:
A COBOL-solution is given by Leif Svalgaard in CERTPK 
(from the download area at ETK http://www.etk.com/).
 
Thanks in advance.

eckhard
___
```

#### ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72kdt7$lu5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de>`

```

Eckhard Prinz <100.162651@germanynet.de> wrote in message
<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de>...
>How to convert a number (year) to Latin numerals:


I think the last roman standards group died out a few
years back, but you might try emailing the Vatican ...
;<)
```

#### ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de>`

```
It seems to me there is (was?) a rule that lower roman numeral value, in
this case I, can only be used to decrement the next higher roman numeral
value, in this case X, so the roman numerals MCMXCIX would be correct for
the arabic numbers 1999.

BTW, according to another thread, it is now MCDXCI in Saudi Arabia (no Y2K
problem over there?)

Eckhard Prinz <100.162651@germanynet.de> wrote in message
<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de>...
>How to convert a number (year) to Latin numerals:
> 1999 = MCMXCIX is ok
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>___
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** as999@torfree.net (Adrian Boldan)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net>`

```
: Richard Anderson wrote:
: > 
: > >BTW, according to another thread, it is now MCDXCI in Saudi Arabia (no Y2K
: > >problem over there?)

The same in Israel. According to the Jewish calendar, we just started 
year 5759.

: > They don't use Arabic numerals in Arabia?  Rats!!

Actually, they do... but we don't :-) The digits 0, 1, 2, 3 4 as we see 
them on the American display are not the real Arabic digits. And not only 
this, but we are also using the "wrong" order - left to right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3650e93b.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net> <eZJ6ZgcE#GA.215@upnetnews03>`

```
Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>>Dennis J. Minette wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Do you know of a reason for capping the maximum Roman numeral at 4999?
With
>respect to the global economy, it is a VERY good thing that the Arabic
>numeral system came along when it did.
>Is Arabic 4999 then = Roman MMMMCMXCIX ?


The Roman numeral system does not allow 5 like symbols in a row,
when that happens a new symbol is used, e.g.
5 = V not IIIII
50=L not XXXXX
500=D not CCCC
but there is no symbol for 5000.
Some people say that the maximum number is 3999,
because not even 4 symbols are allowed, thus
4=IV not IIII
40=XL not XXXX
etc,
but there is some doubt here because on every watch
with Roman numerals, 4 is indeed IIII.
The rule is a bit murky here, but certainly 5 symbols in a row
is NOT permitted, because there is a special symbol for 5, 50, 500.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AD05E787A8C70CEE.4D930A30B6816D4C.675D18E435B32B0A@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net> <eZJ6ZgcE#GA.215@upnetnews03> <3650e93b.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Dennis J. Minette wrote in message ...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> is NOT permitted, because there is a special symbol for 5, 50, 500.


Lief,

The "Roman" four on watch faces is a jewelry tradition and not a Roman
numeral.  Most clocks and watches will have "IIII" on the face only when
the numerals at the bottom of the dial are in the upside down position. 
Faces with Roman numerals right side up at the bottom of the dial are
usually shown as "IV".

Our watchmaker (my company is in the jewelry business) told me this
story.  According to tradition, a clockmaker had completed a clock for
his king but improperly fashioned the Roman numeral 4 as IIII.  He went
before the king to beg forgiveness and asked to be given the opportunity
to fix it before the rest of the kingdom viewed his mistake.  The king,
however, was so taken with the clock that he suggested the rest of the
clocks in his kingdom be made to look the same.  Hence the IIII remains
on clocks and watches.
```
