###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981118140603.22086.00000967@ng17.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<72uhrt$gb5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It will be in Rome. 
On  MM / I / I
at XXII;_ _ GMT
The _ _ has to be considered a provisional encoding for zero - zero, since the
standards committee has not set the ciphers for that yet, or even agreed to
permit zero at all. Perhaps we could use apostrophes to indicate position
holders, XXII;''.

And many apologies in advance for my presumptuous use of all this white-space
between words, which surely would have seemed very needless to the originators
of the roman numeral system. For it is true that in addition to abstaining from
the ZED, they also had no BLANK in the earliest, and most august of times.
Seems that real readers don't need blanks.

See ya' thar',



Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72vfvb$35e$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<72uhrt$gb5$1@news.igs.net> <19981118140603.22086.00000967@ng17.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981118140603.22086.00000967@ng17.aol.com>...
>It will be in Rome.
>On  MM / I / I
>at XXII;_ _ GMT
>The _ _ has to be considered a provisional encoding for zero - zero, since
the
>standards committee has not set the ciphers for that yet, or even agreed to
>permit zero at all. Perhaps we could use apostrophes to indicate position
>holders, XXII;''.


Now that we have 16 bit coding, don't you think it early to
introduce something as profoundly unsettling as a zero?
The users will never understand it in a M years. We have
close to 20 unused symbols yet;  I think that with judicious
allocation of those, plus some simple syntactical rules, we
should be able to handle up to 64 bits *without* place
holders.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Hf42.434$956.664329@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>
>No it is not. The largest number that can be written with
>Roman numerals is 4999.

Not quite. :-)  Here's a handy chart.  The overstrike letters
are just not used very often, because they present character set
problems.  I used underscore characters on the line above to do
it for this chart, so everybody should be able to print it okay.

                      ROMAN NUMERALS
                                                      __
   1  I                   50  L                9,000  VX
                                                      _
   2  II                  60  LX              10,000  X
                                                      __
   3  III                 70  LXX             20,000  XX
                                                      ___
   4  IV                  80  LXXX            30,000  XXX
                                                      __
   5  V                   90  XC              40,000  XL
                                                      _
   6  VI                 100  C               50,000  L
                                                      __
   7  VII                200  CC              60,000  LX
                                                      ___
   8  VIII               300  CCC             70,000  LXX
                                                      ____
   9  IX                 400  CD              80,000  LXXX
                                                      __
  10  X                  500  D               90,000  XC
                                                      _
  11  XI                 600  DC             100,000  C
                                                      __
  12  XII                700  DCC            200,000  CC
                                                      ___
  13  XIII               800  DCCC           300,000  CCC
                                                      __
  14  XIV                900  CM             400,000  CD
                                                      _
  15  XV               1,000  M              500,000  D
                                                      __
  16  XVI              2,000  MM             600,000  DC
                                                      ___
  17  XVII             3,000  MMM            700,000  DCC
                               _                      ____
  18  XVIII            4,000  MV             800,000  DCCC
                              _                       __
  19  XIX              5,000  V              900,000  CM
                              _                       _
  20  XX               6,000  VM           1,000,000  M
                              _
  30  XXX              7,000  VMM
                              _
  40  XL               8,000  VMMM
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> several choices on how to write some large numbers?

Some of the forms are new, but yes, the ancient romans didn't 
always write the same number in the same way (illiteracy may
have played a part there). BTW, the ancients also wrote 5000
as VM (literally "five" "thousand" I guess) or its equivalent
(I'm not sure they actually used those characters at the time), 
and there was a similar expression for five million.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36519ad1.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net> <9Hf42.434$956.664329@news3.mia> <36519849.D2DA5851@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36519849.D2DA5851@home.com>...
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>several choices on how to write some large numbers?

I have not yet seen the chart (news servers work in funny ways).
The Romans did have a way of cheating their number system:
if you overlined (made a line above the symbols) any Roman numeral
you multiplied it by 1000, so in principle you could write numbers as
large as needed by using enough lines. The rule of not allowing
5 or more like symbols in a row still stands.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net> <9Hf42.434$956.664329@news3.mia> <36519849.D2DA5851@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>What are the rules for deciding some of the numbers where we have
>alternative possibilities?  Are these rules new, did Julius Caesar have
>several choices on how to write some large numbers?

There aren't a lot of ancient Romans around anymore to ask. :)  But I do
know that originally the 'smaller preceding larger indicates subtract'
rule did not exist.  You just tacked them together, larger to smaller,
using up to four of each letter.  Some older clock faces still have that
form, making me think the new 'subtract' rule is pretty recent, say 19th
century.  Except for the 'subtract' rule, writing Roman numerals is not
ambiguous.  You always use the largest value letter which will serve,
and use no more than three of any letter.

I have read that rules for long division of Roman numerals exist.  The
very thought of such makes me shudder.  Ugh!  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72svh9$f7a$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364d875d.4296947@news-la.rhein-neckar.de> <#DxsW8BE#GA.284@upnetnews03> <72l2dl$2a6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <364E2D64.FAA0B70A@att.net> <F2JBn4.6D8.0.bloor@torfree.net> <#X#hcLbE#GA.257@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net> <9Hf42.434$956.664329@news3.mia> <36519849.D2DA5851@home.com> <I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>I have read that rules for long division of Roman numerals exist.  The
>very thought of such makes me shudder.  Ugh!  :)


Have you ever learnt to use an abacus?  It is very interesting
for a computer person.  Essentially, they use two separate number
systems, one a count by 1 up to 6 or 11, and then a base five system
that can go up to 15. It is sort of hard to explain without an abacus
in front of you, but you do an add pass, then a clear carry pass where
a 6 would carry to a one and a five, while multiple fives become 1's
one over.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>one over.

Yes I have, a cousin sent me one from Japan when I was a boy.  In Japan
businessmen still use them.  For adding and subtracting, which is mostly
what business math is, the abacus is as fast or faster than a calculator.
Many of the souvenir type abacus' you find in the US are not constructed
correctly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia>`

```
Please correct this if someone is confident that it is wrong, but I believe
that the device of muliplying a symbol by one-thousand by means of an
over-score did  not apply to I. So that to list thousands, the basic technique
was to use M.

Also we impose a bit of our modern Western culture on this discussion, when we
discuss "... the rule ...".  It did not work that way.  There were no standards
committees. 

Instead there were competing traditions and during brief periods schools that
influenced practice. Roman enumeration is best understood as convention, not
rule.  I think that four I's (as in IIII) was not really uncommon, especially
in isolation as the value four. 

In combinations, in the position that we would think of the last digit (the
units digit), the IV was rather more consistent over time, perhaps.

But the reason for this would not necessarily be obvious to moderns. For the
truth is, the device of placing the smaller number symbol in front of the
larger number symbol is not subtraction. It is a counting down.

Just as we are currently counting down to the Y2K millenium event threshold,
the ancients would count up for a while, and then begin to count down to the
next group, or to the next start point.

By the time that a zero basis decimal numeric system took hold in Europe nearly
all evidence of the primitive counting culture had been obliterated. But the
earlier Roman numerals (and word specified calendaring of numeric day names) do
preserve the more primitive systems, though ever so vaguely. These folks,
however, did not have standards as we do.

It may interest readers of this thread to note that some authorities claim that
Greek Phi was originally used for one-thousand. And that the Roman D, used for
five-hundred was merely one-half of a Phi. And, yes, that is controversal.

So if you are feeling in a mood to decipher a true puzzle, then try this

I=1
V=5
X=10
L=50
C=100
D=500
M=1,000
(possibly ancient Phi = 1,000)

So, ... why? And relate your answer to when and where it all started. Be
prepared to do a lot of digging.

And for extra credit, if you learned any English literature, then it is
possible that Shakespeare preserved for you the lexical element "The Ides". 
What was "The Ides," why in gods name is it not fixed (what is the reason?),
What was The Calends, and the Nones? What is the derivation of those words? Did
we count down to those thresholds or count up? 

Counting is not arithmetic? Arithmetic, pardon the phrase, 'is advanced';
counting is primitive. Roman Numerals reflect something that is astonishingly
primitive. 

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kuV42.1487$4I1.1874295@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <3651B163.768102CF@home.com> <3652417D.AA1808DD@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
>
>PS: On my earlier post re: the "Nones". When the Romans counted they
>included the starting point in the count, e.g., Sunday to Sunday was
>8 days.

The Hebrews did this as well, dating from much earlier.  Don't want to
get too far afield, but I think this is interesting.  There is in the
Bible in the book of Daniel a prophecy called the '70 Week Prophecy'.
Among other things, it tells the time when the Messiah would appear.
I had read that it was exact to the day.  Always skeptical, I wrote a
program to verify that for myself.  The prophecy was that there would
be 173,880 days (69 'weeks of years', or 483 years of 360 days, Daniel
9:25) from the day the order would be given to rebuild Jerusalem 'until
the Messiah the Prince'.  The program calculated the exact number of
days from the date Darius gave that order until Jesus presented Himself
publicly at His 'Triumphal Entry' into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday before
He was crucified on Good Friday.  But the program kept giving an answer
that was one day too few.  That puzzled me until I remembered that the
Hebrews counted both the first and last days as you describe, instead
of the difference as we do today, and as the program had been doing.
That made it dead on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981122034152.09594.00000930@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3652417D.AA1808DD@att.net>`

```
William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
Date: 18 Nov 1998 03:39:19 GMT

posted a self-review per the 8/9
>>
PS: On my earlier post re: the "Nones". When the Romans counted they
included the starting point in the count, e.g., Sunday to Sunday was 8
days. Would we consider the "Nones" to be 8 days before the "Ides"? I
suspect so, but don't know.
<<

The Nones was always Eight days before the Ides. That is, _we_ would call it
the 'eighth' before the Ides; we would also call the day before the ides the
'first' day before the Ides.

The difficulty here is that we moderns are being zero based in our ordinal
names.  The Ides being the zeroith, the day before the 1st day back, etc. Until
we get to the Eighth day back which we know they called the Nones.

The ancients who evolved these practices did more than not use a zero cipher. 
They had no zero concept. Counting is utterly corporeal. Zero is cerebral. It
is said by some scholars that the ancient actually distrusted the idea of
nothing.

At any rate, when counting with reference to the Ides, the Ides was the first. 
The day before was the eve, and the next day back was the third as the ancients
named it (which to us would be the second back). And so it is that the eighth
day back, was refered to as the Ninth.

Throughout the centuries of trial and error with the calendar, the relationship
between the Ides and the Nones remained constant.

The day before the Calends, the day before the Nones and the Day before the
Ides, were refered to as the Eve of that date.  And in each case the day prior
to each of these was the third.

As they experimented with the length of certain months either to meet the
demands of celestial mechanics or political expediency, the insertion
(deletion) point for new days was thus just past the Ides; that is, the middle
of the month. This, perhaps, should be accounted as the first random access
system.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Left brain, right brain

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36596F8D.E81B7397@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3652417D.AA1808DD@att.net> <19981122034152.09594.00000930@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
It
> is said by some scholars that the ancient actually distrusted the idea of
> nothing.

I heard there is a new book out written by a scholar who noticed lots of
Greek temples which used to be for goddesses and had been converted to
temples of gods.  After studying it a while, he noticed that they
changed over shortly after literacy became common.  His conclusion was
that literacy required more use of the part of the brain which is
natural for men to use, and men gained political power when literacy
became important and useful.  And when men could compete in politics,
they took control of temples, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365240CD.FF080B10@att.net>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> And for extra credit, if you learned any English literature, then it is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> we count down to those thresholds or count up?

The Ides is the 15th of March, May, July, & October; otherwise, the 13th
of the month (Roman calendar, of course). The nones is the 9th day
before the Ides. The Calends, of course, is simple, it's the first of
the month.

I suspect "Calends" and "calendar" derive from the same root (maybe
"calends" is the root of "calendar"?). "Nones" derives from the latin
word for "nine" (it might even *be* the Latin word for "nine", memory
fails). I've no idea where "Ides" came from:-(

As to *why* the Ides is not fixed, why should it be different, e.g., "30
days hath September, April, June and November; all the rest have 31,
except February, which has 28 (plus some "leap year" nonsense)? While
we're on the topic, why is leap year called a "leap" year?

For those of us veterans (of paying taxes), how was the phrase "Beware
the Ides of March" related to filing Federal income taxes?

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ui9c$gln$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net>...

>As to *why* the Ides is not fixed, why should it be different, e.g., "30
>days hath September, April, June and November; all the rest have 31,
>except February, which has 28 (plus some "leap year" nonsense)? While
>we're on the topic, why is leap year called a "leap" year?


I think we should revise it all.  A new calendar.  I propose
a thirteen month year, with 28 days per month.  Each month
will start on a Monday, so that the days of the week always
fall on the same numbers.

New Year's day will be a separate day, called "New Years
Day".  It will not be a Monday, Tuesday, etc., but simply new
years day, and a holiday. The days will go Sunday, New
years day, Monday of the new year.

Leap years will be handled by having a Leap Year's Day when
required.  It will also be a holiday, outside the "days of the week".

Whatcha think?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3652DF90.44322D27@home.com>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net> <72ui9c$gln$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>As to *why* the Ides is not fixed, why should it be different, e.g., "30
>days hath September, April, June and November; all the rest have 31,
>except February, which has 28 (plus some "leap year" nonsense)? While
>we're on the topic, why is leap year called a "leap" year?

At one time it 30 and 31 days alternated, and the following months made
sense:
7 = september
8 = october
9 = november
10= december

Then Julius Caesar inserted month July before September.
Augustus Caesar followed his lead by inserting August, and of course he
wanted a 31 day month, so he stole a day from February.  And the months
named 7-10 are now the 9th-12th months.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** RandallBart <Barticus@att.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36535BC4.CAB992F@att.spam.net>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net> <72ui9c$gln$1@news.igs.net> <3652DF90.44322D27@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
 
> At one time it 30 and 31 days alternated, and the following months made
> sense:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> wanted a 31 day month, so he stole a day from February. 

False.  The Julian Calendar always had two 31 day months in the summer. 
The seasons are deliberately of differing lengths because the earth is
closest to sun in the winter.  The month names were out of whack long
before Caesar, when January and Febuary were added.

Also, MCMXCIX doesn't = MIM because IM is not a valid digit in Roman
numerals.  Though each digit is represented by zero to four characters,
never does one character or group of characters represent multpiple
decimal digits.

And I'm not sure but I think 4000 is properly represented as 
__
IV, but MMMM might be an acceptable alternate.   
 _
MV is highly unlikely. The nice thing about the overscore notation is that
even when you go into double overscores for millions (which the Romans
did), it's usually pretty obvious what the overscore applies to.  Where
there are two or more consecutive zero digits, they would leave extra
space, eg 
_
VI would be read as 6000, so 5001 would look like this:
_
V I
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 = MCMXCIX = MIM ? (Chart)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36542CD0.F35A3699@home.com>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net> <72ui9c$gln$1@news.igs.net> <3652DF90.44322D27@home.com> <36535BC4.CAB992F@att.spam.net>`

```
> False.  The Julian Calendar always had two 31 day months in the summer.
> The seasons are deliberately of differing lengths because the earth is
> closest to sun in the winter. 

I don't see any relationship between that reason and what it is supposed
to explain, unless the calendars were designed around the equinoxes.

(But I will accept the possibility that there are many published, but
wrong explanations on how the month names changed.  Just because a
newspaper or a magazine publishes something does not make it right.  My
explanations have been published several times by others).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Seasons

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** RandallBart <Barticus@att.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3654A55F.EEA8EA0C@att.spam.net>`
- **References:** `<I1h42.449$956.703569@news3.mia> <19981117121023.05873.00003235@ng134.aol.com> <365240CD.FF080B10@att.net> <72ui9c$gln$1@news.igs.net> <3652DF90.44322D27@home.com> <36535BC4.CAB992F@att.spam.net> <36542CD0.F35A3699@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > False.  The Julian Calendar always had two 31 day months in the summer.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to explain, unless the calendars were designed around the equinoxes.

TATAIUI:

The Julian Calendar was originally designed with Winter Solstice on
January 1, Spring Equinox on April 1, Summer Solstice on July 1, and
Autumnal Equinox on October 1.  To make this work as close as they could
according to their astronomical data, they made Winter 90 (or 91) days,
Spring 91 days, Summer 92 days, and Fall 92 days.   

But before this was implemented, one of Caesar's advisors observed that
many people held celebrations on the solstices and equinoxes, and they
kept celebrating for several days after.  The new calendar was for civil
not religious use, and they wanted to avoid having first of the month
business collide with these celebrations.  So the new calendar was set
seven days behind the actual seasons, which was actually eight days behind
the actual seasons, because it was based on 2nd and 3rd Century BC
observations.  By Pope Gregory's time the calendar had further slipped by
13 days, but the Greorian calendar was corrected by only 10 days to allign
it to some 4th Century AD event.

The story of Augustus changing August to 31 days is spurius, dating from
the 14th century.  He did correct the erroneous implementation of leap
years.
```

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3650afd2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
...snip...
>No it is not. The largest number that can be written with
>Roman numerals is 4999.
>
Actually, this isn't true because a Roman numeral written with an overscore
multiples it by 1000 (except for 'I' since 'overscore I' = 'M').  For larger
numbers, I can't say, but I wouldn't be surprised if there was a double
overscore to multiply the base numeral by 1000000.

-CB
```

<a id="t-6ab88b48b7"></a>
## Thread: Realia Cobol Version 3 Conversion to What? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Realia Cobol Version 3 Conversion to What?

- **From:** "Beth Thomas" <beth_t@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72lk33$d3k$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
Long ago I wrote an accounting & inventory system (circa 1985).  It has been
running without a problem.  I have been advising the business owner that I
wrote it for since 1995 that Year 2K was a problem and he needed to find
another product/package.  He has been unable to find anything that comes
close to what he has now.

Long story short.  I have agreed to change his system for Y2K.  I want to
convert it to a Cobol Compiler that is currently supported so that if I run
into problems I can get help.  I also used Screenio as the screen handler.
With extensive ISAM file use.

What Compiler will offer me the easiest transition?   Is Realia (CA-Cobol) a
viable solution these days?  It has been 7 years since I did any serious
programming or followed the companies developing compilers so I am really
out of touch.

Please help.  Your comments and experiences will be appreciated.

Scott
```

#### ↳ Re: Realia Cobol Version 3 Conversion to What?

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be1055$8c6a64c0$96bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<72lk33$d3k$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```


Beth Thomas <beth_t@mindspring.com> wrote in article
<72lk33$d3k$1@camel29.mindspring.com>...
> Long ago I wrote an accounting & inventory system (circa 1985).  It has
been
> running without a problem.  I have been advising the business owner that
I
> wrote it for since 1995 that Year 2K was a problem and he needed to find
> another product/package.  He has been unable to find anything that comes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> convert it to a Cobol Compiler that is currently supported so that if I
run
> into problems I can get help.  I also used Screenio as the screen
handler.
> With extensive ISAM file use.
> 
> What Compiler will offer me the easiest transition?   Is Realia
(CA-Cobol) a
> viable solution these days?  It has been 7 years since I did any serious
> programming or followed the companies developing compilers so I am really
> out of touch.
> 
> 

Not sure if Realia is still selling COBOL + - their version 5.0 product. 
There are significant issues in linking for a conversion.

Let me rank your potential solutions.

1.  Current Realia and Screenio - even If CA-Realia does not have a
screenio version, you can get it from Norcom.  This is the easiest solution
with the lowest learning curve.  (BTW- I have used Realia since Verison
1.0, and I still have applications in production that use Screenio).

2.  If COBOL + is not available - you may have to move to Windows, and the
32 bit version of CA-Realia - their Workbench product - which is the
currently supported version - but it does not make DOS based executables.

3.  Another solution - if you wish to move to Windows at the same time -
Convert to the COBOL compiler of your choice - I recommend Fujitsu - and
use COBOL sp2 from Flexus as a SCREENIO replacement
(http://www.flexus.com).  I have converted several of my systems that were
written using Screenio to COBOL sp2 - with relative ease.

If you have specific questions or concerns about any of these approaches -
please feel free to e-mail me!
```

#### ↳ Re: Realia Cobol Version 3 Conversion to What?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732gn4$bi9@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<72lk33$d3k$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
Realia is definitely still a player (via CA-Realia) in the PC COBOL market
(and still supports screen I/O).  HOWEVER, the last time that I saw
something, it seemed to indicate that they were only marketing their product
for mainframe "off-loading" and not for production PC  application
development.  I know there are many who have (and continue) to use it for
production PC use, but I suggest that you contact them (CA) for their
current position on this topic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10b917ea9fd037f5989684@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1>`

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

In article <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1>, wileypj@hotmail.com 
says...
> what if you have been assigned the task of taking COBOL output and
> converting it to HTML ?

Nope, no one has asked me to try that little trick yet. Thank God!
> 
> Sounds strange but it is true. Even worse we barely know HTML.

No, it does not sound strange. As a matter of fact, I was talking with
a coworker I joked that they would want to put the reports out on the 
Web. He just stared at me and didn't laugh, management had thought of 
that already!

> 
> At home I use VisualPage on the MAC and Fetch to do the  file transfer.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
You will need to be a Jedi to get that to work with NT.<g>
> JEDI
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I am sure there are going to be a *few* nasty little bits in the process 
but it does not sound all that bad. First get yourself a good book. Right 
now I am using HTML 4.0 Sourcebook by Ian S. Graham. It covers the topics 
fairly well and the writing style seems to be the best of the bunch.

Then get a Javascript book. I would recommend JavaScript Bible by Danny 
Goodman, 3rd edition. This is one the better books on the topic.

As for news group help, comp.lang.javascript is a good source for 
information if you have to use Javascript. And there is 
comp.infosystems.www.authoring.html for some help using HTML.

And you have my email address. If you have any questions I would be happy 
to try and answer a few.

Good Luck,
Jeff
JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IQq42.3$lt2.10464@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1> <MPG.10b917ea9fd037f5989684@news.earthlink.net> <3650354D.B0FFAA6A@home.com> <3651e056.2684560@news.freeserve.net> <3651E3B0.E2D30502@home.com>`

```

(Shameless self-promotion)

The COBOL and the Web chapter (chapter 29) in COBOL Unleashed has several
examples of CGI scripting using Micro Focus COBOL 4.0.32 and file-mapped I/O
with STDIN and STDOUT. I think they are pretty easy to follow, but then I'm
pretty biased.

PS: They're filed in the wrong directory (chapter 30) on the CD-ROM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** oberoi@ibm.net (Bal Oberoi)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3650586e.3827779@news.110.net>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1> <MPG.10b977cf7fd3fecf9896aa@news.earthlink.net>`

```
On Sun, 15 Nov 1998 23:44:57 -0500, JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff
Farkas) wrote:

>> what if you have been assigned the task of taking COBOL output and
>> converting it to HTML ?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I guess there is probably a way in NT.

The simple text output of the COBOL is easy enough to display as a web
page. And unless one wanted to add stuff like web page Title, etc. one
can do without any HTML.

One  issue you'll have to address is the question of printer-specfic
control characters that the COBOL program is generating (assuming the
program's output is presently going to the printer). In such a case,
I'd begin by first directing the output (on the host side)  to a disk
file, using JCL. Next run a utility against the output to convert the
control characters  (you may want to ensure that each text line with a
'0' in column 1 is followed by one line of spaces in the report, a
text line with '-' in col 1 is followed by two lines of spaces, etc.).


FTP the converted file to the web server and save it there simply as a
.txt file. None of the principal browsers have any difficulty in
displaying a .txt file. 

Someone on the web server side will have to write up a menu page (in
HTML) that lists report files available for viewing. The URL
associated with each menu item will then point to the appropriate .txt
file residing on the web server.

Hope this is of some help.

Bal
-----
oberoi@ibm.net
http://www.geocities.com/~oberoi/mainfrme.html
-----
"The universe is full of magical things, 
patiently waiting for our wits to grow sharper." - Eden Phillpotts
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

- **From:** "Wayne L. Beavers" <wayneb@mail.beyond-software.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365059FD.BF17BE7B@mail.beyond-software.com>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1>`

```


Pamela Wiley wrote:
> 
> what if you have been assigned the task of taking COBOL output and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Check to see what tools come with your web server. We sell a web
server (EnterpriseWeb/MVS) that can take the output from a COBOL
program and use a COBOL Copy Book to generate an HTML table. You
still need to generate the HTML for the static fields and pictures
using some other tool or just edit the HTML. If you have ever
coded documentation using the Document Composition Facility (DCF)
then you can easily do HTML.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3651dfa2.2504290@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1>`

```
On Sun, 15 Nov 1998 11:03:00 -0800, wileypj@hotmail.com ("Pamela
Wiley") wrote:

>what if you have been assigned the task of taking COBOL output and
>converting it to HTML ?

Extremely easy.

open the file
write out a simple TITLE, BODY etc
write out <PRE>
write out your output
write out </PRE> and the finishing /BODY etc commands and close the
file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol pgmrs using visual tools for web pages ?????

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365086D8.AAA1A021@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1>`

```
If you simply wish to publish a report on the web, just do it in text. 
Am I over simplifying the situation?

There is a basic set of HTML commands that will get you going.  I had to
create the output for the operational instructions for our system.  If
you need to do this use something like composer and front page to mock
up an output then look at the text produced.  Pick up a copy of the
standard off W3C (I think).  This is what I read and it was not too
difficult.

To do a basic report look at tables, it will keep things in columns for
you without you having to balance it for many screen types.

Ken

Pamela Wiley wrote:
> 
> what if you have been assigned the task of taking COBOL output and
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.supernews.com/ - Host to the the World's Discussions & Usenet
```

- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3651c6cb.1851466@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01be104b$c5e89be0$b0bc48a6@default> <364E12DC.1E6A@netbox.com> <MPG.10b7ef1ec49aa9859896a5@news.earthlink.net> <gOF32.12246$C5.7450661@WReNphoon1> <365086D8.AAA1A021@zip.com.au>`

```
wrote:

>If you simply wish to publish a report on the web, just do it in text. 
>Am I over simplifying the situation?

Assuming you already have a http server, and that you don't want any
links to each of the reports, then you are right on the bullseye. But
minis may not have a server, and may want links to different report
types, or want it generated on the fly.
```

```
waste time on a blind ad.
```

## Thread: CGI vs Traditional "Green Screen" data entry program — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### CGI vs Traditional "Green Screen" data entry program

- **From:** Patrick.Archibald@HomeTelco.Com
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72k9vv$gue$1@supernews.com>`

```
Hi

I just finished moving a simple COBOL data entry program 
on an AS/400 to a CGI COBOL program also on an AS/400.  I 
have logged some observations at 
http://hometelco.com/pla/COBOLCGIcomparison.html
if anyone would like to read them.  I've included
the COBOL source too.

Thanx, Patrick 

------== This message was posted by EasyUsenet's Web Based Newsreader ==-----
-----== http://www.easyusenet.com/ ==-----
```

#### ↳ Re: CGI vs Traditional "Green Screen" data entry program

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36508f23$0$12775@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<72k9vv$gue$1@supernews.com>`

```
Synkronix (www.synkronix.com) has a COBOL compiler for the AS400 that will
enable ANSI-85 standard COBOL to run as an applet or application without
modification.  It also supports an EXEC-HTML section for embedding HTML
directly in COBOL code.  Check it out!

David
```

## Thread: US-MN $60K-$70K Project Leader, MVS Mainframe/COBOL/Conversion — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### US-MN $60K-$70K Project Leader, MVS Mainframe/COBOL/Conversion

- **From:** Julie <julieo@nycor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364DCB15.933C29C1@nycor.com>`

```
US-MN $60K-$70K  Project Leader, MVS Mainframe/COBOL/Conversion


Our client offers one of the best benefits packages we have ever seen.
It not only
includes healthcare, dental, life, 401K, bonuses, three weeks vacation,
etc., but it also
 includes perks, like in-house workout room and in-house daycare center.

The job they are adding to staff, is a mission critical position, that
will work within
the IT part of the Claims department of this insurance company.  This
"managed care"
project is implementing a new managed care strategy for the work comp
line of business.
You will be working on a cross-functional team, and implementing a
robust measurement and
 reporting environment by buiding a managed care loss warehouse.  You
will be heavily
involved in developing new processes which integrate with the existing
vendors.

To qualify, you must have 5+ years experience having designed and coded
COBOL
applications, have acted as a Project Leader for at least 1 year, be
able to interface
with many groups within an organization, handle complex projects.

To apply: send/fax/e-mail your resume with cover letter indicating
current salary
information.

The Nycor Group is a full service search firm, specializing in working
with computer and
engineering professionals.  We look forward to helping you with your
next job endeavor.

Reference #: 5147-1807
Julie O'Connell
The NYCOR Group
4930 West 77th Street, Suite 300
Minneapolis, MN  55435
Phone: (612) 831-6444 ext
FAX: (612) 835-2883
E-Mail: julieo@nycor.com
```

```
arbitrary changes in long-standing, well-understood COBOL definitions and
terminology.  Repertoire vs. set?  yuck!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert from RM to Fujitsu

- **From:** nop06685@mail.telepac.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36532cce.669928@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<hgI32.3524$yE1.10386@server1.news.adelphia.net> <36505f18$0$12761@nntp1.ba.best.com> <3652A2C6.1126C559@infonet.com.py>`

```
On Wed, 18 Nov 1998 07:34:46 -0300, Albert Ratzlaff
<albert@infonet.com.py> wrote:

>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Albert Ratzlaff


Hi

Fujitsu does not have any support for the regular accept and display
as RM/COBOL has.
If you do not use the screen section then you would have to convert
every accept/display to work with it. And I am not sure it would work
well after that.

Best

FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert from RM to Fujitsu

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72uhbn$g1f$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<hgI32.3524$yE1.10386@server1.news.adelphia.net> <36505f18$0$12761@nntp1.ba.best.com> <3652A2C6.1126C559@infonet.com.py>`

```

Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <3652A2C6.1126C559@infonet.com.py>...

>Are you sure about that? I mean, does Fujitsu have the equivalent (or
>any work-around) of all the RM accept and display clauses?


I never had much problem converting screen sections, though
the actual adis module is very weak.  The cursor controls are
badly implemented (read not), and the display has a few quirks
when handling decimals, but it is workable as a stage towards
something like Flexus.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL help

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36504395.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<72nove$4tg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
There is a lot of information on the COBOL language.

Did you want web pages, the NG FAQ, which books are best, class locations?

If you need web pages, try Alta Vista or another search engine, with a
search on COBOL.

If you want the NG FAQ, you'll see it posted here from time to time, or
check out www.dejanews.com, with a search for comp.lang.cobol and FAQ

If you want to know what books are best, also check out www.dejanews.com,
with a search for COBOL, books, and best or recommended.

If you want class locations, you'll have to check out you're local learning
opportunities.
```

<a id="t-f266fdef00"></a>
## Thread: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator — 8 msgs

### Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I've got a handy routine that I use under Unix to read the graphics
adaptor card to capture both test and display attributes (colours)
using "C". Does anyone know of a way to capture these from a terminal
emulator or perhaps know of a terminal emulator that can "dump" this
information to a file ? I use this to "read" existing displays I've
created using MicroFocus Cobol and then put the display and attributes
into my own version of their Screens editor that then generates what
they descibe as generic display attributes which can be used with
"CBL_WRITE_CHATTR". Any help would be most appreciated but please
don't try and sell me "Flexus", thanks.

Nick Lucas
```

#### ↳ Re: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72nebv$hm9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
If you can run them under DOS, then I have a TSR that
might do the job for you ... it traps the screen print key and
dumps to an ASCII file ... I use it for stage one documentation.
It does not capture attributes, though I can give you source
and you could modify it to do that as well.  The routine is not
terribly elegant, you can only stop it by re-booting, and it
is only designed to grab up to 100 screens, but it does work
pretty reliably. (And it will work in a windows95 dos window).

Nick & Carol Lucas-White wrote in message
<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>...
>I've got a handy routine that I use under Unix to read the graphics
>adaptor card to capture both test and display attributes (colours)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nick Lucas
```

#### ↳ Re: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72nfgu$k5g$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Further to that last message.  I just tested the TSR
running in the background while I ran a DOS terminal
emulator in a '95 window (an older version of
ProComm), and it was able to capture the "terminal"
screen quite nicely. ProComm can emulate most of
the common terminals, so it should work quite well
if you have a PC you can run as your terminal.  You
would have to do a bit of modification to get to
write attributes as well, but the code is well written
and easy to read. (My "rules" for assembler specify
a comment for every line, so you have code on the
left and a running commentary on the right).

Nick & Carol Lucas-White wrote in message
<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>...
>I've got a handy routine that I use under Unix to read the graphics
>adaptor card to capture both test and display attributes (colours)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nick Lucas
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **From:** nop06685@mail.telepac.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1998-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364f605e.9871290@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk> <72nfgu$k5g$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>Nick & Carol Lucas-White wrote in message
><364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi.

One terminal emulator that allows you do do that is TUN-EMUL from
www.esker.fr.
You can contact you local distributor of this product and as for a
demo cd (it will run for 15 days after installation) and test it
for you purposes.

best

Frederico Fonseca
```

#### ↳ Re: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **From:** "Thane Hubbell" <redsky@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be10f6$dbeb9040$87bc48a6@default>`
- **References:** `<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>`

```


Nick & Carol Lucas-White <nick@asheath.demon.co.uk> wrote in article
<364f02d4.961210@news.demon.co.uk>...
> I've got a handy routine that I use under Unix to read the graphics
> adaptor card to capture both test and display attributes (colours)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't try and sell me "Flexus", thanks.

MicroFocus has two routines to do what you want CBL_READ_SCR_CHAR and
CBL_READ_SCR_ATTR.  The syntax is documented in the "Library Routines" of
the manual.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Capturing Text And Display Attributes From Terminal Emulator

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
used to read another terminal window.
```

<a id="t-d2dd52608e"></a>
## Thread: Z80 - Dupavoy — 23 msgs

### Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** dupavoy@aol.comNOSPAM (Dupavoy)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com>`

```
I have Z80 Cobol compiler.  It doesnt seem to be working although the progs
which came with it run on other compilers.  Has anyone heard of it?  What is
Z80 anyway?



****************************************************************
Please remove 'NOSPAM' at end of my E-mail address 
and put 'REAL E-MAIL' anywhere in the subject header if 
you want to E-mail me privately.
```

#### ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ut6aNVbE#GA.315@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com>`

```

Dupavoy wrote in message <19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com>...
>I have Z80 Cobol compiler.  It doesnt seem to be working although the progs
>which came with it run on other compilers.  Has anyone heard of it?  What
is
>Z80 anyway?


Z80 is a microprocessor chip made by Zilog that was more or less a clone of
the Intel 8080 chip.  It was often supported by the CP/M operating system
developed by Digital Research.  There were a number of proprietary computers
put together around the Z80 chip, including an add-in board for the Apple
II+ computer that was manufactured by Microsoft.  That was about mid-1980
IIRC, not too long before IBM got Microsoft to put together PCDOS/MSDOS for
the original IBM PC.  One COBOL compiler I used under CP/M was UTAH COBOL
(anyone remember that one? sort of a toy compiler for a toy computer at the
time, but it seemed to follow the COBOL standard pretty well and produced
usable executable code.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** "DENDEN" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<731f9o$b9do$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <ut6aNVbE#GA.315@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`

```
<snip>
One COBOL compiler I used under CP/M was UTAH COBOL
(anyone remember that one? sort of a toy compiler for a toy computer at the
time, but it seemed to follow the COBOL standard pretty well and produced
usable executable code.)
<snip>
At least Utah was an inexpensive toy.  For $59, you could get the compiler,
the companion "Saywhat" screen generator program and a license free run time
module.  And it all even worked! Ah....those were the days ;-).

Denny
```

```
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> created/distributed by a company named Ellis something-or-other.

Ellis Computing, Inc.
3917 Noriega Street
San Francisco, CA 94122

At least that's what it says in UTAH COBOL manual, dated "9-2-85".  I
think they went out of business by 1988.  They also offered Basic,
Pascal, Pilot, and a text editor.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ellis Computing (UTAH COBOL)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Carl Bauman" <cbauman@impulse.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7346dl$s16$1@sienna.impulse.net>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <ut6aNVbE#GA.315@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <731f9o$b9do$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <731luj$7i4$1@clarknet.clark.net> <#gDTugFF#GA.228@upnetnews05> <36552CF6.B97B6C01@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley wrote in message <36552CF6.B97B6C01@worldnet.att.net>...
>Dennis J. Minette wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >>time, but it seemed to follow the COBOL standard pretty well and
produced
>> >>usable executable code.)
>> >><snip>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >Utah COBOL... hmmmm, might that have once been called 'Nevada COBOL' and
...snip...
>> Does Nevada FORTRAN ring any bells??  IIRC, these compilers were
>> created/distributed by a company named Ellis something-or-other.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The 'Nevada' and 'Utah' compilers were Ellis Computing products.  IIRC the
'Nevada' series were for 8080/Z80-type systems and 'Utah' for PC-types (some
under MS/IBM DOS or CP/M-86).  As stated, they were cheap and they worked,
but some were missing some features.  i.e. Utah COBOL 4.0 didn't have
IF/ELSE or exponentiation.  A later version had IF/ELSE, but shortly after
this, they stopped advertising and I assume went out of business.

-CB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The good ole days (Was Nevada/Utah COBOL, Z-80, etc.)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** John <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365DDA6D.43D2CF3D@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <ut6aNVbE#GA.315@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <731f9o$b9do$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <731luj$7i4$1@clarknet.clark.net> <#gDTugFF#GA.228@upnetnews05> <36552CF6.B97B6C01@worldnet.att.net> <7346dl$s16$1@sienna.impulse.net>`

```
I can confirm what Carl says: Nevada COBOL was the 8-bit (8080 and Z-80) product
and UTAH COBOL was the 16-bit (IBM-PC 8088) product.

I spent many an hour with Nevada COBOL and a Morrow MicroDecisions computer in
the late 70s early 80s writing a series of business applications for a small
weekly newspaper I published at the time. I developed a good accounts receivable
system, subscription/mailing list  management system, and contact management
system. Still kicking myself for not marketing them.

My copy of Nevada COBOL cost me $29. (Years later, I coughed up $39 for Utah
COBOL.) As I recall, not only did Nevada not support ELSE, it didn't support
nested IFs, which made for some pretty convoluted code. Still, I was extremely
grateful to have it. Here's why (listen up kids):

I had left a large Honeywell mainframe shop to start my newspaper. At that shop
the compile process involved: writing code on a coding pad, giving it to a
keypunch operator, sending the resulting deck of cards by messenger to the
computer room, waiting for a return printout by return messenger. We got maybe
two turnarounds per day.

What a thrill it was to sit in front of my own little $2000 computer, press a
key, and get a complete compile back inside of ten minutes! It was apparent that
a revolution was underway... and it seemed that it would be a revolution that
would empower the little man. (It was really an time exciting time, kiddo.)

I still have my old computers stashed away, along with a Wordstar manual or two
and a BASIC manual by an obscure company named Microsoft. Most all the 8-bit
computers came with CP/M and were bundled with WordStar and MS BASIC. (But it
seemed obvious to me that COBOL was a superior language for a people's computer,
and that some advanced derivative of it [even more English-like] would
eventually dominate programming on the PC platform.)

Now about WordStar... [but I see this thread is drifting a bit]

Just sign me...

--Old and In the Way

- - - - - - - -- -- ---
Carl Bauman wrote:
Arnold Trembley wrote in message

> >Dennis J. Minette wrote:
> >> docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <731luj$7i4$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> -CB
```

```
Aye, that be the one, matey.  Ellis Computing in Reno, Nevada. Never did
know why it was called Utah Cobol.  It used 4 digit line numbers to save 2
characters of precious memory/storage space.  Not unlike the year 2000
situation ;-).   But we still have a couple of utility programs that run
every day in a DOS Window under Windows 95.  It wasn't exactly up to
standards, but it worked and still does.

Denny
```

#### ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3650C206.9F5C0322@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com>`

```
I had a Radio Shack TRS-80 with a COBOL compiler by, I think Ryan McFarland.
The compiles were slower than a sorghum in January, but what do you expect of a
Z80 purring along at 2 or 3 MHz and four floppy disk drives.  The code ran
fairly well and I did a couple of data entry apps.  Later they had the TRS-80
Model-12 with 8" floppy disks and I did a pretty decent Bill Of Materials app in
COBOL.  The Model-12 had a 1 or 2K keyboard buffer so I would very carefully
type in a few compile commands and turn my attention to something else for
awhile.  The programs ran great, especially if you had a hard disk (which I
didn't -- too many $$$).  Eventually I sold the TRS-80, COBOL and all, to a guy
at a swap meet who needed one piece of software.  I told him he either took all
40 pounds of stuff or nothing at all -- I was determined to go home with nothing
but money.  The Z80 was pretty spiffy for it's time and I think Zilog is still
cranking out CPUs for obscure applications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nVg42.447$956.700029@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <72qne8$5ai$1@news.igs.net> <428B950E8454784B.2232C6BDD8569980.09D3A44CBAAA96D6@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
>
>Donald Tees enlightened us:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wasn't the Z80 really a chip by Motorolla, not an actual computer?

The Z80 was a chip, but made by Zilog, not Motorola.  It was in essence
a souped up 8080 with some extra instructions.  The Z80 was to the 8080
a bit like the Pentium MMX is to the Pentium.  Faster, and a few more
instructions that few programs will ever utilize.  When the Z80 came out
it ran up to 4 MHz, and the fastest 8080s were 2 MHz.  In those days,
that was >>BLAZING>> fast. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be1253$cf150780$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <72qne8$5ai$1@news.igs.net> <428B950E8454784B.2232C6BDD8569980.09D3A44CBAAA96D6@library-proxy.airnews.net> <nVg42.447$956.700029@news3.mia>`

```
Yeah, and it was the last time Intel found itself following, not leading,
a competitor.  But Zilog, like Motorola, did a clean break design when
going from 8 bits to 16 bits; Intel, instead, went for compatibility
(whence
the arcanity of the x86 design).  The short term gains of backward
compatibility
were overwhelming in the marketplace at that time when coupled with the
"blessing" of IBM in the form of the original IBM PC.  The Z8000 was a 
very clean design, as was/is the 680x0 family, but technical merit has
rarely
been the deciding factor in marketplace decisions (remember betaMax?).

Lots of embedded systems people got started with 8080/8085/Z80's  and
Z80's and derivatives are still being used in lots of NEW embedded designs.
The chips are dirt cheap and for people who are already comfortable using
them, and have a stockpile of tools (crossAsms &c) , it can be a very good
decision.  But not for general purpose end-user computers in today's
market.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Z80 - Dupavoy

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72s82c$3dh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981116171642.14766.00002131@ng39.aol.com> <72qne8$5ai$1@news.igs.net> <l3h42.1270$LY1.1013@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote in message ...

>The Z80 microprocessor is still in production.


I figured it might be, those older chips tend to be produced
forever for imbedded applications.  There were a whole slew
of "almost" Z80 chips developed with memory included, EPROM
and mask programmable prom included, ports of various sorts,
etc., etc., etc.

Nowadays, though, I doubt that anyone is putting a Z80 into
a real computer as a CPU, though I would not be surprised if
various controllers are somewhat Z80 based.  I programmed
the Z80 on several occasions for instrumentation, as it was
very popular as a base chip in things like cash registers and
weigh scales. Input of a button array and output of an LED
display is somewhat different than Cobol<G>.

I still have a card in service that is a Z80 based card talking
to a printer port on one side, and turning high voltage lines on
and off via optical decoupling  and heavy duty relays on the
other.  The code reading the printer port is Z80 assembler,
while the code writing it is an 8086 Cobol assembler subroutine.

Damned if I know how I am going to convert it when it wears out,
since that stuff will only work under DOS.
```

<a id="t-ca382230c9"></a>
## Thread: Any solution to this problem ? — 12 msgs

### Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** mbsevns@hotmail.com (Matt)
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>`

```

Hi

Perhaps someone can tell me if the following problem can be solved or not. 

I am usimg MF COBOL 4.1 on HP-UX 10.20.


working storage
===============

01 w-fld-name               pic x(20)    value "w-fld2".

01 w-fld2                   pic x(01)    value "B".

01 w-result                 pic x(01)    value space.

01 w-command                pic x(72)    value space.


I need to achieve the following:

          move w-fld2      to w-result.


by specifying:

          move w-fld-name  to w-result.


Where w-fld-name can be any variable specified in the data division.


Obviously by using the latter move statement "w-result" ends up with a "w" in 
it. I need the "B" from w-fld2 to end up in w-result without having to specify 
"w-fld2". (I hope this is not too confusing)

I have been messing around trying to solve this problem with pointers but not 
much luck.
 
I have also tried:

        string      "move "         delimited by size
                    w-fld-name      delimited by size
                    " "             delimited by size
                    "to w-result."  delimited by size
               into w-command
        end-string.

and I was hoping to find a way of "compiling" and executing the contents in 
w-command on the fly at run time as one is able to do in the MF animator.

Another solution might be to call a C program and pass it the necessary 
pointers to memory. If this is a possible solution, perhaps someone could 
sketch out the C program for me.

One may ask why would do I need this. Well basically and briefly, I have want 
to set up a data file that contains the names of fields in other files as well 
as some other information. I then want to read through this data file and 
process each field name that I come accross. In order for this to succeed, I 
need to solve the above problem.

Any help will be greatly appreciated.


Thanks in advance

Mathew

P.S. Remove the letters 'ns' from my e-mail address to reply direct.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<euQgTcXE#GA.146@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>`

```
Trying to put together some kind of report writer? You will most likely need
a pointer (not part of the COBOL standard yet) to the location of the data
in w-fld2 to accomplish your goal.

Matt wrote in message <72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>...
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <thomass@logica.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be117e$8a792820$0b47ea9e@GDLPPC04.logica.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com> <euQgTcXE#GA.146@upnetnews03>`

```
Actually pointers are supported on the complier Mathew is using. 

My thought would be to do something like this:

working storage section.

01  ws-fld-name			usage pointer.
01  ws-fld2			pic x(01) value "B".
 
linkage section.

01  ws-any-field          pic x(1000). *> size is arbitrary .. it's just a
place holder.

procedure division.

set ws-fld-name to address of ws-fld2.   

*> the when you want to to the move

set address of ws-any-field to ws-fld-name
move ws-any-field to ws-result.

Hope that helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rbf42.1266$LY1.1009@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>`

```
It is not possible to do what you want, directly.

The reason it cannot be done directly is that the value of pointers
can not be known prior to execution of the program; therefore, one
must know the name of the field before one may use a pointer to
reference that field. An EVALUATE could be used to set a pointer
depending upon the name of the various field.

       EVALUATE w-fld-name
         WHEN "w-fld-2"
           SET ws-pointer TO ADDRESS OF w-fld2
etc.

Since the field name must be known to get its address, it would
be easier to replace the SET pointer with the actual move.

There is an interesting means for accomplishing this, indirectly.
Create a skeleton program, compile the program, then call it.
See the source code below.

Matt wrote in message <72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>...
>
>Hi
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>Obviously by using the latter move statement "w-result" ends up with a "w"
in
>it. I need the "B" from w-fld2 to end up in w-result without having to
specify
>"w-fld2". (I hope this is not too confusing)
>
>I have been messing around trying to solve this problem with pointers but
not
>much luck.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>One may ask why would do I need this. Well basically and briefly, I have
want
>to set up a data file that contains the names of fields in other files as
well
>as some other information. I then want to read through this data file and
>process each field name that I come accross. In order for this to succeed,
I
>need to solve the above problem.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>P.S. Remove the letters 'ns' from my e-mail address to reply direct.


The following source code was run on DOS using MF 3.2.50.

------- my-ws-1.cbl -----------
       01 my-ws-1.
         05 ws-01-field-1 pic x(20) value "abcdefg".
         05 ws-01-field-2 pic 9(5) value 1234.

-------- my-ws-2.cbl ----------
       01 my-ws-2.
         05 ws-02-field-1 pic s9(8) comp value 87654.
         05 ws-02-field-2 pic s9(7)v99 comp-3 value 45.35.

-------- my-defs.cbl -----------
       01 ws-table-defs.
         05 ws-td-01.
           10 td-name pic x(30) value "ws-01-field-1".
           10 td-pic pic x(20) value "x(20)".
           10 td-copy pic x(10) value "my-ws-1".
         05 ws-td-02.
           10 td-name pic x(30) value "ws-01-field-2".
           10 td-pic pic x(20) value "z(4)9".
           10 td-copy pic x(10) value "my-ws-1".
         05 ws-td-03.
           10 td-name pic x(30) value "ws-02-field-1".
           10 td-pic pic x(20) value "z(7)9-".
           10 td-copy pic x(10) value "my-ws-2".
         05 ws-td-03.
           10 td-name pic x(30) value "ws-02-field-2".
           10 td-pic pic x(20) value "z(4),zz9.99-".
           10 td-copy pic x(10) value "my-ws-2".
       01 ws-lookup-table redefines ws-table-defs.
         05 ws-td-entry occurs 4.
           10 ws-td-name pic x(30).
           10 ws-td-pic pic x(20).
           10 ws-td-copy pic x(10).

--------- my-skel.cbl -------------
       01 ws-skeleton-program.
         05 pic x(60) value "program-id. temp.".
         05 pic x(60) value "data division.".
         05 pic x(60) value "linkage section.".
         05 s-pic pic x(60) value "01 my-result pic ".
         05 s-copy pic x(60) value "copy ".
         05 pic x(60) value "procedure division using my-result".
         05 s-head pic x(60) value "    ".
         05 s-var pic x(60) value "    move ".
         05 pic x(60) value "      to my-result.".
         05 pic x(60) value "    exit program.".
       01 redefines ws-skeleton-program.
         05 ws-skeleton-line occurs 10 pic x(60).
       01 ws-skeleton-locations.
         05 s-pic-loc pic s9(4) comp value 18.
         05 s-copy-loc pic s9(4) comp value 6.
         05 s-head-loc pic s9(4) comp value 5.
         05 s-var-loc pic s9(4) comp value 10.

----------- view-ws.cbl --------------
       program-id. view-ws.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select temp-program-file assign to "temp.cbl"
               organization is line sequential.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd temp-program-file.
       01 tpf-record.
         05 pic x(7).
         05 tpf-data pic x(60).
       working-storage section.
      * Any copybooks containing data to view.
           copy my-ws-1.
           copy my-ws-2.
      * A table of data-names and display pic clauses
           copy my-defs.
      * A skeleton program to be compiled
           copy my-skel.
      * Local variables to accept the name and display results
       01 my-result pic x(60).
       01 ws-sub pic s9(4) comp.
       01 ws-tpf-sub pic s9(4) comp.
       01 ws-compile-flag pic x.
         88 ws-compile-failed value "f".
         88 ws-compile-successful value "s".
       01 ws-call-flag pic x.
         88 ws-call-failed value "f".
         88 ws-call-successful value "s".
       01 ws-char pic x.
       01 ws-result pic x comp-x.
       01 ws-function pic x comp-x.
       01 ws-parameter.
         05 ws-length pic x comp-x.
         05 ws-command pic x(80).
       procedure division.
           perform varying ws-sub from 1 by 1
             until ws-sub > 4
               perform create-program
               perform call-compiler
               if ws-compile-successful
                   perform call-temp
                   if ws-call-successful
                       display ws-td-name (ws-sub)
                       display my-result
                   else
                       display "call to temp unsucessful"
                   end-if
               else
                   display "compile failed"
               end-if
           end-perform
           accept ws-char
           stop run
           .

       create-program.
           delete file temp-program-file
           move ws-td-pic (ws-sub) to s-pic (s-pic-loc:)
           move "." to s-pic (60:1)
           move ws-td-copy (ws-sub) to s-copy (s-copy-loc:)
           move "." to s-copy (60:1)
           move ws-td-copy (ws-sub) to s-head (s-head-loc:)
           move "." to s-head (60:1)
           move ws-td-name (ws-sub) to s-var (s-var-loc:)
           open output temp-program-file
               perform varying ws-tpf-sub from 1 by 1
                 until ws-tpf-sub > 10
                   move spaces to tpf-record
                   move ws-skeleton-line (ws-tpf-sub)
                           to tpf-data
                   write tpf-record
               end-perform
           close temp-program-file
           .

       call-compiler.
           set ws-compile-successful to true
           display "cobol temp.cbl /omf(gnt); > nul:"
             upon command-line
           move 35 to ws-function
           move 0 to ws-length
           move space to ws-command
           call x"91" using ws-result ws-function ws-parameter
           if ws-result not = 0
               set ws-compile-failed to true
           end-if
           .

       call-temp.
           set ws-call-successful to true
           move spaces to my-result
           evaluate ws-td-copy (ws-sub)
             when "my-ws-1"
               call "temp" using my-result my-ws-1
                 on exception
                   set ws-call-failed to true
               end-call
             when "my-ws-2"
               call "temp" using my-result my-ws-2
                 on exception
                   set ws-call-failed to true
               end-call
             when other
               set ws-call-failed to true
           end-evaluate
           cancel "temp"
           .
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00

```
Matt wrote in message <72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>...
>Perhaps someone can tell me if the following problem can be solved or not.
>I am usimg MF COBOL 4.1 on HP-UX 10.20.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Where w-fld-name can be any variable specified in the data division.


One way would be:
if w-fld-name = "w-fld1"
     move w-fld1 to w-result
else
if w-fld-name = "w-fld2"
     move w-fld2 to w-result
else
if w-fld-name = "w-fld3"
     move w-fld3 to w-result
else
if w-fld-name = "w-fld4"
     move w-fld4 to w-result
else
....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** RandallBart <Barticus@att.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3653F246.441F3E92@att.spam.net>`
- **References:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com> <36533115.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Matt wrote in message <72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>...

> >01 w-fld-name               pic x(20)    value "w-fld2".
> >01 w-fld2                   pic x(01)    value "B".
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> else

Except that he was asking for "any variable".  The answer is that a
feature like you describe is usually not available in a compiled language
like Cobol.  It may be available as part of an interactive debugger, but
not in normal code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any solution to this problem ?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00071ba3@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<72pfc1$kfg$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>`

```
Dear Mat,

m> 01 w-fld-name               pic x(20)    value "w-fld2".
m> 01 w-fld2                   pic x(01)    value "B".
m> 01 w-result                 pic x(01)    value space.
m> 01 w-command                pic x(72)    value space.
m>
m> I need to achieve the following:
m>           move w-fld2      to w-result.
m> by specifying:
m>           move w-fld-name  to w-result.
m>
m> Where w-fld-name can be any variable specified in the data division.
m> Obviously by using the latter move statement "w-result" ends up with a
m> "w" in it. I need the "B" from w-fld2 to end up in w-result without
m> having to specify "w-fld2". (I hope this is not too confusing)

Indeed - it is confusing :-) But it's also very simple:

EVALUATE w-fld-name

   WHEN "w-fld2"
      MOVE w-fld2 TO w-result
   ...

   WHEN OTHER
      DISPLAY "What do you mean - " w-fld-name " ?!?"

END-EVALUATE

Best regards

Sven

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```
