###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36597484.1EBFF6B6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3655f11c.0@news1.ibm.net> <OmB52.1091$Sf1.1090000@news3.mia> <3656ed2c.0@news1.ibm.net> <36579F79.329A@swbell.net>`

```
> > Could I ask you to  post here a good (real) example of the use of
> > GO TO xxxx-EXIT to show us how useful it is?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> bailing out immediately.

First, I am very concerned about maintenance efficiency, hardly
concerned about run efficiency.  My attention is towards the most costly
element.

I don't like having paragraphs with umpteen lines in them.  If there are
logical choices to make, I prefer to do performs instead of rechecking
switches over and over again.  I try to make a paragraph do one thing
only.  Sure it may make the optimizer not work as well, but see my first
paragraph!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6KV52.112$7a.146@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3655f11c.0@news1.ibm.net> <OmB52.1091$Sf1.1090000@news3.mia> <3656ed2c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3656ed2c.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>I think that having *a* style is better than having *no* style. Consistency
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I hope that you understand that GO TO xxxx-EXIT is not separable from
PERFORM paragraph-name THRU paragraph-exit-name. It is the
pairing of these two, along with some rules, that creates the "style."

From the book, Modular Programming in COBOL by Russell M.
Armstrong, 1973, page 77.

" 1. GO TO should refer only to a name within its own paragraph-
group. ...

" 2. GO TO should branch only forward in the program (downward
on the page), and (by rule 1) only to an intermediate paragraph or
to the EXIT paragraph within the paragraph-group. ...

" 3. GO TO should branch forward only to the EXIT paragraph in
its paragraph-group.

" The most desireable degree of GO TO control is achieved by
adhering as closely as possible to rule 3. This is not always
feasible, but because of habit rather than for technical reasons.
GO TO has always provided an easy way out of structural
difficulties, so much so that programmers have been able to
avoid designing their programs. As an individual gains
experience in developing modularity and consciously seeks
to reduce the untamed use of GO TO, this style of programming
will become more natural; it takes practice to use new tools.
The reward will be modules that are more readable, easier
to document, and eventually faster and easier to program and
verify (a term I prefer to the pesticidal "debug").

" The two main tools for achieving COBOL program modularity
thus are: (1) separation of the code into PERFORMed paragraphs,
and (2) preservation of their separateness by the restriction of the
of the use of GO TO."
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
>It sounds like you clearly have the experience to write a book on this
>subject.  Why don't you?  You could include evidence from the various shops
>that have adopted your methodology (for whatever reason) about how defect
>rates go down and code writing rates go up.

I realize you may be being facetious Chris, but I'm already working on a
book.  Two of my clients have asked me to document all this stuff in more
detail than I ever have, and agree to pay me for it.  I hope to turn it
into a book.  The (shall we say cool? :) reception some of my ideas have
received here make me wonder if such a book would be well received by the
general public? ;-)  No sour grapes, I'm serious.  If it is not clear to
you knowledgeable and experienced folks whether this stuff is any good
(though you have only seen small pieces of the whole), what good would a
book do?  I wouldn't be doing it for the money, but to 'pass along what I
have learned', so to speak.  We old folks start thinking like that, you
know. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Scott McKellar <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365A2D2A.3355@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Chris Osborne wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> general public? 

[snip]

So some of us don't like your GO TOs -- big deal.  That doesn't mean
we couldn't take some inspiration from your code generator, or your
code checker, or the thought processes that lead you to the very
conclusions that we may still disagree with.  It's the thinking that
matters, not the petty preferences of style.  I'd take a well-placed
GO TO any day over an ill-considered jumble of formally structured
paragraphs.  I can always eliminate the GO TO if I don't like it, but
it's tougher to fix a bad design.

I wouldn't expect to agree with everything you say.  I don't always
agree with everything I say, either.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365b4685.581029@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia>`

```
On Mon, 23 Nov 1998 20:43:17 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Chris Osborne wrote:
>>It sounds like you clearly have the experience to write a book on this
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>know. :-)

FYI...
before you start cashing your royalty checks, be aware that many
programmers refuse to buy ANY book.  I've been writing for 8 years and
been in contact with some major mainframe (i.e., cobol)publishers and
the books don't sell as you might anticipate. yes, they do sell; yes
it is a nice piece of additional income, but it's amazing how many
programmers refuse to buy any book at all.  i recently coordinated an
effort with a publisher to allow a 45% discount with no tax and no
shipping costs for a team of 100+ programmers ---  only 4 bought
books, for a total of 10 books.   it's not a place to get rich.... but
my best wishes to your success....
david
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365C107E.C384F255@home.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia> <365b4685.581029@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> FYI...
> before you start cashing your royalty checks, be aware that many
> programmers refuse to buy ANY book. 

I wonder.  Lots of shops do not have sufficient documentation, and
programmers need some references.  The non-company book which I see the
most often in MVS shops is SYSTEM/370 JOB CONTROL LANGUAGE.  I think the
trick is to supply a book which is a great reference so that programmers
don't have to search down manuals to convert between EXAMINE, TRANSFORM,
and INSPECT.

The trouble is, what they want most is compiler dependent.  What to look
for when it isn't obvious what the error message is pointing to.
```

general public? ;-)  No sour grapes, I'm serious.  If it is not clear to
you knowledgeable and experienced folks whether this stuff is any good
(though you have only seen small pieces of the whole), what good would a
book do?  I wouldn't be doing it for the money, but to 'pass along what I
have learned', so to speak.  We old folks start thinking like that, you
know. :-)
<<

Knowledgeable, smoledgeable! You have frequently demonstrated a thoroughness
that is all too rare. And very few people are as knowledgeable as you about
COBOL.

You are definitely one of the best writers contributing to this newsgroup. You
have very good ideas and the experience to back up your views. You definitely
should publish and get some reward for your hard earned understandings.

If you do provide your clients with preliminary releases of your book, be sure
to get advice about how to structure any agreements (so that you retain all
rights for further publishing). Protecting your rights is not easy to
understand. Hope you do not enter into casual agreements (or atleast not enter
agreements casually).

You definitely should publish. But don't try to do it all at once. Brush up
what you have. Try to package that much (put a cover on it and a table of
contents).  Pick reasonable goals for yourself, like during the coming holiday
finish one additional important subject (not the whole book).  Keep picking the
next goal in a way that can just be put under the next wrapper (cover plus a
revised table of contents).

You konw a modern CPU is like that.  They send a simple mask to the foundary
and start stamping out chips (call them books). Next they add a few
instructions, and release the next version. Then they add the advanced math or
fast graphics, and out the door goes release 3.  

Think of your early releases as something that can do a lot of good just like
your consulting and personal advising. You are a generator. You are a source.
There is nothing wrong with you making a permanent recordation of your
inspiring style.

Publish.

Find meaningful break points at which to put wraps on it and publish. Then do
it again.

You are one of the good ones. Publish.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

like somebody's Daddy," that meant he probably drove a BIG, OLD car.

ditch the Lincoln.

I can remember the good ol' days, when I could flirt my way out of
getting a ticket -- doesn't work now, since I probably look like
somebody's mama . . .

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8TR62.5339$w_6.3111189@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia> <6Yx62.2430$Gy.2518049@news4.mia> <365b1077.27021075@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:
>
>...AND just to make you feel even better, it is an undisputed fact
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it!)

Sadly, I must admit that I have traded the 3000GT VR-4. :-(  I had no
choice.  It was either that or the state of Alabama was going to pull
my drivers license when I got another speeding ticket.  That car would
do 0-60 in under 5 seconds.  It was like driving a carnival ride!  Just
normally accelerating onto an Interstate, I would look down and be
doing 90-100.  The steering on that car was extremely tight, you twitch
the wheel, the car moves.  It was a driver's car, Motor Trend placed it
in the same performance class as the Ferrari Testarosa.  Driving this
kind of car is a constant rush.  It would go 60-100 in two or three
seconds.  Just passing another car was a kick, and the handling was
indescribable.  It took curves effortlessly at speeds you would never
think of in a regular car, as if it was on rails, and the brakes would
pop your eyes out.  It is impossible (for me anyway) to drive a car like
that without breaking the speed limit.  It's like riding a huge tiger,
straining at the leash, ready to leap at your slightest whim.  In my
younger days, I logged many miles in excess of 100 mph, but never over
about 135.  One night about 2 AM on a long straight stretch of empty
road I decided to see how fast the VR-4 would go.  As the needle climbed
past 155, the yellow lines became a solid blur, and the telephone poles
did indeed look like a picket fence.  I began to think about mortality
and what a person would look like spread over an acre of highway.  I
decided I didn't *really* need to know just how fast it would go.  But
for a while, it was really, really fun. :-)

Cost?  With every option available, $36k new in 1992.  A set tires was
over $1000.  Worth every penny.

For you single guys, women would follow me around in that thing. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365ad5e6.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia>`

```
Judson,

I don't know how that you perceived that I was being facetious.  Honest, I
wasn't.


Personally, I'm over in the 'perform a paragraph without ever using gotos or
sections' group.  However, I am not stuck on that.  I find that discussions
about how structured programming or oop are just tools apply to programming
styles as well.  When you are a journalist, you write one way.    Sometimes,
writing style differs between newspapers, and often is based on an editor's
desires.  I think that programming styles often work the same way.

The number of highly efficient coding styles developed by individual
programmers is probably equal to the number of highly efficient programmers.
Lots.  This goes directly into such questions as shop-level or even
enterprise-level coding and style standards, are they a good or bad thing?
Does style influence system architecture?  Or does style eventually, in the
hands of the experienced, finally equate to automatic architecture (i.e.
your own style automatically defines certain sub-types of programming
architecture)?

I have no good answers, just ones that lead to more questions.  Questions
that bring the desire to 'do things your own way' face to face with 'do
things the same way'.  I'm not about to announce that one way is better than
another.  I'll just have to continue exploring the matter.


Whether I ever adopt your style is a different matter (as you said, your
client/employer often determines what style will be used), but I thought
reading about it would be a) fun and b) give me a look at something beyond
what I'm doing here.  Your data dictionary system sounds fascinating.


If you're already going to put something into print on a limited basis like
this, as long as the legal bits and pieces allow you to, you should propose
a commercial book to one of the major publishers.  The worst they can do is
say no.



Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Chris Osborne wrote:
>>It sounds like you clearly have the experience to write a book on this
>>subject.  Why don't you?  You could include evidence from the various
shops
>>that have adopted your methodology (for whatever reason) about how defect
>>rates go down and code writing rates go up.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365ADA85.755ACE84@home.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia> <365ad5e6.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
> The number of highly efficient coding styles developed by individual
> programmers is probably equal to the number of highly efficient programmers.

I think a primary need though is to define "highly efficient
programmers".

If you can't turn your completed program over to a junior programmer for
easy, trusted maintenance, then you are not a "highly efficient
programmer".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365c3401.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia> <365ad5e6.0@news2.uswest.net> <365ADA85.755ACE84@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <365ADA85.755ACE84@home.com>...
>> The number of highly efficient coding styles developed by individual
>> programmers is probably equal to the number of highly efficient
programmers.
>
>I think a primary need though is to define "highly efficient
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmer".

I will immediately agree with that last statement.


And here are my two cents.

Being "highly efficient" as a programmer combines elements of the following
(different people have more, or less, of these characteristics; these are
not ranked in order):

1-Typing Speed & Quality.
2-Determination/Tenacity.
3-Good organizational skills.
4-Good writing skills (for documentation, not code; avoiding spelling and
basic grammar errors doesn't matter nearly as much as communicating the why
behind the code; the code by itself usually tells you what).
5-Really liking working with computers doesn't hurt.
6-Patience.
7-Problem/Puzzle solving skills.
8-Language knowledge.
9-OS knowledge.


Well, I've written a bunch of characteristics, but I find they don't really
determine what is "highly efficient".  Maybe we could say that a "highly
efficient" programmer is someone who gets the job done to the satisfaction
of the user (or boss), on time, within budget, without ruining their life,
and who leaves behind a system (or portion of a system) that can be admired
by other programmers (this last comes directly from what Howard said in his
post).


All of this is a pretty narrow definition, though.  Other ideas?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vrB62.2699$Sf1.2620922@news3.mia>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
>Judson,
>
>I don't know how that you perceived that I was being facetious.  Honest,
>I wasn't.

I didn't think you were, Chris.  I was just admitting the possibility that
you might be, so I wouldn't look stupid by taking you at face value if you
were not serious. :-)

[snip]
>If you're already going to put something into print on a limited basis like
>this, as long as the legal bits and pieces allow you to, you should propose
>a commercial book to one of the major publishers.  The worst they can do is
>say no.

I have approached a publisher, and they're interested.  Up to now I have
been collecting information and planning my approach.  Parts of it have
been written, though not as a coherent whole.  I'm now ready to begin the
writing in earnest.  Thanks for the encouragement and advice. :-)

If anybody is interested, I may post more ideas occasionally as a 'trial
by fire' to get some feedback. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73hs9r$gjj@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia>`

```
Judson - 
  I think that what you are running into is a paradigm shift. <grin>
I *strongly* suggest working in a OO environment, using Java or 
Smalltalk or another OO language. Even a relatively small project,
such as a Purchase Order system or something. Don't use a hybrid
langauge such as C++ or Basic.

  It will paint a lot of things you already know in a very different
light, and I expect that you will at some point, click over and 
become an OO aherent, with fervor. :) 
 
  It would certianly make a difference in the way you would view things
like PERFORM THRU and functions.

-Paul


Judson McClendon (judmc123@bellsouth.net) wrote:
: Chris Osborne wrote:
: >It sounds like you clearly have the experience to write a book on this
: >subject.  Why don't you?  You could include evidence from the various shops
: >that have adopted your methodology (for whatever reason) about how defect
: >rates go down and code writing rates go up.

: I realize you may be being facetious Chris, but I'm already working on a
: book.  Two of my clients have asked me to document all this stuff in more
: detail than I ever have, and agree to pay me for it.  I hope to turn it
: into a book.  The (shall we say cool? :) reception some of my ideas have
: received here make me wonder if such a book would be well received by the
: general public? ;-)  No sour grapes, I'm serious.  If it is not clear to
: you knowledgeable and experienced folks whether this stuff is any good
: (though you have only seen small pieces of the whole), what good would a
: book do?  I wouldn't be doing it for the money, but to 'pass along what I
: have learned', so to speak.  We old folks start thinking like that, you
: know. :-)
: --
: Judson McClendon      judmc123@bellsouth.net  (remove numbers)
: Sun Valley Systems    http://personal.bhm.bellsouth.net/~judmc
: "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that
: whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

paulr wrote:
>Judson -
>  I think that what you are running into is a paradigm shift. <grin>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>-Paul

Perhaps so, Paul, I'm not opposed to the idea.  Now, if I can just find
the time...  :-)

I'll actually be glad to get rid of PERFORM/THRU.  I only use it at all
because I believe it is the better choice of the options available until
EXIT PARAGRAPH is available.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73iif7$k08@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <lSl52.587$Gy.710084@news4.mia> <3659bf9d.0@news2.uswest.net> <FNj62.2086$w_6.2122497@news2.mia> <73hs9r$gjj@lotho.delphi.com> <gG_62.5499$w_6.3386611@news2.mia>`

```
Probably a better option than perform through is to perform a single
paragraph and have that paragraph perform an subsequent paragraphs, 
returning  to the caller from the orignally called paragraph.

That sounds a lot more confusing than it really is, but it lends
a different mindset; the caller gives up execution control at 
the call, and the when of a return is determined by susequent 
processing. 

That is of course, how 99% of all function calls work, and procedure
calls in langs that support procedure calls. It allows you to better
encapsulate the processing logic, especially from a design standpoint
since you can defer implementation descisions to a much later point
in development.

Yes, I know that sounds bad to old school programmers, but it is actually
a very good way to design and implement. <grin> Come to think of it,
I just ran into that today and had to get rather stubborn.

-Paul

that create rules and live by them.  Good programmers are NOT
created by making rules and enforcing them.

Donald,

I'll second that motion!  Consistency in whatever programming standards that
a programmer adopts is the key to easier maintenance.  Really good
programmers are so consistent in the work that others familiar with their
style can recognize their work instantly when opening up a particular
program for some maintenance work.

Dennis J. Minette
CCCO

The TOC says who did what <G>.  But I BET you could.  However, it's

review some things in TY COBOL in 24 Hours, and he pointed out some
things that I was able to change in the coding style that helped to
eliminate potential problems.  My aim is to teach it "right" the first
time - although "right" is in the mind of the beholder.

download page, but there's nothing there for them presently! :(

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Agree completely. I read something many years ago when I followed motor
racing that the difference between the amateur and the professional is
not who can cut a fast lap, they both can. But the pro can crank out 100
copies in a row. I see the same observation applied to programming. I
also suspect that the bulk of us who are active on this NG really enjoy
programming, which probably outweighs any differences in concepts or
styles.

Bill Lynch

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PARAGRAPH, PERFORM...THRU etc. (was: First COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365971C0.5FA7AC15@home.com>`
- **References:** `<eJ4nMCA$x$U2EwSx@hayford.demon.co.uk> <19981119102634.09602.00000036@ng-cr1.aol.com> <Tpc52.297$Sf1.435979@news3.mia> <3655866a.0@news3.uswest.net> <3655926c.0@news1.ibm.net> <7349e3$aac$1@news.igs.net> <eYIOYlLF#GA.256@upnetnews03>`

```
> I'll second that motion!  Consistency in whatever programming standards that
> a programmer adopts is the key to easier maintenance.  Really good
> programmers are so consistent in the work that others familiar with their
> style can recognize their work instantly when opening up a particular
> program for some maintenance work.

I knew a programmer who wanted to be a lawyer.  He was in love with
indices and tables, and liked flowery, hard to read documentation which
was as hard to understand as his code was.  We could easily recognize
his code and his comments.  And we all dreaded maintaining his code.

Consistency isn't everything!
```

But, even in this example, recognizing that individuals consistent style
instantaneously DID allow you to possibly sluff off the maintenance tasks on
that code onto some rookie programmer, and in the process give him/her some
real-world samples of how NOT to code programs.
```

## Thread: COBOL CGI Forms — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL CGI Forms

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72t0e1$lhf$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```

How do I make the web plug-in for AcuCOBOL's CGI forms available to first
time visitors to my websites?

___________________________
Rob Annandale;   ICQ#  5760258
Programmer
rob_a@unipharm.com

* It is better to live one day as a tiger, than a thousand years as a sheep.
*
- Nepalese proverb.

I do not represent or speak for my employer in anway whilst
participationg in this newsgroup.
All opinions, ideas and suggestions are mine and mine alone.
...unless they offend, then they are not mine.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL CGI Forms

- **From:** Michael Entwistle <mikent@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36523B78.DE6090DF@san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<72t0e1$lhf$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
If you are using the runtime on the server to power a CGI application, users do
not need the plugin.  If your application is such that you are downloading COBOL
objects to the client machine then the users will need the plugin installed on
their machine.  They will have to install the plugin from disc as there is no
plugin download site currently available.

Mike

Robert Annandale wrote:

> How do I make the web plug-in for AcuCOBOL's CGI forms available to first
> time visitors to my websites?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...unless they offend, then they are not mine.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL CGI Forms

- **From:** MJHOPKI@ertltoys.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73fhu4$h0k$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<72t0e1$lhf$1@fountain.mindlink.net> <36523B78.DE6090DF@san.rr.com>`

```
Also at this time the plug-in is not free.  It probably should be to help
promote acucobol web application acceptance.  In the meantime using the CGI
method allows you to produce an HTML only result which your site vistors
should be able to see just fine.  (Unless of course your HTML is coded using
proprietary extensions not supported in all browsers - Be careful).

In article <36523B78.DE6090DF@san.rr.com>,
  Michael Entwistle <mikent@san.rr.com> wrote:
> If you are using the runtime on the server to power a CGI application, users
do
> not need the plugin.  If your application is such that you are downloading
COBOL
> objects to the client machine then the users will need the plugin installed on
> their machine.  They will have to install the plugin from disc as there is no
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-27d9f376ba"></a>
## Thread: Roman Numerals chart to 1,000,000 — 11 msgs

### Roman Numerals chart to 1,000,000

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<4Qf42.436$956.667955@news3.mia>`

```
Here's a handy chart.  The over-bar letters are not used very often,
because they present character set problems.  I used the underscore
character on the line above to do it for this chart, so everybody
should be able to print it okay.

As you can see, the over-bar'ed letters represent 1000 times the value
of the letter without the over-bar.  A moment of thought should tell
you that the Romans had to deal with budgets and other amounts far in
excess of the 5,000 limit normally assumed today for Roman Numerals
without the over-bar.

                      ROMAN NUMERALS
                                                      __
   1  I                   50  L                9,000  VX
                                                      _
   2  II                  60  LX              10,000  X
                                                      __
   3  III                 70  LXX             20,000  XX
                                                      ___
   4  IV                  80  LXXX            30,000  XXX
                                                      __
   5  V                   90  XC              40,000  XL
                                                      _
   6  VI                 100  C               50,000  L
                                                      __
   7  VII                200  CC              60,000  LX
                                                      ___
   8  VIII               300  CCC             70,000  LXX
                                                      ____
   9  IX                 400  CD              80,000  LXXX
                                                      __
  10  X                  500  D               90,000  XC
                                                      _
  11  XI                 600  DC             100,000  C
                                                      __
  12  XII                700  DCC            200,000  CC
                                                      ___
  13  XIII               800  DCCC           300,000  CCC
                                                      __
  14  XIV                900  CM             400,000  CD
                                                      _
  15  XV               1,000  M              500,000  D
                                                      __
  16  XVI              2,000  MM             600,000  DC
                                                      ___
  17  XVII             3,000  MMM            700,000  DCC
                               _                      ____
  18  XVIII            4,000  MV             800,000  DCCC
                              _                       __
  19  XIX              5,000  V              900,000  CM
                              _                       _
  20  XX               6,000  VM           1,000,000  M
                              _
  30  XXX              7,000  VMM
                              _
  40  XL               8,000  VMMM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals chart to 1,000,000

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<3657c6a9.73942286@netnews>`
- **References:** `<4Qf42.436$956.667955@news3.mia> <3654F037.583C@compu_nerd.com> <LPAk7DAx9VV2Ew5$@pattinson.demon.co.uk>`

```
'Twas Fri, 20 Nov 1998 12:24:17 +0000, when Robert E Wightman
<bob@pattinson.demon.co.uk> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>>Judson McClendon wrote:

>>>                       ROMAN NUMERALS
>>>                                                       __
>>>    1  I                   50  L                9,000  VX

This is just a typo, right?  
__
IX is the correct form for 9,000.

>>>                                _                      ____
>>>   18  XVIII            4,000  MV             800,000  DCCC
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>   40  XL               8,000  VMMM

AIUI, the overbar wasn't mixed within a digit position.  Thus 
__
IV is 4,000
__
VI is 6,000
___
VII is 7,000
____
VIII is 8,000.

Not that someone didn't write it the other way somewhere, but these were
the usual forms.  Judson's form for 4000 is awkward.  Imagine the number
14,000.  
___
XIV is easier to write than 
_ _
XMV.  The over bar was written with a single stroke, leaving a gap for MV
would be odd.  In fact, 11,000 was written as 
__
XI rather than
_
XM, though the latter may have been used by some.


>PS did they use a double overbar for 5,000,000?

Yes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals chart to 1,000,000

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<o4U52.1514$Gy.1538965@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<4Qf42.436$956.667955@news3.mia> <3654F037.583C@compu_nerd.com> <LPAk7DAx9VV2Ew5$@pattinson.demon.co.uk> <3657c6a9.73942286@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
>
>>>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>IX is the correct form for 9,000.

                                       _
Yep, that's a typo.  Sorry!  However, MX would have been my choice,
because I prefer not to use two different symbols for the same
value.  Your point about alternating over-barred and plain letters
is a good one, but in practice most large numbers are not going to
be multiples of 1,000.  Those would have some plain letters anyway,
though most would be on the right.
```

#### ↳ Re: Roman Numerals chart to 1,000,000

- **From:** dave@powerbasic.com (Dave Navarro)
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10c39d05c0732e63989959@news.redshift.com>`
- **References:** `<4Qf42.436$956.667955@news3.mia>`

```
This doesn't go as high as your chart, but someone might find it useful:

' Roman routine for PowerBASIC
' by Dave Navarro, Jr. (dave@powerbasic.com)
' Last Revision: September 12, 1993

' Convert a number between 1 and 3999 to a roman numeral.
' Example: CopyRight$ = "(c) " + Roman$(1993)

DEFINT A-Z         'Required for all numeric functions, forces PB to not
                   'include floating point in UNIT (makes it smaller)

FUNCTION Roman$(BYVAL Number) PUBLIC
  LOCAL RomanChar$, RomanCharSub$, I
  STATIC Rv(), Rv1()

  RomanChar$ = "MDCLXVI"
  RomanCharSub$ = "CCXXII?"
  DIM Rv(1:7), Rv1(1:7)
  Rv(1)=1000 :Rv(2)=500 :Rv(3)=100 :Rv(4)=50 :Rv(5)=10 :Rv(6)=5 :Rv(7)=1
  Rv1(1)=900 :Rv1(2)=400 :Rv1(3)=90 :Rv1(4)=40 :Rv1(5)=9 :Rv1(6)=4 :Rv1(7)=1

  IF Number < 4000 THEN
    FOR I = 1 TO 7
      WHILE Number >= Rv(I)
        Temp$ = Temp$ + MID$(RomanChar$,I,1)
        Number = Number - Rv(I)
      WEND
      IF Number >= Rv1(I) THEN
        Temp$ = Temp$ + MID$(RomanCharSub$,I,1)
        Temp$ = Temp$ + MID$(RomanChar$,I,1)
        Number = Number - Rv1(I)
      END IF
    NEXT I
  END IF
  Roman$ = Temp$
END FUNCTION
```

<a id="t-5b4e73090b"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol for Unix — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol for Unix

- **From:** "Administrator" <guest@corvus.ru>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be1235$5575c220$2a020101@stand>`

```
	Dear MF COBOL programmers! Help me please!

I have got Micro Focus COBOL OSX 4.0 for UNIX.
This COBOL compiler (cob.) shows me the next message each time it runs -
cob aaa.cob :

Load error : '/opt/lib/cobol/check'. 
 error code: 173, pc=0, call=-1, seg=0
 173 Called program file not found in drive/directory

What is the reason? 
May be I have got not complete MF COBOL version?

I know that there is Micro Focus COBOL Developer V4.0 for UNIX.
May be this product is neccessary for me?

If it's true, where I can find this product?

	Thanks for any information.
					Aleksandr Arkhipov  
					E-mail: aaa@corvus.ru
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol for Unix

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36559e7c.3507357@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<01be1235$5575c220$2a020101@stand>`

```
You need to set your environment variables.  The ones that come to
mind are COBDIR and COBPATH. 

	COBPATH=.;export COBPATH
	COBDIR=.;export COBDIR

There are other switches you can set, so I would take a look at the
book.

Paul

On 17 Nov 1998 14:14:00 GMT, "Administrator" <guest@corvus.ru> wrote:

>	Dear MF COBOL programmers! Help me please!
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol for Unix

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36554011.C3F841E3@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01be1235$5575c220$2a020101@stand>`

```
Administrator wrote:
> 
>         Dear MF COBOL programmers! Help me please!
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> May be I have got not complete MF COBOL version?

Hi.

The OSX is a runtime-only product. You are trying to invoke the
compiler, which is only a part of the full version. I'm not sure why
'cob' is supplied - probably to allow you to re-link an RTS from
pre-built object files.

> I know that there is Micro Focus COBOL Developer V4.0 for UNIX.
> May be this product is neccessary for me?

Yes, I believe you will need the developer version.
 
> If it's true, where I can find this product?

Sorry, I don't have an exact URL to hand - start at:

http://www.microfocus.com/

Cheers, Kev.
```

<a id="t-e5f6c7f16a"></a>
## Thread: money question — 5 msgs

### money question

- **From:** Jody Bell <whest127@kansas.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3651D93A.5A9DCAFE@kansas.net>`

```
I don't want to be flamed for this~~but here goes.  I learned cobol
quite a while back (86), what I want to know is, would it be worthwhile
career-wise to brush up and try to get an entry level programmers job in
cobol?  I learned it and due to starting a family, I decided not to
pursue a career change at that time (money, ins., etc).  I love
programming and find myself wanting to change now.
Any replies can also be emailed to me.
I am serious about my questions and would like all feedback on this.
whest127@kansas.net
```

#### ↳ Re: money question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72uheo$g1p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3651D93A.5A9DCAFE@kansas.net>`

```
Jody Bell wrote in message <3651D93A.5A9DCAFE@kansas.net>...

There is lots of work if you like it.

>I don't want to be flamed for this~~but here goes.  I learned cobol
>quite a while back (86), what I want to know is, would it be worthwhile
>career-wise to brush up and try to get an entry level programmers job in
>cobol?  I learned it and due to starting a family, I decided not to
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: money question

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981118153258.22086.00000992@ng17.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<72uheo$g1p$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I think that you should wiegh some current market factors very heavily in your
determination.  Things have changed since 1986!

Today, if you have a pulse yor are hired.  You can get a COBOL job with any
verifiable training. You should be a lot smarter than this though. Don't just
get this job, get the next job.

All programmers increase in value during their first year or so, faster than
any organization will increase your pay. You must move to get what you are
worth, come twelve or eighteen months. Make that move create the maximum
benefit for you and your family by getting a job that does more than solidify
your COBOL knowledge.

Get a COBOL job where you will learnd SQL (DB2/Oracle).

Or get a COBOL job where you will learn Java / Html / Perl.

Or get a COBOL job where you will learn CICS or IMS.

Combined skills are worth much more eighteen month down the road. Don't ask
for, instead, demand training.

It may seem completely impossible to you, but this is not 1986 in one very
important sense. As of right now you are in the driver's seat.

FYI, there are COBOL compilers with full tutorial documentation available on
the net for free to help you re-familiarize your self with the syntax.

Demand is very high. You should be able to pick the one that will leave you
with two or more marketable skills in the near future. Development or
maintenance are both okay for beginners. The key is to get more and more
training. Never quit that strategy. 
More and more training leading to more and more training.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: money question

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36525e75.473058850@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3651D93A.5A9DCAFE@kansas.net>`

```
On Tue, 17 Nov 1998 14:14:51 -0600, Jody Bell <whest127@kansas.net>
wrote:

>I don't want to be flamed for this~~but here goes.  I learned cobol
>quite a while back (86), what I want to know is, would it be worthwhile
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am serious about my questions and would like all feedback on this.

I'll probably get drummed out of the programmers union for saying this
- if you are in it for the money, give it up.  Most GOOD programmers
are programmers becasue they love to program, not because of the
money. 

If however, you really love programming (as you state, which is at
odds with your chosen message title), by all means, come and join the
fray!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: money question

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36531d78$0$12780@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<3651D93A.5A9DCAFE@kansas.net> <36525e75.473058850@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I think that this is a legitimate question.   I like Art, and I like to eat,
but I don't think I could do both for a living.  One thing that you can do
is to find out what the average COBOL programmer's salary is.  If that
salary is satisfactory, find out what you need to know to match your
knowledge and experience with the average COBOL programmer.  If you can
afford it, do it.  If not, make it a hobby.  :-)

David

## Thread: 'who am i' from unix — 6 msgs

### 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** "Paulo" <c.m@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Hi !

How can i get the result to the cobol of the command 'who am i' from unix ?
any ideas ?

TIA

Paulo
c.m@mail.telepac.pt
```

#### ↳ Re: 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72svsg$lao$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
I once did this with AcuCOBOL.

CALL "SYSTEM" USING "who am i > /usr/support/whoisuser".
OPEN INPUT WHO-USER.
READ WHO-USER NEXT AT END CONTINUE.

Then I used a character reference and presto!.

Paulo wrote in message <72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>...
>Hi !
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>c.m@mail.telepac.pt
```

#### ↳ Re: 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** Glenn Estes <blevy@epix.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365177AC.BE582E9A@epix.net>`
- **References:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Paulo wrote:
> 
> Hi !
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> c.m@mail.telepac.pt

The only way we ever got it to work was to redirect the output from 'who
am i' to a file.
Then you can define a line-sequential file in your cobol program, read
in your data,
and away you go.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3653F0CF.ACB9A64@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt> <365177AC.BE582E9A@epix.net>`

```
Glenn Estes wrote:
> 
> Paulo wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> and away you go.

If you are using Micro Focus COBOL, you can set up a pipe to do this
using the
DD_ filename mapping mechanism.
Try something line:

SELECT PIPEFILE ASSIGN "PIPE"
    ORGANISATION LINE-SEQUENTIAL
    .

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD PIPEFILE.
01 PIPELINE PIC X(80).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    DISPLAY "DD_PIPE" UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME *> map the file "PIPE"
    DISPLAY "<who am i" UPON ENVIRONMENT-VALUE *> The command to exec
    OPEN INPUT PIPEFILE
    READ PIPEFILE *> only one line expected ...
    CLOSE PIPEFILE
    DISPLAY PIPELINE
    .

Normally, DD_ mapping just maps one filename to another.
However, you can use '<' as a leading character to read the stdout of a
shell command (OPEN INPUT file), '>' to write to the stdin of a shell
command (OPEN OUTPUT file) or '|' to access both stdin and stdout of a
shell command (OPEN IO file). You can't access the stderr unless you
merge it with stdout on the shell command line (using "2>&1").

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** mbsevns@hotmail.com (Matt)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72unbe$n04$1@nnrp01.iafrica.com>`
- **References:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
We have an environment variable set up as:
       
       LOGNAME=`whoami`; export LOGNAME 

automatically when each user logs in. Then we have a program that basically 
does the following:

       display "LOGNAME" upon environment-name.
       accept w-user from environment-value.

Then from any program just:

       call "GETUSER" using w-user.
       cancel "GETUSER".


We Use MF COBOL 4.1, so whether this will work in other compilers I am not 
sure.


Regards
Mathew

In article <72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>, "Paulo" <c.m@mail.telepac.pt> 
wrote:
>Hi !
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: 'who am i' from unix

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3652A420.B4774BC0@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<72rlrr$e4b$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```


Paulo wrote:

> Hi !
>
> How can i get the result to the cobol of the command 'who am i' from unix ?
> any ideas ?
>

What compiler, what version?

In RM/COBOL you can easily add a routine to the runtime interpreter and then
CALL "LOGNAME" USING MY-NAME.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

On Thu, 10 Sep 1998 20:55:05 -0500, "Gene Webb"
<gene.webb@newsguy.com> wrote:

>I have finished porting the classes for my OO tutorial from NetExpress to
>Fujitsu (well at least they compile now) and from that experience I am VERY
>curious to see if anyone is using Fujitsu to do a production OO COBOL

Any suggestions?
Thanks
H. Schepker
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is Anyone Using Fujitsu Object Oriented COBOL to Produce a Production Applications? (0/1)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72snt5$aou$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t9vuc$juv@enews4.newsguy.com> <365217d6.5708628@news.btu.de>`

```

Hajo Schepker wrote in message <365217d6.5708628@news.btu.de>...

I have been banging my head against the wall for a couple hours
a week trying to do something useful with the stuff, but without
much luck.  I am using Fujitsu 4 in production, and a few utilities
written in PowerCobol. Frankly, PowerCobol as it sits scares me
for anything big.  Even being able to dump a project out into the
component Cobol files would make me a lot more comfortable.
In effect, to use it one has to scrap every bit of Cobol in ones
library, and start from scratch.

I have also been trying to use the two to-gether.  I would like to
create tools with PowerCobol that I could use with regular Cobol,
but it appears to be almost impossible ... though it is easy to use
regular cobol as part of PowerCobol.  The fact that PowerCobol
cannot create a subroutine, nor include a copy of anything that
has an object reference has a very crippling effect, though maybe
once I have the object oriented stuff down I will see it differently.

>>I have finished porting the classes for my OO tutorial from NetExpress to
>>Fujitsu (well at least they compile now) and from that experience I am
VERY
>>curious to see if anyone is using Fujitsu to do a production OO COBOL
>>application? If so, I would love to hear your experience with the product.
```

<a id="t-6c121f31d5"></a>
## Thread: Porting Micro Focus COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Porting Micro Focus COBOL from Windows 95 to AIX

- **From:** bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ssvk$isk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We just purchased an AIX box and installed the Micro Focus Developer Suite.
My "mission" is to port COBOL programs developed with Micro Focus COBOL in
Windows 95 to the AIX box.  I have no UNIX experience.	I'm trying to draw
parallels between our Windows 95 run-time system and AIX. 1.) PARM PASSING -
We execute our programs and pass parms into linkage via a Visual Basic
front-end.  I understand you run a program on AIX by typing it's name.	How
do you pass the parm ? 2.) EXTERNAL FILES - We SET our files to the system
via a bat file (that executes before the GUI comes up).  Is there such a
thing as a bat file (with SET commands)on AIX ?  Would it be run the same way
as a program - type it's name ? 3.) PACKAGING - On Windows 95, we linked our
programs into DLLs (with the Visual Basic being the exe which called the
first DLL which, in turn, calls other DLLs).  Would we still create DLLs and
have an "executable" program to call the DLLs ?

Thanks,
Bill Gaphardt
Software Developer
The HARRIS Group

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

---

## Thread: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP — 7 msgs

### Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** coboguy@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72tblt$vhu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I offered to do a Wang VS COBOL to MF COBOL conversion for a friend of mine.
He sent me the copybooks and data files (in ASCII text format) from the Wang.
Here's the problem... They didn't convert the COMP fields to DISP before
transferring that data. They did copy the files from Indexed/Variable to
Sequential/Fixed. Then due to an upper-management brainstorm, they
diconnected the VS and tossed it in the dumpster (well it was only a VS 100).

Now I've got data files that I've been trying to convert to MF files. In my
conversion programs I have a routine that moves the Hex field (formerly COMP)
to an Alpha field that is then interrogated from right to left converting the
bytes into numeric data. The problem is that MF does some strange things with
hex data and I'm only getting a 75% success rate.

Does anyone know of a utility or programming method the remedy this situation?
I would appreciate any input.

Thanks,
Bill K.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5E6F872365A7ADED.7B327052D86CEC32.978965C65D694490@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<72tblt$vhu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Wang COMP is packed decimal, but you also have to be aware that Wang
used an "F" in the sign portion of the packed field for zero or positive
numbers.  (Negative numbers used the conventional "D".)  When we
migrated from the VS to Micro Focus, we had to convert all packed fields
so the signs were either "C" or "D".

Example:
Data = 123.99	Wang COMP = 12399F   Converted = 12399C

Fortunately, Micro Focus could *MOVE* comp fields with the "F" sign
(just don't attempt to do any math or comparisions).  In our MF
conversion utilties we moved the Wang data to a signed ZONED-DECIMAL
field, then moved it back again.  MF correctly changed the sign to "C".

Example:

01  WS-ZONED-FIELD	PIC S9(3)V99 VALUE ZERO.

01  WANG-COMP-FIELD	PIC S9(3)V99 COMP VALUE ZERO.

--conversion--

MOVE WANG-COMP-FIELD TO WS-ZONED-FIELD.
MOVE WS-ZONED-FIELD  TO WANG-COMP-FIELD.

Hope this helps.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** coboguy@aol.com (Coboguy)
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981122223921.03422.00001846@ng115.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<5E6F872365A7ADED.7B327052D86CEC32.978965C65D694490@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
Thanks for the assist Dan. Too bad this doesn't solve my problem. 

When I try to move a field with the 'F' sign, I get the message "Illegal
character in numberic field (Error 163)". I can work around this by redefining
the field as PIC X and interrogating the rightmost byte changing the 'F' to a
'C'.
My current stopper is negative numbers. For some reason, MF is removing the
byte if it = x'0D'. The rest of the record is then shifted left one byte.

I've also found that I need to use the CALL x"91" with Function 47. This
disables the insertion of a null character before data whose value is less than
x"20" in sequential files. This call also has Functions 52 & 53 for UNIX that
determine the record terminator (x"0D0A' or x'0A'). These are invalid functions
on the PC and I'm wondering how the x'0D' is being interpreted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3658e097.181816832@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<5E6F872365A7ADED.7B327052D86CEC32.978965C65D694490@library-proxy.airnews.net> <19981122223921.03422.00001846@ng115.aol.com>`

```
On 23 Nov 1998 03:39:21 GMT, coboguy@aol.com (Coboguy) wrote:


>I've also found that I need to use the CALL x"91" with Function 47. This
>disables the insertion of a null character before data whose value is less than
>x"20" in sequential files. This call also has Functions 52 & 53 for UNIX that
>determine the record terminator (x"0D0A' or x'0A'). These are invalid functions
>on the PC and I'm wondering how the x'0D' is being interpreted.

This is going to sound wierd -- but I have had trouble in the past
with files created by Unix type machines that terminate only with the
X"0d".  I had COBOL programs that converted these files so that
whenever a X"0d" was encoutered, a X"0a" was inserted.  But then I
learned that if you use DOS EDIT to edit the file, and then save it,
the X"0A"s will be inserted for you.  You might give it a try.
```

#### ↳ Re: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

```
In article <72tblt$vhu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,  <coboguy@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
>I offered to do a Wang VS COBOL to MF COBOL conversion for a friend of mine.
>He sent me the copybooks and data files (in ASCII text format) from the Wang.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Wang file conversion utilities - HELP

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<911380660.1201.0.nnrp-08.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<72tblt$vhu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
COMP on the VS is packed decimal not binary.  Of course if you mean COMP as
on a pc and the fields are long integer then you are probably hitting the
big endian/lttle endian problem.   VS will store a long integer 1 as
X'00000001' whereas on a pc this is held as X'00010000'.  (I believe this is
the correct representation).  In this case you would need to swap the first
2 and last 2 bytes before using your routine.

Alternatively I think there is a COMP type in MF COBOL which defines an
integer in non pc format.   If you use this to describe your input files you
should just be able to move these fields to the numeric format you want.

Rick

coboguy@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<72tblt$vhu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>I offered to do a Wang VS COBOL to MF COBOL conversion for a friend of
mine.
>He sent me the copybooks and data files (in ASCII text format) from the
Wang.
>Here's the problem... They didn't convert the COMP fields to DISP before
>transferring that data. They did copy the files from Indexed/Variable to
>Sequential/Fixed. Then due to an upper-management brainstorm, they
>diconnected the VS and tossed it in the dumpster (well it was only a VS
100).
>
>Now I've got data files that I've been trying to convert to MF files. In my
>conversion programs I have a routine that moves the Hex field (formerly
COMP)
>to an Alpha field that is then interrogated from right to left converting
the
>bytes into numeric data. The problem is that MF does some strange things
with
>hex data and I'm only getting a 75% success rate.
>
>Does anyone know of a utility or programming method the remedy this
situation?
>I would appreciate any input.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **From:** user@tiac.net (user)
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ugjg$d86@news-central.tiac.net>`

```
coboguy@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>I offered to do a Wang VS COBOL to MF COBOL conversion for a friend of mine.
>He sent me the copybooks and data files (in ASCII text format) from the Wang.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>diconnected the VS and tossed it in the dumpster (well it was only a VS 100).

Do you mean Wang COMP which is the 1985 Standard's PACKED-DECIMAL, or
MF COMP which is the standard's BINARY?

If BINARY, are you taking into account BYTE FLIPPING of the Little
Endian native Binary on the PC?

>Now I've got data files that I've been trying to convert to MF files. In my
>conversion programs I have a routine that moves the Hex field (formerly COMP)
>to an Alpha field that is then interrogated from right to left converting the
>bytes into numeric data. The problem is that MF does some strange things with

<a id="t-b93abe91a2"></a>
## Thread: Need CICS - APPC help — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Need CICS - APPC help

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72ul58$1is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Any heavy-duty CICS guys here?

We have started a project to develop a web-based app, connecting to apps on
the MVS host for database access.  We are using MS SNA Server with COMTI and
MS Transaction Server to make the connection.  The connection from SNA Server
appears as an LU6.2 device to the host.  The active components of the web
pages are written in VB and are expecting to talk to a Cobol pgm running
under CICS.

The basic connections are working, and the page is causing the CICS pgm to
start, but it (the Cobol pgm) is failing with a CICS abend ADPL.  I have
traced this back to a INVREQ on a RECEIVE.  The error manual says this is one
of two things:

1. The command is used on an APPC conversation that is not using the EXEC CICS
interface or that is not a mapped conversation.

2. A distributed program link server application specified the function-
shipping session (its principal facility) on the CONVID option (RESP2=200).

Investigation of the EIB block in the program dump showed RESP2=x'C8'
(d'200')  so I guess we have nailed the problem, but how to fix it?  (I used
to do a lot of CICS programming, but never any LU6.2/appc stuff -- and the
last few years I have drifted over to the 'dark side' and been working in
DBA, tech support, and a variety of PC based special assignments.)

FWIW, we also see the input data supplied by the VB pgm is in the TIOA, so we
assume that everything on the PC end is doing its job correctly.

So far, our Cobol program is just a very small 'proof of technique' example
based on sample programs included with the PC components - very small.  Here
are the relevant parts:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  FILLER.
	05  PASSED-DATA-LEN     PIC 9(4) COMP    VALUE ZERO.
01  WS-LU-IO-AREA.
	03  CA-PART-NUM                PIC X(5).
	03  CA-PART-DESC               PIC X(30).
	03  CA-BIN                     PIC X(3).

LINKAGE SECTION.
01 DFHCOMMAREA.
	02  CA-COMMAREA 	PIC X.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
	MOVE LENGTH OF WS-LU-IO-AREA TO PASSED-DATA-LEN
	EXEC CICS RECEIVE
		INTO (WS-LU-IO-AREA)
		LENGTH (PASSED-DATA-LEN)
		NOTRUNCATE
	END-EXEC

*--------------------------------------------------------------
*   MODIFY THE INPUT FIELDS IN A RECOGNIZABLE WAY:
*   - REVERSE STRING OF ALPHA'S
*   - COMPUTE NINES-COMPLEMENT OF NUMERICS
*--------------------------------------------------------------
	MOVE LENGTH OF WS-LU-IO-AREA TO PASSED-DATA-LEN
	EXEC CICS SEND
		FROM (WS-LU-IO-AREA)
		LENGTH (PASSED-DATA-LEN)
	END-EXEC
	EXEC CICS RETURN END-EXEC.
	GOBACK.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need CICS - APPC help

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72v026$c6n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<72ul58$1is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Never mind.  We found the solution.  If you're curious, in poking around the
transaction dump, I found that a commarea had been allocated and the input
data placed there.  So, instead of trying to RECEIVE and SEND the data, I
simply looked in the DFHCOMMAREA, did what I needed with it, and placed the
return data back in the DFHCOMMAREA.  The only CICS command needed was EXEC
CICS RETURN END-EXEC.

In article <72ul58$1is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
> Any heavy-duty CICS guys here?
>
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Need CICS - APPC help

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72vekn$pt4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<72ul58$1is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <72ul58$1is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
> Any heavy-duty CICS guys here?
>
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ed,

Code looks fine. I'm assuming the conversation is the principal facility and
that CICS is the Back-End Transaction?

In your VTAM Definitions, are you using Dependent or Independent LU's? Does
this occur on the very first handshake-session between the Server and CICS?

Are these sessions conversational or non-conversational? In other words, once
the server executes an APPC-ALLOCATE, the Back-End Transaction (Session)
remains active (Conversational) or it dies after you issue the SEND (that's
what it looks like)? Are the Sessions executing at SYNCLEVEL ZERO?

Just for kicks, define a fullword and specify the STATE parameter on the
RECEIVE and see what CVDA you get. This may tell you much more.

You also may want to post this to bit.listserv.cics-l.

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta




-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

well as ways (when the time comes) to get jobs without any experience
whatsoever.

requires 2 years experience or whatever.   I don't have any experience.

#### ↳ Re: Professional advice requested by an underachiever from this group

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be13cc$a2be7700$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<20439-3653752A-65@newsd-164.iap.bryant.webtv.net>`

```
1)Download the Fujitsu v3 (free) compiler.  
2)Get one or more good Cobol books. 
  a) Choose a style from amongst those recommended
  b) draft code fragments according to chosen style
3)START WRITING CODE.(this is over until
it passes the compiler without any warnings when
the compiler is set to "nitpick" mode AND the
runtime behavior is correct -- a few possibilities are:)
  a) Hello World
  b) "Guess" number from 1 to 100
  c) Sieve of Erasthosthenes
4) Try to find someone to CRITICIZE your code (once the compiler
no longer complains and it runs right, NOW the real criticism starts:)
  a) did you stick to chosen style?
  b) did you make any logic errors?

Cycle 3) and 4) with increasingly complicated (and more realistic)
problems.
```

```
Hi !

I'm having some probs compiling the source code to .gnt, can you give me a
hand ?

the message i get when compiling as the mf manual describes :

C:>xm cobol emp0000 omf"gnt";

Ltd
URN AXCPA/000183167    [Protocol: DPMI]                                  Ref
009
Micro Focus COBOL
Version 3.2.20 Copyright (C) 1985-1994 Micro Focus Ltd.

#### ↳ Re: MF compiler directives

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3654b24b.838042@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<72usv1$d65$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Paulo,

This is what I came up with for the compile command:

The line for compiling is:

      xm cobol program_name, gnt_name, list_name, NUL

	prgram_name is the name of the program you are compiling

	gnt_name is the name of the object file created

	list_name is the name of the error listing created

	The last parameter is unkown to me for its use.

So for you to compile the program emp0000 try:

  xm cobol emp0000, emp0000.gnt, emp0000.lst, NUL

This should get you on the track,

Paul


On Wed, 18 Nov 1998 16:37:35 -0000, "Paulo" <c.m@mail.telepac.pt>
wrote:

>Hi !
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
