<a id="t-54da8bf620"></a>
## Thread: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR? — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I attended the (still going on) J4 meeting in Carmel, CA Sun, Mon, Tues, and
half of Wednesday.  The meeting (along with the co-located WG4 meeting) goes
until Friday - so anything I say in this note is based on "tentative" at
best information, but ...

On Sunday evening, I was convinced that there never would be another
approved/official COBOL Standard.  By the time that I left on Wednesday, I
had been swayed to the belief that there will (possibly - maybe even
probably) be an approved Standard some time between 2002 and 2005.  It is my
expectation (and fear) that anything new and "hard" that was planned for
(and possibly even included in earlier drafts of) the next Standard:
  - will be removed
  - will be made implementor defined
- will become "processor dependent" (which actually means optional)
    or
- will simply be specified WRONG (incomplete, un-implementable, with
contradictions, completely NOT upward compatibly, or a combination of any of
the above)

OK - before giving some examples of what has caused me to reach this
depressing and depressed state, let me say (again) a little so that the
"reader will beware" of my opinions.

A)  I have a real Cassandra complex (OK - Doc, want to explain about
Cassandra to our non-classically trained readers?).  I keep on seeing awful
things happening to the Standard; I keep warning them about them; they keep
thinking I am crazy; and my worst fears keep coming true.

B) I am clearly "out of touch" with the accumulated knowledge and opinions
of the majority of BOTH the J4 and WG4 committees.  Almost every time that I
voted (everyone - even non-members - can vote on their "straw votes") I
would end up on the "losing" end of the debate.  Normally, I tell others
when they keep on being on the wrong-side of discussions with a large body
of experts, that such a person should at least CONSIDER that maybe it isn't
the rest of the world that is wrong.

C) Although it is a real simplification of a complex situation, it is J4
(the ANSI-ish group) that does the "nitty-gritty" technical work and WG4
(the ISO-ish group) that sets direction.  As I (intentionally so as to not
be bound by US positions or to "endorse" a consensus of countries "knows
best" policy) did not attend the WG4 sessions, I was not privy to either the
discussions or decisions of the "policy" and "direction setting" side of
this meeting.  Maybe, if I had seen this side of the "debate" I would have a
more hopeful view of the future of this Standard (but I don't really think
so).

D) and final "preface" statement, we all know what they say about statistics
and statisticians (no, Doc - I don't think you need to explain this one).
Therefore, I will freely admit that the votes and decisions that I include
in the following section are selected NOT at random - but to justify my
pessimism.

    *****

OK, what were some of the things that happened from Sunday to Wednesday in
Carmel or previous to this meeting. (wording may be completely off - see the
actual minutes for correct "details".)


  NOTE WELL:
     Some (but not many) of the following are things that I actually agree
with.  The point of including them all is to show you how many items are
STILL changing and how basic some of the changes are (and some of the
"reasonable" things that are being rejected).

1) Straw vote:
   Users are one of the Standard's primary target audience:
       Lost about 12 to 4 (I don't have the exact vote)

2) Remove the Composite Language Skeleton
      J4 member vote  -  Yes-9 No-1 Abstain-2

3) Remove allowing mixed alphanumeric and national characters in an
alphanumeric literal
  A) remove T-literals - consensus
  B) remove mixed data in traditional literals (HAVING such data is what
makes it impossible to allow all 256 bit-patterns as valid alphanumeric
characters)
    J4 member vote: Yes-1 No-8 Abstain-1

4) After several hours of J4 discussion, Vote on remove the
File-Sharing/Record-Locking definition from the Standard
   Yes-6 No-4 Abstain-2
(WG4 ended up keeping file sharing, but removing some of the more complex
aspects of record locking)

5) GLOBAL in USE statements (not for files or data) to be made OBSOLETE
  (consensus - I think)

6) Don't add a new relational operator "<>" to mean "not equal to"
   (consensus to reject the operator - because it really is too late to add
even "small things")

7) Hours and hours were spent on figuring out what types of groups could and
could not have "bits" and where they were aligned.  By the time I left,
there was still no resolution on this.

8) Remove new "dynamic tables" feature

9) Remove dynamic setting of SORT/MERGE collating sequence (they had already
removed setting other dynamic collating sequences - these have now been
replaced by using _locale processing)

10) Keep CALLs and INVOKEs with object data to "default" to BY CONTENT (as
opposed to everything else that defaults to BY REFERENCE).  This was done
because inline function references and method-invocations can't specify any
BY-phrase - therefore, CALL and INVOKE were changed to be consistent.

11) PADDING CHARACTERS were made archaic

12) The entire PICTURE clause is being re-written (hopefully compatibly -
but if you have ever read those rules, you will see the challenge)

13) NOT phrases and "positive" conditional phrases can now be specified in
any order, e.g.
    Read ABC
       NOT INVALID KEY  perform xyz
       Invalid Key perform lmn
    end-read

14) Allow "empty parenthesis" for empty function lists, e.g.
       Function Random ()

15) There needs to be another INTERNAL review of the document after the
current problems are fixed.

16) Allow GLOBAL on 77-levels
   rejected by consensus

17) Allow UNSTRING on referenced modified items
   rejected (postponed until "next" Standard)

18) But Object Orientation and National Character support in the processor
dependent list.
    Both of these were rejected

19) CANCEL should be defined as releasing storage
   rejected (although it was recognized this is what most users expect)

20) In the current J4 agenda, there are over 50 items that are STILL not
completed from the LAST "official" CD review (which ended some time in '97 I
think)

21) There are over 800 individual items identified during the current
"informal review" - and over 80 recommendations already made for changes
from this review (and still over 60 papers that haven't even been processed
yet).  This review doesn't even end until January of next year.

22) During this J4 meeting, it took usually over a half hour just to
"phrase" (word) straw votes (not motions which have some "binding nature" -
but straw votes that are simply intended to explore committee sentiment.)

23) The current draft (before already decided upon changes)
  - includes 20 USAGEs (some with options) - 13 are NEW
  - 14 formats to the SET statement  - I think 10 are new
  - 35 items identified as "Substantive changes potentially affecting
existing programs"
 - 140 items identified as "Substantive changes not affecting existing
programs"
 - 226 items in the implementor defined list
 - 60 items in the explicitly "undefined" list
 - 30 items in the "processor dependent" list

24) The following are major features that are still (or again) being
revised:
  - internationalization (use of locales)
  - how to process bit items
  - file sharing/record locking
  - how and when COPY, REPLACE, and compiler directives are processed (as
opposed to syntax processing)
 - how to define the new features for allocating and freeing memory
 - several bits and pieces related to object orientation
```

#### ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3655c0ca.256752821@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 19 Nov 1998 21:51:15 -0800, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:


>On Sunday evening, I was convinced that there never would be another
>approved/official COBOL Standard.  By the time that I left on Wednesday, I
>had been swayed to the belief that there will (possibly - maybe even
>probably) be an approved Standard some time between 2002 and 2005. 

20 years between standards! Shocking. Meanwhile the vendors are doing
it themselves anyway.

[snip]

>
>1) Straw vote:
>   Users are one of the Standard's primary target audience:
>       Lost about 12 to 4 (I don't have the exact vote)

If that isn't the case, then the standard is for who? 

>
>3) Remove allowing mixed alphanumeric and national characters in an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    J4 member vote: Yes-1 No-8 Abstain-1

ie. allow national characters in PIC X etc? fine by me.

>
>4) After several hours of J4 discussion, Vote on remove the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>aspects of record locking)

Confusing.

>
>6) Don't add a new relational operator "<>" to mean "not equal to"
>   (consensus to reject the operator - because it really is too late to add
>even "small things")

Ha!

>9) Remove dynamic setting of SORT/MERGE collating sequence (they had already
>removed setting other dynamic collating sequences - these have now been
>replaced by using _locale processing)

How do they intend to support multiple locales in one locale. Eg SORT
by english locale whilst running an arabic OS?

>
>10) Keep CALLs and INVOKEs with object data to "default" to BY CONTENT (as
>opposed to everything else that defaults to BY REFERENCE).  This was done
>because inline function references and method-invocations can't specify any
>BY-phrase - therefore, CALL and INVOKE were changed to be consistent.

Great - there goes easy multi-language work I guess.

>
>15) There needs to be another INTERNAL review of the document after the
>current problems are fixed.

how many times have we heard that now.

>
>17) Allow UNSTRING on referenced modified items
>   rejected (postponed until "next" Standard)

40 years time?

>
>18) But Object Orientation and National Character support in the processor
>dependent list.
>    Both of these were rejected

I know a lot of people think those should be optional but I think most
vendors, certainly in the PC realm, would have big problems not
implenting them. 

>
>19) CANCEL should be defined as releasing storage
>   rejected (although it was recognized this is what most users expect)

So we'll still have to use propriety 'hacks' to do the same. Even if
they just allowed the implementor to mark the cancelled storage as
invalid so it can be chucked out to disk would be fine by me.
Insisting it's left around until the end of the run unit is dumb.


>
>24) The following are major features that are still (or again) being
>revised:
>  - internationalization (use of locales)

It's always been a mess. Locales only solve part of the problem IMHO.

>  - how to process bit items
>  - file sharing/record locking
>  - how and when COPY, REPLACE, and compiler directives are processed (as
>opposed to syntax processing)

That's always been a mess too.

> - how to define the new features for allocating and freeing memory

Needs doing - 20 years ago.
```

#### ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ob52.274$Sf1.415285@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
There is a saying that it is better for those who eat sausage not to
see it being made. :)  I admit that I have never had the opportunity
to participate in the standards process, and have only recently had
direct exposure to any of the people and processes.  But I wonder if
it won't be better for everybody if this proposed standard does *not*
become finalized anytime soon.  As many of you, I have spent the last
several decades mostly designing and writing COBOL systems, and I have
developed a pretty good idea of what is important or not, particularly
in small to medium sized business and local government, where about
80-90% of all people are employed here in the U.S. (66% work for
companies with fewer than 20 employees).  I see three areas which are
important in any significant system I have ever worked with.  The user
interface, the internal processing, and database access.  The proposed
standard adds the Screen Section and associated Procedure Division
constructs, a long overdue plus.  But the last time I looked, there is
no real consensus across the computer profession even that OO is a
'Good Thing', though its proponents are vociferous.  And even if OO is
a 'Good Thing' in general, it is certainly not clear to all that OO is
a 'Good Thing' for COBOL.  Much of the OO community do not understand
the long time strengths of COBOL in the first place.  Even so, a truly
enormous amount of effort has been expended on adding OO constructs to
COBOL.  At the same time, standard COBOL has almost ludicrously limited
capabilities in Indexed File access.  For example, I do not have one
client who would be satisfied with a program which could only allow
them to search through a file forward but not backward.  Before I had
the construct available in Micro Focus COBOL, I actually wrote code to
store viewed records in a temporary relative file so the users could
at least back up to the beginning point of an inquiry search.  My
clients had demanded it.  "Why on earth can't we go backwards as well
as forward?" they asked.  I have been very excited about the proposed
new capabilities in that area.  But I understand that there is some
sentiment to actually remove the READ PREVIOUS construct from the new
proposed standard.  I cannot imagine that anyone even suggesting such
a thing has actually ever written a real system using Indexed Files!
And Bill is reporting below that J4 actually voted to remove the file
sharing/record locking from the standard.  If I correctly understand
what that sounds like (please correct me if I am wrong), I am appalled
by the sheer lunacy of such thinking!  Do these people actually *use*
COBOL, or is it simply some intellectual exercise for people who merely
talk about it?  When I consider the long term ramifications of bringing
out a poorly considered standard which we will probably have to live
with for decades, and live with much of the stuff forever, I really
have my doubts.  I am looking forward to reading what the rest of you
have to say about this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36558dca.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <9ob52.274$Sf1.415285@news3.mia>`

```
When I started writing COBOL II, I was perplexed at why so much time had
passed since the last release of a Standard.  Several of the books I read
did not even mention the '89 Addendum, as they were just reprints with
minimal or no revisions of books published before '89.

Now I have had the opportunity to hear what is going on in the J4 committee
from more than one member.

I can only say that the committee acts, at least to the viewpoint of an
outsider, like a maniac.

If this committee spent an entire week just feeling out what people wanted,
I don't think that any amount of time will ever suffice to release an
acceptable version of the next standard.

OO issues and processor issues (like bits) aside, maybe some of the desired
and needed features could be assembled and released as a Standard in and of
themselves right now (I know, I'm dreaming), and other, tougher issues could
be left to a nebulous "future" standard.



I didn't think of a READ PREVIOUS construct in the thread about what would
be nice to see in the Next Standard a week ago or so, but it is obviously a
good idea.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7348q6$9q9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <9ob52.274$Sf1.415285@news3.mia>`

```
Well, I pretty well agree with you Judd.  The areas that are
most important to me are standardization of file locking,
and a standardized Screen Section. With those, plus
perhaps a few very minor changes (select clauses come
to mind), I would be quite content.  As far as a GUI goes,
one could easily have a module that replaces a standarized
screen section and extends it.  That can quite easily be
a third party tool.

The Isam read previous is not nearly as important, in
my opinion.  I seldom read a file backwards, and the
few times that I have needed to, I have been able to
fake it. It would be a pleasant addition, nothing more.

The rest, to me, is fluff.  The only other thing I would like
to see (and it is not even proposed) is an absolutely
standard calling procedure.  I think *any* Cobol program
should be able to call *any* Cobol subroutine, using
a standard call, regardless of the compiler.  Obviously,
the data would have to be in a compatible format, but
that is trivial.

Judson McClendon wrote in message <9ob52.274$Sf1.415285@news3.mia>...
>There is a saying that it is better for those who eat sausage not to
>see it being made. :)  I admit that I have never had the opportunity
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I attended the (still going on) J4 meeting in Carmel, CA Sun, Mon, Tues,
and
>>half of Wednesday.  The meeting (along with the co-located WG4 meeting)
goes
>>until Friday - so anything I say in this note is based on "tentative" at
>>best information, but ...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>probably) be an approved Standard some time between 2002 and 2005.  It is
my
>>expectation (and fear) that anything new and "hard" that was planned for
>>(and possibly even included in earlier drafts of) the next Standard:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981124003019.01706.00002066@ng121.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<73c6q3$5a1$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
>Agreed, to a point... but I've usually been sleazy 'n cheesy and only
>saved the *keys* for the records at the top of the previous dozen or so
>screens.

May be sleazy 'n cheesy but it always worked and I still use it.  Not only
that, but if you constructed your code right it was only two extra
statements to accomplish this.  One to store the key, the other to
prime the key before the START.

In defense of going bass-ackwards through a file, it's nice to be able
to do that instead of computing a nines complement alternate key for
total sales (for example) to produce a top down report for the sales manager.

JC Jones.
```

#### ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>

entirely within possibility for the Standards committee to destroy it,
if they don't understand its strengths, and want to make it into
something else.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365b8226.8725401@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <365B33FE.D337BFB1@home.com>`

```
On Tue, 24 Nov 1998 15:32:30 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>Maybe I'm wrong, Maybe COBOL won't survive the next 30 years.  It is
>entirely within possibility for the Standards committee to destroy it,
>if they don't understand its strengths, and want to make it into
>something else.

I have my own twisted view of this.

The '85 standard made it much easier for people to change compiler
vendors.  That said, it is in the economic interests of compiler
companies to retain their customers by "enslaving" you to their
features.  The longer the delay to the new standard, the more
entrenched your position with them becomes.

In recent years the compiler companies have added features in variance
of the standard - they don't require the standard, they have no
incentive to get it done quickly.

I don't think the foot dragging is purposeful or malicous - there's
just no incentive to get the task completed.  Once completed the
compilers will again come into conformity and the great compiler
migration can begin.  But that's only a small PART of the delay in the
standard.

The committee has a desire to do it RIGHT - but not FAST.  I can' t
disagree with that approach unless it puts the language at risk -
which is not presently happening.

Hows that for a twisted view.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365C55DD.EE5DB21D@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <365B33FE.D337BFB1@home.com> <365b8226.8725401@news1.ibm.net>`

```
COBOL '60 "developed by the Short Range Committee" had a three month
deadline based upon adopting Flowmatic, and a few other things. It
took them a year! The data base features development (already widely
used in some flavor) took six or seven years to bring to spec as
a lot of learning had to go on. Meanwhile, IDS, TOTAL, and IMS in some
form or another filled the gap. Now we have several "relational"
products, and I have no idea how uniform they are. It looks to me like
the standard COBOL I/O is not easily converted from one platform to
another. There are many miles to go, but the trip is worth while to
those that follow. Market forces as Thane said in my opinion have their
effect upon this. User resistance, and contribution has one, too.

I would like to see you knowledgeable people contribute something in the
way of marketplace education. In the right hands, you can make a 
big impact on the whole picture. CLC is a great place to do that.
However, some of your "conversations" should appear in COMPUTERWORLD
or other widely read places where management will see them.

Warren Simmons

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 24 Nov 1998 15:32:30 -0700, Howard Brazee
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >entirely within possibility for the Standards committee to destroy it,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (Next) COBOL Standard - is it dead or just in serious need of CPR?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365E30E9.227916BC@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<7330ao$olu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <365B33FE.D337BFB1@home.com> <365b8226.8725401@news1.ibm.net> <365C55DD.EE5DB21D@inficad.com> <365c7c73.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif,

I am pleased to be asked, but there are too many problems here for
me to do something like that as much as I would like to see it done.
Over the past few years, I have collected a lot of messages that
told me something about the status of COBOL, and of late I've kept some
of the better comments on the whole issue, but it's because of how good
they make me feel. A great deal of my working life is wrapped up in
the COBOL project in one way or another.

Now, I'm just trying to stay healthy enough to be of some assistance
to my wife who cares for her 94.75 year old mother on an around the
clock basis.  Anything strenuous involves my using Oxygen, and that
includes many hours in bed every night.

On the other hand someone who is very busy, would undoubtedly be
better at organizing, and collecting the material. Even the selection
of items is better left in the hands of those of you who have the
current experience, and examples to cite.

My last excuse is that while I do have an internet connection, it's not
that good, and I'm unable to put the time on it and on the editorial
work that would be necessary.  

I believe many exchanges between several of you regulars striped of the
headers, and duplication would make excellent letters to the editor to
several publications. It might start a language war in higher places,
but considering the waste we see with the current not-invented-here
situation, the ox that would be gored would be mainly those who are
making a fast buck and will not be around to help in the next
conversion.  We either learn from our mistakes or we make them again.
Most of you people sound like you could hold your own with anyone, and
this energy could best be directed outside of your group in terms of the
greater good.

If someone can organize something, I might be able to aid in some way.

Warren Simmons

Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> Warren G. Simmons wrote in message <365C55DD.EE5DB21D@inficad.com>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> form the various services (e.g. Deja News) that store these things?
```

<a id="t-601999c592"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Samples & Validation — 3 msgs

### COBOL Samples & Validation

- **From:** Richard Schooler <schooler@incert.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.compilers,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98-11-092@comp.compilers>`

```
We're looking for samples of COBOL code, especially large applications
and exhaustive coverage of COBOL features.  Has anyone used the NIST
suite (http://www.ntis.gov/fcpc/cpn5682.htm)?  That web page mentions
about 300 programs, but doesn't give an indication of total size or
completeness.  Since it's rather expensive, I'd rather learn a little
about it first.

Our intent is to compile these programs on our P390 mainframe
development system and use them as test cases for our binary
instrumentation tool.  So for any applications, we're also looking for
sample input and output.  We're willing to pay.

Thanks,

	-- Richard
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Samples & Validation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.compilers,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98-11-104@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<98-11-092@comp.compilers>`

```
Richard Schooler wrote...
>We're looking for samples of COBOL code, especially large applications
>and exhaustive coverage of COBOL features.  Has anyone used the NIST
>suite (http://www.ntis.gov/fcpc/cpn5682.htm)? ...

The NIST test are definitely NOT a very good set of programs to test
actually COBOL usage.  The can cover most verbs and syntax - but not in
anything resembling "real world" usage.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Samples & Validation

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.compilers,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98-11-120@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<98-11-092@comp.compilers>`

```
Richard Schooler wrote...
>We're looking for samples of COBOL code, especially large applications
>and exhaustive coverage of COBOL features....

>Our intent is to compile these programs on our P390 mainframe
>development system and use them as test cases for our binary
>instrumentation tool.  So for any applications, we're also looking for
>sample input and output.  We're willing to pay.

Richard,

The ETK toolset (http://www.etk.com) comprises about 500 programs with
a total of 700,000 lines. These programs are of great complexity and
should be excellent test cases, although some esoteric features of
Cobol (e.g. ALTER) are not used. There are both interactive programs
and batch programs. They will all run under TSO.

To get an idea you can go to
http://www.etk.com/products/etkf32/index.htm and download the sources.
```

---

### EBCDIC to ASCII converters

- **From:** <halbaker@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com>`

```
I have an ebcdic data file that includes comp-3 fields that I am trying to
convert to ascii. Are there any freeware/shareware utilities available? As
this is a one time deal I don't want to put a lot of money into it.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII converters

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>

- **References:** `<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com>`

```
If you're using an IBM Mainframe, write a simple Read/Write Program,
specifying CODE-SET ASCII for your Output File. This facility may be
available on other platforms. I'm just familiar with Big-Iron.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com>...
>I have an ebcdic data file that includes comp-3 fields that I am trying to
>convert to ascii. Are there any freeware/shareware utilities available? As
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII converters

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365ABBD5.327AFA4@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com> <732n8v$sgi$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Not a good choice.

When you convert from EBCDIC to ASCII it does all sorts of damage to the
data and this conversion is on a record level not field.

My recommendation is write a simple cobol program that converts the same
layout into clear text and downloading that.  Remember to use sign
separate though.

If you still want to do it from the original source then each character
field will need to be ebcdic/ascii converted and then each COMP3 or
number field.  Good luck this is major fun for a programmer to solve!!!

Ken

WOB wrote:
> 
> If you're using an IBM Mainframe, write a simple Read/Write Program,
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII converters

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sJz62.2471$Gy.2564806@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com> <732n8v$sgi$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <365ABBD5.327AFA4@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>If you still want to do it from the original source then each character
>field will need to be ebcdic/ascii converted and then each COMP3 or
>number field.  Good luck this is major fun for a programmer to solve!!!

You are correct.  In fact, if you have any kind of binary data, then you
cannot convert EBCDIC to ASCII or vice-versa and recover all the data.
There is not a 1:1 mapping between the two character sets, and some of
the binary data will inevitably get zapped.  I once had to send binary
graphics data from a COBOL program on an EBCDIC mainframe to a laser
printer attached to an ASCII PC.  I broke down the stream of binary data
into 6 bit chunks which I translated into EBCDIC characters that would
translate into ASCII characters that, when bit shifted to the right one
bit on the PC, resulted in the original 6 bit binary chunks, which were
reassembled into 8 bit binary bytes for the printer.  I wrote an assembly
language TSR to intercept the printer interrupt and do the translation
and reassembly of the data on the PC, to make the process transparent to
the application software.  As you said, it was major fun! :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII converters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<911921971.25917.0.nnrp-05.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<732hve$5n7$1@camel19.mindspring.com> <732n8v$sgi$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <365ABBD5.327AFA4@zip.com.au> <sJz62.2471$Gy.2564806@news4.mia>`

```
If you have ftp,  just convert the character fields to ascii and then do a
binary transfer.  Everything should be OK.

We move data frequently between a VS (ascii), an AS400 (EBCDIC) and
rs6000/hp (ascii) but I must admit we convert binary/packed decimal fields
to display on the sender and back again on the recipient.

Which raises the perpetual moan, why doesn't MOVE CORRESPONDING
automatically work with occurring fields/structures?  We tend to take the
record layout structure and remove binary/packed decimal usage on the output
and use MOVE CORRESPONDING.  This is a no brainer when all the fields are at
the same level but unfortunately that is not normally the case.

Regards
Rick

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Ken Foskey wrote:
>>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII converters

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365584BB.BA995919@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
You can use the Cobol statement  INSPECT ... CONVERTING ... to handle the non
comp-3 fields to convert them to ascii.  For the comp-3 fields move them at
the field level, removing the comp-3 usage clause on the target fields if you
want to convert them.

halbaker@mindspring.com wrote:

> I have an ebcdic data file that includes comp-3 fields that I am trying to
> convert to ascii. Are there any freeware/shareware utilities available? As
> this is a one time deal I don't want to put a lot of money into it.
>
> Thanks
```

```

the file layouts..

Thanks,
Hal Baker

## Thread: ISO Utility to convert FDs to layout format — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### ISO Utility to convert FDs to layout format

- **From:** jamesj@usit.net (James Jones)
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365438c7.1644174@news.usit.net>`

```
For the past 2 days I've been converting COBOL file descriptions to
record layout, ie:

FIELD NAME                 STARTS   LENGTH      FORMAT

CUSTOMER NUMBER           1             6           ALPHA
CUSTOMER NAME                7           25           ALPHA
CUSTOMER SLSMN            32             3           NUMERIC
etc.etc.etc. until the end of time....

To say this is boring work makes light of Y2K remediation.
Has anyone written a utility to perform this type of operation?  If
not I just may write one myself.

Thanks,
James Jones.
```

- **References:** `<365438c7.1644174@news.usit.net>`

```

WS areas, but it's in C.  This might not be the right usegroup for it.

> For the past 2 days I've been converting COBOL file descriptions to
> record layout, ie:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: ISO Utility to convert FDs to layout format

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3654BEEC.CE31FFBD@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<365438c7.1644174@news.usit.net>`

```
James Jones wrote:
> 
> For the past 2 days I've been converting COBOL file descriptions to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> CUSTOMER SLSMN            32             3           NUMERIC

We have a tool called CobolReporter that produces exactly what you need.
It even produces more than you need:
- Offset from the start of parent DD level
- Offset from the start of 01 DD level
- Offset from the start of WS/FILE/LINKAGE section
- Length of the data item

The tool is not officially released yet
(will be released in 1-2 weeks), but I can send you an
evaluation copy today. The price for the tool is $495.

Please note that we correctly compute lengths and offsets
in presence of REDEFINES and RENAMES clauses.

You also can specify your own memory model
(how many bytes you have in your words, alignment rules, etc.),
so the tool can compule lengths&offsets for any CPU architecture.

Sample of the CobolReporter output is enclosed.

The tool is built using CobolTransformer, and by the way,
you have all this data on the Program Tree produced by CobolTransformer
so if you decide to automate the documenting/migration process
you can simply license CobolTransformer and have it do this work for you.

http://www.siber.com/sct/


Best regards,
Vadim Maslov

-------------------------------------------------------

#### ↳ Re: ISO Utility to convert FDs to layout format

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EX452.49$NF5.250905@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<365438c7.1644174@news.usit.net>`

```

>FIELD NAME                 STARTS   LENGTH      FORMAT
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>not I just may write one myself.


I don't mean to sound like an add for Compuware, but FileAid does this exact
thing. You would extract the FD or 01 to a copybook and use the
PRINT->RECORD LAYOUT option. This can be done in batch and run against many
different copybooks.

Since you are probably asking this because you don't have FileAid, I can
suggest a techique I partially developed in the past. Extract all FD's to
copybooks. Set up a COBOL compile with
IN-STREAM COBOL in the compile instead of referring to a PDS member. The
COBOL code contained would contain a skeleton which does nothing, but must
contain a WORKING-STORAGE section. Within the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION put
the copybook you wish to get a cross reference for IN-STREAM:

......
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
/*
//         DD DSN=USERID.COPYLIB(COPYMEM),DISP=SHR
//         DD *
...... rest of COBOL skeleton

or even:
//SYSIN    DD DSN=USERID.COPYLIB(TOP),DISP=SHR         TOP SECTION OF COBOL
SKELETON
//         DD DSN=USERID.COPYLIB(COPYMEM),DISP=SHR     COPYBOOK OF FD
//         DD DSN=USERID.COPYLIB(BOT),DISP=SHR         BOTTOM SECTION OF
COBOL SKELETON

Rout the COBOL listing to a DSN. Feed LISTING into a program, such as REXX
or COBOL whatever is easier for you. Use the REXX/COBOL program to parse out
each fields offsets near the right margin. Keep a running total of the field
lengths to generate your report.


Its not the easiest way, but it works, and of course it is free.

You could possibly even write an EDIT MACRO to do something similar in an
EDIT session and insert your offset report into the source code as a comment
or INFOLINE.

Hope some of this helps.
```

## Thread: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

- **From:** Fred Oberhauser <fo@kappmeyer.saarlink.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de>`

```
Hello,

is there a way to access the different XXXs in the following two
records:

      * global XXX
       01 XXX PIC X

      * XXX inside record AAA
       01 AAA
          05 XXX PIC X

      [...]

      *
           MOVE XXX OF ???how to qualify the global XXX
             TO XXX OF AAA.



thank you very much!


Fred
```

#### ↳ Re: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<732die$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de>`

```

Fred Oberhauser wrote in message <87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de>...
>Hello,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fred


Your example has me a  little confused.  Assuming what you really have is

  Identification Division.
  Program-ID.  Pgm1.
  Data Division.
  Working-Storage Section.
 *  Not File Section.
 01  AAA Global Pic  XXX.
 Procedure Division.
   Whatever.
        Call "pgm2"
        Stop Run.
 Identification Division.
 Program-ID.  Pgm2.
 Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
  01 BBB.
     05  AAA Pic XXX.
  Procedure Division.
   Still-Whatever.
       Display "I want to reference the OUTSIDE program AAA"
            AAA
       Exit Program.
  End Program Pgm2.
  End Program Pgm1.

Then, the bad news is that it can't be done.  Assuming that a data-name is
"unique" in the nested program and is an 01 level (outside the File
Section - where you can qualify by the file-name) in the containing program,
then the nested program can NEVER reference the containing program's name.

Some "easy" work-arounds would be to change the 01 structure in the outside
program to

 01  AAA.
       05   I-can-be-referenced Pic XXX.

In which the name is NOT found in the nested program.  Another, is to change
the name in the nested program.

My assumption is that there are other reasons why you want the two names to
be identical - in which case, I don't see how to solve this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

- **From:** Fred Oberhauser <fo@kappmeyer.saarlink.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87g1bepv9x.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de>`
- **References:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <732die$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:

> 
> 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your example has me a  little confused.

Indeed i should not use X as identifyer in such an example :-)

so i refine my example, using your example:

   Identification Division.
   Program-ID.  Pgm1.
   Data Division.
   Working-Storage Section.
  *  Not File Section.
  01  AAA Pic  X.
  01  BBB.
      05 AAA Pic X.
  Procedure Division.
    Whatever.
        MOVE AAA OF ???how to qualify the global AAA
          TO AAA OF BBB.


this can't be done either? or can it be done?


thank you very much!

Fred

PS:
> My assumption is that there are other reasons why you want the two names to
> be identical - in which case, I don't see how to solve this.

i work in some environment where CALLs are generated in a way i have
no choice for the argument names, so the call will allways be USING
AAA (in the above example).

On the other hand, the name AAA would be the most intuitive choice
inside the BBB record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365638cc.61165029@netnews>`
- **References:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <732die$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <87g1bepv9x.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de>`

```
On 20 Nov 1998 10:29:14 +0100, in comp.lang.cobol Fred Oberhauser
<fo@kappmeyer.saarlink.de> wrote:

>so i refine my example, using your example:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>this can't be done either? or can it be done?

Can't be done.  AAA is ambiguous, and the first one cannot be made unique
via qualification.  This can even happen with a field that can be
qualified.

 FD THE-FILE.
 01  THE-RECORD.
      05  AAA Pic  X.
      05  BBB.
           07 AAA Pic X.

Even if you type AAA OF THE-RECORD OF THE-FILE that's not unique, so there
is no way to access the first AAA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** nop06685@mail.telepac.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3656ea7e.1661302@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <732die$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <87g1bepv9x.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <365638cc.61165029@netnews>`

```
On 21 Nov 1998 04:24:01 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
TO AAA OF BBB.
>>
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>is no way to access the first AAA.

AAA OF THE-RECORD OF THE-FILE is unique.
The other AAA has to be refered as
AAA OF BBB OF THE-RECORD OF THE-FILE


Small correction

FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: qualifying global data name agains nested data name: HOWTO?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7379mq$1g9@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<87ogq4f1gk.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <732die$ou7@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <87g1bepv9x.fsf@pc-fo.kappmeyer.de> <365638cc.61165029@netnews> <3656ea7e.1661302@news.indigo.ie>`

```

Frederico Fonseca wrote in message <3656ea7e.1661302@news.indigo.ie>...
>On 21 Nov 1998 04:24:01 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
>TO AAA OF BBB.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't agree,
    AAA OF THE-RECORD OF THE-FILE

Is NOT unique - it can refer to either of the AAA's - just as it is
perfectly legal to have

   01  A.
       05   B.
           10   C   Pic X.

and refer to C as C of A.

You can skip levels when doing qualification.

I agree with the original statement that the 1st AAA in the example can
NEVER be reference.  (In the '85 Standard this is actually legal - if it is
never referenced and cannot be made unique.)
```

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<000781dc@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<36548223.95F1D00B@rz.uni-duessledorf.de>`

```
Am 19.11.98 schrieb stefan.frimmersdorf # rz.uni-duessledorf.de zum Thema Suche
Cobol Routine, auch kommerziel !:

Hallo,

sf> meine Firma sucht eine Cobol Routine, die DM Betraege in Klartext
sf> wandelt.

Das kannst Du auch selbst. Du schaust Dir immer drei Stellen der Zahl an,  
uebersetzt die erste Ziffer in ein Wort ("ein, zwei"), ergaenzt um die  
Wertigkeit ("e Million", "tausend",...). Im deutschen gehts Du jetzt auf  
die dritte Ziffer und dann auf die zweite. Jetzt fehlt nur noch die  
sonderbehandlung fuer elf und zwoelf und die Dekaden. Einen Studenten  
sollte das doch nicht ueberfordern.

MfG

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VISUAL OBJECT COBOL (HELP)

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36558172.F5C1A5FF@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<3653EA5C.D02ACE7D@edinfor.pt> <36546D30.DA851C58@ASPMnetdoor.com> <36552C33.6FE7B5C5@edinfor.pt>`

```
I can't tell you the syntax on your system;  we use copybooks for the WS &
PD that do the same thing.  In general though, those procedures are used
to either find the number of days between two dates or determine what date
occurs a certain number of days before or after a given date.  For
example, to find a date 45 days from today, convert today's date to an
integer, add 45 to it, and convert it back to a date.  To determine the
number of days between two dates, convert both of them to their respective
integer values and subtract.  HTH

Josï¿½ Capucho wrote:

> I want to know, how to use cobol tools that already does it.

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > day)

<a id="t-f6ceaa8459"></a>
## Thread: unix printing using lpd — 3 msgs

### unix printing using lpd

- **From:** "Paulo" <c.m@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<731rku$vnd$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Hi !

how can i send something to printer using unix lpd ?

TIA

Paulo
c.m@mail.telepac.pt
```

#### ↳ Re: unix printing using lpd

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36553ECB.8F0C30A4@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<731rku$vnd$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Paulo wrote:
> 
> Hi !
> 
> how can i send something to printer using unix lpd ?

If you're using Micro Focus COBOL (and really, these are OS issues
not COBOL issues you are raising so you should be stating which
compiler and version you are using as it will have a strong bearing
on whether you get a useful response) you should check out the
response I gave yesterday about using DD_ mapping - this will let you
write directly to a print scheduler in the COBOL program. Alternatively
write the data to a LINE-SEQUENTIAL file and then do something like:

CALL "SYSTEM" USING "lpr filename" & x"00".

Cheers, Kev.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: unix printing using lpd

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<733stk$8th$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<731rku$vnd$1@duke.telepac.pt> <36553ECB.8F0C30A4@mfltd.co.uk>`

```
In article <36553ECB.8F0C30A4@mfltd.co.uk>,
Kevin Digweed  <ked@mfltd.co.uk> wrote:
>Paulo wrote:
>> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> write directly to a print scheduler in the COBOL program.

For example, in your program:

   SELECT PRT-FILE ASSIGN EXTERNAL "PRTOUT"

and in your shell script:

   DD_PRTOUT='>lpr'   ; export DD_PRTOUT

See the COBOL System Reference Manual chapter "External
File Name Mapping" for details, especially the section
"Setting Up Pipes."
```

Hi.

I'm converting a cobol program from using Btrieve to a Oracle 8.0
database.

When I read the next record everything works fine, but when I try to
read the previous record I get error -19519 in SQLCODE  ...

I can't find the correspondent SQLCODE value in the help-file...

Could anyone help me ??

Does anyone have a working example of a program that reads the
next/previous record
on a Oracle 8.0 database??

Is there a difference in using embedded sql on MS SQL Server 6.5 and
Oracle 8.0 ??

Cheers,
Tommy Nilsen

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress embedded sql and Oracle 8.0.

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>

- **Message-ID:** `<731nrb$eaa@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36543859.351440B@yahoo.com>`

```
Hi,

You could try looking at the SQLERRMC field in SQLCA as this should give you
some text which may help.

Also if you have a data item MFSQLMESSAGETEXT in your module then this will
also have some textual description.

Hope this helps.
Dave.

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<731ai7$bb7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi:

To make a long story short, I'm trying to get my application to run under
this version of MF (3.0.54).  Currently the same source runs ok under RM,
Accu, but, I can get the programs to compile ok, but several are returning
this 108 error with a "Failure to initialise Data Division".  Using Animator,
it seems to be at the point that a call is being made to another COBOL
program.  I've made sure that the call parameters all contain data and both
the caller and the called program are expecting the same data.	At one time
(3 years ago!), the ancestor's of these programs did run ok using this
compiler.

this behavior?  Could I be so lucky?

Thanks in advance for everyones help!

Chris Loy
Maxom Holdings, Inc.

## Thread: cobcap file — 5 msgs

### cobcap file

- **From:** "Paulo" <c.m@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<733k73$r7p$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
on the older cobol compiler (MF Cobol /2 v.1.1) i've a file named 'cobcap',
this file is :
PRINTER01:W:lp:-s:-dprinter_device_name
PRINTER02...... and so on

to print from the cobol i've just to assign the 'PRINTER01' (ex) to the
SELECT clause. Can you tell me if this 'cobcap' is crated by any mf prog ?
or it is already configured ?

If possible show me a sample how can use it. Thanks

Paulo
c.m@mail.telepac.pt
```

#### ↳ Re: cobcap file

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73br0l$8jc$1@saltmine.radix.net>`
- **References:** `<733k73$r7p$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
In article <733k73$r7p$1@duke.telepac.pt>, Paulo <c.m@mail.telepac.pt> wrote:
>on the older cobol compiler (MF Cobol /2 v.1.1) i've a file named 'cobcap',
>this file is :
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>If possible show me a sample how can use it. Thanks

Wow, that's a pretty old version!  I'm no expert in
the older features, and I suspect there is no comparable
feature in the current products, although the 16-bit
compiler might have this.  Maybe Kevin can enlighten us.
I suspect the DD_xxxx='<lpr' method is your only
reasonable approach.  The Compatibility Guide doesn't
mention a cobcap feature, so I'm at a loss.  Where and
how was this discussed in your compiler's documentation?
```

thanks

Paulo

## Thread: Setting COBDIR for statically linked MF cobol application — 3 msgs

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1998-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3655b233.1030782@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Has anyone an idea for setting COBDIR to point elsewhere than
/usr/lib/cobol under Unix. My application is compiled with cob -x to
get a stand alone executable. The application is started by inetd and
works fine under SCO Unix 3.2 if the COBDIR is exported in
/etc/rd2.d/s85tcp where inetd is originally started but this fails
under SCO Unix 5+. Worse case scenario is to actually use
/usr/lib/cobol but it's not ideal. Any help gratefully received, as
always.

Nick Lucas
```

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Setting COBDIR for statically linked MF cobol application

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3656df93.3368897@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3655b233.1030782@news.demon.co.uk> <3655c603.258089172@news.freeserve.net>`

```
On Fri, 20 Nov 1998 19:43:02 GMT, scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C.
Murray) wrote:

>On Fri, 20 Nov 1998 18:21:26 GMT, nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick &
>Carol Lucas-White) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree it does seem crazy - it's trying to find rts.msg in
/usr/lib/cobol and then gives an error 181 ( I think - I'm not at work
right now so it may be 180) file not found on '' (quote quote) and
terminates. It makes no difference if the rts.msg is in the same
directory or any of the other .msg files. I can't understand why it
should look for these files either if it's statically linked because
that's the whole purpose of statically linking it ! MicroFocus
suggested seting COBDIR in /etc/rc.d/7 but this makes no difference.
I've been using a program called tcpclient that I got from a comms
site that's really handly for debugging socket applications as you can
specify a mahine IP and socket to use.
```

<a id="t-20dece04fd"></a>
## Thread: Check Digit — 10 this month / 12 total

### Check Digit

- **From:** suevarma@aol.com (Sue Varma)
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com>`

```
CAn anyone out there help me. I am working with check digits at the moment and
need to know how to work out the modulus of a number. What is the modulus? and
how is it calculated. Many thanks in advance to anyone who can help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Check Digit (modulo arithmetic)

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3657BE1E.3BA29B4C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com>`

```
Sue Varma wrote:
> 
> CAn anyone out there help me. I am working with check digits at the moment and
> need to know how to work out the modulus of a number. What is the modulus? and
> how is it calculated. Many thanks in advance to anyone who can help.

If your compiler has intrinsic functions, there is a FUNCTION MOD, I
believe.  If not, then modulo arithmetic can generally be done using
DIVIDE ... GIVING ... REMAINDER.  It's the remainder that you're
interested in.

For example, if you have a date expressed as the absolute count of days
from a base date, and you know the day of week for the base date, then
divide the day-count by seven and save only the remainder (daycount
modulo 7).  You can easily use that to determine the day of the week.

Another example is Leap Year calculation.  The simplest rule is if YEAR
modulo 4 equals zero, then it's a leap year (ignoring the 100-year and
400-year rules).  
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Check Digit (modulo arithmetic)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3659700B.1B03DA49@home.com>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com> <3657BE1E.3BA29B4C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
> Another example is Leap Year calculation.  The simplest rule is if YEAR
> modulo 4 equals zero, then it's a leap year (ignoring the 100-year and
> 400-year rules).

So far, I haven't seen cases where COBOL programmers have ignored the
100 and 400 year rules.  Maybe we're not as dumb as the press indicates.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Check Digit (modulo arithmetic)

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp@netdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%OH62.2309$b53.11587@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com> <3657BE1E.3BA29B4C@worldnet.att.net> <3659700B.1B03DA49@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3659700B.1B03DA49@home.com>...
>> Another example is Leap Year calculation.  The simplest rule is if YEAR
>> modulo 4 equals zero, then it's a leap year (ignoring the 100-year and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>100 and 400 year rules.  Maybe we're not as dumb as the press indicates.

I don't know if ignore is the right word.  No living COBOL programmer will
ever see a century year that won't be a leap year.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Check Digit (modulo arithmetic)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73pvm7$q4u$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com> <3657BE1E.3BA29B4C@worldnet.att.net> <3659700B.1B03DA49@home.com> <%OH62.2309$b53.11587@axe.netdoor.com> <enU3JbVG#GA.220@upnetnews05> <365E8B40.28FBAD94@infonet.com.py>`

```
Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <365E8B40.28FBAD94@infonet.com.py>...

>Just in case, I code all date with ten digits ;-)


I hear we have another year two thousand problem on our hands.
It seems that someone at Microsoft has discovered that all their
code stops running correctly at midnight of the day it is installed.

The bug has been in production at a base level now for seven years
and has infected all their product lines and DLLs.  It was only noticed
last week, as this was always considered normal for Microsoft
products; however, now that it has been noticed, MS says it will
be working on it ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Check Digit (modulo arithmetic)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00

#### ↳ Re: Check Digit

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EFC4A6F2D059FB23.557850B3553EF866.2BC28761154215A5@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121183013.09762.00001484@ng40.aol.com>`

```
On 21 Nov 1998 23:30:13 GMT, suevarma@aol.com (Sue Varma) enlightened
us:

>CAn anyone out there help me. I am working with check digits at the moment and
>need to know how to work out the modulus of a number. What is the modulus? and
>how is it calculated. Many thanks in advance to anyone who can help.

A modulus is defined as:  A number by which two given numbers can be
divided and produce the same remainder.  It can also mean:  The
absolute value of a complex number.  When working with check digits,
the modulus or mod, as it is sometimes called, is usually a single
number.  In the banking industry where I've worked the most common
ones I've seen are mod 11 and mod 9.  Each has its own formula for
calculating the check digit.  If you need an example, let me know and
I'll dig up what I have and post it.

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
I went to a bookstore and asked the saleswoman where the Self Help
section was. She said if she told me it would defeat the purpose.


 Steve
```

Thanks,

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981121083804.16647.00000979@ng-fr1.aol.com>`

```

In article <19981121083804.16647.00000979@ng-fr1.aol.com>, aziola@aol.com
(AZIOLA) writes:

>cljclJCLJCLJCLJCL
>
>YOU GOTTA LEARN JCL!....COBOL IS WORTHLESS IN MOST PLATFORMS IF YOU DONT KNOW
>JCL....IT'S WHAT RUNS THE COBOL PROGRAMS IN A MAINFRAME 

That is ASSUMing that the MainFrame environment is IBM or IBM-clone(Amdahl).

Not all environments are set on the use of JCL.  There are mainframe platforms
that permit straight up execution of programs just as on the PC.  Filenames are
supplied internally or via control files or via command line overrides.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981121232824.25467.00000642@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com>`

```

(Sff5ky) writes:

>That is ASSUMing that the MainFrame environment is IBM or IBM-clone(Amdahl).
>

Worse yet, it is assuming IBM MVS or OS/390 enviornment.

There are actually more VSE licenses than MVS + OS/390 licenses, and teh JCL
for VSE is significantly different from MVS & OS/390.
Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3658484c.142824041@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com> <19981121232824.25467.00000642@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
On 22 Nov 1998 04:28:24 GMT, mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young) wrote:

>
>In article <19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com>, sff5ky@aol.comxxx123
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Mark A. Young

I'm at an OS/390 shop now - but I came from a VSE shop.  What I can't
figure out, is if there are so many more licenses for VSE how come
there are VERY  FEW job postings/ads that mention VSE?  I just find it
strange.  When I started looking for my current job, all I ran into
were people requiring MVS - not one VSE position (other than the one
at your location) presented itself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<739qeb$2m9@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com> <19981121232824.25467.00000642@ngol04.aol.com> <3658484c.142824041@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3658484c.142824041@news1.ibm.net>...
>On 22 Nov 1998 04:28:24 GMT, mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young) wrote:
>
>>
>>In article <19981121103439.19943.00000309@ngol05.aol.com>,
sff5ky@aol.comxxx123
>>(Sff5ky) writes:
>>
>>>That is ASSUMing that the MainFrame environment is IBM or
IBM-clone(Amdahl).
>>>
>>
>>Worse yet, it is assuming IBM MVS or OS/390 enviornment.
>>
>>There are actually more VSE licenses than MVS + OS/390 licenses, and teh
JCL
>>for VSE is significantly different from MVS & OS/390.
>>Mark A. Young
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


although it is a GROSS oversimplification (with lots of exceptions), the
average MVS shop has 50+ (often hundreds) of application and systems
programmers - while the average VSE shop has less than 50 (and often less
than 10 application programmers).  Another issue is how much VSE code is
"purchased" versus home-grown (lots) versus that in MVS shops (some - but
usually well under half).  All of this adds up to significantly less demand
for application programmers for VSE than MVS (even though the licenses go
the other way).

P.S.  The same is true (if more so) for AS/400 which has more licenses than
VSE (as I recall) but even fewer application programming openings.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981124010759.27057.00001094@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3658484c.142824041@news1.ibm.net>`

```

In article <3658484c.142824041@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
writes:

>What I can't
>figure out, is if there are so many more licenses for VSE how come
>there are VERY  FEW job postings/ads that mention VSE?

I think William has it. MVS and OS/390 is designed to make use of IBM larger
mainframes, and that is where one typically finds many programmers, dollars to
pay top price, etc.

VSE is desinged to be sleek and have less overhead, so typically there are
fewer programmers at a VSE shop, and it is relativley common to purchase
packages rather than develop in-house, or at least to have major applications
be purchased systems. Also, since VSE shops are smaller, they tend to not be
able to pay top dollar for a programmer, making it counterproductive to
advertise in the papers against higher-paying MVS shops.

Also, many VSE licenses are for companies that have MVS for main processing and
use VSE machines as nodes controlled by a central MVS site, or program in a
central location and deploy applications at their offices in other states.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL student

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981124013121.25361.00001206@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981124010759.27057.00001094@ngol02.aol.com>`

```

In article <19981124010759.27057.00001094@ngol02.aol.com>, mark0young@aol.com
(Mark0Young) writes:

>VSE is desinged to be sleek and have less overhead

I forgot to add: so VSE would run efficiently on the lower end of the IBM
mainframes. (In fact, it couldn't make use of multiple processors until
relatively recently, and even then, using VSE's "Turbo Dispatcher", a partition
can be serviced by only one processor at a time during its "time slice".
Contrast that to MVS that can have multiple processors working on the same
partition at the same time, e.g., typical of CICS and DB/2 under MVS.)

Mark A. Young
```

- **From:** "GoJaye" <Gojaye@geocities.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
