## Thread: Source file compare utility? — 5 msgs

### Source file compare utility?

- **From:** Michael Russell <m006s300@mcmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365B12FC.8C6FBF5B@mcmail.com>`

```
Working with unix-based Cobol system - there is no
source-file compare available that gives good results.

What is available is the unix diff (or sdiff) command -
which operates on the whole width of a source-line - ie
columns 1-80. Naturally, what is needed is a comaparison
from 8-72, and a difference-report that *does* include the
line-numbers, ie columns 1-6.

Anyone help? Of course, it doesn't have to be particularly
Cobol-oriented; however, I do need to be able to run it
under NT-Dos (Dos shell).

Any help would be appreciated.

Mike Russell
```

```
Kindrick
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)

What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** e=mc^3@netcom.com (Richard Anderson)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73i4nf$6er@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 25 Nov 1998 07:28:57 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

Moving LOW VALUES to the first 1024 bytes of your Intel x86 will
prove the obsolescence of the branch table construct.

Seriously, the GO TO construct having been generally superseded by
various PERFORM constructs, I would suggest not bothering with GTD
-- it just starts an argument.  But, if you want to code Assembler
style in COBOL, feel free to do so.

Now if they would just implement PERFORM DEPENDING ON ..........

Rick Anderson
Seattle
anderson  ï¿½atï¿½  pobox  ï¿½fullstopï¿½  com
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** Rodger Whitlock <totototo+newsgroups@mail.pacificcoast.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z3ctS48bTBWQ@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

Gotodepending uses, as you know, a numeric index. Few, if any, problems 
*naturally* generate a numeric quantity suitable as such an index. In all 
other cases, you have to transform a series of Boolean expressions into 
appropriate index values. Thus, when you use gotodepending you have 
introduced a conceptual level of masking and indirection that is 
unnecessary. Moreover, you may have spatially separated the boolean 
conditions from their use. And if the gotodepending variable accidentally 
gets used for some other purpose, you are going to have a very nasty bug to 
resolve, one that can express itself in several ways.

It is far simpler and far easier to understand a program that directly 
references the boolean expressions in its conditional branching.

Class exercise: think of three distinct situations in which numeric 
quantities suitable for use in a gotodepending sentence arise naturally.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981128035531.27866.00001580@ng133.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Z3ctS48bTBWQ@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`

```

Rodger Whitlock

has it cornered with 
<<
Class exercise: think of three distinct situations in which numeric 
quantities suitable for use in a gotodepending sentence arise naturally.
>>

Well, even with the several _generous_ follow up postings, that is a tough clas
assignment.

And then Rodger does my work for me relating to the linguistic / mathematical 
subtheme by illustrating linguistic preference, ...
<<
It is far simpler and far easier to understand a program that directly 
references the boolean expressions in its conditional branching.
>>

For you and for me, and for bunches of people.  But not for every one.  If you
abstract a text editor to the enth degree you can render the entire thing into
an array of procedure addresses that are indexed by the input key.

((Pardon my simplistic expression of that fact, I gloss a tad there, actually,
you must take account of key-chords not just keys)).

Such an abstracted text editor could then be infinity configurable by the user
who could issue commands to reset certain keys to go to different procedures
upon her direction. And to be really salable you could offer object module
mainlines that allow the user to attach their own procedural subroutines.

Now is that flexible or what?

Now is that or is that not a description of an actual product called
Programmers WorkBench by Microsoft? (That is an example, they by no means,
authored the idea of a dynamically configurable editor).

Under this circumstance the mainline can not possibly name the procedures to be
executed.  This mainline could perhaps be considered  mathematical, and
non-linguistic. 

And so the distinction I am after is a difference in level of abstraction.
Mathematical people tend to abstract more deeply.  Mathematical people tend to
abstract a larger portion of their world.

The way this relates to modern data processing is that the net represents a
truely massive explosion of available information that can be processed for a
gain.  If our procedures attempt to enumerate all references linguistically,
the text refering to that information explosion will be atleast as large as the
explosion itself, which seem to be a formula for unprofitable processing.

By profit I mean more than just costs in relation to revenues, which is very
important.  I emphasize that profit involves minimizing risk.

So, as magnificent a rabit as it might seem to come from such an unpresupposing
hat, a PERFORM DEPENDING ON construct could be a major new avenue of growth in
applicable problem domain for COBOL.

PERFORM DEPENDING ON in simple syntax could be harnessed to an integer.  To
help people learn what method selection is really all about in the first
instance.  But we could certainly harness it to more interesting engines.

Obviously it would be reasonable to consider strapping this syntax onto a table
of procedure names listed in the data division, which table could, of course be
altered, (and I used that word on purpose).  

We could also wire this construct onto a two dimmensional table, the first
element could be text for those who like it litteral, and the second element
the name of the procedure to PERFORM when the text is found.
(We could have a fun attribute to tell the compiler to worry or not about
case).

We could tack the syntax onto a table that had 88 levels in column one, and
procedure names in column two.

COBOLers can definitely understand tables.  The weakness in GOTO DEPENDING ON
is the lack of structure.  PERFORM DEPENDING ON would fit modern style readily.

In a related post another, very interesting comment came from 
Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Nov 1998 01:55:55 -0800


<<
Yes, well, HANDLE CONDITION is evil itself.  I personally either let the
program abend (for those conditions that really shouldn't occur, unless
there's a major problem), or I use NOHANDLE and process the error in
a sensible manner!
>>

The truth is CICS uses the HANDLE CONDITION table of vectors to do what the
postor prefers. For when a coder decides to  "...  let the program abend  ..."
we are just vectoring to the procedure address that CICS has placed into the
table by default, and which we have resisted overriding.

So when we choose to not override, we do not choose to avoid the tabularized
vector technology, we use it to its fullest, exiting directly out, rather than
back to us then out.

The postor is exactly right that the defaults are appropriate in many cases.
But that doesn't avoid computed GOTO logic at all. 

More and more people are coming to agree with Frank Swarbrick  that, if you do
override by means of EXEC CICS statement, it is preferable to do NOHANDLE and
handle it directly (that is don't mix GOTO logic into your innocent structured
COBOL program).

I apologize to those who are not into CICS, some of the above is almost
unreadable to you I bet.

But vectoring to procedures is not unstructured in any way at all.

As an asside I would also assert that PERFORM DEPENDING ON would be much more
efficient coding, when your discriminant is an integer, than EVALUATE. Please
don't take that as anything like a negative expression about EVALUATE, it's
wonderful.


But in the simple cases:

PERFORM 
  DEPENDING ON interger-xyz
     A100-PROC
     B200-PROC
     C300-PROC
     D400-PROC
  OTHER
     Z999-THIS-AINT-KANSAS
END-PERFORM.

beats
    
EVALUATE  interger-xyz
   WHEN 1 
     PERFORM  A100-PROC
   WHEN 2 
      PERFORM  B200-PROC
   WHEN 3 
     PERFORM  C300-PROC
   WHEN 4  
      PERFORM  D400-PROC
  WHEN OTHER
     PERFORM                      Z999-THIS-AINT-KANSAS
END-EVALUATE.

It's the dreariness of the repeated WHEN go to another line PERFORM, you know.
All this over languaging that goes on.


Yet Rodger Whitlock's

<<
Class exercise: think of three distinct situations in which numeric 
quantities suitable for use in a gotodepending sentence arise naturally.
>>
Is definitely right. It is not that easy to think of suitable applications. But
there are some, and they seem to increase as we move our attention towards the
web.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uyI9HbVG#GA.205@upnetnews05>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>...
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

While I was working with the development and marketing of Interactive COBOL
at Data General in 1977 I was a big advocate of creating a PERFORM xxxx ...
DEPENDING to take some of the rough edges off the GO TO xxxx ... DEPENDING
construct.  An equivalent means to the same end later became available with
the advent of the EVALUATE ... WHEN ... PERFORM construct.  It seems to me
that the availability of the EVALUATE verb obviates any future need for
using GO TO DEPENDING (but IMO it should be retained in the COBOL standard
to recognize the relative prevalence of it's prior usage.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ViW62.5415$w_6.3238045@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

GO TO DEPENDING comes right after ALTER on my list of statements to be
avoided. :-)  I have only used this construct once, and that was in a
p-code interpreter written in COBOL.  COBOL was as the only language
available, and efficiency in the op code decoder was extremely important.
The p-code was used in the DEPENDING ON clause to GO TO the appropriate
processing paragraph, each of which terminated with a GO TO back to the
-EXIT paragraph terminating the paragraph with the GO TO DEPENDING.  So
it was 'structured', but I still don't like it. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73hp1r$1p8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>...
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

There have been quite a few responses to this post, but I have been
interested in the LACK of anyone mentioning what the (IBM mainframe) CICS
translator does.  I haven't looked at the translator output in YEARS, but at
least it used to convert all (most?) EXEC CICS HANDLE statements into GO TO
DEPENDING ON.

NOTE: this is NOT a recommendation to use this structure, but is a case
where it was (is?) commonly used.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981125164916.03997.00000501@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<73hp1r$1p8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
Date: Wed, 25 Nov 1998 12:23:31 -0800
Stated  

<< 
There have been quite a few responses to this post, but I have been
interested in the LACK of anyone mentioning what the (IBM mainframe) CICS
translator does.  I haven't looked at the translator output in YEARS, but at
least it used to convert all (most?) EXEC CICS HANDLE statements into GO TO
DEPENDING ON.

NOTE: this is NOT a recommendation to use this structure, but is a case
where it was (is?) commonly used.

>>

The CICS HANDLE logic does use a table of vectors.  When the condition happens
it jumps (hope you have your parachute) directly to the address that your COBOL
or ASSEMBLER program has set for that condition that last time it was set. 

Handles remain set.  Invoking a call successfully does not reset a handle.
Setting any other handles also does not reset a handle that is not mentioned.
So sometimes a down stream call will encounter an unexpected condition and CICS
will vecotr to some previous handle address; from there who knows what will
happen.

At the very least you get a confusing error message that sends you off
analyzing code that was successful and possibly long past.


The vendor now discourages use of HANDLE technology. Although the technique
works quite reliably, it is very powerful and can snow even an experienced
programmer. (Direct management of RESP and RESP2 are recommended now,
strongly).

In the modern era, it is certainly true that the HANDLE CONDITION table is a
GOTO syntax that mixes poorly with perform syntax.

It is, however technically, wrong to characterize vector tables as
unstructured.

The computed goto (in COBOL GO TO DEPENDING ON), is a miniature facility that
delivers an easily indexed array of program vectors to the coder of a procedure
division.

In languages like C you have robust facilities for this: multi-dimensional
matrices of function pointers.  The key to using this kind of technology is to
think in terms of indexes. Most COBOLers do not think that way.

There was even a recent post in this newsgroup _complaining_ about a programmer
who had asperations for the legal profession AND liked indexes. (Well of all
things!) The sentiment expressed there (even though mild) records a simple
fact. People who map their program environment mathematically do not mix well
with people who map the environment linguistically.

And, yes, it would be very difficult to nail down those terms. But I think most
readers will know what I am talking about. Proportionately speaking there are
fewer geeks into COBOL than say UNIX C/C++ kernel writing.

Linguistic people fit COBOL business world requirements well. And many of them
can not really fathom what the HANDLE CONDITION vector table is. (Not all!
Don't get upset with that opinion. For example, the 
original poster had it exactly).

It is just that we chase the egg-heads out, and still our COBOL world functions
smoothly.  It must be that the problem domain just does not demand dispatcher
logic coding structures very often. Atleast not in the past.

All the same, I feel confident that if we just had a computed _perform_,
everyone and her uncle would be out thar' codin' 'em.  As in, ...

PERFORM 
  DEPENDING ON 
      OBJECT-MANAGER-INDEX
          PAGE-UP-PROC
          PAGE-DOWN-PROC
          PF4-OUT-A-HERE-PROC
          ENTRY-VALIDATION-PROC
          CLS-PROC
    OTHER
          MAJOR-CONCERN-PROC
END-PERFORM.

Off hand, I see no reason why such syntax would preclude alternation between
'procedure-name' and 'procedure-name thru proc-exit', for those that are hooked
on screen consumption. 

Control vectors are the quintessence of object oriented programming. If the
COBOL language and its practitioners do not make room for it, then it's going
to continue to be coded in other languages.

NOTE: this IS a recommendation to use this structure, to keep it in the
language and to grow it.

Best Wishses,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73i0f0$kl2@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<73hp1r$1p8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <19981125164916.03997.00000501@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message
   <much snippage>
>
>All the same, I feel confident that if we just had a computed _perform_,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
   <a little more snippage>

Can you tell me what you think is better with your code than the (current
Standard) code of:

 Evaluate  OBJECT-MANAGER-INDEX
     When "U" Perform PAGE-UP-PROC
     When "D" Perform PAGE-DOWN-PROC
     When "4" Perform PF4-OUT-A-HERE-PROC
     When "E" Perform ENTRY-VALIDATION-PROC
     When "C" Perform CLS-PROC
     When OTHER
           Perform MAJOR-CONCERN-PROC
END-Evaluate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365E3844.5407B8DE@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<73hp1r$1p8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <19981125164916.03997.00000501@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
Because Robert waxed so well on this subject, a little history may
be useful.

Most of the people who first put the spec of COBOL on paper where
UNIVAC I programmers. This means they were used to having only
1000 twelve character words of memory for I/O buffers, and program.
Overlay (segmentation) was common.  Programming was done i machine
language based upon the letters of the alphabet. A = add, etc.
Addresses ranged from 000 to 999.  R00100U00120 represented a perform.
A Go to was 00000U00nnn.  We were taught to modify instructions to
achieve our goals. It was easy to plug-in a number in the nnn portion of
the skip U instruction or any other instruction followed by a U
instruction, the number of the address one needed to go to. Remember
go to had not been invented.

When COBOL was invented, the Go to Depending on was added to allow
the generation of this kind of code.

Now, the part I consider funny about the whole thing is that Univac
's first compiler decided that no one would want to go to more than 26
paragraphs so that was a given limitation. Initial usage by USERS
showed that address modification needed to be more than 26.  I remember
beating on someone's desk to make this point.

Now, I agree with Robert that those who are not used to it may find this
a difficult concept. Yet, I read that "pointers" are among the features
of the working new standard. With pointers, one could code IDS if there
is a good randomizer. I may be off the deep
end here, but I wanted to share this history to place it into
perspective.

Warren Simmons

RKRayhawk wrote:

-----snip of go to depending on material ----------
> The vendor now discourages use of HANDLE technology. Although the technique
> works quite reliably, it is very powerful and can snow even an experienced
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> division.

------more snip-----
> 
> Control vectors are the quintessence of object oriented programming. If the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365D410B.423E0F7F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<73hp1r$1p8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <19981125164916.03997.00000501@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
First my opinion:

   The ONLY go to is a 'go to exit' and this should be avoided!   This
is really a 
   return statement or end-of-paragraph (whatever the next standard
said).

RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
... The scissors are out ...

> 
> In languages like C you have robust facilities for this: multi-dimensional
> matrices of function pointers.  The key to using this kind of technology is to
> think in terms of indexes. Most COBOLers do not think that way.
> 
...   snipping again   ...
> 
> All the same, I feel confident that if we just had a computed _perform_,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> RKRayhawk@aol.com


  how about:
	01  table.
	    05 call-names pic x(08) occurs x times.
        01 filler redefines table.
	    05 filler  pic x(08) value 'call1'.
	    05 filler  pic x(08) value 'call2'.
	     ....

	move call-names(index) to run-me.
	call run-me using standard-parameters.	

It is not a perform but it is the same thing in cobol terms.   Be
careful of your 
compiler options in MVS though.

If you really want to do these sort of things then you really are
talking OO and
inheritance. Use it, don't play with some midway solution.

Now when Rational Rose releases a cobol generator for the UML stuff I
mught be in but
the typing for OO in cobol has totally put me off.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

- **Message-ID:** `<36613113.C3AB260A@zip.com.au>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> RKRayhawk@aol.com

If you look at the IBM MVS spec that I did it appears that you more or
less construct a COMPLETE cobol program for every operation in a class.
With fair dinkum OO programs the number of operations tend to be very
large.  Each simple operation has a routine to handle it, SET-DOB,
GET-DOB, CURRENT-AGE, AGE-AT-DATE would all be operations on the DOB
field which would be one field of many in a customer object.  This would
quickly add up.

I only read the manual, was I wrong?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981130104316.16775.00000147@ng131.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36613113.C3AB260A@zip.com.au>`

```
From: Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
Date: Sun, 29 Nov 1998 22:33:39 +1100

Commented further concerning being put off by some of what he sees in OO, ...

<<
If you look at the IBM MVS spec that I did it appears that you more or
less construct a COMPLETE cobol program for every operation in a class.
With fair dinkum OO programs the number of operations tend to be very
large.  Each simple operation has a routine to handle it, SET-DOB,
GET-DOB, CURRENT-AGE, AGE-AT-DATE would all be operations on the DOB
field which would be one field of many in a customer object.  This would
quickly add up.

I only read the manual, was I wrong?
>>

You are not wrong, nor are you alone in your concern.  The practice in the
field is sometimes (emphasis _sometimes_) great over elaboration of field
manipulation.

Once you climb to the high diving platform of 'insulating data types' from
outside perception, you must have a deep pond to jump into. Which frequently
means that every single field gets wrapped with a function for every single
kind of manipulation and interrogation.

Even though this seems to increase some folks' belief in the longevity of the
code, the geometric increase in the amount of code necessary must in fact
reduce reliability.

Much of what appears in OOP code is overhead for the coding methodology itself,
crowding other meaningful expressions off of the viewing surface. Coders and
maintainers necessarily see less at one time.

There must be an impact on an individuals ability to see the over all picture
when that individual must view more detail to understand the discrete
components.

So a diminished capacity to see the larger picture combined with the
statistical probability of increased error from the growth of the coding
artifacts means that OOP can not be more reliable in all cases.

The benefit that comes from OOP is perhaps manifold. And maybe no one should
undertake to summarize such a large social trend as either all good or all bad.
 

IMHO, the primary benefit of OOP derives from the fact that the approach gets
people to think differently about the problem domain. For some applications
that is perhaps greatly beneficial. 

And the syntax tools for OOP tend to keep coders in the same reference point to
the problem. And that is where the tough issues arise.

Practitioners of object oriented design frequently insist that you can conceive
of the whole environment as objective, even down to the field level.  The
question is, what are the benefits weighed against the costs.

Surely we can, but _should_ we think of each field as an object?

It is as though the greatest return from application of Object Oriented
Analysis comes from applying it to system design and data design at a high
level.  But the closer you get to fields (attributes) the more object
orientation costs you in code artifacts and concomitant reliability.

In object oriented programming at the code level functions and data items are
_both_ conveived of as objects.
Right there is where we lose control of costs and reliability. Not because of a
falacy in theory. The thoery is right. The universe _is_ a fluid recursion. 

But you can not program representations of problem domains with infinitely
recursive symbols.
The paradox comes from the fact that money and programmer consciousness are not
recursive at all, they are terribly linear in availability.

What is actually true today, I think ('today' is a pretty big world), is that
work environments when guaged in units of projects, is highly segregated.
Managers, human resource personnel, recruiters and team leaders select only
adherents of object orientation for object oriented projects; and only
classical methodologists for flatlander projects.

Frankly, individuals seem to have the prejudices about this stuff and there is,
if my perceptions are correct, a preference to work with your own kind
vis-a-vis the object/classic methodology axis.

Consequently, projects seem to be handled by groups which behave without checks
and balances. Coding results are sometimes extreme.

I believe that the results in object oriented projects are sometimes extreme
over insulation of fields, and a form of code giganticism that will cost some
organizations dearly at a future date.

In some cases (emphasis _some_) object oriented programs are atleast an order
of magnitude larger than boring flat model programs.  I am certain that we do
not at this time have any institutions that are increasing the number of
available programmers by an order of magnitude.

Perhaps the thing to do is to end the prejudice. We probably must first end the
segregation at the academic institutions.  Faculties should be rebalanced,
adding back in _experienced_ practitioners of classical methodologies.
Curriculums should be less dominated by object oriented preferences.

We should seriously examine the bias of the universities in favor of the
Microsoft operating system and tools, with a keen eye to understanding what
this myopia is doing to cost fore corporate and government users of data
processing technology.

We should have an open public discussion of the fact that many object oriented
methods are nothing but advertisements for the universities that propose them.
In such circumstances it is very difficult for students to question the
applicability of the object oriented approach to some problem domains.

And, of course, staffing practices deserve a general review. If projects are
staffed with at least a few flat model experts, some of the excesses can be
prevented.

The current wisdom is that anyone who opposes object oriented methods per force
should not be on an object oriented project. And people wonder why it is taking
so much money! Duh!

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3663574C.31BB@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<36613113.C3AB260A@zip.com.au> <19981130104316.16775.00000147@ng131.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> From: Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> kind of manipulation and interrogation.

[snip]

If you find yourself writing access functions for all of an object's
internal fields, then at least one of two things is probably 
happening:

1. You are abusing the class by violating its encapsulation;

2. You are trying to apply the OO paradigm to an area where it is
   not a good fit.

Much of the point of wrapping something up in an object is to hide
its internals -- first so that the client code doesn't have to be
concerned with the internals, and secondly so that you can change
the implementation of the internals without changing the client code.

If you don't have anything to hide, then you might as well use an
ordinary group item, leaving its component parts defined in a copybook
and open to the public.  You can still put procedural code in a 
subprogram, using different entry points to take the place of methods.

This approach some of the advantages of true object orientation but is 
more flexible than IBM's version of OO COBOL (which I think is what Ken 
Foskey is using.)  In particular, it lets you declare per-class data
as well as per-instance data without breaking encapsulation.

On the other hand: if you want to use inheritance and polymorphism,
there's no very easy way to fake it.  Define your base class, swallow
hard, and write a bunch of access routines.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73uqc2$bnr$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36613113.C3AB260A@zip.com.au> <19981130104316.16775.00000147@ng131.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981130104316.16775.00000147@ng131.aol.com>...

>IMHO, the primary benefit of OOP derives from the fact that the approach
gets
>people to think differently about the problem domain. For some applications
>that is perhaps greatly beneficial.


A very interesting post.  The above point struck home hard.
The more I think about OO programming, the more I think
that it should be implemented at a low level, not a high level. It
strikes me that rather than implementing (for example) an
application at the OO level, we should be thinking of things
like the "screen" as an object, and respecifying the screen
section.

I can see each data name in a screen section as an object
with the from/to portions being methods that are triggered
by field changes, for example. You would invoke a screen
name, and the data would update.  Now if someone would
write code that read an existing screen section as a source
document and produce the appropriate Cobol Code, that
would be a worthwhile use of OOP.

The second major point that you make, and an extremely
important one, is that OOP is necessarily recursive. Cobol,
traditionally, has never been recursive, and re-training your
though patterns to include the availability of recursion requires
a major change in thinking.  As a fan of the very simple
recursive threaded languages (I always liked Forth), I have been
struggling for years to overcome the lack in Cobol. My current
efforts involve being able to call EXE modules in a returnable
manner ... thereby using the OS itself to provide the infinite
stack.  That has the added benefit of being language transparent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981130151201.20613.00000246@ng21.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<73uqc2$bnr$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 1998 14:16:04 -0500

said in part,  

<<
As a fan of the very simple
recursive threaded languages (I always liked Forth), I have been
struggling for years to overcome the lack in Cobol. My current
efforts involve being able to call EXE modules in a returnable
manner ... thereby using the OS itself to provide the infinite
stack.  That has the added benefit of being language transparent.
>>

We are in agreement. For my part I would like to say that Forth is one of the
more remarkable achievments in the data processing history.  

It is a language that is inherently recursive but without all the surface
clutter of languages like LISP. And it is a language that can function
mightlily in environments that have very scarce resources.

And you make a good point about the lack of recursion in COBOL. But balancing
this weakness is the fact that to-date we have paid programmers in COBOL
environments very little to deal with memory management, garbage collection and
memory leak problems. (I am not saying we have paid them zero, I wish I could
say that I have never made serious mistakes with CICS get mains).

Forth rips up the pavement in some problem domains. COBOL, ... well, ... it
does't do much ripping of the pavement, but for domains that can be modelled
with flat linguistic enumeration, it has been very very cost effective.

As we try to enhance COBOL to strap it onto new classes of problems, an
automated memory management for recursive calls would be useful. But this will
require bringing to the surface (that is coding) the difference between static
and dynamic data items. The question becomes, when to be that complex and when
to do it the old simple way.  It is a matter of balance.

In the same way, the question of whether to code a key-value as a data-item
versus when to code it as a function, is a matter of balance.

When our judgement leads us to an implementation that contains so much code
that we can not find the piece we need, and can not tell what is going on
over-all when we get to the detail; then we have an issue of costs and
reliability.  

When every field on a single screen is an array of functions, there is a chance
that in _some_ cases that is going too far. It is too far into the pocket book,
and too far into increased unreliability because of all the additional code
points (branches, entries, exits).

And for a text editor, turning every key into a dynamically replacable
function, is justified if the market we serve delivers a reward for the
flexibility. But if there is no need for the flexibility, or if the amount of
reward is not sufficient; then simple, less expensive, and more reliable coding
strategies might be more appropriate.

I think COBOL needs a little more as a language to move with the web. We need
verbs that can dispatch procedures with minimum keystrokes, whether those
procedures are paragraphs or CALLed programs. We need to be able to model
function lists as tables, and to be able to harness those lists to actuators
easily. And you are right, we need to be able to do it in a way that is
language transparent, and a lot less cumbersome than engaging the OS with .EXE
triggers.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73v4fv$hco$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73uqc2$bnr$1@news.igs.net> <19981130151201.20613.00000246@ng21.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981130151201.20613.00000246@ng21.aol.com>...

>easily. And you are right, we need to be able to do it in a way that is
>language transparent, and a lot less cumbersome than engaging the OS with
.EXE
>triggers.


I have been playing with an idea.  Picture a machine with
a 35-40 BYTE address space, and an 80 BYTE memory
space.  Addresses can be any combination of printable ASCII,
so the entire thing can be an ISAM file.  Creating memory is
as simple as writing a record; memory space can appear
and disappear.

The machine code for said machine consists of
OPCODE,  ARG1,ARG2.  All three are ASCII. The OPCODE
is any exe module, the arguments are any command line
arguments.  Write a forth interpret for said machine.
What opcodes do we need?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36631f79.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<73uqc2$bnr$1@news.igs.net> <19981130151201.20613.00000246@ng21.aol.com> <73v4fv$hco$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <73v4fv$hco$1@news.igs.net>...
>I have been playing with an idea.  Picture a machine with
>a 35-40 BYTE address space, and an 80 BYTE memory
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>What opcodes do we need?


A Dutchman (Van der Poel) showed in 1956 that the minimum
number of opcodes for a general machine that can do everything
a 'normal' machine can do is ONE. A more practical example
is the earliest version of the Manchester MU1 (1951), which had
seven operations: subtract, load negative, store, skip on zero,
absolute and relative unconditional branch, and stop. One can
reduce this list to three: subtract, store, and branch on minus,
which again are sufficient.

So, the question is not: what do we need?, but: what do we want?
Interesting idea, Donald...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ralph Jones <leclay@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36633EFF.3E8B4741@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<73uqc2$bnr$1@news.igs.net> <19981130151201.20613.00000246@ng21.aol.com> <73v4fv$hco$1@news.igs.net>`

```
You've just re-invented the CDC 160A & the 8090 teleprogrammer. :-)

Now you could expand it to be a 3 address machine:

OPCODE,  Mvariant, Nvariant, ARG1,ARG2,ARG3
And we have the Boroughs B280 Improved.
The characters set is BCD, Binary Coded Decimal, defined by 6 bit
characters.

This allows you to test two addresses and branch to a 3rd.
Or in two instructions:
Test two addresses
Branch High, Low, Equal based upon the result from the previous
instruction.
etc. ... etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
>
>Now you could expand it to be a 3 address machine:
>
>OPCODE,  Mvariant, Nvariant, ARG1,ARG2,ARG3


I am thinking of OPCODES that are Cobol programs.  A typical
opcode might look like:
        merge fileone,filetwo
(or the above, or both).  The idea is sort of a Forth
that is really an extremely powerful Batch programming
language that can deal with largish objects like menus
and accounting subsystems.
You could, in fact, design such a system that was a virtual
computer.  In fact, every machine ever built is a subsystem.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <73hjqn$s6o$1@news.igs.net> <73hltr$j32$1@callisto.clark.net> <73idbt$cf6$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <73idbt$cf6$1@news.igs.net>,
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <73hltr$j32$1@callisto.clark.net>...
>>In article <73hjqn$s6o$1@news.igs.net>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

[snippage of blinding lights and presumptous assumptions]

>I assume that you assumed that I had
>not tried it.

And *there* you have it... I am always bemused by this presumption of
assumption when all which has occurred is a question of fact or a
nominative statement.  Consider the following:

A: 'There is no joy in Mudville.'
B: 'Why are you assuming that?'
A: 'I am assuming *nothing*, I am stating that there is no joy in
Mudville.  I may be *wrong*, there *may* be joy in Mudville and my
statement is incorrect... but there is no assumption in my statement.'

A: 'Is there any joy in Mudville?'
B: 'Why are you assuming that there is/isn't?'
A: 'There is no assumption of quantity in my question... merely one of
capacity.  Given that Mudville is a place sufficient like all
other places in which joy might/might not be found; given *that*
assumption then no others are made when inquiring into quantity.'

Likewise with my question to you, Mr Tees (was anyone wondering when I
might get back to that?); the question of 'have you or have you not'
indicates no assumption of condition of use.

>Assuming you can figure that out, why too
>easy?

The above, of course, for the accusation of assumption.. the analogy, of
course, because there are conditions under which insulin is required and
beneficial.  To broaden the argument, then: to say 'I have never/I do not
(perform action x)' indicates only what one's experiences, up to this
point, have shown to be surviveable actions.

>
>BTW, Can you punctuate the following so it makes sense?
>
>Doc where Don had had had had had had
>had had had had been prefered.

Something along the likes of that that that that that that that sentence
had, was wrong, perhaps?

>
>Let the end conditional gang handle that.

Perhaps they'll take it under conditional advisement.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: English parsing.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365f46a3.20394805@netnews>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <73hcrd$ol9$1@news.igs.net> <73hdq7$h3p$1@callisto.clark.net> <73hjqn$s6o$1@news.igs.net> <73hltr$j32$1@callisto.clark.net> <73idbt$cf6$1@news.igs.net>`

```
'Twas Wed, 25 Nov 1998 21:18:54 -0500, when "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>BTW, Can you punctuate the following so it makes sense?
>
>Doc where Don had had had had had had
>had had had had been prefered.

It parses two different ways.  Either 

Doc -- where Don had had "had had" -- had had "had".  
"Had had" had been prefered.

Or

Doc -- where Don had had "had" -- had had "had had".  
"Had had" had been prefered.

Punctuate this:

There are three subspecies of gorilla gorilla gorilla gorilla gorilla
gorilla gorilla gorilla manyema and gorilla gorilla berengei

Or punctuate this:

Woman without her man is nothing
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365e5646.3824469@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 25 Nov 1998 07:28:57 -0700, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?
Howard,

a GO TO DEPENDING is more or less a disguised CASE statement, without a common exit.  As
such it is not a "structured programming" element and might decrease readability of the
source.  I, personnally, don't really like, and prefer EVALUATE very much (which makes a
perfect match with my other love, the 88 level....)


With kind regards
Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365C6640.30C4447D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <365e5646.3824469@news3.ibm.net>`

```
> a GO TO DEPENDING is more or less a disguised CASE statement, without a common exit.  As
> such it is not a "structured programming" element and might decrease readability of the
> source.  I, personnally, don't really like, and prefer EVALUATE very much (which makes a
> perfect match with my other love, the 88 level....)

I suspect everybody would prefer EVALUATE.  But I don't use it very much
either.  This IS post-structured, but tends to make long conditionals. 
Sometimes it is the best tool for the job though.  I never found GO TO
DEPENDING to be the best tool for the job.
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)

reformatting job from 9 hours to 1 hour. Today, I would use
reference modification for the same problem.

Still, I like having it available, just in case.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<365C4483.FC84DCCE@home.com>`

```
> I remember using it once, in 1977. Reduced a month-end tape
> reformatting job from 9 hours to 1 hour. Today, I would use
> reference modification for the same problem.

Could you give some more detail?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%9Z62.1$Lx2.57@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <35X62.307$7a.274@news1.atlantic.net> <365C4483.FC84DCCE@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <365C4483.FC84DCCE@home.com>...
>> I remember using it once, in 1977. Reduced a month-end tape
>> reformatting job from 9 hours to 1 hour. Today, I would use
>> reference modification for the same problem.
>
>Could you give some more detail?

The monthly processing created multiple tapes containing print
lines for several reports. The records were all 133 characters.
The first position contained a character for spacing of the print
lines. The remaining 132 characters contained the print line
itself.

The output tapes were sent to remote locatons for printing.
Each tape was formatted with 256 character blocks. During
the processing, the printer spacing character from the input
tape was converted to control characters for the printer used
at the remote locations.

The original program processed the input one character at a
time. First processing the spacing character then copying the
print line to the output block until the block was filled. It would
then write the block then start on a new block. Each output
tape was limited to no more than 10 000 blocks because the
first tape to each location was sent using a modem.

The program I wrote used a 512 character work area. The
work area was redefined into 256 elementary items of 132
characters each, with an offset of one character for each
redefines This allowed me to copy the print lines into the work
area with one move rather than character by character. To get
to the proper move statement, I used a GO TO DEPENDING
on the destination in the work area. This starting location was
between 1 and 256. Each paragraph that was a destination for
the GO contained the paragraph-name-xxx, a MOVE print-line
TO work-area-xxx, and a GO to a common paragraph which
added 132 to the output location, wrote the output record, etc.

This would have been a lot of time at the 029 but I wrote a
small COBOL program to punch all of the cards in the redefines
and in the body of the loop replacing xxx with the correct
number. I then inserted the cards for the rest of the program.

The previous program created one tape every 45 minutes.
The replacement program created one tape every 4 minutes
but the rewind time explains the "only" 9:1 improvement.

I later rewrote the program in Assembler F using FEOV
to use two output drives. The time dropped to 3:45 per tape
with no rewind delay. The times were wall clock times. The
machine was a 360/40 DOS 26.2.

Is that enough detail?
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick Ownby)
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365c7147.8881976@news2.humboldt1.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 25 Nov 1998 07:28:57 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

I generally try to avoid it, but it can be useful in some cases and
may be desireable under older compilers (w/o EVALUATE).

In the 70's I wrote a program to analize high volume transmissions
from remote terminals in a service bureau environment. The lines in a
transmission related to transactions within multiple subsystems (A/P,
A/R, ect.).  At any point in the data the process was in a specific
state; for example, A/R system - Payment journal - Payment
transaction.  A portion of each transmission line was a numeric code
which could have slightly different meanings within each subsystem.

My memory is somewhat fuzzy here, but the point is that I used
multiple GO TO DEPENDING's in conjunction with ALTERed GO TO's
to process the different states, and everyone was impressed at the
processing speed of the new program.

I now shun the ALTERed GO TO, but still carefully and clearly use GO
TO DEPENDING when it seems reasonable under IBM System/36 COBOL.

Kindrick
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XGX62.142$Pc1.455278@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>...
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

I have used it once in 13+ years of COBOL development. I was porting a
mainframe assembler routine for bit operations to COBOL for an AS/400. The
combination of a rigid definition of the call parameter interface, and  the
need for optimum performance made GO TO DEPENDING the right choice. In any
other circumstance I would have used an EVALUATE/PERFORM or redesigned the
program with multiple entry points.
```


#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365CD1C2.8EDF784A@att.net>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

It also smacks of the basic FORTRAN IF statement, in which you code
three possibilities. I'm super rusty in FORTRAN, but didn't it have a
computed GOTO very much like a GOTODEPENDINGON? Would Bob Rayhawk
conclude/suspect the early programmers were mapping more mathematically
than linguistically?

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365f2a02.13089726@netnews>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <365CD1C2.8EDF784A@att.net>`

```
'Twas 26 Nov 1998 03:57:02 GMT, when William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>It also smacks of the basic FORTRAN IF statement, in which you code
>three possibilities. I'm super rusty in FORTRAN, but didn't it have a
>computed GOTO very much like a GOTODEPENDINGON? Would Bob Rayhawk
>conclude/suspect the early programmers were mapping more mathematically
>than linguistically?

The Fortran IF statement existed because some old computer (IBM 1401 comes
to mind) had an operator which did the same thing.

Also, in the 1950 programmers used to generate branch tables by adding a
variable to an address.  It was feared that higher level languages would
be too inefficient if programmers lost the branch table, so it was
implemented in Fortran and Cobol.

IIRC, the Fortran statement looked like this:

    GOTO (10, 20, 50), K

Omission of the last comma was a very common source of syntax errors.

I wouldn't use GO TO DEPENDING in a new code, but I recall one that would
have been hard to remove.  The Burroughs Thrift System was designed for a
service bureau serving multiple banks.  The dumb terminals sent a very
short transaction, which contained an account type, a transaction type,
and a modifier.  For each bank, there was a table indexed by these three
variables, which yielded a two digit number.  There was then a large GO TO
DEPENDING which directed the transaction to the correct routine.  Thus
account type 2, tran type 1, modifier 1 might map to routine 12 for bank
#1 yet map to routine 15 for bank #2.  Then when bank #2 decides they also
want to run routine 12 we might define account type 2, tran type 1,
modifier 5 to routine 12.

This would likely be implemented completely differently today, but in it's
time GO TO DEPENDING was a sensible way to do this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NTS72.11540$Gy.4795301@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com> <365CD1C2.8EDF784A@att.net> <365f2a02.13089726@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
>
>I wouldn't use GO TO DEPENDING in a new code, but I recall one that would
>have been hard to remove.  The Burroughs Thrift System was designed for a
>service bureau serving multiple banks. [snip]

Randall, did you ever work with BHAS (Burroughs Hospital Administrative
System, I think)?  Getting a problem BHAS system up and running was my
first assignment with Burroughs in 1972 (after 4 years programming in the
USAF).
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73ib0k$tc7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
In article <365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>,
  Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
> Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
> used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Howard,

I've used this when Parsing and Formatting ISO8583 Authorization Messages,
which have the potential of having bits 001 through 128 present. I convert the
Bit-Map to a Byte-Map and test each byte for a TRUE value. The routine has
paragraph labels that coincide with each positive condition and address the
specific requirements of that field (bit). The generated PMAP is much more
efficient than 128 EVALUATE WHEN tests, which generate 128 CLI instructions.

However, in most practical situations, EVALUATE should be used. Also, the
Structured Programming folks feel that any type of GO TO is a No-NO. In my
case, speed and maintainability were the most critical issues.

Well, that's my two cents.

HTH....

Happy Holidays,

WOB,
Atlanta


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp@netdoornospam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tqy72.2443$b53.12265@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```
Our shop uses a product called "cscript" to create new programs and
generated code makes use of this twice in each program.  On maintenance
programs the user chooses a numbered field to change and a generated GO TO
DEPENDING will handle the update.  To take it out of our shop would be far
worse than our Y2K conversion.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>...
>Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I have
>used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored with GO
>TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
>
>What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?
```

#### ↳ Re: Go To depending

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00083be7@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<365C1429.C42261A9@home.com>`

```

HB> Most of my work has been done with older compilers, but even when I
HB> have used newer compilers that allowed them, I have not been enamored
HB> with GO TO DEPENDING.  (I have used it in other languages)
HB>
HB> What do people here think of this statement in COBOL?

Well - it is a GO TO and a GO TO is a GO TO...

To code GO TO DEPENDING can be a good idea where performance is a vital  
issue (it's a lot faster than EVALUATE), but normally COBOL-performance is  
a matter of file I/O not of CPU time.

In all other cases (menu branches, etc.) where GO TO DEPENDING is often  
used, it leads to bad style, because it leads to more GO TOs at the end of  
the subroutines or more that one program exit.

Best regards

Sven

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

<a id="t-dcb94365c7"></a>
## Thread: re Fujitsu V 4 — 24 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### re Fujitsu V 4

- **From:** "John & Marie Sampson" <jamsam@cygnus.uwa.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>`

```
I have recently acquired version 4 and have noted the following:
1. The editor is the same as version 3 except that it no longer colours
reserved words.
    The colouring still works because comments  show up in green.
2.The ordinary screen handler will not recognise the esc key ,nor will it
recognise
    F10,F11,or F12.
3.In general it is a much less tolerant compiler than MF.There is a program
from MF
   published on the net called "case".It is interesting because it  parses
source code.
   The  V4 compiler stopped when it ran out of space in the error table
after 500 errors.
   The screen handler from Judson published in Cobol Unleashed also runs
foul of the
   compiler because of esc as outlined above,exit paragraph,level 78,use of
named
   colours instead of explicit integers and the use of an identifier instead
of an integer
   in an occurs clause.
I`d be grateful for feedback if the group experts think  I`ve failed to read
the manual
  or made some mistake in installing or operating,

  John Sampson
   Western Australia
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews>...

>I can beat that.  I once got 900 errors on one line!


Years ago (early 70's) I wrote Cobol for the Cyber 7400. It had three
categories of errors: non-fatal (assumption made), fatal (no object code,
but continued compilation), and catastrophic. I only saw one
catastrophic error.  It said: Catastrophic error -- more than 5000
assumptions made causing stack overflow. Compilation halted.
I think I had dropped the deck in missing the identification division
card.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6d82.8829$w_6.5432044@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews> <73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>Years ago (early 70's) I wrote Cobol for the Cyber 7400. It had three
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>card.


My first COBOL program had so many errors it blew the compiler.  It was
probably about 100 lines, but the listing stretched 30' across the room.
Don't know how many errors there were, because the compiler blew before
printing the count and I didn't count them.  The other students were
impressed. :-)  When I wrote my first production program (in the USAF)
I sent it over to operations for a compile on the brand new Burroughs
B3500.  A couple of hours later I received an urgent call to get my butt
to the computer center, *A*S*A*P*.  Scared to death, knees shaking, I
walked into a room of pandemonium.  The system was down, hardware and
software techs were crawling all over the place, and my program listing
was spread out on one of the disk units with three or four techs pouring
over it.  This was Headquarters, Tactical Air Command, we were at war in
Vietnam, and my first program had just brought down the entire system.
I was certain that within the next few minutes I would be arrested and
eventually shot.  Turns out, I had omitted one of the division or section
headers.  They were so relieved at finding the problem that they didn't
shoot me.  They kept the listing, and I didn't ask how many syntax errors
there were.  It never occurred to me before this minute, but maybe that
event is what made me such a fanatic for precision? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73so7g$1re$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews> <73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net> <B6d82.8829$w_6.5432044@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>over it.  This was Headquarters, Tactical Air Command, we were at war in
>Vietnam, and my first program had just brought down the entire system.
>I was certain that within the next few minutes I would be arrested and
>eventually shot <snip>

I wonder who in here can claim the most catastrophic error ... I once
managed to fill a truck with 850,000 pounds of crushed stone.  It only
took the front end loader twenty minutes to dig it out though, and I had
asked the driver to get out ... mind you, that loader had a 20 yard
bucket.

I will plead outside influences though, on that one.  The gate controlling
the flow was hydraulic, and I had designed the system with a failsafe
switch that cut power to the interface.  Power down, and the gate
automatically closed as the hydraulic solenoid was normally closed.
Unfortunately, some electrician bypassed the switch the day before.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fDv82.10604$Sf1.5961969@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews> <73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net> <B6d82.8829$w_6.5432044@news2.mia> <73so7g$1re$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>I wonder who in here can claim the most catastrophic error ...

This wasn't catastrophic, and it wasn't mine, but the error which put
the programmer under the most pressure I ever saw was around 1970.  You
can probably only truly appreciate this if you've been in the military.
This little two-striper, an easy going, soft spoken, timid programmer
from Miami worked in payroll at Headquarters, Tactical Air Command, with
20 bases.  He had made an error in an update to the bi-monthly military
payroll system.  When they started the payroll run it bombed.  Panic
time.  At TAC, two things took top priority, first was 'mission critical'
stuff, and next was military payroll.  Nothing, but nothing, got in the
way of finishing those on time.  They even had full computer systems set
up on trucks and in airplanes that could be put onsite in hours, even in
Vietnam, to get those out.  We're talking probable congressional hearings
if the troops don't get paid on time.  Heads would roll, big time.  This
little programmer was sitting in his cubicle all alone, working on the
bug.  You could almost hear a pin drop, because every one of the 75
programmers in the building were huddled together whispering, watching in
awe and amazement.  The comptroller of TAC, a bird colonel, was pacing up
and down outside the guy's cubicle, muttering.  The commander of TAC, a
general, was on the phone to the comptroller's office, messengers were
scurrying in and out.  The base commanders of all TAC bases were pacing
up and down in their offices, on the phone to the TAC commander's office.
Operations had shut down everything but this guy's compiles and test runs,
waiting to make 20 copies of the final tape.  Fighter planes were sitting
on the flightline with pilots in the cockpits and the engines running,
ready to ferry the tapes to all the TAC bases across the country.  We
knew all this because we could overhear the conversations between the
comptroller and the messengers.  The little guy made it, but how he got
it done under those conditions, I do not know.  When he came out of there,
he looked like he had been run over by a truck.  Everybody cheered, and
the little guy staggered home for the rest of the day.  Ever since then,
when I think of real pressure, I remember that day.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73u9rc$1q7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews> <73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net> <B6d82.8829$w_6.5432044@news2.mia> <73so7g$1re$1@news.igs.net> <fDv82.10604$Sf1.5961969@news3.mia>`

```
Pressure I have been under, and worse than a payroll. I did
the concrete plant software for the building of five nuclear
reactors.  Once you start pouring concrete for one of those,
you have to keep going.  If you stop long enough for the
concrete to dry, then you tear it out and start over.  Each
major pour takes about two months.  Because they were
fast-tracking, they were changing my hardware as I worked.

A few times I sat there coding like hell to get things working
again, and knowing that if I took more that an hour it was
going to be a two or three million dollar bill, a minimum
shutdown of four months, and three thousand men out
of work for the period.

To make it even more pleasant, the main boss was an
alcoholic that used to stand behind me and curse at the
length of time things took as I worked.  I remember one
day getting so damned annoyed at this guy that I turned
around and told him to "get the f out of my computer
room or I would stick the damned computer up his ass
and his assistant could press the keys with the tip of
his ...

What is that old military saying?  Grasp them firmly by
the balls and their hearts and minds will follow?
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73s3be$jss$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <365CD227.3546DBDE@mindspring.com> <365f3b1f.17456702@netnews> <73q0op$qtu$1@news.igs.net> <B6d82.8829$w_6.5432044@news2.mia>`

```
I think the one thing that Cobol can claim above all other languages
is the length of its error listings when you blow it.  When I see pages
of errors, I seldom fix more that a couple before a recompile. 99% of
the time, it is a copy statement spelt wrong or some such ..

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365DDB0E.623C6F2B@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>`

```
John & Marie Sampson wrote:
> 3.In general it is a much less tolerant compiler than MF.There is a program
> from MF
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    in an occurs clause.

Well, Fujitsu follows ANSI-85 standard and not MF implementation of it.
It is an interesting question what standards are more important,
official standards or de-facto standards.
Apparently, Fujitsu decided to follow the official standard path.
As to 500 errors, try running case.cbl thru C++ compiler -- 
you may get even more errors.

On a brighter side, new version of mf2fsc (3.2.1) that has 
a nice looking manual will be released soon and FSC is expected
to start selling it when it's released.

After it converts case.cbl, there is only 1 S-level error left,
so it's an improvement over 500 original errors.
More info on mf2fsc is available at http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/

Let's hope that mf2fsc would solve Micro Focus to Fujitsu
migration issue.

As to editor, you can use any editor you prefer,
even though integration with ProjectManager will not be as 
good as with native editor. I personally use Emacs.
Also I understand that FSC has a newer versio of editor
that is better than what you describe.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73rtjt$bum$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>`

```
In article <73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au>,
  "John & Marie Sampson" <jamsam@cygnus.uwa.edu.au> wrote:
> I have recently acquired version 4 and have noted the following:
> 1. The editor is the same as version 3 except that it no longer colours
> reserved words.
>     The colouring still works because comments  show up in green.

Having just installed my V4, let me explain how you can fix this.  I appears
that some settings in V3 conflict or confuse those in V4.  Unstall the entire
product.  Then DELETE the \FSC directory and all subdirectories.  Then
re-install V4.	All these problems will go away (Including the problem of
searching with a -).  Your source will be properly colorized.

> 2.The ordinary screen handler will not recognise the esc key ,nor will it
> recognise
>     F10,F11,or F12.

These can be customized - Fujitsu provides some excellent documentation on
how to handle this in the Users Guide and in the Version 4 release notes.

> 3.In general it is a much less tolerant compiler than MF.There is a program
> from MF
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> after 500 errors.

MicroFocus COBOL is not fully ANSI compliant and has many "Extensions" that
are used - some of these are not tolerated by Fujitsu - it is much closer to
ANSI standard COBOL.

>    The screen handler from Judson published in Cobol Unleashed also runs
> foul of the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    in an occurs clause.

78 levels are a MicroFocus extension.  A little conversion is necessary for
screen sections.  Fujitsu stuck VERY close to the X/Open standard for this -
and they do not support all of the features that MicroFocus supports with the
screen section.

> I`d be grateful for feedback if the group experts think  I`ve failed to read
> the manual
>   or made some mistake in installing or operating,
>

A lot of your problems can be corrected by uninstalling, deleting the \FSC
directory and all subs, and re-installing.

There are a lot of users of Fujitsu in this news group.  Let us know if you
need more help!

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: re Fujitsu V 4

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73sfbg$snm@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<73i52s$ugu$1@enyo.uwa.edu.au> <73rtjt$bum$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

redsky@ibm.net wrote in message

   <much snippage>
>
>> 3.In general it is a much less tolerant compiler than MF.There is a
program
>> from MF
>>    published on the net called "case".It is interesting because it
parses
>> source code.
>>    The  V4 compiler stopped when it ran out of space in the error table
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>are used - some of these are not tolerated by Fujitsu - it is much closer
to
>ANSI standard COBOL.
>
   <more snippage>

This last statement is simply NOT true.  Micro Focus COBOL is FULLY ANSI
conforming (or as you say compliant).  If you run with either the FLAGSTD or
FLAG(ANSI) directives, you can find every single extension in your source
program.  Furthermore, unlike Fujitsu which canNOT handle all required ANSI
syntax (for example both literals and data names in the Select/Assign clause
for SD files), the Micro Focus compiler accepts ALL required ANSI syntax.

If you have any example of where Micro Focus
  A) fails to provide full ANSI conforming support (when the required
directives are selected)
        or
  B) fails to flag as an extension (as described in both the ANSI and FIPS
documentation) any non-Standard syntax, I know that I would be very
interested in it - and would be curious as to whether or not you have ever
reported the problem to Micro Focus and what their response was. (I *have*
reported a number of ANSI failures to Fujitsu - including the sort file
problem - and have seen NO interest on their part in fixing their product to
be ANSI conforming.)
```
