<a id="t-e1b8cb72bd"></a>
## Thread: When to use OO — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### When to use OO

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365D7EEE.2864@swbell.net>`

```
I've written up some thoughts about the application of Object Oriented 
techniques -- when they're appropriate and when they're not.  It's a 
bit long to post in a news group, so I posted it on my website at:

	http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/ooc/whenoo.html

I'm not sure how much I agree with my own conclusions.  I hope to spark 
some debate among those who are better qualified than myself (or at 
least more opinionated).

Summary:

I. OO is good for some things and not so good for others.  It's
   useful to know which is which.

II. Factors which favor the use of OO:

   A. Requirements are stable and well understood.  E.g.:

      1. Mathematical objects

      2. Principles of accounting

      3. Physical objects

   B. Requirements are largely under the control of the developer,
      typically because the classes represent entities which are 
      internal to the program.  E.g.:

      1. Graphical User Interfaces

      2. Data structures, i.e. container classes

      3. Games

   C. Classes can map recognizable entities in a natural and 
      intuitive way

   D. There is a natural and satisfying use for polymorphism

III. Factors which render OO less likely to succeed:

   A. Requirements are complex, capricious, and unstable, typically 
      due to:

      1. Laws, regulations, or business rules

      2. Poor or muddled understanding of problem domain

   B. Difficulty in coming up with a satisfying class design

   C. The use of classes as coding gimmicks rather than as
      representations of recognizable entities

   D. The need to store persistent objects

   E. The need, or temptation, to use multiple inheritance

Finally: happy Thanksgiving to all.  (For those who are not in the 
U.S.: Thanksgiving is a holiday whereon we give thanks to God for 
letting other people starve instead of us.)

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

---

## Thread: AcuCobol — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### AcuCobol

- **From:** schepker@geocities.com (Hajo Schepker)
- **Date:** 1998-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<365ee0c3.25968400@news.btu.de>`

```
Hi,
I have downloaded the evaluation copy and making some application.
Now I have a question:

How can I add code to menu-entries.

When I compile the project, I got many files, most of them are
generated and told, not to change manually.

Thanks,
H. Schepker
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol

- **From:** mattr@mentis.com.au
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73t3ed$8va$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<365ee0c3.25968400@news.btu.de>`

```
In article <365ee0c3.25968400@news.btu.de>,
  schepker@geocities.com wrote:
> Hi,
> I have downloaded the evaluation copy and making some application.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You'll notice that each file generated matches a section, like Working
Storage, etc.

You can change the code in the .cbl file, in these sections.  The reason it
says not to, is that if you change something in the Screen Painter, and
regenerate, you'll lose those chages.  Oh, one other thing, turn off Generate
Program File in the Options section of the Screen painter once you start
changing the .cbl file, so you don't overwrite your previous changes.

Matt Robinson
Support Consultant, Mentis Technologies
---------------------------------------
<insert something witty here>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73ugh1$hm0$1@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<365ee0c3.25968400@news.btu.de>`

```

Hajo Schepker wrote in message <365ee0c3.25968400@news.btu.de>...
>Hi,
>I have downloaded the evaluation copy and making some application.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

in the screen section click on the fourth icon from the bottom
drag it into the form area.
double click on it.
now you should have a dialog box.
double click where you see the <...>
that will open up and allow you to enter menu names.

Rob Annandale
Vancouver, BC
```

- **From:** "Kevin Cooley" <cooley@severnapark.com>

Kevin

<a id="t-a014cfa8f6"></a>
## Thread: power cobol — 4 this month / 5 total

### power cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73pmjr$k6s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Has anybody figured out a way for a power Cobol program
to either:  1.  return an error code (as an exe module)
                 2. accept an input argument (as a linkable [DLL or
otherwise] subroutine).
or              3. produce an output argument (ditto to number 2).

The only way that I can seem to pass data either into or
out of a power Cobol program is as a global external area
and that simply will not work in my application.  I can get data
into an exe module produced by power Cobol by introducing
it on the command line, but there appears to be no way to get
an answer back as the PROGRAM-STATUS register is used
internally and never makes it back to the starting program.

There also appears to be no way to write a power Cobol
subroutine, as all power Cobol programs *are* subroutines
started by the "opensheet" subroutine, and you cannot vary
the opensheet arguments.
```

#### ↳ Re: power cobol

- **From:** "DENNIS A BROUSE" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73u13a$4tlu$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<73pmjr$k6s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,
   Although I'm a novice at Powercobol, I have a Powercobol application that
communicates both ways with another application using DDE.  I realize that
this is not exactly what you are looking for, but the use of DDE in itself
implies that Powercobol can handle passing data between to different
applications.  If I have some time at work today, I'll see if I can dig into
it a little deeper.  Of course, it IS Monday!  Good luck.

Denny
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: power cobol

- **From:** "DENNIS A BROUSE" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73vn27$3444$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<73pmjr$k6s$1@news.igs.net> <73u13a$4tlu$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <73u8pm$16m$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,
     Looked in my notes today regarding Fujitsu Powercobol "called"
subroutines and it looks like the "global external data/opensheet"
environment is the only way to do it unless there's an undocumented feature
laying around somewhere.  I could have sworn that I read something on
creating custom controls that might fit the bill here, but I'll be darned if
I could find it today.  I'm in the process of re-writing our MicroFocus
COBOL programs into Fujitsu Powercobol, and I have a BBS program that will
need this feature to update the BBS database.  I may call Fujitsu support
when I get to that point and just ask.  Right now I'm still finishing up the
custom DDE interface to our mainframe terminal emulation program.  At least
for that application, we're communicating just fine and dandy.  It just
seems weird that you can call a Powercobol subroutine from regular Fujitsu
COBOL, but can't  call one from another Powercobol program.  If you get it
to work, it would be great if you could post how you did it.  I'll certainly
do the same, if I happen upon it relatively soon.

Denny
```

<a id="t-3537ec0cf1"></a>
## Thread: "structured programming" & code reusability — 21 this month / 60 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```
I have heard & seen much about code reusability as a benefit of
stuctured programming since the mid-70's when structured coding was the
buzzword of the industry (for mainframers).  But in reality, how much
code is actually suitable for reuse in other programs, and what methods
are available to catalog and document these routines, after all these
years.  Zilch !!

I have seen programs or routines cloned many times, and find that the
extraneous data areas and/or procedure code has not been removed, so
that it obscures to some extent the actual usable portion, and thusly
does not contribute to the maintainability of the new program.  The
reality is that most "production programmers" (as opposed to academics)
simply don't have time to clean up this cloned code, and in fact don't
normally even know of usable code routines other than those they have
either written or  maintained personally, as there is no facility to
search for these routines within an organization's code bank. 

I myself have found, over the years, that by the time I locate a
suitable  candidate segment of code, and clean it up, I might have well
have written it again from scratch, with everything about it tailored to
the task at hand.  It is simply more cost-effective, and that is what IT
as an organizational P&L center is judged on.  (Please let's not digress
into Dilbertism here).

Code reuse is an admirable goal, but the reality is far short of the
goal !


Bob  
```

#### ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PW082.300$Lx2.479@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```

Bob Berman wrote in message <366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>...
>I have heard & seen much about code reusability as a benefit of
>stuctured programming since the mid-70's when structured coding was the
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>goal !

" My experience is that the conditions necessary for reuse will exist
only if someone makes it their business to make such [code] sharing
work. In a small group, this typically means that an indiviidual, by design
or by accident, becomes the keeper of the common libraries and
documentation. In a larger organization, this means that a group or
department is chartered to gather, build, document, popularize, and
maintain software for use by many groups."
Bjarne Stroustrup, The C++ Programming Language, 3d Edition

If you want reuse to work, make it somone's responsibility to make
it work; then back them up 100%!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3660A528.3749@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <PW082.300$Lx2.479@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> " My experience is that the conditions necessary for reuse will exist
> only if someone makes it their business to make such [code] sharing
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> -------------------------------
Rick -
I agree with you (and your quoted reference) in theory, but have not
been able to sell the concept of this overhead position (or group) to
management since the downsizing of large corporations began in the late
80's.  

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qX382.341$Lx2.691@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <PW082.300$Lx2.479@news1.atlantic.net> <3660A528.3749@netbox.com>`

```

Bob Berman wrote in message <3660A528.3749@netbox.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>> If you want reuse to work, make it somone's responsibility to make
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Then let us begin by not thinking of the position as overhead. If 20%
of other types of products use standard parts or components, we would
call them purchased or say they were produced by another department.
Whoever selects the vocabulary, controls the discussion.

Alternatively, work with other like-minded people to identify parts of
each others projects which may be componentized. Each may then
work on a component that will be used by all who participate.
Proof of concept is a powerful sales tool -- even to management.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
I personally dont see much difference between many programs I've written. 

I steal my report routines especially data division titles again and again.
01 report detail.
    05           pic x.  (print attribute)
    05           pic x(10) value 'this is th'.
    05           pic x(10) value 'e way i se'.
    05           pic x(10) value 'ldom need '..
    05           pic x(10) value 'to count t'.
    05           pic x(10) value 'he number '.
    05           pic x(10) value 'of bytes i'.
    05           pic x(10) value 'n an 80 byt'.
    05           pic x(09) value 'e FBA rpt'.

In my shop, we enjoy the luxury of robust call routines that other groups
can call and get the data.
I sure dont want to hear from them with special extract file requests.  DO
IT YOURSELF.
My routines are well documented and point to samples that utilize these
call modules.


My structure of stand alone programs is almost all the same
1000-initialize.
2000-read.
3000-process.
4000-clean-up-after-yourself.
I copy in template after template. I hate to type.

I perform 3200-routine thru 3200-routine-exit if it makes it easier to
debug.
Of course 3200-routine is the last name i would name it.  If it works and
is tucked away in its own area, it doesnt clutter up my screen.

I think I have 8 programs that show me every COBOL trick I have seen on a
daily basis over the past years.

Wish all good luck.

#### ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3660afd1.929359@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```
Bob,

I work in a small shop (I am the only programmer), and I ALWAYS clean
up unsued code from cloned programs.  When I steal routines, I only
take what I need.  On the other hand, I usually leave the WS area
alone.  Sometime though, I will leave unused stuff in a progam if I
feel I may need to use it in the future.

Before I worked for this company, the past programmers objective was
to pump out code as fast as possible, without any clean up work, or
even thought on maintainabiliy.  After I started, it took about a year
but I was able to show and prove to the owner that spending a little
extra time keeping everything neat and tidy would pay off in the
future.

I also document ambiguous and complicated routines.  Not because it is
good practice, but because I have a lousy memory.  More than likely I
will be the one who has to fix/update the program in the future. : )

Take it easy,

Paul

><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: "structured programming" & code reusability
>From: pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

IMHO, one of the major stumbling blocks to reusability is the lack of
continuity - of staff and software.

Downsizing and the use of short-term consultants will impede any attempts to
develop in-house reusable components.  

Software "thrashing" - the rapid introduction of incompatible releases of what
is purported to be the same software - e.g., VB,  JAVA, PEOPLESOFT, SAP, 
MS-OFFICE and, sad to say, recent COBOL releases - has a tendency to render any
resuable components obsolete within a year or two, resulting in the phenomenon
of "one shot" reusable components.
```

#### ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```
Bob Berman wrote:
> 
> I have heard & seen much about code reusability as a benefit of
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> the task at hand.

The copy-and-modify approach is the lowest form of reuse, and it 
suffers from the kinds of problems you describe.

A better form of reuse is the use of separately compiled subprograms,
because the reuser doesn't (or shouldn't) have to look at the internals
of the subprogram, only at the interface and the documented behaviour
of the subprogram.

However, this approach calls for careful design, identifying chunks of
generic and reusable functionality.  In my experience most COBOL
programmers just don't think that way.  They are used to doing
everything in one huge monolithic top-level module.  At most they make
a few CALLs to some low-level utility modules, often written in 
Assembler because they do things you can't readily do in COBOL.

Documenting and publishing the available routines is another issue.
It's time-consuming and rather tedious, but I don't know why it would
be any worse for COBOL than it is for other languages.  For C, at 
least, there is a long tradition of buying commercial libraries of
routines for various purposes, or downloading free ones off the Net.
I don't know how many shops develop such libraries in house or how
well they reuse them across applications.

For my own application I developed a number of reusable routines and
documented them with web pages on the company intranet.  That's a
pretty good form of documentation because of the usefulness of
hypertext.  However, none of the people in other applications has
shown the slightest interest.  I guess I'm not a very good evangelist.

COBOL has no tradition of freeware or public domain code.  Our code
belongs to our employers and we can't share it, so we continually
rewrite the same code others have already written, feeling no
incentive to make it generally useful because we're the only ones
who will ever use it anyway.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73sdeq$of4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>`

```

Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>...

>COBOL has no tradition of freeware or public domain code.  Our code
>belongs to our employers and we can't share it, so we continually
>rewrite the same code others have already written, feeling no
>incentive to make it generally useful because we're the only ones
>who will ever use it anyway.


Leif has been attempting to change this by making available
a web page for a Cobol Shareware Library.  I have stuck a few
small things into it, but plan on putting a lot more stuff into it
over the next few months.

As our systems get more complex, and group gets larger, I
think there is a tremendous opportunity here to *start* sharing
code a lot more.  I have to evolve a serial device library, for
example.  I plan to make all the low level routines freeware.

I own Copyright on everything I write, which makes the job
easier.  However, there are lots of independents in this NG.
Sharing code makes everybody money.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3661c178.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <73sdeq$of4$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <73sdeq$of4$1@news.igs.net>...
>Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>...
>>COBOL has no tradition of freeware or public domain code.  Our code
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sharing code makes everybody money.


One of the reasons for a lack of sharing is that shared code
must be Documented code. Since documentation costs money
up front, management is often unwilling to pay for it, even if they
know that it will save money later (most managers don't care
about THEIR eventual replacement).

The shared code library that I'm promoting includes shared
documentation and therefore has a chance of being used.
I'll eventually cross-index everything for easier reference.

I accept Donald's wish with respect to his copyright, but shall
make a plea for Open Source, because it improves the
quality of the code. See: http://www.opensource.org
and
http://www.tuxedo.org/~esr/writings/cathedral-bazaar/cathedral-bazaar.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73sht8$r82$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net> <73sdeq$of4$1@news.igs.net> <3661c178.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
The stuff that I will be putting in will include source. One of
the reasons that I have not placed a lot more into the area
is that most of the code I have that would be suitable is
written for DOS only, and so is obsolete.  Another is that
a lot of it is written in MS Cobol, which is no longer available.

With Fujitsu making their Version 3 compiler available cheaply,
a lot of that is changing very fast. It is now quite simple to share
code, and with the effort that I see in this group towards
standardization, it is also becoming a lot more practical.

I think that in the past, the Cobol linkers (for the PC anyway)
were just not good enough to write code with too many levels
of subroutine calls.  Code did tend to be monolithic ... but
there is very little excuse for that anymore.  The styles that
I see evolving with pretty well unrestricted memory usage
are a lot different than what we coded in the past.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KIi82.10164$Sf1.5597123@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>`

```
I believe the problem here is simply programmer training and discipline.
Teach them to do it correctly, and enforce that they do it correctly.
One of the marks of good programmers is that they do this for themselves
without being forced.  Human nature being what it is, most people won't
do it without being required to do so.  It is my opinion that one of the
failures of OO is the idea that you can 'constrict' programmers into
doing it correctly by rules.  It's a bit like expecting a society to be
entirely controlled by a rules.  It doesn't work that way.  You must
first teach and 'evangelize' people to believe in the correct methods,
then you must enforce them to keep the lazy ones in line.  Without both,
no attempt to change a society or a bunch of programmers is going to be
really successful.  People have to know, and believe, and they have to
be occasionally 'reminded'. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662B383.3179F426@home.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com> <3661559B.1AA3@swbell.net>`

```
> COBOL has no tradition of freeware or public domain code.  Our code
> belongs to our employers and we can't share it, so we continually
> rewrite the same code others have already written, feeling no
> incentive to make it generally useful because we're the only ones
> who will ever use it anyway.

And we become pretty efficient at rewriting code too.  It doesn't take
long to write a program which does EXACTLY what is needed, no more, no
less, and which does not impact any other programs in our system.

There are big advantages to an integrated system which share code and
objects all over the place.  But as with any choice, there are costs
involved also.

I keep hearing about the advantages of maintaining a single object which
is used by the whole enterprise.  But in real life, I find those
interlinks harder to maintain and much harder to test.  In real life I
can't assume that testing it once is sufficient.  I test it every place
it exists.  It seems safer to change it for one program, test, &
implement, then change it for another program, test, & implement, etc.

I also don't like having one person maintaining a called program while
another person maintains the calling program.  Or worse replace those
with objects.
```

#### ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3671b800.9795654@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```
On Sat, 28 Nov 1998 18:13:59 -0500, Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
wrote:

>But in reality, how much
>code is actually suitable for reuse in other programs, and what methods
>are available to catalog and document these routines, after all these
>years.  Zilch !!

Thats cause of economics:
Since only a programmer could re-use the code, only a programmer would
get paid to find reused code. But if a programmer gets paid more to
write new code, then cataloging it would mean loss of income.

The other thing, which non-programmers don't understand, is re-use is
a buzz-word. It's one thing to say re-using code is a strategy. It's
another thing to actually go through an existing program, and find
source or data which *matches* the re-use portions, and make certain
the re-use itself doesn't get in the way.

Pretend you re-use a portion of code which is in a normal accounting
program. Now pretend you take that same code, and re-use it in a
device driver. A stupid programmer could simply cut and paste, or
place it into a copy file. A programmer with experience would know the
device drivers *compile* must be *assured* to still have interrupts
off or on, and the code as compact as possible.

The other thing is, re-use is nice, but eventually you get to a point
where a pointer to the re-used code is *harder* to maintain than
duplicating it. An example is porting mainframe code to PCs. A PC
editor might require you to load the source into seperate windows, or
just edit one file at a time. A mainframe editor might have the copy
books virtually pasted and updated. The mainframe code, when ported to
a PC, if it reuses too much would take forever to edit, *because* the
PC programmer would have to open and close for each piece of reused
code. The mainframe programmer would not understand this when
designing the code. And if the PC editor doesn't work like the
mainframe editor, are you going to hire a person to rewrite the
mainframe source code editor as well as porting the code? How many
others would invest in such a change?

Considering tech support gets $40/ call, it would cost $1000 for this
renovation, I would think, at least.

The main catch to code re-use, though, is to *pay* some programmer to
review code.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: "structured programming" & code reusability

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vZz82.1458$7l2.1064412@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>`

```
Bob Berman wrote in message <366083B7.5DE3@netbox.com>...
>I have heard & seen much about code reusability as a benefit of
>stuctured programming since the mid-70's when structured coding was the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>years.  Zilch !!

I have worked on large projects where as much as 93% of the code was
provided by reusable components. Getting that kind of reuse cannot be
attained using cloning, copybooks, subroutines or any of the code-centric
approaches (although they do help). It requires a paradigm shift to get the
organization to rethink how it builds systems.

Today, most software shops use what I would call a 'job shop' mentality to
writing software. They follow a waterfall methodology to define a number of
processes. Having defined these processes, they then assign a number of
analysts to define specifications which eventually get distributed to
programmers to crank code. Some time later all these independent pieces are
thrown together in a test environment for integration testing.

This kind of thinking is very much like the techniques used before the
industrial revolution, with a corresponding degree of productivity, quality,
etc. If industrial engineering techniques are applied to software
development then dramatic productivity increases can be achieved. Today the
term 'software engineering' is an oxymoron.

Check out Paul Bassett's book "Framing Software Reuse: Lessons from the Real
World" http://www.netron.com/pbbook.htm#sample for more details.


<<SNIP>>

>The reality is that most "production programmers" (as opposed to academics)
>simply don't have time to clean up this cloned code, and in fact don't
>normally even know of usable code routines other than those they have
>either written or  maintained personally, as there is no facility to
>search for these routines within an organization's code bank.

There are two issues here:

1) Making software reusable

Cloning is an ad-hoc technique which may help get the code out faster but it
leaves you with two pieces of code to maintain. Reuse needs to be designed
into a system from the start. The goal is to reduce the number of points of
maintenance.

If there is no time to find and use a reusable component, why is there time
to make changes to the same or similar code in several programs every time
it is maintained. The 'no time to do it right, plenty of time to do it over'
attitude is responsible for the sorry state of many systems.

2) Making reusable software accessible

I will grant you that making reusable software accessible is a huge problem.
There are numerous examples of failed projects on trying to create software
Repositories. Reusable software is an answer in search of a question.
Software documentation tends to be written in a similar fashion. So the hunt
for a reusable component is akin to playing Jeopardy. "I'll take date
validation routines for $100, Alex".

>
>I myself have found, over the years, that by the time I locate a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>into Dilbertism here).

There are some circumstances where it may be more cost effective. However if
you take maintenance into account (80% or more of IT dollars are spent on
maintenance), then the cost of maintaining custom code in all the programs
of a system, when compared to maintaining a single reusable source code
component that is sharedby all those programs, cannot be justified.

>
>Code reuse is an admirable goal, but the reality is far short of the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+

<a id="t-eafbd7b9a3"></a>
## Thread: AcuCobol - launch simple data entry app within a browser? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### AcuCobol - launch simple data entry app within a browser?

- **From:** rghcomputg@aol.com (Rghcomputg)
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
Greetings:

What is required to launch a simple data entry app within a browser? Initially,
this app would serve in an intranet, but ideally also on the internet. Are
there coding issues for IE vs Netscape?
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol - launch simple data entry app within a browser?

- **From:** mattr@mentis.com.au
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73t362$8r5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>,
  rghcomputg@aol.com (Rghcomputg) wrote:
> Greetings:
>
> What is required to launch a simple data entry app within a browser?
Initially,
> this app would serve in an intranet, but ideally also on the internet. Are
> there coding issues for IE vs Netscape?
>

Probably the most robust method would be using the AcuCobol-GT Web-Plugin,
maybe add AcuServer when you go Internet.  There aren't any worries about
whether it's Netscape or IE, everything inside the program is controlled by
the runtime.

If you were going to use CGI (built into AcuCobol-GT 4.0), the only IE vs
Netscape issues will be HTML formating.

AcuCorp has a book called The Programmers Guide to the Internet, which you
might find useful.

Matt Robinson
Support Consultant, Mentis Technologies
---------------------------------------
<insert something witty here>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol - launch simple data entry app within a browser?

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662ef6c$0$220@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
Use PERCobol (www.synkronix.com), your code will generally run "as is" on
the net.  (No plug-in needed..)

David



Rghcomputg wrote in message
<19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>...
>Greetings:
>
>What is required to launch a simple data entry app within a browser?
Initially,
>this app would serve in an intranet, but ideally also on the internet. Are
>there coding issues for IE vs Netscape?
>
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AcuCobol - launch simple data entry app within a browser?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36630e14.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com> <3662ef6c$0$220@nntp1.ba.best.com>`

```
>Rghcomputg wrote in message
><19981128182032.07074.00002049@ng-fu1.aol.com>...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>there coding issues for IE vs Netscape?


yes, there are some IE/Netscape issues. But with AOL's recent purchase
of Netscape and its decision to keep IE as its main browser, the
IE/Netscape issues may go away shortly as IE finally wins. I'm no
Micro$oft fan, but IE has since version 3.0 been the better browser
(faster, less buggy, more standards compliant, free, etc).


If you want to get a feeling for what a browser interface to a simple
data-entry program looks like and to see what kind of HTML could
be generated, go to http://www.etk.com and download the
"Web interface in 30 seconds"  fully functioning demo that
will take one of your file layouts (copybook) and generate
the browser/data-entry program automatically.
```

<a id="t-df71f59ccb"></a>
## Thread: Automated cobol year 2000 compliance. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Automated cobol year 2000 compliance.

- **From:** "William Gray" <wrg@ncinter.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,fj.lang.cobol,alt.talk.year2000,comp.software.year-2000,gov.topic.info.systems.year2000
- **Message-ID:** `<YIX72.278$td3.1259@news12.ispnews.com>`

```
Cyber Systems offers a total automated solution for for cobol year 2000
compliance.
http://www.ncinter.net/~wrg/CYBER.htm.
```

#### ↳ Re: Automated cobol year 2000 compliance.

- **From:** jmhart@world.std.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,fj.lang.cobol,alt.talk.year2000
- **Message-ID:** `<73s40a$gnn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<YIX72.278$td3.1259@news12.ispnews.com>`

```
In fact, there are dozens of tool and service vendors who can renovate COBOL
as well as other languages such as PL/I, RPG, C, and others. The best tools
employ data and control flow analysis to identify the date-sensitive items
(pattern matching and analysis is, in general, not adequate). Tools are
highle recommended for all but the smallest systems. Be aware, of course,
that tool quality varies from excellent to horrible (and everything in
between).

Feel free to contact me directly if you want more information on this subject.

John Hart
jmhart@world.std.com


In article <YIX72.278$td3.1259@news12.ispnews.com>,
  "William Gray" <wrg@ncinter.net> wrote:
> Cyber Systems offers a total automated solution for for cobol year 2000
> compliance.
> http://www.ncinter.net/~wrg/CYBER.htm.
>
>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-ef1c589569"></a>
## Thread: Beginner - Next record in index order — 8 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli)
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```
Hi,
  
   FILE-CONTROL.
   SELECT    INDEXED-FILE
                ASSIGN       TO  "F:\COBOLV3\PCOBOL32\OUTFILE"
                ORGANIZATION IS  INDEXED
                RECORD KEY   IS  ID-NUMBER          
                ACCESS MODE  IS  RANDOM.

  DATA DIVISION.
  FILE SECTION.
  FD   INDEXED-FILE.
  01   INDEXED-RECORD.
       02  ID-NUMBER     PIC 9(6).
       02  NAME-VALUE    PIC X(40).

  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01 RIGA                    PIC 999.
  01 CMP1                  PIC 9(6).
  01 CMP2                  PIC X(40). 

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

      OPEN I-O INDEXED-FILE.

      MOVE 10 TO RIGA
      PERFORM VARYING RIGA FROM RIGA BY 1 UNTIL RIGA = 20 
              MOVE ID-NUMBER  TO CMP1
              MOVE NAME-VALUE TO CMP2 
              DISPLAY CMP1  LINE RIGA COLUMN 2
              DISPLAY CMP2  LINE RIGA COLUMN 8
              READ INDEXED-FILE    //in this line  i want
              // to skip to the  *next*  record in the  *index order* 
              // but i cant' do it. 
              // I tried with :  access mode is Dynamic but the sequence of
              // records read and displayed is sequential.
              // I need a little help from you. 
              // TIA
              // Eugenio.
             
      END-PERFORM  
      GO TO LOOP-POINT.
*
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<csh82.397$Lx2.561@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```

Eugenio Belli wrote in message <36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Since the index order is sequential (by key value), it is not entirely
clear to me what you want to accomplish but you might try the
following:

Change the ACCESS MODE to DYNAMIC
In WORKING-STORAGE add the item:
01 EOF-FLAG PIC 9.

MOVE an-id-number TO ID-NUMBER.
MOVE 0 TO EOF-FLAG.
START INDEXED-FILE KEY IS NOT LESS THAN ID-NUMBER
    INVALID KEY
        MOVE 1 TO EOF-FLAG
END-START.
PERFORM VARYING RIGA FROM 10 BY 1
          UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 1 OR RIGA = 20
    READ INDEXED-FILE NEXT RECORD
      AT END
        MOVE 1 TO EOF-FLAG
      NOT AT END
        MOVE ID-NUMBER TO CMP1
        MOVE NAME-VALUE TO CMP2
        DISPLAY CMP1 LINE RIGA COLUMN 2
        DISPLAY CMP2 LINE RIGA COLUMN 8
    END-READ
END-PERFORM.

This will read 10 records displaying their values on lines 10 thru 19
of the display. The first of these records will be the record with an
ID-NUMBER value greater than or equal to the inital ID-NUMBER.
If fewer than 10 records are available, then only the available
records will be displayed.

If this is not what you intended, please explain in more detail.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** terrywin.nospam.@spectra.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662eb9d.1260743@supernews.spectra.net>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```
On Sun, 29 Nov 1998 18:47:30 GMT, swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli) wrote:

>Hi,
>  
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Try using access mode DYNAMIC with a START and READ NEXT statement.
Of course you'll have to alter your logic path to support this method.



HTH
Terry
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<366270a5.82613@news>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```
On Sun, 29 Nov 1998 18:47:30 GMT, swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli) wrote:

In other word (Clipper)

with a index like this : 
index on ID-NUMBER TO Fkey

if I write :
use OUTFILE index Fkey
For t=10  to 20
        cmp1:= ID-NUMBER
        cmp2:=NAME-VALUE
         @ t,2 say cmp1
          @ t,8 say cmp2
        SKIP
next

The result is a list in alphabetic order.
How I can do this in COBOL ?

TIA
Eugenio.
-
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662836e.4891857@news>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com> <366270a5.82613@news>`

```

Ooops...
My  examples is wrong

The index is : 
 index on NAME-VALUE TO Fkey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l7x82.415$Lx2.858@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com> <366270a5.82613@news> <3662836e.4891857@news>`

```

Eugenio Belli wrote in message <3662836e.4891857@news>...
>On Mon, 30 Nov 1998 10:33:25 GMT, swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> index on NAME-VALUE TO Fkey

COBOL does not allow the dynamic creation of an index. All indices
must be defined when the file is created. In the following, I added an
alternate key to allow moving through the file based upon the value
of the NAME-VALUE field.

   FILE-CONTROL.
   SELECT    INDEXED-FILE
                ASSIGN       TO  "F:\COBOLV3\PCOBOL32\OUTFILE"
                ORGANIZATION IS  INDEXED
                ACCESS MODE  IS  DYNAMIC
                RECORD KEY   IS  ID-NUMBER
                ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS NAME-VALUE
                        DUPLICATES ALLOWED.

  DATA DIVISION.
  FILE SECTION.
  FD   INDEXED-FILE.
  01   INDEXED-RECORD.
       02  ID-NUMBER     PIC 9(6).
       02  NAME-VALUE    PIC X(40).

  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01 RIGA                    PIC 999.
  01 CMP1                  PIC 9(6).
  01 CMP2                  PIC X(40).
  01 EOF-FLAG         PIC 9.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

      OPEN I-O INDEXED-FILE.

      MOVE a-name-value TO NAME-VALUE.
      MOVE 0 TO EOF-FLAG.
* In this START statement I tell the compiler to use the alternate key
* so that subsequent reads will be in the ascending order of that key.
* In this case, NAME-VALUE
      START INDEXED-FILE KEY IS NOT LESS THAN NAME-VALUE
          INVALID KEY
              MOVE 1 TO EOF-FLAG
      END-START.
      PERFORM VARYING RIGA FROM 10 BY 1
                UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 1 OR RIGA = 20
          READ INDEXED-FILE NEXT RECORD
            AT END
              MOVE 1 TO EOF-FLAG
            NOT AT END
              MOVE ID-NUMBER TO CMP1
              MOVE NAME-VALUE TO CMP2
              DISPLAY CMP1 LINE RIGA COLUMN 2
              DISPLAY CMP2 LINE RIGA COLUMN 8
          END-READ
      END-PERFORM.
      GO TO LOOP-POINT.
*

This will read 10 records displaying their values on lines 10 thru 19
of the display. The first of these records will be the record with an
NAME-VALUE greater than or equal to the inital NAME-VALUE.
If fewer than 10 records are available, then only the available
records will be displayed.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662ACCC.DB895CE5@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```
Eugenio :

This will work for Micro Focus, and most others.  You didn't statement
your compiler.

Make access method DYNAMIC and in the "READ" statement do a:

READ INDEXED-FILE NEXT RECORD
    AT END . . . .
    NOT AT END . . . 
END-READ

You can also use the START statement to begin somewhere other than BOF.

Regards,

Scott

Eugenio Belli wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> *
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner - Next record in index order

- **From:** swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662dbd2.5877763@news>`
- **References:** `<36618e8f.2692101@news.software.ibm.com>`

```
On Sun, 29 Nov 1998 18:47:30 GMT, swp@hi-net.it (Eugenio Belli) wrote:

Problem solved
I'm sorry.
-
```

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662B335.76CA987@bdssoftware.com>`

```
Dunno if this will help but this will get the login name with SCO ODT. 
You can modify it for other variables.
I hope I didn't make a typing error while copying this bit of code from
another screen.

      $set sourceformat"free" ans85
01  aaaa-user-name  pic x(30).
display "LOGNAME" upon environment-name
accept aaaa-user-name from environment-value
display aaaa-user-name
stop run.

<a id="t-6474f343ae"></a>
## Thread: Available to Train in Australia in June — 1 msgs

### Available to Train in Australia in June

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981129105045.16918.00001954@ng106.aol.com>`

```
Hi,

One of our instructors / course authors, Hunter Cobb, will be in Australia in
June '99 and he said he would be interested in doing some teaching while he's
there. He can teach just about all of our curriculum: TSO, ISPF, REXX, CLIST,
JCL, Assembler, COBOL, PL/I, DB2, VSAM, LE, debugging, CICS (check out
www.trainersfriend.com for the complete list and descriptions).

Hunter is the author of all our DB2 and PL/I courses, plus a few others. He has
a bacehlor's degree in math from Princeton University, has worked as a
programmer, manager, and other titles. Hunter has been a full time trainer and
course author with us now for over ten years and he always gets terrific
reviews.

Since he will be over there anyway, Hunter is willing to work for a rate below
our standard of USD3000 per class day for work outside the US. The rate for any
classes would be USD2400 per class day; this includes instructor expenses and
tuitions for up to 20 students. Customer would have to work with us to get the
proper work permits / papers needed to do this, including figuring the best way
to structure things in light of any relevant taxes.

If you're interested, check out the website and send us an email, give us a
call, or send us a fax, and we'll go from there.

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-66e40f94f2"></a>
## Thread: student need help please — 7 this month / 26 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### student need help please

- **From:** marc <marc_d2000@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662F6E2.6B402CB9@yahoo.com>`

```
Hello i'm a beginner in cobol
i made a little program, but it doesn't run, i don't anderstand, the
program always reject my accept, it pass it ;-(
(when i use cobol650, the linker say that object is invalid and when i
use compiler from deskware when it arrive at accept, it pass it ;-( )

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. marc.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 NOM PIC X(15).
       01 mont-achat PIC 9(10).
       01 remise PIC 9(10).
       01 cli-ex PIC X(10).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Calcul-rem.
           PERFORM obtention.
           PERFORM UNTIL cli-ex = "faux"
           PERFORM Calcul-remise
           PERFORM sortie
           END-PERFORM.
       STOP RUN.

       obtention.
           DISPLAY "donner le nom ou fff pour stopper".
           ACCEPT NOM.
           IF nom = "fff" THEN MOVE "faux" TO cli-ex
           ELSE MOVE "vrai" TO cli-ex
           DISPLAY "donner le montant d'achat"
           ACCEPT mont-achat
           END-IF.
       Calcul-remise.
           IF mont-achat >= 5000 THEN MULTIPLY mont-achat BY .25 GIVING
remise
           ELSE
           IF mont-achat > 3000 THEN MULTIPLY mont-achat BY .15 GIVING
remise
           ELSE
           MOVE 0 to remise
           END-IF
           END-IF.
       sortie.
           DISPLAY "le nom".
           DISPLAY NOM.
           DISPLAY "mont achat".
           DISPLAY mont-achat.
           DISPLAY "remise".
           DISPLAY remise.

       END PROGRAM marc.
```

#### ↳ Re: student need help please

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>

Marc, first your Cobol program should be correct.
See below for a correct version of your program;
then the compiler must work. It is not likely that
any of the two you mention work, so better go
get a real compiler, e.g. at http://www.adtools.com

marc wrote in message <3662F6E2.6B402CB9@yahoo.com>...
>Hello i'm a beginner in cobol
>i made a little program, but it doesn't run, i don't anderstand, the
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. MARC.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 NOM PIC X(15).
       01 mont-achat PIC 9(10).
       01 remise PIC 9(10).
       01 cli-ex PIC X(10).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Calcul-rem.
           PERFORM obtention
           PERFORM UNTIL cli-ex = "faux"
               PERFORM Calcul-remise
               PERFORM sortie
               PERFORM obtention
           END-PERFORM
           STOP RUN
           .
       obtention.
           DISPLAY "donner le nom ou fff pour stopper"
           ACCEPT NOM
           IF NOM = "fff"
               MOVE "faux" TO cli-ex
           ELSE
               MOVE "vrai" TO cli-ex
               DISPLAY "donner le montant d'achat"
               ACCEPT mont-achat
           END-IF
           .
       Calcul-remise.
           IF mont-achat >= 5000
               MULTIPLY mont-achat BY .25 GIVING remise
           ELSE
           IF mont-achat > 3000
               MULTIPLY mont-achat BY .15 GIVING remise
           ELSE
               MOVE 0 TO remise
           END-IF
           END-IF
           .
       sortie.
           DISPLAY "le nom"
           DISPLAY NOM.
           DISPLAY "mont achat"
           DISPLAY mont-achat
           DISPLAY "remise"
           DISPLAY remise
           .

```

#### ↳ Re: student need help please

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3663434b.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Marc,

ACCEPT NOM is what you have.

ACCEPT NOM FROM DATE (or DAY-OF-WEEK, or TIME, or terminal-id, etc.) is what
you want.

You cannot do ACCEPT NOM without identifying what is getting accepted into
NOM.

The same goes for all the other ACCEPT statements you use in the code.

I'm not sure how your compiler treats that END-PERFORM in Calcul-Rem, but
mine would blow up if I had coded it that way.  Usually END-PERFORM is only
for inline PERFORMS, and this PERFORM is a normal out-of-line PERFORM.


marc wrote in message <3662F6E2.6B402CB9@yahoo.com>...
>Hello i'm a beginner in cobol
>i made a little program, but it doesn't run, i don't anderstand, the
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

ACCEPT NOM is a valid form of accept. On a PC it will wait until
something is keyed at the keyboard.

The END-PERFORM looks wrong because indentation is non-existent.
The END-PERFORM is associated with the PERFORM UNTIL that is
three lines above.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith

```
Chris Osborne wrote in message <3663434b.0@news2.uswest.net>...
>You cannot do ACCEPT NOM without identifying what is getting accepted into
>NOM.
>The same goes for all the other ACCEPT statements you use in the code.

You CAN accept from nothing. Don't need a FROM.

>
>I'm not sure how your compiler treats that END-PERFORM in Calcul-Rem, but
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

If you fix the indentation, you'll see that it IS an inline PERFORM:

        Calcul-rem.
            PERFORM obtention.
            PERFORM UNTIL cli-ex = "faux"
                    PERFORM Calcul-remise
                    PERFORM sortie
            END-PERFORM.
            STOP RUN.

The STOP RUN should not start in col 8 or 2 either.
```

<a id="t-8ecfce6e34"></a>
## Thread: ELIF Proposal — 8 this month / 91 total

### ELIF Proposal

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981130123924.16775.00000169@ng131.aol.com>`

```
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
Date: 29 Nov 1998 06:47:11 GMT
Was kind enough to commented within an ongoing discussion of possibilty of
having an ELIF construct in the COBOL language:

<<
..., most of the modern languages have it.  In Fortran there's an explicit
ELSIF (I think that's how it's spelled), in COBOL-85 it's implemented by
the slightly obtuse EVALUTATE statement, and in C, Algol, and Pascal
there's no need for a special construct, because ELSE followed immediately
by IF is semantically identical to an ELSE-IF.

Judson (McClendon) mentions he's strung so many ELSE IFs together that the
compiler
blew it's stack.  In COBOL-85 this is just a fact of life, because the
compiler has to know how many END-IFs can follow (though a really cleaver
compiler could just have a counter of nested ELSEs); but in a competently
written COBOL-74 compiler, ELSE IF should not increase the nesting level.
>>

His posting was, in part, a response to one of my own. His surveying is
probably more complete than my own, and probably he is right that most
languages have an ELSE IF construct than do not. But the view to the effect
that in COBOL, "...there's no need for a special construct, because ELSE
followed immediately by IF is semantically identical to an ELSE-IF.", deserves
further interaction if you don't mind.

ELSE followed immediately by IF is not _syntactically_ identical to an ELSE-IF.
This creates a weaknesses in COBOL.  

As a standalone statement a long IF-ELSE-IF sequence tends to be indented
further and further in COBOL programs. In many cases that indentation is not
representing anything semantically. In other words, IF-ELSE-IF constructs are
really just sequential cascades of prioritized mutually exclusive tests.  These
sequences bear no more  structural relationship than do sequences of imperative
statements.

Furthermore, IF-ELSE-IF expressions are frequently followed by abusive END-IF
sequences.  In a standalone statement these END-IF dragons are useless. When an
IF-ELSE-IF is imbedded within some other conditional expression, the nesting
becomes compounded, and the denesting END-IFs frequently get confused.
_Syntactically_ ELSE IF constructs are a generator of END-IF tokens,
_syntactically, ELIFs are not. This difference has a dramatic impact on the
human machine interface.

The END-IF dragon consumes valuable vertical realestate. As some generous
commentors have indicated there is a strong tendency for people to place the
ELSE and IF on two separate lines, further removing related code from a coders
focal point. An ELIF could not be placed upon two lines. There would be no
END-IF dragon on an ELIF sequence, just one little puppy, END-IF.

ELIF expressions could easily be nested inside of other conditionals. This is
actually a double benefit. Relatively simple IF-THEN-ELSE expressions are
sometimes broken into pieces because one of the branches has a IF-ELSE-IF
sequence that causes the over all structure to be hard to undersand as a result
of indentation.  An ELIF construct would actually make some of these
salvagable, reducing by a _small amount_ the need to break conditional
expressions into separate paragraphs simply because of abuse of the horizontal
attribute of the visual interface with excessive indentation.

In many cases EVALUATE is an answer to this.  But EVALUATE has serious
problems.  To begin with it is verbose, somewhat more prone to typing errors
then smaller constructs. But more importantly EVALUATE is structurally rigid. 
Your WHEN tests most have the same topology as that announced in the EVALUATE,
unless you code EVALUATE TRUE, at which point the header of the EVALUATE
dissolves to superfluidity that takes a chunk of vertical realestate and helps
reveal that the header itself always necessitates a needless increment of
indentation. 

EVALUATE is very useful. It has helped a lot.  If you agree with what it has
helped us do, why would you resist another construct that offers further help. 

EVALUATE has as its greatest strength the feature that it implies a subject for
a sequence of tests. The need for the alternative EVALAUTE TRUE syntax shows
that it is somewhat dubious when used outside of its strongest area.

There are a number of situations in which the subject or object of tests vary a
lot through a sequence. It is not rare that early test in a sequence are just
short circuit triggers. For example, if the subject is not there or not
testable as a numeric, then don't do the following tests.  Same for the object.
 Today to use EVALUATE in these situations, we have IF-ELSE-IF sequences
indenting further and further, until by the time we get to the core EVALUATE
with its extra indentation the real tests in the WHEN clauses are frequently
severely indented.  I believe ELIF would relief many of these situations of
their artificial horizontal pressure.

Also, I must add, that EVALUATE statements start simple, and get complex late
in life. They are very hard to maintain if there is a need to change the
topology of the conditionals on just one branch of many. This is especailly
true if you are committed to an early naive coding form that tries to express
the subject in the EVALUATE phrase (in contrast to the WHEN clauses). In
comparison, an individual branch of an ELIF sequence is highly modifiable,
having all the flexibility of an IF statement.

A WHEN clause is usually constrained to the shape of the world projected by the
EVALUATE phrase. 

It is not that EVALUATE is bad. We just need more.

Also I would argue that ELSE-IF is easier to teach than EVALUATE. And indeed,
that the EVALUATE construct actually trains people to think in
non-combinatorial fashion, leading to code that is broken out into many pieces
far removed from one another.

In the easiest case it is better, as in faster and more clear, to make combined
test where relevant.  In the cases that are more difficult to understand I
think we get conjunction of tests when it is irrelevant and dangerous to
combine because the EVALUATE topology makes it hard to combine in the subset
where appropriate. Thus we have patch code that starts out, AM I HERE, BUT I
SHOULDN'T BE, THEN GO TO MY-FAVORITE-EXIT. (But this latter point is involved).

In parting, let me add as an important aside, concerning stacks in the compiler
program, ELIFs would not have to be stacked as ELSE IF sequences are.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: ELIF Proposal

- **References:** `<19981130123924.16775.00000169@ng131.aol.com>`

```

>>ELSE followed immediately by IF is not _syntactically_ identical to an
ELSE-IF.
>This creates a weaknesses in COBOL.
>ELIF expressions could easily be nested inside of other conditionals.

delays.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ELIF Proposal

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)

<<

long.  

Your suggestion of ELSE-IF _is_ more readable and has the attractive feature
that it is graphically distinct, in contrast to ELIF which looks a lot like
ELSE. The tag 'ELIF' may be useful for NG discussions,being tight.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk

#### ↳ Re: ELIF Proposal

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-oEmIvfLzlGxt@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<19981130123924.16775.00000169@ng131.aol.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Nov 1998 17:39:24, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

> Also, I must add, that EVALUATE statements start simple, and get complex late
> in life. They are very hard to maintain if there is a need to change the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Robert, 

I appreciate your posting!  Lots to think about.  I started using 
Evaluate about two years ago.  I have since moved to a shop that has 
used it much longer.  That said, I have had the distinct pleasure of 
having the maintain this code.  I find the Evaluate construct EASIER 
to maintain than the old nested if style of structure.

I think the harder to maintain versions might stem from programmers 
not understanding the construct fully before implementing it.  There 
is extreme power in this statement.

When you start combining multiple selection subjects, and using ANY, 
you find that it can condense a complex decision tree into an easy to 
UNDERSTAND and maintain segment of code.

The fact that it exits upon the first true condition means that it 
DOES have to be carefully constructed.  It requires THOUGHT up front. 
However, this feature also means that it can be efficient.  (In many, 
but not all implementations <G>).

Can you site an example we can hash about where your "need for more" 
is expressed?
```

#### ↳ Re: ELIF Proposal

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73v6ck$c4n@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981130123924.16775.00000169@ng131.aol.com>`

```
If someone (other than me - given my current reputation at J4) were to write
a "concise" request for a new feature "ELSE-IF" for the FOLLOWING Standard
(not the one currently in draft status), there is a place in the J4 agenda
where this could be placed.

I would be happy to pass along the suggestion or you can send it directly to
J4 via its chair at
    das@microfocus.com
(but please make it clear that it is NOT for the current draft in progress)

Note: I have read the posts showing how it is syntactically separate from
just another ELSE IF - but have not understood whether this means there
would also need to be another scope terminator, e.g. END-ELSE-IF  (which
seems "clumsy" to me).

Note2: When writing your suggestion, please include a BRIEF reason why you
think it would be a useful enhancement over the current "ELSE IF" *and*
"EVALUATE" structures.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ELIF Proposal

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36634B6F.73A9@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<19981130123924.16775.00000169@ng131.aol.com> <73v6ck$c4n@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
[snip -- concerning the proposed ELIF or ELSE-IF syntax] 
> 
> Note: I have read the posts showing how it is syntactically separate from
> just another ELSE IF - but have not understood whether this means there
> would also need to be another scope terminator, e.g. END-ELSE-IF  (which
> seems "clumsy" to me).

Not only clumsy, but also illogical.  END-ELSE-IF would appear to
terminate only the most recent unterminated ELSE-IF.  A chain of
ELSE-IFs would then require a chain of END-ELSE-IFs, and we'd be
back where we started.

A chain of ELSE-IFs would start with an IF, and it is the scope of
that IF that we want to terminate.  Hence the most natural terminator
would be END-IF.  Thus this keyword would be overloaded, as has 
happened to other keywords such as TO and THRU.  It's not obvious to
me that this overloading would lead to any serious ambiguity.

I don't like spelling this new token "ELSE-IF" because it invites
troublesome typos -- all you have to do is omit the hyphen, or put
one in when you shouldn't.  I would prefer "ELSIF", as it is spelled
in PL/SQL.  It's less vulnerable to typos, but visually is more
distinct from "ELSE" than "ELIF" is, being one character longer.

Other possibilities, with or without hyphens:

	OR-IF
	OTHERWISE-IF
	ON-THE-OTHER-HAND-IF

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

---

<a id="t-1e5336402a"></a>
## Thread: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix — 4 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** andyho99@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi,

Currently, someone is modifying our Cobol program for Y2K compliance. What she
did is using Oracle system call to get current Y2K compliant date. I wonder
there should be a easier way to achieve this. In C program, we could make Unix
system call to get a date. Can't Cobol do the same thing?

Thanks, Andy

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp@netdoornospam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fMI82.2577$b53.12893@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

andyho99@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Currently, someone is modifying our Cobol program for Y2K compliance. What
she
>did is using Oracle system call to get current Y2K compliant date. I wonder
>there should be a easier way to achieve this. In C program, we could make
Unix
>system call to get a date. Can't Cobol do the same thing?


Certainly.  ACCEPT data-name FROM DATE is the old format and returns YYMMDD.
Some compilers can have that statement return YYYYMMDD as an option or the
syntax ACCEPT data-name FROM CENTURY-DATE might work.  If neither of these
work with your compiler, a subprogram or copied code can look at your two
digit year and insert 19 or 20 accordingly.  HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73uhqh$4of@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

andyho99@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

>

Unix
>system call to get a date. Can't Cobol do the same thing?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Yes,

  Move Function Current-Date (1:8) to full-date

works with all Micro Focus COBOLs under Unix for the last 8+ years.  (There
are also some newer methods that will work with the newer compilers.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K solution in Microfocus Cobol under Unix

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3662CDB2.A73F2CF@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<73udug$aq6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


andyho99@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Yes it can.

MOVE FUNCTION current-date TO <receiving field>

The format of the receiving field is  ccyymmddhhsshh+oo where:

yyyy - four digit year
mm - month
dd - day
hh - hour past midnight
mm - minutes past midnight
ss - seconds past the minute
hh - hundredths of second 
+  = + or - for offset from GMT
oo - gmt time offset hours

My explanation is a little sketchy and the GMT offset stuff is a little
more involved, nevertheless, the intrinsic function "current date" is
well documented (and simple).

Regards,

Scott
jj - gmt time offset minutes
```

<a id="t-70d914b64a"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus And dates — 2 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus And dates

- **From:** "Frik Lombard" <lombardfj@sabc.co.za>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73u598$ig7$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
When i use tha accept w-date from date the system returns a date in the
format yymmdd. What can i do for the system to return a 8 digit date fro me
in the format ccyymmdd.

I am using a Microfocus personal cobol v 2.2 and win 95.

Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus And dates

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W_w82.10755$Sf1.5995640@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<73u598$ig7$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
Frik Lombard wrote:
>When i use tha accept w-date from date the system returns a date in the
>format yymmdd. What can i do for the system to return a 8 digit date fro me
>in the format ccyymmdd.
>
>I am using a Microfocus personal cobol v 2.2 and win 95.

What you are looking for is FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE, added with the
1989 update to the COBOL-85 standard.  It returns a 21 character
alphanumeric value formatted like this:

01  HOLD-CURRENT-DATE.
    03  HCD-YEAR            PIC  9999.  GREGORIAN YEAR
    03  HCD-MONTH           PIC  99.    01-12
    03  HCD-DAY             PIC  99.    01-31
    03  HCD-HOUR            PIC  99.    00-23
    03  HCD-MINUTE          PIC  99.    00-59
    03  HCD-SECOND          PIC  99V99. 00.00-59.99
    03  HCD-GREENWICH-DIFF  PIC  X.     0,+,-
                        '0' = GREENWICH TIME (ZERO)
                        '+' = AHEAD OF GREENWICH (SEE DIFF BELOW)
                        '-' = BEHIND GREENWICH   (SEE DIFF BELOW)
    03  HCD-DIFF-HOURS      PIC  99.    00-12
    03  HCD-DIFF-MINUTES    PIC  99.    00-59

    MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO HOLD-CURRENT-DATE.
```

professionals to our permanent staff. We have current assignments

professional staff has the option of salary or hourly pay with all 
the added benefits.  We also subcontract to legitimate corporations

hire directly. For further information regarding our current

be contacted as soon as possible. US citizens or permanent residents

Dear Mainframe Professionals:

I am an employment professional with ACS 
Government Solutions Group, Inc.
(GSG).	I wanted to let you and others in this 
field know that I'm currently

and modify existing programs
for our Department of Education Contract located 
in Rockville, MD.

The
first position is full-time and responsibilities will include evaluating and 
modifying existing programs to apply changes in processing or the type and 
content of reports or files and developing new software and catalogued 
procedures from the technical specifications prepared by the Systems
Analyst(s). 
The position requires 5-7 years programming experience on major
software 
development projects involving COBOL, JCL, MVS, and JES2. 
MicroFocus 
COBOL is desirable. 

The second position is full-time and
responsibilities will include providing expert 
technical advice to the
project staff and the government on all aspects of 
hardware and software
capabilities and characteristics. Will also be responsible 
for reviewing
resource and equipment plans and technical and database 
specifications, 
providing guidance to the project staff in how to establish VSAM 
and
relational database management system files, COBOL code and JCL 
procedures
to maximize processing efficiency and to provide data integrity and 
data
security.  The position requires 6-8 years programming experience on major 
software development projects involving COBOL, Extra, VSAM, and Easytrieve. 
Must possess excellent oral and written communication skills.

ACS GSG is a
leading information technology-outsourcing firm for the Federal 
government
generating over $1.4 billion in revenues.  We offer an excellent 
benefits &
compensation package that includes tuition reimbursement, a 401(k) 
plan, &
medical, dental, & life insurance.  For more information regarding ACS,

someone you may know in
this field.  For more information regarding these and 
other opportunities at

and 5:30 pm.  Please keep in mind that I am 
actively sourcing for this

looking forward to hearing from you.

Peter J. Nasou

GSG

```
Again.  This seems like a beginner's problem, but I have been
fiddling with it for three hours now, so I will throw it out to the
group.  Fujitsu version 4, not PowerCobol.

I wrote a whole pile of code that assigns print files to PRINTER-n,
as documented.  I tested them on my network, where all printer ports
are mapped to network printers.  It works just fine.  I then installed
on a standalone computer. I get an open error whenever I try to
open a printer file.

I can copy files to lpt1: under dos, and the printer works fine. It
is installed as a generic printer, and is a standard dot matrix type
printing on pre-printed forms.

I have tried assigning the printer to PRINTER, PRINTER-n, LPT1,
LPT1:, PRN, PRN:, "LPT1", "LPT1:", "PRINTER", data-name(with
each of the above variants), etc., and cannot get it to print a single
byte.  Having to install a server to run a single dot matrix printer
printing a page per 35 seconds seems a bit extreme ... any ideas
what I am doing wrong?
```

generic dot matrix printer.  If I hook up a laser, it works fine.

a font to use.

a font file at run time.
